Since $n_1$ and $n_2$ are fixed for a given spectral line (e.g., K$_\alpha$: $n_1=1, n_2=2$), the bracketed term becomes a constant $K'$:

$$K' = R_\infty \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$E_{photon} = K' (Z - \sigma)^2$$

**Step 5: Define the Constant K for the Line Series**

For a particular series (K, L, or M), the constant $K$ absorbs all fixed quantities:

$$\boxed{E = K(Z - \sigma)^2}$$

Since $E = h\nu$, we can also write:

$$\boxed{\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - \sigma), \quad \text{where } a = \sqrt{\frac{K}{h}}}$$

This is **Moseley's Law**, the cornerstone of EDS-based elemental identification.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Identifying an Unknown Peak

**Problem**: A K$_\alpha$ X-ray emission is observed at energy **$E = 6.40$ keV** from an unknown sample. Using Moseley's Law, identify the element. Given: For K$_\alpha$ series, $K = 1.04 \times 10^{-2}$ keV and $\sigma \approx 1$.

**Step 1: Apply Moseley's Law**

$$E = K(Z - \sigma)^2$$

$$6.40 = (1.04 \times 10^{-2})(Z - 1)^2$$

**Step 2: Isolate $(Z-1)^2$**

$$(Z - 1)^2 = \frac{6.40}{1.04 \times 10^{-2}} = 615.38$$

**Step 3: Take the Square Root**

$$Z - 1 = \sqrt{615.38} = 24.81$$

**Step 4: Solve for Z**

$$Z = 25.81 \approx 26$$

**Step 5: Identify the Element**

$Z = 26$ corresponds to **Iron (Fe)**.

> [!NOTE] **Practical Consideration**
> Real EDS systems use automated software with calibrated standard references. The Moseley's Law calculation is shown for **exam purposes** and to demonstrate the underlying physics.

---

### 3.3 Electron-Hole Pair Generation in the Detector — Derivation

**Step 1**: An X-ray photon of energy $E_{photon}$ enters a Si detector.

**Step 2**: It interacts via the **photoelectric effect**, liberating a high-energy photoelectron.

**Step 3**: The photoelectron loses energy by creating **electron-hole pairs** in the silicon lattice. The average energy required to create one pair is the **ionization energy $\epsilon_{Si} = 3.8$ eV** at 77 K.

**Step 4**: The number of pairs generated:

$$N = \frac{E_{photon}}{\epsilon_{Si}} = \frac{E_{photon}}{3.8 \text{ eV}}$$

**Step 5**: The charge collected is:

$$Q = N \cdot e = \frac{E_{photon} \cdot e}{3.8 \text{ eV}}$$

**Example**: For Mn K$_\alpha$ ($E = 5.895$ keV):

$$N = \frac{5895}{3.8} = 1551 \text{ pairs}$$

This charge is then converted to a voltage pulse, the height of which is proportional to $E_{photon}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EDS System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete interaction pipeline from electron source to elemental identification. This is the most commonly drawn schematic for KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Electron Source FEG or W Filament] --> A1[Accelerating Voltage 5 to 30 kV]
    A1 --> A2[Electromagnetic Lenses Condenser and Objective]
    A2 --> A3[Focused Electron Beam Probe 1 nm to 1 micrometer]
    A3 --> A4[Sample Surface Nanomaterial under analysis]
    A4 --> A5[Inner Shell Ionization K L M ejection]
    A5 --> A6{Decay Pathway}
    A6 -->|Radiative| A7[Characteristic X Ray Emission Unique Energy]
    A6 -->|Non Radiative| A8[Auger Electron Emission]
    A7 --> A9[Si Li or SDD Detector at 77 K]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Electron Hole Pair Generation Signal Proportional to Energy]
    A10 --> A11[Preamplifier Charge Sensitive]
    A11 --> A12[Main Amplifier Pulse Shaping]
    A12 --> A13[Multichannel Analyzer MCA Energy Binning]
    A13 --> A14[EDS Spectrum Intensity vs Energy keV]
    A14 --> A15[Elemental Identification Software ZAF Correction]
    A15 --> A16[Final Report Atomic Percent and Weight Percent]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation Guide**
> - **Left to Right Flow**: Energy cascade from generation to display.
> - **Diamond Node A6**: Represents the bifurcation between radiative and non-radiative decay — a key concept in fluorescence yield.
> - **Two paths to A9**: Both X-rays and Auger electrons carry elemental information, but only X-rays are detected in EDS (Auger electrons require AES).

---

### 4.2 Spectral Line Family Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AtomShell
        K1[K Shell n=1]
        L1[L Shell n=2]
        M1[M Shell n=3]
    end
    L1 -->|K alpha| K1
    M1 -->|K beta| K1
    M1 -->|L alpha| L1
    K1 --> A1[Characteristic X Ray Photon]
    L1 --> A1
    M1 --> A1
```

**Reading the Diagram**:
- **K$_\alpha$**: $L \rightarrow K$ transition (most intense, most commonly used)
- **K$_\beta$**: $M \rightarrow K$ transition
- **L$_\alpha$**: $M \rightarrow L$ transition
- The arrow thickness (in reality) corresponds to **transition probability** — K$_\alpha$ > K$_\beta$ for most elements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS). List any two advantages and one limitation of the technique.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Definition (1 Mark):**
EDS is a microanalytical technique used for the elemental analysis of materials. It detects **characteristic X-rays** emitted from a sample when excited by a high-energy electron beam, allowing identification of elements based on the unique energy of these X-rays as described by **Moseley's Law**.

**Advantages (1 Mark — ½ each):**
1. **Simultaneous multi-element detection** — entire spectrum (0–30 keV) acquired in seconds.
2. **Non-destructive** with minimal sample preparation.
3. High spatial resolution (~1 µm in SEM, ~1 nm in STEM mode).
4. Easy integration with SEM/TEM.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Poor sensitivity to **light elements (Z < 11)** such as Li, Be, B due to low fluorescence yield and X-ray absorption.
- Detection limit is typically **~0.1–1 wt%**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State and explain Moseley's Law. Why is it important in EDS analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):**
Moseley's Law relates the frequency (or energy) of characteristic X-rays to the atomic number of the emitting element:

$$\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - \sigma) \quad \text{or} \quad E = K(Z - \sigma)^2$$

Where $Z$ is the atomic number, $\sigma$ is the screening constant, and $a, K$ are constants for a given line series.

**Explanation (1 Mark):**
The law arises from the quantized energy transitions between inner electron shells. The energy of the emitted X-ray photon is uniquely determined by $Z$ (and the transition involved), making each element produce a unique set of "fingerprint" peaks.

**Importance in EDS (1 Mark):**
It provides the **theoretical foundation** for elemental identification in EDS. Since each element has a unique $(Z, E)$ pair, measuring the X-ray energy allows unambiguous identification of the element present in the sample. This transforms EDS from a simple intensity measurement into a powerful **qualitative and quantitative** analytical tool.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern**
> Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question is typically split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy with a neat block diagram. Discuss the various X-ray emission lines (K, L, M series). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive Moseley's Law. An unknown sample emits K$_\alpha$ X-rays at 8.04 keV. Identify the element using Moseley's relation, given $K = 1.04 \times 10^{-2}$ keV and $\sigma = 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Principle & X-ray Lines (7 Marks):**

The principle of EDS involves bombarding a sample with a focused high-energy electron beam inside an SEM or TEM. The incident electrons ionize the inner shells of atoms in the sample. When the ionized atoms return to the ground state, electrons from outer shells fall into the inner-shell vacancies, releasing the energy difference as **characteristic X-rays** of unique energy.

**X-ray emission lines arise from electronic transitions between inner shells** (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):

| Line | Transition | Energy Range (typical) | Relative Intensity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| K$_\alpha$ | $L \rightarrow K$ | 0.5 – 70 keV | **Highest (≈ 2× K$_\beta$)** |
| K$_\beta$ | $M \rightarrow K$ | 0.5 – 70 keV | Lower than K$_\alpha$ |
| L$_\alpha$ | $M \rightarrow L$ | 0.5 – 20 keV | Used for heavy elements |
| M$_\alpha$ | $N \rightarrow M$ | < 3 keV | Very low energy, absorbed easily |

**Selection Rules** governing allowed transitions:
1. $\Delta n \neq 0$ (must be different shells)
2. $\Delta l = \pm 1$ (orbital angular momentum must change by 1)
3. $\Delta j = 0, \pm 1$ (total angular momentum rules)

**Block Diagram** (2 Marks): As shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**(b) Moseley's Law Derivation & Numerical (7 Marks):**

The detailed derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes (5 marks).

**Numerical Identification (2 Marks):**

Given: $E = 8.04$ keV, $K = 1.04 \times 10^{-2}$ keV, $\sigma = 1$

$$E = K(Z - \sigma)^2$$

$$8.04 = (1.04 \times 10^{-2})(Z - 1)^2$$

$$(Z - 1)^2 = \frac{8.04}{1.04 \times 10^{-2}} = 773.08$$

$$Z - 1 = 27.80 \implies Z = 28.80 \approx 29$$

**Element Identified: Copper (Cu, Z = 29).**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the components of an EDS detector system. Compare the Si(Li) detector and the Silicon Drift Detector (SDD). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the qualitative and quantitative analysis of an EDS spectrum. What are the ZAF correction factors used in quantitative analysis? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) EDS Detector System (7 Marks):**

**Components of an EDS detector system** (1 Mark each, 5 Marks total):

1. **Detector Crystal**: Si(Li) or SDD, kept at liquid nitrogen temperature (77 K) to reduce thermal noise and prevent Li migration.
2. **Field-Effect Transistor (FET)**: Mounted close to the crystal, acts as the first amplification stage.
3. **Preamplifier**: Converts the charge pulse into a voltage step.
4. **Main Amplifier**: Shapes and amplifies the voltage pulse (typically to 0–10 V).
5. **Multichannel Analyzer (MCA)**: Sorts pulses by amplitude (energy) and bins them into channels, producing the spectrum.
6. **Display/Computer**: Real-time spectrum display and analysis software.

**Comparison Table** (2 Marks):

| Feature | Si(Li) Detector | Silicon Drift Detector (SDD) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operating Temperature | 77 K (LN₂) required | Can operate at -30°C (Peltier) |
| Resolution at Mn K$_\alpha$ | ~130–135 eV | ~121–130 eV |
| Counting Rate | Lower (~10 kcps) | **Very high (>100 kcps)** |
| Cooling Down Time | Hours to days | Minutes |
| Maintenance | High (LN₂ refill) | **Low (Peltier cooling)** |
| Output Count Rate | Limited by FET | **Limited by anode capacitance** |
| Era | Older (1970s) | **Modern (2000s onward)** |

**(b) Qualitative and Quantitative Analysis & ZAF Correction (7 Marks):**

**Qualitative Analysis (2 Marks):**
- The X-ray energy peaks are identified by their position on the energy axis.
- Each element produces a set of characteristic peaks (K, L, or M lines).
- The energy is matched against a database (e.g., NIST X-ray database) to identify the element.
- Software automatically marks peaks and labels elements.

**Quantitative Analysis (2 Marks):**
- The intensity (peak area/height) of a characteristic peak is proportional to the concentration of that element.
- The basic equation is the **Castaing "First Approximation"**:

$$C_A = \frac{I_A}{I_{A,std}}$$

Where $C_A$ is the mass fraction of element $A$, $I_A$ is its measured intensity, and $I_{A,std}$ is the intensity from a pure standard.

**ZAF Correction Factors (3 Marks):**

Due to matrix effects, the simple ratio above is inaccurate. The **ZAF correction** accounts for:

$$C_A = Z \times A \times F \times \frac{I_A}{I_{A,std}}$$

| Factor | Name | Accounts For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Z** | Atomic Number | Differences in electron backscattering and stopping power between sample and standard |
| **A** | Absorption | Absorption of generated X-rays within the sample matrix |
| **F** | Fluorescence | Secondary fluorescence (additional X-rays generated by other elements' X-rays) |

Modern EDS software performs these corrections automatically using algorithms like $\phi(\rho z)$ or the PAP model.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting screening constant $\sigma$**: Many students write $E = KZ^2$ instead of $E = K(Z - \sigma)^2$. Always include the screening effect — it costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Auger electrons with X-rays**: In the decay pathway, students often list only one. Examiners look for the bifurcation into radiative vs. non-radiative paths.
> 3. **Writing K$_\alpha$ and K$_\beta$ transitions incorrectly**: K$_\alpha$ is **L → K**, not M → K. This is a common error worth 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing EDS with WDS**: WDS (Wavelength Dispersive Spectroscopy) uses crystal diffraction and has better resolution (~5 eV) but is much slower. EDS uses solid-state detectors. Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Ignoring the ZAF correction in quantitative analysis**: Forgetting to mention matrix effects in Part B answers costs at least 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **EDS** = Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy; detects characteristic X-rays from electron-beam excitation.
- [ ] **Principle**: Inner-shell ionization → outer-shell electron fills vacancy → X-ray photon emitted.
- [ ] **Moseley's Law**: $E = K(Z - \sigma)^2$ — the foundation of elemental identification.
- [ ] **Key Transitions**: K$_\alpha$ (L→K), K$_\beta$ (M→K), L$_\alpha$ (M→L).
- [ ] **Fluorescence Yield $\omega$**: Increases with $Z^4$; low for light elements, high for heavy elements.
- [ ] **Detector Types**: Si(Li) [older, LN₂ cooled] vs. **SDD** [modern, Peltier cooled, faster, better resolution].
- [ ] **Pair creation energy in Si**: $\epsilon_{Si} = 3.8$ eV.
- [ ] **Energy resolution**: ~121–130 eV at Mn K$_\alpha$ (5.895 keV).
- [ ] **Detection limit**: ~0.1–1 wt%.
- [ ] **ZAF Correction**: Z (atomic number), A (absorption), F (fluorescence) — required for quantitative analysis.
- [ ] **Beam energy must exceed $E_c$** of the target shell; typically use 15–30 kV for K-line excitation of medium-Z elements.
- [ ] **EDS vs WDS**: EDS = energy-based, fast, multi-element, lower resolution. WDS = wavelength-based, slow, sequential, higher resolution.
- [ ] **Auger Yield + Fluorescence Yield = 1** for a given shell — remember both decay pathways.
- [ ] **Light element problem**: For $Z < 11$ (Na and below), use either a **windowless detector** or a **thin polymer window (UTW/Super-ATW)** to detect low-energy X-rays.
- [ ] **In TEM mode**, EDS can achieve ~1 nm spatial resolution via STEM-EDS mapping.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)** is a high-resolution imaging and analytical technique in which a beam of **high-energy electrons** (typically accelerated through **60–300 kV**) is transmitted through an **ultra-thin specimen** (thickness < 100 nm). The interaction of the transmitted electrons with the sample produces a magnified image, diffraction pattern, or spectroscopic signal that is projected onto a fluorescent screen, photographic film, or a CCD/camera detector. TEM routinely achieves **sub-nanometer (and even sub-Ångström) point-to-point resolution**, making it the workhorse instrument for direct imaging of nanomaterials, crystal lattices, and atomic columns.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Giant Slide Projector"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A TEM works exactly like an **optical slide projector**, but with three key upgrades:
> 1. Instead of **white light**, a TEM uses a beam of **electrons** (because electrons have a wavelength ~100,000× smaller than visible light, allowing you to "see" things at the atomic scale).
> 2. Instead of **glass lenses**, it uses **electromagnetic lenses** (coils of wire that bend electron paths the way glass bends light).
> 3. Instead of a **35 mm slide**, the "specimen" must be sliced to less than **100 nanometers thin** — thin enough for electrons to punch straight through. Anywhere the sample is denser, fewer electrons pass, and that contrast is what builds the image on the viewing screen.

> [!TIP]
> **Why electrons, not light?** The resolving power of any microscope is limited by the wavelength of its probing radiation (Abbe's limit). Visible light (λ ≈ 550 nm) can never resolve features below ~200 nm. A 200 kV TEM electron has λ ≈ 0.0025 nm — small enough to "see" individual atoms.

### 1.3 Core Specifications at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Accelerating Voltage | 60 – 300 kV (commonly **200 kV**) | Higher V → shorter λ → higher resolution |
| Electron Wavelength | 0.0017 – 0.0049 nm | ~10⁵× smaller than visible light |
| Point Resolution | 0.19 – 0.05 nm | Sub-Å resolution is achievable in aberration-corrected TEM |
| Specimen Thickness | < 100 nm (often < 50 nm) | Thicker samples scatter/absorb electrons excessively |
| Working Vacuum | 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Pa | Electrons would be scattered by air molecules otherwise |
| Magnification Range | 50× to > 1,500,000× | Spans optical microscopy up to atomic scale |

### 1.4 Historical Anchor

> [!NOTE]
> The first TEM was built by **Max Knoll and Ernst Ruska in 1931** (Berlin). Ruska received the **Nobel Prize in Physics in 1986** for this invention, which laid the foundation of all modern electron microscopy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

1. **Electron Generation (Gun):** A thermionic emitter (tungsten filament) or field-emission gun (FEG) releases electrons, which are accelerated toward the anode by a high potential difference **V** (typically 60–300 kV).
2. **Condenser Lens System:** Two or more electromagnetic condenser lenses focus the electron beam into a near-parallel, mono-energetic, coherent probe of diameter ~ 1–2 µm that impinges on the specimen.
3. **Specimen Interaction:** As the beam traverses the ultra-thin specimen, several interactions occur — elastic scattering, inelastic scattering, and diffraction — producing a complex exit wave.
4. **Objective Lens:** This is the **most critical lens** in the entire instrument. It forms the first intermediate image and (crucially) the **diffraction pattern** in its back focal plane.
5. **Intermediate and Projector Lenses:** These further magnify either the image or the diffraction pattern and project it onto the final viewing screen or detector.
6. **Image/Pattern Formation:** Two distinct outputs are possible:
   - **Image mode:** contrast comes from amplitude (mass-thickness) and phase differences.
   - **Diffraction mode:** the back focal plane of the objective is imaged, yielding a Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

| Component | Function | Why It Is Needed |
|---|---|---|
| High vacuum column | Removes air molecules | Electrons scatter off gas molecules (mean free path at 10⁻⁴ Pa ≈ meters) |
| Electromagnetic lenses (Cu coils in soft iron pole pieces) | Bend electron trajectories | Only magnetic fields can act as "lenses" for charged particles in vacuum |
| Condenser aperture | Restricts beam convergence angle | Improves coherence and controls beam current |
| Objective aperture | Selects diffracted beams for image formation | Used to switch between bright-field and dark-field imaging |
| Specimen holder (often a double-tilt stage) | Holds the thin foil in the column | Required to be thin AND crystallographically orientable |
| Phosphor screen / CCD | Converts electron intensity to visible photons | Direct viewing by the human eye or digital recording |

### 2.3 Contrast Mechanisms in TEM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass-Thickness (Amplitude) Contrast:** Heavy atoms and thicker regions scatter electrons out of the primary beam, appearing dark in **Bright-Field (BF)** imaging. By inserting a small objective aperture, the scattered (diffracted) electrons are blocked, leaving only the direct beam to form the image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffraction Contrast:** Arises from local variations in crystal orientation or lattice plane spacing. A crystalline grain in a Bragg condition will diffract strongly and appear dark in BF. This is the primary contrast mechanism in conventional TEM of metals and ceramics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Contrast (HRTEM):** When two or more diffracted beams are recombined in the image plane, their phase differences produce interference fringes that directly reveal **lattice planes and atomic columns**. This is the operational principle of High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM).

### 2.4 Modes of Operation

- **Bright-Field (BF) Imaging** — only the direct (000) beam forms the image.
- **Dark-Field (DF) Imaging** — a specific diffracted beam is used to form the image; useful to highlight one phase or orientation.
- **Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED)** — diffraction pattern from a chosen region (set by a selector aperture).
- **High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM)** — phase-contrast lattice imaging.
- **Scanning TEM (STEM)** — focused probe is scanned across the sample; Z-contrast (HAADF) and EELS/EDX spectra are obtained pixel-by-pixel.
- **Electron Energy-Loss Spectroscopy (EELS)** — measures energy lost to inelastic scattering → elemental, chemical, and electronic information.
- **Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX/EDS)** — characteristic X-rays emitted from atoms in the sample are detected for compositional analysis.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> All formulas below are essential for the ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the **form**, the **standard algebraic variables**, and the **typical numerical values** (λ at 100 kV, 200 kV, 300 kV).

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$ | Non-relativistic de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through potential V | λ in m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$ | **Relativistic** de Broglie wavelength (use this for V > ~50 kV) | λ in m |
| 3 | $\lambda \;(\text{Å}) \approx \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{V\,\bigl(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6}\,V\bigr)}}$ | **Practitioner's formula** — V in volts, λ in Ångströms (very common in KTU numericals) | λ in Å |
| 4 | $d_{\text{res}} = 0.61\,\dfrac{\lambda}{n \sin \alpha} \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{2\,\text{NA}}$ | **Abbe / Rayleigh resolution limit** (for an incoherent source; for TEM typically written as $d \approx 0.66\,\lambda^{3/4} C_s^{1/4}$ when spherical aberration dominates) | nm |
| 5 | $M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{obj}} \times M_{\text{int}} \times M_{\text{proj}}$ | Total magnification = product of all lens magnifications | dimensionless |
| 6 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2 r}\sin\theta$ | Magnetic field B at the center of an electromagnetic lens (parallel to axis); controls focal length | Tesla |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{e}{8 m_e E}\,B^2$ | **Focal length of an EM lens** — f depends on electron energy E and lens current (through B) | m |
| 8 | $s = \lambda \cdot d_{hkl}$ | Camera constant relation in SAED: $R \cdot d_{hkl} = L \lambda$ (R = distance to spot, L = camera length) | Å·Å |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{i \cdot \tau}{e}$ | Number of electrons striking the screen in time τ (current i) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,N_A \sigma_t\,\rho\,t$ | Optical density of photographic plate, N_A Avogadro's number, σ_t total cross-section, ρ density, t exposure time | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake to avoid:** Do **NOT** mix up the two forms of the electron wavelength. For V ≤ ~10 kV, the non-relativistic form is fine. For V = 100 kV, the relativistic correction is already ~6%; for V = 200 kV, it is ~12%. **Always use the relativistic form for KTU numericals.**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> TEM is **indispensable** in:
> - **Nanomaterials characterization** — confirming size, shape, and crystallinity of nanoparticles, nanowires, and quantum dots.
> - **Semiconductor industry** — defect analysis, dopant profiling, interface characterization at sub-nm scale.
> - **Catalysis research** — identifying active sites, lattice strain, and surface reconstruction in nanoparticle catalysts.
> - **Biomedical / Virology** — negative-staining and cryo-TEM of viruses, proteins, and drug-delivery vesicles.
> - **Materials failure analysis** — pinpointing crack initiation sites, precipitates, and grain-boundary segregation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Relativistic de Broglie Wavelength of an Electron

We start from the relativistic energy-momentum relation and de Broglie's hypothesis, then apply the accelerating potential.

**Step 1:** The total relativistic energy is
$$E = \gamma \, m_e c^2$$
where
$$\gamma = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}.$$
The rest energy is
$$E_0 = m_e c^2.$$

**Step 2:** The kinetic energy gained by an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is
$$T = eV.$$
By conservation of energy,
$$T + E_0 = E = \gamma m_e c^2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \gamma m_e c^2 = m_e c^2 + eV.$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the relativistic momentum p. Using $E^2 = (pc)^2 + (m_e c^2)^2$:
$$(pc)^2 = E^2 - (m_e c^2)^2 = (m_e c^2 + eV)^2 - (m_e c^2)^2.$$
Expanding,
$$(pc)^2 = 2\,m_e c^2\, eV + (eV)^2.$$

**Step 4:** Therefore,
$$p = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,m_e eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}{c}.$$

**Step 5:** Apply de Broglie's relation $\lambda = h/p$:
$$\boxed{\;\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2\,m_e eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}\;}$$

**Step 6:** Numerical evaluation at **V = 200 kV**. Using $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $c = 3.00 \times 10^8$ m/s, and $eV = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.00 \times 10^{5} = 3.204 \times 10^{-14}$ J:

$$m_e c^2 = 8.187 \times 10^{-14}\;\text{J}.$$
$$1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} = 1 + \dfrac{3.204 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 8.187 \times 10^{-14}} = 1 + 0.1957 = 1.1957.$$
$$p = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.00 \times 10^{5} \times 1.1957}}{3.00 \times 10^{8}}.$$
$$p = \dfrac{\sqrt{6.981 \times 10^{-44}}}{3.00 \times 10^{8}} = \dfrac{2.642 \times 10^{-22}}{3.00 \times 10^{8}} = 8.81 \times 10^{-31}\;\text{kg·m/s}.$$
$$\lambda = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{8.81 \times 10^{-31}} = 7.52 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{nm} = 0.00251\;\text{nm} = 0.0251\;\text{Å}.$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the well-known tabulated value: at 200 kV, the electron wavelength is **0.00251 nm (0.0251 Å)**.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Rayleigh / Abbe Resolution Limit Applied to TEM

For a perfect lens with semi-convergence angle $\alpha$, the smallest resolvable distance between two points is given by the **Rayleigh criterion**:
$$d_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{n \sin \alpha}.$$

In a TEM, the medium is vacuum ($n = 1$) and the lens is electromagnetic, so we write:
$$d_{\text{res}} = 0.61\,\dfrac{\lambda}{\sin \alpha} \approx 0.61\,\dfrac{\lambda}{\alpha} \quad (\text{for small}\; \alpha).$$

**Step 1:** For an ideal (aberration-free) lens with $\alpha \approx 10$ mrad $= 0.01$ rad and $\lambda = 0.00251$ nm:
$$d_{\text{res}} = 0.61 \times \dfrac{0.00251}{0.01} = 0.153\;\text{nm}.$$

**Step 2:** For a real lens, **spherical aberration** $C_s$ dominates at high resolution. The information limit becomes:
$$d_{\text{info}} \approx 0.66\,\lambda^{3/4}\,C_s^{1/4}.$$

For a typical 200 kV instrument with $C_s = 1.2$ mm $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m:
$$d_{\text{info}} = 0.66 \times (2.51 \times 10^{-12})^{3/4} \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{1/4}\;\text{m}.$$
$$= 0.66 \times (2.51 \times 10^{-12})^{0.75} \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{0.25}.$$
$$(2.51 \times 10^{-12})^{0.75} = 1.99 \times 10^{-9}.$$
$$(1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{0.25} = 0.186.$$
$$d_{\text{info}} = 0.66 \times 1.99 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.186 = 2.44 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{m} = 0.244\;\text{nm}.$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aberration-corrected ("C_s-corrected") TEMs** use multipole lenses to cancel $C_s$ and other aberrations, routinely achieving point resolutions **below 0.05 nm (0.5 Å)**, sufficient to image individual atomic columns.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison TEM vs SEM vs Optical Microscope

| Property | Optical Microscope | SEM | TEM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radiation | Visible light | Electrons (scanned) | Electrons (transmitted) |
| Wavelength | 400–700 nm | 0.001–0.01 nm | 0.001–0.01 nm |
| Specimen form | Bulk, transparent | Bulk, conductive (or coated) | Ultra-thin foil (<100 nm) |
| Resolution limit | ~200 nm | ~1–10 nm | ~0.05–0.2 nm |
| Magnification | up to ~2000× | up to ~2,000,000× | up to ~1,500,000× |
| Image type | Topological, color | 3-D surface topography | 2-D internal structure / atomic lattice |
| Typical use | Cells, microbes | Surface morphology of bulk materials | Internal structure, defects, atomic columns, nanoparticles |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

> **Q:** Calculate the wavelength of an electron accelerated through 100 kV, using (a) the non-relativistic formula, and (b) the relativistic formula. Comment on the difference.

**(a) Non-relativistic:**
$$\lambda_{NR} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV}} = \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{100000}} = \dfrac{12.27}{316.23} = 0.0388\;\text{Å}.$$

**(b) Relativistic:**
$$\lambda_{R} = \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{100000 \times (1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \times 100000)}} = \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{100000 \times 1.0978}} = \dfrac{12.27}{331.33} = 0.0370\;\text{Å}.$$

> [!NOTE]
> The relativistic value is **~4.6% smaller** than the non-relativistic one. At higher V the gap widens; at 200 kV it is ~12%, justifying the use of the relativistic form for KTU-level problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TEM Column

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of electrons, lenses, apertures, and the dual output (image vs. diffraction) inside a TEM column. It is drawn as a block-level functional flow rather than a scaled physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Source[Electron Source Module]
        A1[Tungsten Filament / FEG]
        A2[Anode at +V_kV]
        A3[Wehnelt Cylinder - Bias]
    end

    subgraph Illum[Illumination System]
        B1[Condenser Lens 1 C1]
        B2[Condenser Aperture]
        B3[Condenser Lens 2 C2]
        B4[Beam Deflector Scan Coils]
    end

    subgraph Stage[Specimen Stage]
        C1[Ultra-Thin Specimen less than 100 nm]
        C2[Double-Tilt Holder]
        C3[Vacuum Locks]
    end

    subgraph Obj[Objective Lens Module]
        D1[Objective Lens - Primary]
        D2[Selected Area Aperture]
        D3[Objective Aperture]
        D4[Back Focal Plane - Diffraction]
    end

    subgraph Proj[Imaging Projection System]
        E1[Intermediate Lens]
        E2[Projector Lens 1]
        E3[Projector Lens 2]
    end

    subgraph Det[Detector Bank]
        F1[Fluorescent Screen - View]
        F2[CCD or CMOS Camera]
        F3[EELS Spectrometer]
        F4[EDX Detector]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3
    D4 --> E1
    E3 --> F1
    E3 --> F2
    E3 --> F3
    E3 --> F4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Electrons are generated at the top, conditioned into a near-parallel beam by the **Illumination System**, made to interact with the **Specimen**, then collected and focused by the **Objective Lens** (the heart of the instrument). The **objective aperture** sits in the back focal plane and selects which beams form the image. The **Projector System** then expands either the image or the diffraction pattern onto the final **Detector Bank**, where visual, photographic, and spectroscopic outputs are obtained.

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Image Mode vs. Diffraction Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IF[In Column]
        S[Specimen]
        OL[Objective Lens]
        BFP[Back Focal Plane]
        IP[Image Plane of OL]
    end

    subgraph Out1[Diffraction Mode]
        IL1[Intermediate Lens 1 - Focused on BFP]
        SCR1[Screen - Diffraction Pattern]
    end

    subgraph Out2[Image Mode]
        IL2[Intermediate Lens 2 - Focused on IP]
        SCR2[Screen - Magnified Image]
    end

    S --> OL
    OL --> BFP
    OL --> IP
    BFP --> IL1 --> SCR1
    IP --> IL2 --> SCR2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Switching modes** is done by simply changing the excitation of the intermediate lens — focusing on the back focal plane gives you a **diffraction pattern (SAED)**, while focusing on the image plane gives you the **real-space image (BF/DF/HRTEM)**. This dual-output capability is one of the greatest strengths of TEM.

### 4.3 Conceptual Block — TEM Instrument Layout (Top to Bottom)

| Stage # | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electron Gun | Produces monochromatic, coherent electron beam |
| 2 | Condenser Lenses + Apertures | Forms parallel or convergent beam on specimen |
| 3 | Specimen Stage | Holds and orients thin foil in high vacuum |
| 4 | Objective Lens + Aperture | Forms first image and first diffraction pattern |
| 5 | Intermediate Lenses | Selects between image mode and diffraction mode |
| 6 | Projector Lenses | Final magnification onto screen |
| 7 | Detectors (screen, CCD, EELS, EDX) | Records image, diffraction pattern, and spectra |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define Transmission Electron Microscopy. Mention the range of wavelength used in TEM and explain why electrons are preferred over visible light for high-resolution imaging.**

**Model Answer:**
TEM is a microscopy technique in which a high-energy electron beam (60–300 kV) is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (< 100 nm) to form a magnified image on a fluorescent screen or detector. The electron wavelength used is in the range **0.0017 nm to 0.0049 nm** (0.017 Å to 0.049 Å) depending on the accelerating voltage. Electrons are preferred over visible light because the **resolving power of any microscope is limited by the wavelength** of its probing radiation (Abbe's limit). The electron wavelength is **~10⁵ times smaller** than that of visible light (~550 nm), allowing TEM to resolve features at the atomic scale (sub-nm to sub-Å) — far below the ~200 nm diffraction limit of optical microscopy. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the role of the objective lens in a TEM? Why is it called the most critical lens in the instrument?**

**Model Answer:**
The objective lens is the **first lens below the specimen** and is responsible for forming the **first intermediate image** of the specimen and the **first diffraction pattern** in its back focal plane. It is the most critical lens because (i) any spherical or chromatic aberration introduced at this stage is magnified by all subsequent projector lenses, (ii) it determines the achievable resolution of the instrument, (iii) it hosts the objective aperture that selects the imaging beam (BF/DF), and (iv) it generates the diffraction pattern that is used for SAED analysis. Any defect in the objective lens cannot be corrected later, so its quality ultimately governs the performance of the entire TEM. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a) Derive the relativistic de Broglie wavelength expression for an electron accelerated through a potential V, and calculate the wavelength at 200 kV. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation proceeds in five steps:

1. Write the total relativistic energy: $E = \gamma m_e c^2 = m_e c^2 + eV$.
2. Use the relativistic energy–momentum relation $E^2 = (pc)^2 + (m_e c^2)^2$ to obtain $(pc)^2 = 2 m_e c^2 eV + (eV)^2$.
3. Solve for momentum: $p = \dfrac{1}{c}\sqrt{2 m_e eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}$.
4. Apply de Broglie's relation $\lambda = h/p$:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}.$$

5. Convert to the practical formula: $\lambda\,(\text{Å}) = \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{V\,(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} V)}}$ with V in volts.

**Numerical evaluation at 200 kV:**
$$\lambda = \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{200000\,(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \times 200000)}}$$
$$= \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{200000 \times 1.1956}} = \dfrac{12.27}{489.04} = 0.0251\;\text{Å} = 0.00251\;\text{nm}.$$

Therefore, the relativistic electron wavelength at 200 kV is **0.00251 nm**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Explain the different contrast mechanisms in TEM (mass-thickness, diffraction, and phase contrast). Describe the principle of HRTEM and how it reveals atomic columns. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Mass-Thickness Contrast:** Arises because heavier atoms and thicker regions scatter more electrons out of the primary beam. In **Bright-Field (BF)** imaging — formed using only the direct (000) beam — such regions appear dark. This mechanism dominates in amorphous and biological specimens.
- **Diffraction Contrast:** Dominant in crystalline materials. When a local crystal region satisfies the **Bragg condition** ($2 d \sin\theta = n\lambda$), its diffracted beam is intense; blocking this beam with an objective aperture produces a **Bright-Field** image where the diffracting region is dark. This is used to image **defects (dislocations, stacking faults, grain boundaries)**.
- **Phase Contrast (HRTEM):** When the objective aperture allows **two or more beams** (direct + one or more diffracted beams) to recombine at the image plane, the phase differences between the beams produce an interference pattern. The result is a **lattice fringe image** or, in the Scherzer defocus condition, a direct image of the atomic columns. HRTEM is the only technique that can **directly image atomic arrangements** in a crystal.

The principle of HRTEM is therefore to tune the objective lens defocus (typically to the Scherzer defocus $\Delta f_S = -1.2 \sqrt{C_s \lambda}$) so that the contrast transfer function passes a wide spatial-frequency band, transferring atomic-scale information faithfully to the image. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a Transmission Electron Microscope. Label all major components. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A TEM consists of the following major functional blocks arranged vertically along an evacuated column (see the block diagram from SECTION 4):

1. **Electron Gun (Top):** A tungsten hairpin filament (thermionic) or a field-emission tip produces electrons by thermionic emission / field emission. The Wehnelt cylinder biases the emission, and the anode (held at +60 to +300 kV) accelerates the electrons into a beam.
2. **Illumination System:** Two condenser lenses (C1, C2) and a condenser aperture demagnify the source into a small, near-parallel, coherent probe of diameter 1–10 µm.
3. **Specimen Stage:** A high-precision, double-tilt goniometer holds the ultra-thin specimen (< 100 nm) at the column's mid-section. The stage has airlocks so samples can be inserted without breaking column vacuum.
4. **Objective Lens and Apertures:** The objective lens forms the first image and the first diffraction pattern (in its back focal plane). A **selected-area aperture** in the image plane restricts the region that contributes to the diffraction pattern, and the **objective aperture** in the back focal plane selects which beam forms the image (BF / DF).
5. **Intermediate and Projector Lenses:** These magnify the chosen signal — either the first image (image mode) or the diffraction pattern (diffraction / SAED mode) — by factors of 10–100 each, yielding a total magnification of up to ~1.5 million.
6. **Detector Bank:** A retractable fluorescent screen allows direct viewing, a CCD/CMOS camera records digital images, an EELS spectrometer measures energy losses, and an EDX detector captures characteristic X-rays. The entire column is maintained at 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Pa by ion-getter and turbomolecular pumps. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) What is Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED)? Derive the camera-length equation $R \cdot d_{hkl} = L \lambda$ and explain its use in identifying crystal structures. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SAED definition:** SAED is a TEM technique in which an aperture (the selected-area aperture) is inserted into the image plane of the objective lens to limit the diffraction information to a small, chosen region of the specimen. The diffraction pattern from that region is then projected onto the screen by switching the intermediate lens to focus on the back focal plane of the objective.

**Derivation of $R \cdot d_{hkl} = L \lambda$:**

From Bragg's law for a diffracting plane $(hkl)$:
$$2 d_{hkl} \sin\theta = n\lambda.$$

For fast electrons, $\theta$ is very small (typically $< 2°$), so $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians). Hence:
$$2 d_{hkl} \theta = \lambda \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 d_{hkl}}.$$

The diffracted beam leaves the specimen at an angle $2\theta$ with respect to the direct beam. The distance $R$ from the central (000) spot to the diffracted $(hkl)$ spot on the screen is:
$$R = L \tan(2\theta) \approx L \cdot 2\theta \quad (\text{small-angle approximation}).$$

Substituting the value of $\theta$:
$$R = L \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{2 d_{hkl}} = \dfrac{L \lambda}{d_{hkl}}.$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\;R \cdot d_{hkl} = L \lambda\;}$$
where $L$ is the **effective camera length** (set by the intermediate and projector lens excitations).

**Use in identification:** Because $L$ and $\lambda$ are known (or kept constant during the experiment), measuring $R$ for each spot gives $d_{hkl}$ for the corresponding lattice planes. The set of $d_{hkl}$ values and the angles between spots constitute a unique "fingerprint" of the crystal structure. By comparing these with the JCPDS/ICDD database, the phase, lattice parameters, and symmetry of the nanocrystal can be identified. SAED can also distinguish between single-crystal, polycrystalline (rings), and amorphous (diffuse halos) specimens. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on TEM questions:**
> 1. **Using the non-relativistic formula** for V > 50 kV. Always use the relativistic form for KTU problems.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — express wavelength in **pm, nm, or Å** clearly, and quote V in **volts** (not kV) inside the formula.
> 3. **Confusing the camera length L with the physical distance** to the screen — L is an *effective* quantity set by the lens excitations.
> 4. **Mixing up BF and DF** — BF uses the direct beam; DF uses a specific diffracted beam.
> 5. **Skipping the small-angle approximation** in the SAED derivation — examiners explicitly award 1 mark for stating $\sin\theta \approx \theta$.
> 6. **Omitting the role of the vacuum** in TEM — a 1-mark conceptual point that is frequently missed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **TEM = high-energy electrons transmitted through a thin (<100 nm) specimen** to form a magnified image and/or diffraction pattern.
- **Resolution** in TEM is governed by the **de Broglie wavelength** of the electron, which depends on the accelerating voltage V.
- **Use the relativistic formula** $\lambda\,(\text{Å}) = \dfrac{12.27}{\sqrt{V\,(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6}\,V)}}$ for any V > ~50 kV.
- At **100 kV, λ ≈ 0.0037 nm**; at **200 kV, λ ≈ 0.0025 nm**; at **300 kV, λ ≈ 0.00197 nm**.
- **Objective lens** is the most critical optical element of a TEM.
- Three contrast mechanisms: **mass-thickness (BF)**, **diffraction (DF, defect imaging)**, **phase (HRTEM, atomic columns)**.
- **SAED** camera equation: $R \cdot d_{hkl} = L \lambda$ — the working formula for identifying crystal phases and lattice spacings.
- **Modes**: BF, DF, HRTEM, STEM (HAADF), EELS, EDX.
- **Vacuum** of 10⁻⁵–10⁻⁷ Pa is essential to prevent electron scattering by residual gas.
- **Specimen preparation** techniques: ultramicrotomy, ion-beam milling (FIB), electropolishing, drop-casting on carbon grids.
- **Difference vs. SEM**: TEM transmits electrons (internal structure, sub-Å), SEM scans them (surface topography, 1–10 nm).
- **Aberration-corrected TEMs** use multipole correctors to push resolution below 0.5 Å, enabling direct imaging of atomic columns.
- **Nobel Prize** for the TEM invention: **Ruska, 1986**.
- **Three formulas to memorize cold**: (1) relativistic λ, (2) Abbe resolution, (3) SAED camera equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of AFM

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** is a high-resolution type of **Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM)** that operates by measuring the interatomic force fields between a sharp nanoscale probe (typically terminating in a single atom) and the atoms of a sample surface. The probe is mounted on the free end of a flexible microfabricated **cantilever beam**, and the local force interaction produces a quantifiable mechanical deflection of the cantilever, which is optically transduced into a topographical image of the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> AFM is classified under the broader family of **near-field probe microscopies** that circumvent the Abbe diffraction limit (~200 nm) of conventional optical microscopy, thereby achieving **sub-nanometer (atomic) lateral resolution** in the *x-y* plane and sub-Ångström vertical resolution along the *z*-axis.

AFM was invented by **Binnig, Quate, and Gerber in 1986** as an extension of the earlier Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM), with the critical advantage of being applicable to **both conducting and non-conducting samples**, including biological specimens, polymers, and insulators.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Blind Man's Cane" Principle

Imagine you are a blind person (the probe tip) walking along a rough surface (the sample). To understand the surface topology, you use a sensitive walking cane (the cantilever). As you slide the cane across, whenever you encounter a bump, the cane bends; whenever there is a dip, the cane springs back. A laser pointed at the tip of your cane (the optical lever) reflects onto a sensor wall (the photodiode) that precisely measures how much the cane is bending at every single point.

By mapping every bend into a 3D coordinate, you reconstruct the entire landscape of the surface — atom by atom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy summary:**
> - **Cantilever + Tip** → A super-sensitive spring-loaded stylus
> - **Photodiode** → A digital strain gauge
> - **Piezoelectric Scanner** → A nanometer-precision *x-y-z* translation stage
> - **Feedback Loop** → The brain that keeps the stylus in constant "tactile contact" with the surface

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are essential for AFM analysis:

- **Cantilever spring constant (k):** typically **0.01 N/m to 50 N/m** depending on mode.
- **Tip apex radius:** **2 nm to 30 nm** for standard silicon tips.
- **Thermal noise limit (minimum detectable force):** on the order of **10⁻¹² N (piconewton)**.
- **Resonant frequency (f₀):** typically **10 kHz to 500 kHz**.
- **Quality factor (Q):** 100 to 1000 in air; >10,000 in vacuum.
- **Spring constant of atomic bond:** ≈ **10 N/m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Reference Constants:**
> - Boltzmann constant: **k_B = 1.3806 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **1.013 × 10⁵ Pa**
> - Young's modulus of silicon cantilever: **E_Si ≈ 130–190 GPa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an AFM

The AFM instrument comprises the following interconnected subsystems, all working in a closed feedback loop:

### (a) Microfabricated Cantilever with Sharp Probe Tip
- Typically made of **silicon (Si)** or **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)** via MEMS lithography.
- The tip has a radius of curvature at the apex of **~2–30 nm**.
- The cantilever acts as a force transducer obeying Hooke's Law.

### (b) Piezoelectric Scanner
- A **piezoelectric ceramic** (commonly lead zirconate titanate, PZT) that expands/contracts on application of a voltage.
- Provides **sub-Ångström positional precision** in *x*, *y*, and *z* directions.
- The applied voltage V produces displacement δ via:
  > **δ = d₃₃ · V**
  where d₃₃ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (~3 × 10⁻¹⁰ m/V for PZT).

### (c) Optical Beam Deflection System (Force Sensor)
- A **laser diode** (typically 670 nm or 785 nm) is focused on the back of the cantilever.
- The reflected beam is directed onto a **position-sensitive photodetector (PSPD)**, usually a four-quadrant photodiode.
- Cantilever deflection Δz rotates the reflected beam by an angle **2θ**, where the angular magnification provides enhanced sensitivity.
- The PSD converts angular shift into a differential voltage signal (A+B) − (C+D).

### (d) Feedback Control Electronics
- A **proportional-integral (PI) controller** compares the actual cantilever deflection to a user-defined **setpoint**.
- The error signal drives the *z*-piezo to maintain constant force (constant-height or constant-force mode).
- The *z*-piezo correction voltage at each (*x*, *y*) point yields the topography map.

---

## 2.2 Force Interactions Probed by AFM

The total interaction force **F_total** experienced by the tip is the sum of multiple contributions:

| Force Type | Physical Origin | Typical Range | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pauli Repulsion** | Orbital overlap at short range | < 0.2 nm | ~10⁻⁹ N |
| **Van der Waals Attraction** | Induced dipole–dipole coupling | 0.2–10 nm | ~10⁻¹² N |
| **Capillary Force** | Meniscus of adsorbed water | ~nm | ~10⁻⁸ N |
| **Electrostatic Force** | Surface charge difference | nm–µm | Varies |
| **Magnetic Force (MFM)** | Dipole interaction (if coated) | nm–µm | Varies |
| **Friction Force (LFM)** | Lateral tip-sample shear | Contact | ~nN |

The **interatomic pair potential** is often modeled using the **Lennard-Jones potential**:

$$ U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right] $$

where $\varepsilon$ is the depth of the potential well, $\sigma$ is the distance at which U = 0, and $r$ is the interatomic separation. The force is then $F(r) = -dU/dr$.

---

## 2.3 Three Principal Operating Modes of AFM

### Mode 1 — Contact Mode (Repulsive Regime)
- Tip is in **physical contact** with the surface.
- Interatomic distance < 0.5 nm; net force is **repulsive** (~0.1–10 nN).
- The cantilever is "dragged" across the surface.
- **High scan speed** but risk of tip and sample damage.
- Best for: hard, robust samples (metals, ceramics).

### Mode 2 — Non-Contact Mode (Attractive Regime)
- Tip oscillates **5–15 nm above** the surface at its resonant frequency.
- Detects **long-range van der Waals** and electrostatic forces.
- Operates in the attractive regime of the force curve.
- Very low force → minimal damage → ideal for soft samples.
- Sensitive to environmental noise; usually requires UHV or controlled humidity.

### Mode 3 — Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact)
- Cantilever oscillates near resonance with **large amplitude (20–100 nm)**.
- Tip intermittently "taps" the surface each cycle.
- Combines high resolution of contact mode with low damage of non-contact mode.
- **Most widely used** AFM mode in industry and biology.
- Phase imaging yields compositional contrast.

---

## 2.4 Force-Distance Curve Analysis

A **force-distance (F-Z) curve** is the fundamental diagnostic plot of AFM, obtained by ramping the *z*-piezo vertically while recording the cantilever deflection. It reveals:

- **Zero deflection region** (tip far from surface; no force).
- **Snap-in event** (tip suddenly jumps into contact due to attractive force exceeding k·z).
- **Linear repulsive region** (slope = k_cantilever; slope of constant compliance).
- **Pull-off / adhesion event** (snap-out due to breaking of adhesive bonds).
- **Adhesion force** is computed as the difference between baseline and snap-out point multiplied by k.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hooke's Law for cantilever | $F = -k \cdot \Delta z$ | $k$ = spring constant (N/m); $\Delta z$ = deflection (m) |
| 2 | Lennard-Jones pair potential | $U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$ | $\varepsilon$ (J); $\sigma$ (m); $r$ (m) |
| 3 | Van der Waals force (sphere-flat) | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{H \cdot R}{6 d^{2}}$ | $H$ = Hamaker constant (J); $R$ = tip radius; $d$ = distance |
| 4 | Resonant frequency | $f_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}}$ | $m^{*}$ = effective mass |
| 5 | Frequency shift (dynamic mode) | $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{f_{0}}{2k}\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$ | Force gradient sensing |
| 6 | Thermal noise limit (force sensitivity) | $F_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_{B} T k}{\pi f_{0} Q}}$ | $k_B$ = Boltzmann const.; $T$ = temperature; $Q$ = quality factor |
| 7 | Optical lever sensitivity | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{2 \Delta z}{L}$ | $L$ = cantilever length |
| 8 | Piezo displacement | $\delta = d_{33} \cdot V$ | $d_{33}$ = piezo strain coeff. (m/V) |
| 9 | Effective force constant (dynamic) | $k_{eff} = k - \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$ | Determines shift in resonance |
| 10 | Adhesion force from pull-off | $F_{adh} = k \cdot \Delta z_{pull-off}$ | From F-Z curve |
| 11 | Sensitivity to force gradient | $\Delta f \approx -\dfrac{f_{0}}{2k} \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$ | Used in frequency modulation AFM |
| 12 | Capillary force (Laplace-Young) | $F_{cap} \approx 4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension; $\theta$ = contact angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse $F_{vdW}$ between two spheres ($F = \dfrac{H R_1 R_2}{6 d^{2} (R_1 + R_2)}$) with the sphere-flat geometry ($F = \dfrac{H R}{6 d^{2}}$). The sphere-flat case is the **default** for AFM because the substrate is approximated as flat.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

AFM is indispensable across engineering domains:

- **Semiconductor industry:** Critical dimension (CD) metrology of photoresist lines, step-height measurement of trenches and vias.
- **Materials science:** Quantifying grain boundaries, surface roughness (R_a, R_q), fracture surface morphology.
- **Biotechnology:** Imaging DNA, proteins, viruses, cell membranes; measuring nanomechanical properties of cells (Young's modulus mapping).
- **Polymer science:** Visualizing lamellae, spherulites, and phase-separated domains.
- **Nanomanufacturing:** Verification of nanopatterned devices (NEMS, MEMS, quantum dots).
- **In-situ studies:** AFM-electrochemistry coupling for battery electrode and corrosion studies.
- **Data storage:** Probe-based nanolithography, millipede memory concept.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Thermal Noise Limit of Force Detection

The thermal noise floor of an AFM cantilever is governed by the equipartition theorem. Each vibrational mode in thermal equilibrium holds an average energy of $\frac{1}{2}k_B T$. For a damped harmonic oscillator cantilever, the spectral density of force noise is:

### Step 1 — Start with the energy balance for a damped harmonic oscillator
The mean square displacement under thermal excitation is given by the equipartition theorem:

$$ \frac{1}{2} k \langle x^{2} \rangle = \frac{1}{2} k_B T $$

This yields the root-mean-square displacement:

$$ x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{k}} $$

### Step 2 — Relate displacement to force via Hooke's law
Since the cantilever responds to forces via $F = kx$, the rms force noise is:

$$ F_{rms} = k \cdot x_{rms} = k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{k}} = \sqrt{k \cdot k_B T} $$

### Step 3 — Incorporate the bandwidth of measurement (Δf) and quality factor (Q)
The force noise spectral density integrated over the measurement bandwidth Δf gives the total fluctuation. For a damped oscillator with quality factor Q and resonant frequency $f_0$:

$$ S_F = \frac{2 k_B T k}{\pi f_0 Q} $$

### Step 4 — Obtain the minimum detectable force
The minimum detectable force in bandwidth Δf is:

$$ F_{min} = \sqrt{S_F \cdot \Delta f} = \sqrt{\frac{2 k_B T k \Delta f}{\pi f_0 Q}} $$

### Step 5 — Numerical sanity check (typical AFM values)
Let $k = 0.5$ N/m, $f_0 = 300$ kHz, $Q = 300$, $T = 300$ K, $\Delta f = 100$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{min} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times 0.5 \times 100}{\pi \times (3 \times 10^{5}) \times 300}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{4.14 \times 10^{-19}}{2.83 \times 10^{8}}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.46 \times 10^{-27}} \\
&\approx 3.8 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value (~38 fN) demonstrates the extreme sensitivity of AFM in tapping mode.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Van der Waals Force Between a Sphere and a Flat Surface

The van der Waals interaction forms the basis of non-contact AFM imaging. We derive the sphere-flat geometry from the Hamaker summation.

### Step 1 — Start with the Hamaker pairwise sum for two flat plates
The van der Waals interaction energy per unit area between two infinite parallel plates separated by distance $d$ is:

$$ E_{flat} = -\frac{H}{12\pi d^{2}} $$

where $H$ is the **Hamaker constant** (typically $10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$ J for condensed phases).

### Step 2 — Use the Derjaguin approximation
The Derjaguin approximation relates the interaction between two curved bodies to that between flat surfaces by integrating over the area of "interaction rings":

$$ F(D) = 2\pi R_{eff} \cdot W_{flat}(D) $$

where $R_{eff}$ is the effective radius and $W_{flat}$ is the interaction energy per unit area.

### Step 3 — Substitute and differentiate to obtain force
With $W_{flat}(D) = -\dfrac{H}{12\pi D^{2}}$ and $R_{eff} = R$ (tip radius, since flat substrate has infinite radius):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(D) &= 2\pi R \left( -\frac{H}{12\pi D^{2}} \right) \\
&= -\frac{H R}{6 D^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the magnitude (force is attractive, hence negative):

$$ \vert F_{vdW} \vert = \frac{H R}{6 D^{2}} $$

### Step 4 — Physical interpretation
- Force scales **linearly with tip radius** $R$: sharper tip → weaker force (smaller contact area for vdW integration).
- Force scales as $1/D^{2}$: rapidly decays with distance, justifying non-contact mode's sensitivity requirement.
- For typical values $H = 10^{-19}$ J, $R = 10$ nm, $D = 0.5$ nm:

$$ F = \frac{10^{-19} \times 10^{-8}}{6 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-9})^{2}} = \frac{10^{-27}}{1.5 \times 10^{-18}} \approx 6.7 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{N} = 0.67 \, \text{nN} $$

This is consistent with experimental AFM force ranges in non-contact mode.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Frequency Shift in Dynamic AFM (Frequency Modulation)

In frequency modulation AFM (FM-AFM), the cantilever is self-oscillated at its resonant frequency $f_0$ with constant amplitude. The force gradient between tip and sample shifts the resonance.

### Step 1 — Equation of motion with force gradient
For small tip-sample separations, the effective spring constant of the cantilever becomes:

$$ k_{eff} = k - \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} $$

The shift arises because the tip experiences a position-dependent force $F(z)$, and to first order, this acts as an additional spring of stiffness $-\partial F/\partial z$.

### Step 2 — Modified resonant frequency
The new resonant frequency $f'$ is:

$$ f' = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eff}}{m^{*}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k - \partial F/\partial z}{m^{*}}} $$

### Step 3 — Linearized frequency shift approximation
Expanding for small force gradient ($\partial F/\partial z \ll k$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f' &\approx f_0 \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{k}\frac{\partial F}{\partial z}} \\
&\approx f_0 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2k}\frac{\partial F}{\partial z} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$ \Delta f = f' - f_0 \approx -\frac{f_0}{2k}\frac{\partial F}{\partial z} $$

### Step 4 — Significance
- FM-AFM measures **force gradient**, not absolute force.
- Allows true atomic resolution imaging (Eigler, Giessibl) on metals, semiconductors, and insulators.
- The sign of $\Delta f$ indicates whether forces are attractive ($\Delta f < 0$) or repulsive ($\Delta f > 0$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of an Atomic Force Microscope

The following diagram maps the complete signal flow and physical architecture of an AFM instrument.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ctrl[Feedback Controller]
        PI[PI Controller]
        SP[Setpoint Reference]
        ERR[Error Signal Generator]
    end

    subgraph pzt[Z Piezo Scanner]
        PZ[PZT Z Actuator]
    end

    subgraph probe[Microcantilever Probe]
        CANT[Si or Si3N4 Cantilever]
        TIP[Sharp Tip Radius 2 to 30 nm]
    end

    subgraph opt[Optical Beam Deflection]
        LASER[Laser Diode 670 nm]
        MIRROR[Mirror]
        REF[Reflected Beam]
        PSD[Four Quadrant Photodiode]
    end

    subgraph scan[XY Scan Generator]
        XYPZ[PZT XY Scanner]
        RAMP[Ramp Generator]
    end

    subgraph sample[Sample Stage]
        SURF[Sample Surface]
    end

    subgraph proc[Data Acquisition]
        ADC[ADC Converter]
        IMG[Topography Map Generator]
    end

    RAMP --> XYPZ
    XYPZ --> SURF
    SURF -.-> TIP
    TIP --> CANT
    CANT -. Reflects .-> REF
    LASER --> MIRROR
    MIRROR --> CANT
    REF --> PSD
    PSD --> ERR
    SP --> ERR
    ERR --> PI
    PI --> PZ
    PZ --> CANT
    PSD --> ADC
    ADC --> IMG
    XYPZ --> IMG
```

---

## 4.2 Operational Mode Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AFM[AFM Operating Modes] --> CM[Contact Mode]
    AFM --> NCM[Non Contact Mode]
    AFM --> TM[Tapping Mode]

    CM --> CM1[Tip in physical contact]
    CM --> CM2[Repulsive regime]
    CM --> CM3[Force 0.1 to 10 nN]
    CM --> CM4[High resolution but tip wear]

    NCM --> NCM1[Tip 5 to 15 nm above surface]
    NCM --> NCM2[Attractive vdW regime]
    NCM --> NCM3[Oscillates at resonance]
    NCM --> NCM4[Requires UHV for best results]

    TM --> TM1[Intermittent contact]
    TM --> TM2[Amplitude 20 to 100 nm]
    TM --> TM3[Phase imaging available]
    TM --> TM4[Most widely used]
```

---

## 4.3 Force-Distance Curve Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    REG1[Region 1: Zero Deflection] --> REG2
    REG2[Region 2: Snap Into Contact attractive force overcomes k] --> REG3
    REG3[Region 3: Linear Repulsive Region slope equals k] --> REG4
    REG4[Region 4: Maximum Adhesion Pull Off Point] --> REG5
    REG5[Region 5: Snap Out and Free Cantilever]

    REG1 -.-> KEY1[No tip sample contact]
    REG2 -.-> KEY2[Jump to contact instability]
    REG3 -.-> KEY3[Constant compliance region]
    REG4 -.-> KEY5[Adhesion force computed here]
    REG5 -.-> KEY4[Returns to baseline]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Signal Flow of the AFM Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Setpoint] --> B[Comparator]
    C[Photodiode Signal] --> B
    B --> D[Error e of t]
    D --> E[PI Amplifier]
    E --> F[Z Piezo Drive Voltage]
    F --> G[Piezo Expansion]
    G --> H[Tip Sample Separation Adjusted]
    H --> I[New Cantilever Deflection]
    I --> J[New Photodiode Signal]
    J --> C
```

---

## 4.5 AFM Tip-Sample Force Regime Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    REGIME[Tip Sample Interaction Regimes] --> REP[Repulsive Regime less than 0.5 nm]
    REGIME --> ATT[Attractive Regime 0.5 to 10 nm]
    REGIME --> FAR[Long Range Regime greater than 10 nm]

    REP --> REP1[Pauli Exclusion dominates]
    REP --> REP2[Contact Mode operation]

    ATT --> ATT1[van der Waals dominant]
    ATT --> ATT2[Tapping Mode operation]

    FAR --> FAR1[Electrostatic and magnetic]
    FAR --> FAR2[Non Contact Mode operation]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Atomic Force Microscopy. List any two advantages of AFM over Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) is a high-resolution scanning probe technique that images surface topography by measuring the interatomic forces between a sharp nanoscale tip mounted on a flexible cantilever and the atoms of the sample surface, with sub-nanometer lateral resolution.

**Two advantages over STM (1 mark):**
1. AFM can image **non-conducting samples** (insulators, polymers, biological specimens), whereas STM requires conducting or semiconducting samples.
2. AFM operates under **ambient, liquid, and vacuum environments** with minimal sample preparation, while STM typically requires UHV conditions.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Contact Mode and Tapping Mode AFM in terms of tip-sample interaction and imaging application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Contact Mode | Tapping Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Tip-Sample Force | Continuous repulsive (0.1–10 nN) | Intermittent, average force ~pN |
| Operating Regime | Always in contact | Oscillates between repulsive and free |
| Sample Suitability | Hard, flat samples (metals, ceramics) | Soft, fragile samples (polymers, biomolecules) |
| Tip Damage Risk | High | Low |
| Output Signal | Cantilever deflection | Amplitude and phase |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of Atomic Force Microscopy. Discuss the role of the cantilever, piezoelectric scanner, and optical beam deflection system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the three operating modes of AFM (contact, non-contact, tapping) with reference to the force-distance curve. Discuss their relative merits and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a)

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
AFM operates by raster-scanning a sharp tip mounted on a flexible cantilever across a sample surface while maintaining a constant tip-sample interaction force. The interatomic forces (repulsive at short range, attractive at long range) cause the cantilever to deflect in proportion to the local topography. This deflection is detected optically, and a feedback loop adjusts the *z*-piezo to maintain a constant setpoint. The required *z*-correction at each (*x*, *y*) coordinate produces the topographic image.

**Cantilever (1.5 Marks):**
The cantilever is a microfabricated beam (typically Si or Si₃N₄) with spring constant $k$ ranging from 0.01 to 50 N/m. It functions as a force transducer obeying Hooke's Law: $F = -k \Delta z$. The cantilever's free end carries the sharp tip with apex radius 2–30 nm. The spring constant must be matched to the stiffness of the sample to avoid damage.

**Piezoelectric Scanner (1.5 Marks):**
The PZT (lead zirconate titanate) ceramic provides three-axis positioning with sub-Ångström precision. The applied voltage $V$ produces displacement $\delta = d_{33} V$. The scanner raster-scans in *x* and *y*, while the *z*-axis responds to the feedback signal to maintain constant force.

**Optical Beam Deflection System (1 Mark):**
A laser diode focuses onto the back of the cantilever. The reflected beam is directed onto a four-quadrant photodiode. Cantilever deflection of $\Delta z$ rotates the reflected beam by angle $2\theta = 2 \Delta z / L$, where $L$ is the cantilever length. The differential photodiode signal is proportional to the deflection. This provides sub-Ångström deflection sensitivity.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b)

**Force-Distance Curve Foundation (2 Marks):**
The F-Z curve is the master plot showing cantilever deflection vs. tip-sample separation. It exhibits:
- **A-B (non-contact):** No interaction; baseline cantilever position.
- **B-C (snap-in):** Attractive gradient exceeds $k$, causing instability.
- **C-D (contact/repulsive):** Linear regime with slope = 1 (constant compliance).
- **D-E (retract):** Adhesive bonds hold tip; pull-off at E yields adhesion force.

**Contact Mode (1.5 Marks):**
Operates in regime C-D. Tip is dragged across the surface with constant deflection (constant-force mode) or constant height. Force: 0.1–10 nN. Pros: high scan speed, high lateral resolution. Cons: tip wear, lateral shear forces distort soft samples, capillary forces present in ambient air.

**Non-Contact Mode (1.5 Marks):**
Operates in regime A-B (5–15 nm above surface). Tip oscillates at resonance; force gradient $\partial F/\partial z$ shifts the resonance frequency by $\Delta f = -\dfrac{f_0}{2k}\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$. Pros: zero tip wear, true atomic resolution in UHV. Cons: low signal-to-noise, requires clean environment (no water layer).

**Tapping Mode (1 Mark):**
Operates at the boundary of A-B and C-D. Large amplitude oscillation (20–100 nm); tip taps surface intermittently. Amplitude damping provides topography; phase shift reveals compositional/mechanical contrast. Pros: combines high resolution with low damage, works on sticky samples. Cons: slower than contact mode.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the thermal noise limit of force detection in AFM and explain its significance in setting the sensitivity floor of the instrument. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the application of AFM in nanomaterials characterization. Explain how AFM is used for surface roughness measurement, particle size analysis, and nanomechanical property mapping. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a)

**Setup and Equipartition (3 Marks):**
At thermal equilibrium, each vibrational mode of the cantilever holds average energy $\frac{1}{2} k_B T$. For a harmonic oscillator of spring constant $k$:

$$ \frac{1}{2} k \langle x^{2} \rangle = \frac{1}{2} k_B T \implies x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{k}} $$

Using Hooke's law, the rms force noise is:

$$ F_{rms} = k \cdot x_{rms} = \sqrt{k k_B T} $$

**Bandwidth and Quality Factor (3 Marks):**
The force spectral density for a damped oscillator of quality factor $Q$ and resonance $f_0$ is:

$$ S_F = \frac{2 k_B T k}{\pi f_0 Q} $$

The minimum detectable force over bandwidth $\Delta f$ is:

$$ F_{min} = \sqrt{S_F \cdot \Delta f} = \sqrt{\frac{2 k_B T k \Delta f}{\pi f_0 Q}} $$

**Significance (1 Mark):**
This thermal noise floor (~10⁻¹² N) defines the ultimate sensitivity of AFM. It cannot be overcome without lowering temperature, increasing $Q$ (vacuum), increasing $f_0$ (small cantilevers), or using stiffer cantilevers (which simultaneously raises the noise floor — a fundamental tradeoff).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b)

**Surface Roughness Measurement (2.5 Marks):**
AFM scans a defined area (e.g., 1 µm × 1 µm) and quantifies roughness parameters:
- Arithmetic average: $R_a = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_i - \bar{z} \vert$
- RMS roughness: $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(z_i - \bar{z})^{2}}$
These are critical in thin-film deposition, coating quality, and semiconductor wafer inspection.

**Particle Size Analysis (2 Marks):**
AFM directly measures the lateral dimensions, height, and volume of nanoparticles by tracing height profiles across particle images. Unlike electron microscopy, AFM provides true 3D morphology without requiring conductive coatings or vacuum, making it ideal for soft nanoparticles (liposomes, polymer NPs, virus capsids).

**Nanomechanical Property Mapping (2.5 Marks):**
By acquiring force-distance curves at each pixel, AFM can map:
- **Young's modulus** via Hertzian or DMT contact mechanics fits to the retract curve.
- **Adhesion force** maps reveal surface chemical heterogeneity.
- **Stiffness mapping** (PeakForce QNM, Force Volume mode) provides quantitative nanomechanical contrast.
- Applications include identifying cancerous vs. healthy cells, measuring polymer phase separation, and characterizing 2D material mechanical properties (e.g., graphene, MoS₂).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Hooke's Law sign error:** Students often write $F = k \Delta z$ without the negative sign. The correct form is $F = -k \Delta z$ (restoring force opposite to displacement).
> 2. **Confusing contact vs. non-contact distances:** Non-contact operates at 5–15 nm gap, NOT at contact. Many students incorrectly describe non-contact mode as "tip just touching the surface."
> 3. **Forgetting cantilever spring constant units:** Always quote $k$ in N/m, not N. Examiner will deduct 0.5 mark if units are missing.
> 4. **Hamaker constant confusion:** Do not confuse the Hamaker constant $H$ (~10⁻¹⁹ J) with Planck's constant $h$ (~6.6 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s). AFM examiners are strict on this.
> 5. **Missing the feedback loop explanation:** Many students describe the optical lever but forget to explain *how* the photodiode signal is processed in a PI controller to actuate the *z*-piezo. This loses 2 marks easily.
> 6. **Piezo strain coefficient confusion:** $d_{33}$ refers to the longitudinal coefficient (field parallel to polarization). Do not confuse with $d_{31}$ (transverse). AFM scanners use $d_{33}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- [x] AFM = Scanning Probe Microscopy based on **interatomic force measurement**, not tunneling current.
- [x] Resolution: **sub-nm lateral, sub-Å vertical**.
- [x] **Three operating modes:** Contact (repulsive), Non-contact (attractive, oscillatory), Tapping (intermittent).
- [x] **Force-distance curve** is the master diagnostic — regions: zero force, snap-in, constant compliance, pull-off.
- [x] Hooke's Law: $F = -k \Delta z$ — the fundamental equation of the cantilever.
- [x] Lennard-Jones potential describes the **repulsive-attractive** interaction; $1/r^{12}$ repulsion, $1/r^{6}$ attraction.
- [x] Van der Waals force (sphere-flat): $\vert F_{vdW} \vert = \dfrac{HR}{6 D^{2}}$.
- [x] Thermal noise limit: $F_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_B T k \Delta f}{\pi f_0 Q}}$.
- [x] Frequency shift in dynamic AFM: $\Delta f \approx -\dfrac{f_0}{2k}\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$.
- [x] Optical lever sensitivity: $\Delta \theta = 2 \Delta z / L$.
- [x] AFM works on **conductors AND insulators** (unlike STM).
- [x] Tip apex radius is typically **2–30 nm**.

### Numerical Constants to Recall

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| Hamaker constant (typical) | $H$ | $10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$ J |
| Water surface tension | $\gamma$ | ~0.072 N/m |
| Silicon Young's modulus | $E_{Si}$ | 130–190 GPa |

### Frequently Asked KTU Topics

- Working principle with block diagram **(high-yield, 7 marks)**.
- Comparison of operating modes **(frequent 7-mark question)**.
- Thermal noise derivation **(standard 7-mark derivation)**.
- Van der Waals force derivation **(common Part A 3-mark question)**.
- Advantages over STM **(almost guaranteed in Part A)**.
- AFM vs SEM vs TEM comparison **(popular comparative question)**.
- Applications in nanomaterials characterization **(7-mark essay)**.

### Quick Mnemonic for AFM Modes

**"CNT" — Contact, Non-contact, Tapping**
- **C**ontact = "**C**rushing" force (largest force, harshest).
- **N**on-contact = "**N**ever touches" (weakest force, cleanest).
- **T**apping = "**T**iptoe" (intermittent, balanced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Note:** For AFM in Part B questions, **always include a labeled block diagram** — examiners in the KTU valuation scheme award 1–2 marks purely for the diagram itself, even if subsequent explanations are partial. The optical lever + feedback loop + *z*-piezo triad is the **canonical diagram** to draw.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
