<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) — Family Overview

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) is a branch of microscopy family that forms images of surfaces by raster-scanning a sharp physical probe across the specimen surface and recording the localized tip–sample interaction (quantum, electrostatic, magnetic, or mechanical) as a function of probe position. The signal is plotted as a 2D/3D intensity map, with **lateral resolution approaching the atomic scale (~0.1 nm)** and vertical resolution of **~0.01 nm**.

SPM is the **umbrella family** that includes:
- **STM** (Scanning Tunneling Microscopy) — electronic/tunneling interaction
- **AFM** (Atomic Force Microscopy) — mechanical/force interaction
- **MFM** (Magnetic Force Microscopy)
- **SNOM/NSOM** (Scanning Near-field Optical Microscopy)
- **SCM** (Scanning Capacitance Microscopy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically covers **STM** under SPM. AFM, while closely related, is generally treated under surface characterization modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Finger-Reading Braille" Method:**
> Imagine a blind person reading a Braille book: the finger (probe) moves letter by letter across the page (sample surface), and each raised dot causes a different sensation. The brain then reconstructs a 3D mental image of the text. Similarly, in SPM, an atomically sharp "finger" (the tip) sweeps across the surface, and a sensor records the *local* physical interaction at every point. The computer builds a nanoscale 3D "Braille map" of the surface. STM uses *electron tunneling* (an electronic sensation) instead of touch.

---

### 1.2 Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) — Core Definition

**Formal Definition:**
> STM is a non-optical, surface-sensitive SPM technique, invented by **Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer at IBM Zurich in 1981** (awarded the **Nobel Prize in Physics, 1986**), that exploits the **quantum mechanical tunneling of electrons** through a thin vacuum (or insulating) barrier between a conductive atomically-sharp tip and a conductive (or semiconductive) sample surface, producing real-space, atomically resolved topographs and electronic structure maps.

**Key Physical Constant in STM:**
- **Decay constant of the tunneling probability in vacuum: κ ≈ 0.51 Å⁻¹ (for typical work function Φ ≈ 4 eV)** — this dictates the extreme surface sensitivity of STM (only the top 1–2 atomic layers contribute to the signal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** STM is one of the most frequently asked techniques in nanomaterial characterization. A typical Part A question (3 marks) asks for the principle; a Part B question (14 marks) asks for principle + instrumentation + advantages/limitations.

---

### 1.3 UV-Visible Spectroscopy — Core Definition

**Formal Definition:**
> UV-Vis Spectroscopy is an absorption-based analytical technique in which the absorption of ultraviolet (180–400 nm) and visible (400–750 nm) electromagnetic radiation by atoms, molecules, or nanostructures is measured as a function of wavelength, yielding information about electronic transitions, optical bandgap, concentration, and **quantum confinement effects** in nanomaterials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Sunglasses + Prism" Test:**
> A pair of cheap sunglasses looks uniformly dark, but a prism splits white light through it into a rainbow, and certain colors are dimmer than others — those dim colors are the wavelengths being absorbed by the dye. UV-Vis spectroscopy does the same with extreme precision: it sends monochromatic light (one wavelength at a time) through a sample, and the detector measures *how much* was absorbed at each wavelength. For nanoparticles, the absorption peak *shifts* (quantum size effect) — like the sunglasses color changing with nanoparticle size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why UV-Vis matters for Nanomaterials:**
> When particle size drops below the Bohr exciton radius (~10 nm for many semiconductors), the bandgap widens — causing a **blue shift** in the absorption onset. UV-Vis is the simplest, cheapest optical probe of this quantum confinement.

**Standard Metric:** The **molar absorptivity (ε)** in the Beer-Lambert law has units of **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹** and can reach **10⁴–10⁶** for strongly allowed electronic transitions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Quantum Mechanical Principle of STM

STM works because of a uniquely quantum phenomenon: **electron tunneling**.

#### Step-by-Step Logic of Tunneling:

1. **Two conductors separated by an insulating barrier (vacuum/air)**: A sharp metallic tip and the conducting sample are brought within ~0.3–1 nm of each other. Classically, electrons cannot cross the vacuum gap.

2. **Wavefunction penetration**: The electron wavefunction (ψ) does not abruptly go to zero at the metal surface — it decays *exponentially* into the vacuum barrier with characteristic **decay length (1/κ)**.

3. **Overlap of wavefunctions**: When the tip is close enough, the decaying wavefunctions of tip and sample overlap. This gives a finite probability of electrons tunneling from tip to sample (or vice versa).

4. **Apply small bias voltage (V, typically 0.01–3 V)**: This drives a measurable **tunneling current (I)** of the order **pA to nA**.

5. **Raster-scan the tip across the surface**: As the tip moves over atoms vs. valleys, the tip–sample distance (d) changes by **~0.1 nm**, but the tunneling current changes by **~one order of magnitude** because of the exponential dependence.

6. **Image construction**: The recorded current (or the feedback-controlled height) is plotted as a function of (x, y) position, giving a 2D image — a 3D topograph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of atomic resolution:** The exponential dependence of I on d means that essentially *only the atom at the tip apex* contributes to the tunneling signal. The tip behaves as a single-atom emitter — this is the origin of STM's atomic resolution.

---

### 2.2 The Tunneling Current Equation (Core STM Formula)

The tunneling current in STM is derived from **Bardeen's Transfer Hamiltonian** (1961) and **Tersoff–Hamann** (1983, 1985) theory:

$$I_t = V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot \exp(-2\kappa d)$$

Where:
- $I_t$ = tunneling current (A)
- $V$ = applied bias voltage between tip and sample (V)
- $\rho_s(E_F)$ = **Local Density of States (LDOS)** of the sample at the Fermi level (states/eV/atom)
- $d$ = tip–sample separation (m or nm)
- $\kappa$ = decay constant of the wavefunction in the barrier (m⁻¹)

The decay constant is given by:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m_e \Phi}}{\hbar} \approx 0.51 \sqrt{\Phi\,\text{[eV]}} \quad \text{Å}^{-1}$$

Where:
- $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg (electron mass)
- $\Phi$ = effective work function of tip + sample (in eV) ≈ **4–5 eV** for clean metal surfaces
- $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s (reduced Planck constant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Implication:** For Φ = 4 eV, 1/κ ≈ 1 Å. So if d increases by just 1 Å, the current drops by a factor of **e² ≈ 7.4**. This is why STM is *spectroscopically sensitive to single-atom-high steps* on surfaces.

---

### 2.3 STM Operational Modes

| Mode | What is held constant | What is measured | Best for | Tip–sample distance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Current Mode (CCM)** | Tunneling current Iₜ (via feedback loop) | Tip vertical position z(x,y) | Rough surfaces, atomic-scale images | ~0.3–1 nm |
| **Constant Height Mode (CHM)** | Tip height z (no feedback) | Tunneling current Iₜ(x,y) | Atomically flat, very fast scanning | < 0.3 nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Constant Current is more common:** At a current of ~1 nA, the feedback loop moves the tip up and down to maintain the setpoint. This gives a direct 3D topograph z(x,y) — which is essentially the surface contour of constant LDOS at E_F.

---

### 2.4 STM Instrumentation Block Diagram

A standard STM has **five key subsystems**:

1. **Atomically Sharp Tip**: Typically etched tungsten (W) wire or Pt–Ir alloy. The tip is the heart — apex should ideally terminate in a single atom.
2. **Piezoelectric Scanner (PZT)**: Three-way (x, y, z) piezoelectric tube/translators that move the tip with sub-Å precision. PZT expands/contracts by ~1 nm per volt.
3. **Vibration Isolation**: Acoustic and seismic dampers (typically hanging stage, eddy current dampers, or spring suspension). Critical because tunneling currents fall on a sub-Å length scale.
4. **Sample/Tip Coarse Positioning (Pan walker / Inchworm)**: For approaching the tip to within tunneling range.
5. **Control Electronics & Computer**: Bias supply, current amplifier (10⁸–10¹⁰ V/A), feedback loop, and data acquisition for image rendering.

**Environmental Requirements:**
- **Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV)** (10⁻¹⁰ Torr) for clean-surface STM
- **Ambient STM** for ex-situ/in-situ biological samples
- **Electrochemical STM (EC-STM)** for samples in electrolyte

---

### 2.5 Principle of UV-Visible Spectroscopy

#### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Photon-matter interaction**: Molecules/nanostructures absorb photons of energy **hν** equal to the energy gap between their electronic states.
2. **Selection rules**: Transitions are allowed if they obey spin selection (ΔS = 0) and Laporte selection rules (for centrosymmetric molecules, g→u is allowed, g→g is forbidden).
3. **Absorption measurement**: The intensity of light transmitted (I) through a sample is compared to the incident intensity (I₀).
4. **Beer-Lambert Law** (governs absorption):
$$A = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$
   - $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless)
   - $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity / extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
   - $c$ = concentration of absorbing species (mol·L⁻¹)
   - $l$ = path length through the cuvette (cm)
5. **Transmittance**: $T = I/I_0 = 10^{-A}$ (or %T = 10⁻ᵃ × 100)
6. **Wavelength scan**: A monochromator sweeps λ from UV to visible, recording A(λ) — the **absorption spectrum**.

---

### 2.6 Tauc Plot for Bandgap Determination (Nanomaterial-specific)

For a semiconductor nanomaterial, the absorption near the band edge follows:

$$\alpha h\nu = A (h\nu - E_g)^n$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹)
- $h\nu$ = photon energy (eV)
- $E_g$ = optical bandgap (eV)
- $A$ = constant
- $n$ = ½ for **direct allowed**, 2 for **indirect allowed**, 3/2 for direct forbidden, 3 for indirect forbidden

**Procedure:** Plot $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n}$ vs $h\nu$; the **x-intercept of the linear region** gives $E_g$.

$$(\alpha h\nu)^2 = A(h\nu - E_g) \quad \text{(direct bandgap)}$$

$$\left( \frac{A \cdot \rho \cdot 2.303}{M} \right) = \alpha \quad \text{(from UV-Vis data)}$$

Where $\rho$ = density, $M$ = molar mass, $l$ = path length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantum Confinement Signature in UV-Vis:** For nanoparticles smaller than the exciton Bohr radius, the bandgap **increases** with decreasing size, shifting the absorption onset to **shorter wavelengths (blue shift)**. This is the **Burstein-Moss shift** combined with **quantum confinement** — and UV-Vis is the routine technique to observe it.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_t \propto V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$ | STM tunneling current | A | STM image interpretation |
| 2 | $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e \Phi}/\hbar \approx 0.51\sqrt{\Phi}$ | Wavefunction decay constant | Å⁻¹ | Estimating STM tip–sample distance sensitivity |
| 3 | $E_{\text{tunnel}} = eV$ | Energy window of tunneling electrons | eV | STS (Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy) |
| 4 | $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I) = \varepsilon c l$ | Beer-Lambert law | dimensionless | UV-Vis absorbance |
| 5 | $T = 10^{-A}$ | Transmittance | % or fraction | UV-Vis %T measurement |
| 6 | $\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^n$ | Tauc relation | eV | Nanomaterial bandgap |
| 7 | $E_g = hc/\lambda_{\text{onset}}$ | Bandgap from absorption onset | eV | Direct estimate from spectrum |
| 8 | $\Delta E_g \propto 1/r^2$ (particle in a box) | Quantum confinement shift | eV | Explaining blue shift in NPs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility — Real-World Application:**
> - **STM**: Used in semiconductor industry (atomic-scale defects, surface reconstruction Si(111) 7×7), single-atom manipulation (IBM's quantum corral, 1990), and surface chemistry of catalysts.
> - **UV-Vis**: Quality control of quantum dots (CdSe, perovskite NCs), sizing Au/Ag nanoparticles (SPR at 520 nm / 400 nm), confirming graphene oxide reduction, and monitoring photocatalyst bandgap tuning.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tunneling Current Dependence on Tip–Sample Distance

We start from the **one-dimensional time-independent Schrödinger equation** for an electron in a rectangular potential barrier:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e} \frac{d^2 \psi}{dz^2} + V(z)\psi = E\psi$$

**Step 1:** Define the barrier region. For a tip–vacuum–sample geometry, the work function Φ acts as a barrier of height $V_0 = E_{vac} - E_F$ (measured relative to the electron energy $E$).

$$V(z) = V_0 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 < z < d$$
$$V(z) = 0 \quad \text{elsewhere}$$

**Step 2:** Inside the barrier ($E < V_0$), the Schrödinger equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dz^2} = \frac{2m_e(V_0 - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi = \kappa^2 \psi$$

**Step 3:** Define the decay constant:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m_e(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar} = \frac{\sqrt{2m_e \Phi}}{\hbar}$$

**Step 4:** The general solution inside the barrier is:

$$\psi(z) = A e^{-\kappa z} + B e^{+\kappa z}$$

**Step 5:** For a thick barrier, $B e^{+\kappa z}$ diverges as z → ∞. The physically acceptable solution is:

$$\psi(z) = A e^{-\kappa z}$$

**Step 6:** The probability density of finding the electron at distance $z$ inside the barrier is:

$$P(z) = \vert \psi(z) \vert^2 = \vert A \vert^2 e^{-2\kappa z}$$

**Step 7:** The tunneling probability $T$ across a barrier of width $d$ is:

$$T \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$$

**Step 8:** From Bardeen's Transfer Hamiltonian, the tunneling current is:

$$I_t \propto V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot T = V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$$

**Step 9:** Evaluate numerically for Φ = 4 eV (typical W or Pt–Ir tip):

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{1.166 \times 10^{-48}}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}} = \frac{1.080 \times 10^{-24}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$\kappa \approx 1.024 \times 10^{10} \text{ m}^{-1} = 1.024 \text{ Å}^{-1}$$

**Step 10:** So the tunneling current at two different tip–sample distances:

$$I_t(d) \propto e^{-2(1.024 \text{ Å}^{-1}) d} = e^{-2.048\, d \text{ (in Å)}}$$

| $d$ (Å) | $\exp(-2\kappa d)$ | Relative current |
|---|---|---|
| 3.0 | $\exp(-6.14) = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1.0 (reference) |
| 4.0 | $\exp(-8.19) = 2.8 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.13 |
| 5.0 | $\exp(-10.24) = 3.6 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.017 |

**Conclusion:** A **1 Å increase in tip–sample distance** reduces the tunneling current by **~7.4×**. This proves STM's extreme vertical sensitivity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Quantum Confinement Energy Shift in a Nanoparticle

The simplest model for a spherical nanoparticle of radius $r$ is the **particle-in-a-sphere** (infinite potential well) model.

**Step 1:** The ground state energy of an electron confined in a 1D box of size $L$ is:

$$E_1 = \frac{h^2}{8 m_e L^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m_e L^2}$$

**Step 2:** For a 3D spherical box of radius $r$, the lowest energy level is:

$$E_1^{3D} = \frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m_e r^2}$$

**Step 3:** In bulk semiconductor, the bandgap is $E_g^{bulk}$. For the confined nanoparticle, the bandgap is:

$$E_g^{NP} = E_g^{bulk} + \frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m_r r^2} - \frac{1.8 e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r}$$

**Step 4:** The first term is **quantum confinement (kinetic energy)**, which **increases** $E_g$ as $r$ decreases.

**Step 5:** The second term is the **Coulomb term (electron–hole attraction)**, which **decreases** $E_g$ slightly.

**Step 6:** For strong confinement ($r < r_{Bohr}$), the kinetic term dominates, and:

$$\Delta E_g = E_g^{NP} - E_g^{bulk} \approx \frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m_r r^2} \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$$

**Step 7:** Numerical example: CdSe, $m_r \approx 0.13\,m_e$, $E_g^{bulk} = 1.74$ eV.

For $r = 5$ nm: $\Delta E_g \approx 0.05$ eV → $E_g^{NP} \approx 1.79$ eV → $\lambda_{onset} = 1240/1.79 = 693$ nm (red).

For $r = 2$ nm: $\Delta E_g \approx 0.32$ eV → $E_g^{NP} \approx 2.06$ eV → $\lambda_{onset} = 1240/2.06 = 602$ nm (orange).

For $r = 1$ nm: $\Delta E_g \approx 1.29$ eV → $E_g^{NP} \approx 3.03$ eV → $\lambda_{onset} = 1240/3.03 = 409$ nm (blue).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Connection:** This is *exactly* why "smaller CdSe quantum dots glow bluer" — the experimentally observed blue shift in UV-Vis is direct evidence of quantum confinement, and the KTU board frequently tests this in Part B questions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of STM vs AFM vs UV-Vis

| Parameter | STM | AFM | UV-Vis Spectroscopy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical principle | Quantum tunneling | Inter-atomic force (van der Waals/repulsive) | Photon absorption |
| Probe type | Conductive atomically-sharp tip | Sharp tip on cantilever (Si, Si₃N₄) | None (light-based) |
| Sample requirement | **Must be conductive** | Any solid (conductive or insulating) | Transparent solution/film |
| Lateral resolution | Atomic (~0.1 nm) | Atomic (in true NC-AFM) | Not applicable (macroscopic) |
| Vertical resolution | ~0.01 nm | ~0.01 nm | Not applicable |
| Environment | UHV / ambient / EC | UHV / ambient / liquid | Ambient |
| Information provided | Topography + LDOS (electronic structure) | Topography + mechanical/magnetic properties | Bandgap, concentration, particle size (indirect) |
| Destructive? | Non-destructive | Non-destructive | Non-destructive |
| Sample preparation | Conductive coating if needed | Minimal | Dissolve/disperse in solvent |
| Approx. cost (₹) | 50 L – 5 Cr | 10 L – 1 Cr | 2 L – 15 L |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the principle of Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

STM is based on the **quantum mechanical tunneling of electrons** between a sharp metallic tip and a conducting sample, separated by a thin vacuum (or insulating) barrier of width **d ~ 0.3 to 1 nm**. A small bias voltage (V) is applied between the tip and the sample, causing a tunneling current $I_t \propto V \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$ to flow. As the tip is raster-scanned across the surface using piezoelectric scanners, the current varies exponentially with tip–sample distance, providing topographic images with **atomic resolution (~0.1 nm lateral, ~0.01 nm vertical)**. A feedback loop maintains either constant current (CCM) or constant height (CHM), and the resulting z(x,y) or I(x,y) map is rendered as a surface topograph. STM was invented by **Binnig and Rohrer in 1981** (Nobel Prize 1986).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State and explain the Beer-Lambert law. Mention its significance in nanomaterial characterization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Beer-Lambert law** states that the absorbance of a solution is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length of light through the sample:

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

where $A$ is absorbance, $\varepsilon$ is the molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $c$ is concentration (mol·L⁻¹), and $l$ is path length (cm).

**Significance in Nanomaterials:**
- Quantifies **concentration** of nanoparticle suspensions.
- The **absorption onset (λ_onset)** gives the **optical bandgap** via $E_g = hc/\lambda_{onset}$.
- The **shift of the absorption peak** with particle size directly evidences **quantum confinement** in semiconductor QDs and **SPR shifts** in metal nanoparticles.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**a)** Describe the principle and construction of Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) with suitable diagrams. Discuss its **operational modes** in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**

**b)** Explain **UV-Vis spectroscopy** and discuss how it is used to determine the **optical bandgap and quantum confinement effects** in nanomaterials, with reference to a **Tauc plot**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### **Solution to Q A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Principle (2 marks):**
STM operates on the principle of **quantum mechanical tunneling**. When a conductive atomically sharp tip is brought within 0.3–1 nm of a conductive sample and a small bias voltage (mV to a few V) is applied, electrons tunnel through the vacuum gap, producing a measurable current $I_t$. This current depends exponentially on the tip–sample distance $d$:

$$I_t = V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$$

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Tip**: Etched tungsten (W) or Pt–Ir wire; atomically sharp (ideally single-atom apex).
- **Piezoelectric Scanner (PZT)**: A three-way (x, y, z) piezoelectric tube scanner, with sub-Ångstrom precision (~1 nm/V sensitivity).
- **Coarse Positioner**: Inchworm or Pan walker for approaching the tip to within tunneling range (~1 mm → ~1 nm).
- **Vibration Isolation**: Stacked metal plates with viton spacers; eddy-current dampers; spring suspension to remove acoustic/mechanical noise.
- **UHV Chamber**: ~10⁻¹⁰ Torr to maintain surface cleanliness.
- **Electronics**: Bias source, current amplifier (10⁸–10¹⁰ V/A), feedback loop (PID), and DAQ for imaging.

**Operational Modes (2 marks):**

| Mode | Constant | Measured | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Current Mode (CCM)** | Tunneling current Iₜ | Tip height z(x,y) | Rough surfaces, real-time topographic imaging |
| **Constant Height Mode (CHM)** | Tip height z | Tunneling current Iₜ(x,y) | Atomically flat, ultra-fast scanning |

In CCM, the feedback loop continuously adjusts the tip's z-position to keep Iₜ at a setpoint; the recorded z(x,y) directly gives the surface contour. In CHM, the tip is held at constant z and the variation in Iₜ is mapped — faster but riskier on rough samples.

---

##### **Solution to Q A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Principle of UV-Vis (2 marks):**
UV-Vis spectroscopy measures the absorption of ultraviolet (180–400 nm) and visible (400–750 nm) light by a sample. When a photon's energy $h\nu$ matches the energy gap between electronic states of the absorbing species, the photon is absorbed, and the transmitted intensity decreases. The absorbance follows Beer-Lambert law: $A = \varepsilon c l$.

**Instrumentation (1 mark):**
Deuterium lamp (UV) + Tungsten-Halogen lamp (visible) → Monochromator → Cuvette holder (l = 1 cm) → Photomultiplier/photodiode → Computer.

**Determination of Bandgap by Tauc Plot (3 marks):**
The optical bandgap is determined using the Tauc relation:

$$\alpha h\nu = A(h\nu - E_g)^n$$

where $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient, $h\nu$ is photon energy, $E_g$ is the bandgap, and $n = 1/2$ for direct allowed, $n = 2$ for indirect allowed transitions. A plot of $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n}$ vs $h\nu$ is drawn. The **linear region** is extrapolated to the x-axis; the **intercept** gives $E_g$.

**Quantum Confinement Evidence (1 mark):**
As the nanoparticle size drops below the exciton Bohr radius, the bandgap **increases** as $1/r^2$. This causes a **blue shift** in the absorption onset — observed experimentally in CdSe, ZnS, PbS QDs. The shift in the SPR peak of Au/Ag NPs is also a signature of size-dependent optical behavior.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**a)** Discuss the **instrumentation and working of UV-Vis spectrophotometer** with a block diagram. How is it used to characterize **metal nanoparticles** (surface plasmon resonance) and **semiconductor quantum dots**? **\[7 Marks\]**

**b)** With necessary theory and equations, explain the principle of **STM imaging in constant current mode**. Discuss the **role of piezoelectric transducers**, **atomic resolution origin**, and compare STM with **AFM**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

##### **Solution to Q B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Instrumentation and Working (3 marks):**
A UV-Vis spectrophotometer consists of:
1. **Light sources**: Deuterium (D₂) lamp for 180–400 nm (UV) and Tungsten-Halogen lamp for 320–1100 nm (Vis-NIR). A beam chopper alternates between the two.
2. **Monochromator**: Diffraction grating (1200 lines/mm) selects one wavelength at a time.
3. **Sample compartment**: Quartz cuvette (transparent in UV) of 1 cm path length.
4. **Detector**: Photomultiplier tube (PMT) for UV/Vis, or Si photodiode.
5. **Signal processing**: Log amplifier to convert transmitted intensity to absorbance directly.

The light is split into two beams: a **reference beam** ($I_0$) and a **sample beam** ($I$). The ratio $I/I_0$ is measured as a function of λ and the **absorbance spectrum** $A(\lambda) = -\log_{10}(T) = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ is plotted.

**Characterization of Metal Nanoparticles (2 marks):**
Noble metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag) exhibit a strong **Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR)** absorption due to collective oscillation of conduction electrons. The SPR peak position depends on:
- **Size** (larger NPs → redshift)
- **Shape** (rods → two peaks: transverse and longitudinal)
- **Dielectric environment** (surrounding refractive index)
- **Interparticle distance** (aggregation → broad red-shifted peak)

A typical **Au NP** shows SPR at **~520 nm** (visible red), and **Ag NP** at **~400 nm** (yellow). UV-Vis is the routine, fastest technique to confirm NP formation and estimate size.

**Characterization of Quantum Dots (2 marks):**
Semiconductor QDs (CdSe, ZnS, PbS) absorb photons of energy ≥ $E_g$ and emit at slightly longer wavelengths. As the QD size decreases:
- The absorption onset shifts to **shorter wavelengths (blue shift)**.
- The bandgap widens as $E_g^{NP} = E_g^{bulk} + \pi^2 \hbar^2/(2 m_r r^2)$.
- The excitonic absorption features become sharper.

UV-Vis spectra of QDs thus provide:
- Confirmation of **quantum confinement**.
- **Size estimation** using empirical curves (e.g., Peng's equation for CdSe).
- **Bandgap determination** via Tauc plot.

---

##### **Solution to Q B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Theory of Constant Current Mode (3 marks):**
In CCM, the tunneling current $I_t$ is maintained at a fixed setpoint (typically 0.1–10 nA) using a **PID feedback loop**. The feedback loop continuously adjusts the z-piezo voltage to keep $I_t$ constant. As the tip scans over atomic-scale features:
- Over a protruding atom: $d$ decreases → $I_t$ rises → feedback retracts the tip.
- Over a depression: $d$ increases → $I_t$ falls → feedback extends the tip.

The recorded $z(x,y)$ is a contour map of constant LDOS at the Fermi level — the STM topograph.

**Piezoelectric Transducers (2 marks):**
Piezoelectric ceramics (PZT – lead zirconate titanate) deform by ~1 nm per applied volt, enabling sub-Ångstrom positioning. Three PZT stacks (x, y, z) drive the tip across the surface. **Piezo creep, hysteresis, and thermal drift** are the main practical limitations; closed-loop scanners with capacitive position sensors correct for these.

**Origin of Atomic Resolution (1 mark):**
The exponential dependence $I_t \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$ (with $\kappa \approx 1$ Å⁻¹) means that an increase in d by just **1 Å** reduces $I_t$ by a factor of **~7.4**. Therefore, only the **atom at the tip apex** contributes to tunneling — the tip effectively acts as a single-atom probe.

**Comparison with AFM (1 mark):**

| Aspect | STM | AFM |
|---|---|---|
| Probe interaction | Electron tunneling | Inter-atomic force (van der Waals/Coulomb) |
| Sample requirement | Conductive | Any (conductive/insulating) |
| Imaging mode | CCM, CHM | Contact, Non-contact, Tapping |
| Provides | Topography + electronic structure | Topography + mechanical/electrical/magnetic |

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 STM / UV-Vis Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing constant current and constant height modes** — students often write "tip is moved to maintain constant height" — but it is the **current** that is held constant in CCM; the **height** is the variable being recorded.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of LDOS** $\rho_s(E_F)$ — STM image is **not pure topography**; it is a map of constant LDOS, so a flat surface with different electronic states can give contrast.
> 3. **Using the wrong Tauc exponent $n$** — must know whether the material has direct or indirect bandgap. CdS, ZnO, TiO₂ (anatase) are **direct** ($n=1/2$); Si, Ge are **indirect** ($n=2$).
> 4. **Writing Beer-Lambert as $A = \varepsilon c l$ without units** — always specify **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**.
> 5. **Missing the requirement of conductive sample for STM** — STM cannot image insulators; AFM must be used instead.
> 6. **Not mentioning the inventors (Binnig & Rohrer, 1981, Nobel 1986)** — KTU examiners reward this ½ mark.
> 7. **Confusing SPR of metal NPs (520 nm) with excitonic absorption of QDs** — plasmon resonance arises from collective electron oscillation, not bandgap transition.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **STM Highlights**
> - Invented by **Binnig & Rohrer, 1981**; Nobel Prize in Physics, 1986.
> - Principle: **Quantum tunneling** of electrons across a thin vacuum barrier.
> - Equation: $I_t \propto V \cdot \rho_s(E_F) \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$, with $\kappa \approx 0.51 \sqrt{\Phi}$ Å⁻¹.
> - Sample **must be conductive**; tip is W or Pt–Ir, atomically sharp.
> - Two modes: **Constant Current Mode (CCM)** — most common; **Constant Height Mode (CHM)** — fast, for flat samples.
> - **PZT scanners** provide sub-Å positioning; feedback loop is essential.
> - Atomic resolution arises from the **exponential dependence** of $I_t$ on $d$ (~7.4× change per 1 Å).
> - STM gives **topography + electronic structure (LDOS)**, not pure geometry.
> - **UHV or ambient** operation; vibration isolation is critical.
> - STS (Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy) = measure $dI/dV$ vs $V$ → local DOS at energy $E = eV$.

> **UV-Vis Spectroscopy Highlights**
> - Absorbance: $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I) = \varepsilon c l$ (Beer-Lambert Law).
> - $\varepsilon$ in **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**; path length $l$ typically 1 cm.
> - Bandgap from Tauc plot: $(\alpha h\nu)^{1/n} = A(h\nu - E_g)$ → $n = 1/2$ direct, $n = 2$ indirect.
> - **Quantum confinement**: $E_g$ **increases** as particle size decreases; absorption onset **blue-shifts**; $\Delta E_g \propto 1/r^2$.
> - **Metal NP SPR**: Au ~520 nm, Ag ~400 nm; size/shape/dielectric environment dependent.
> - Instrumentation: D₂ + W lamps → Monochromator → Cuvette → PMT detector.
> - **Non-destructive**, solution-based, cheapest optical characterization technique.
> - Confirms QD formation, NP size, bandgap, concentration.

> **Cross-Connective Comparison Points**
> - STM/AFM = nanoscale surface imaging; UV-Vis = bulk optical response.
> - STM requires **conductivity**; UV-Vis requires **transparency**.
> - Both are **non-destructive** and routinely used in nanomaterial characterization.
> - STM gives **local** (single-atom) info; UV-Vis gives **ensemble** (millions of particles) info.
> - For **semiconductor QDs**: STM/AFM confirms size & shape, UV-Vis confirms **bandgap & quantum confinement**.
> - For **metal NPs**: TEM/AFM confirms morphology, UV-Vis confirms **SPR** and aggregation state.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raman Spectroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raman Spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Raman Spectroscopy** is a non-destructive vibrational spectroscopic technique that probes the inelastic scattering of monochromatic light by molecular and crystalline systems. It is based on the **Raman Effect**, first observed experimentally by Sir **C. V. Raman** and K. S. Krishnan in **1928** (Nobel Prize in Physics, **1930**). The technique provides information about vibrational, rotational, and other low-frequency modes in a material by measuring the frequency shift between incident photons and the resulting scattered photons.

When a monochromatic beam of light (typically a laser) interacts with a sample, the vast majority of photons are scattered **elastically** (Rayleigh scattering) at the same frequency. However, a tiny fraction (approximately **1 in 10⁷** photons) undergoes **inelastic scattering**, gaining or losing energy quanta equal to the vibrational energy of the molecule. This inelastic component constitutes the **Raman signal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Raman spectroscopy is fundamentally a tool to measure the change in **polarizability** of a molecule during vibration — a concept that distinguishes it from infrared (IR) spectroscopy, which measures the change in **dipole moment**.

## 1.2 The Three Scattering Regimes

| Scattering Type | Energy Exchange | Photon Frequency | Relative Intensity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rayleigh (Elastic)** | None | $\nu_s = \nu_0$ | ~99.999% |
| **Stokes Raman (Inelastic)** | Photon **loses** energy | $\nu_s = \nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$ | Weak |
| **Anti-Stokes Raman (Inelastic)** | Photon **gains** energy | $\nu_s = \nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$ | Very weak (at room T) |

Where $\nu_0$ is the frequency of the incident laser and $\nu_{vib}$ is the vibrational frequency of the molecule.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tennis Ball and Wall" Analogy:**
> Imagine throwing a tennis ball at a stationary wall. The ball bounces back with the **same speed** (Rayleigh scattering — elastic). Now imagine the wall is actually a bell that vibrates when struck. Some of the ball's kinetic energy is transferred to the bell, causing it to ring, and the ball returns **slower** (Stokes scattering). Occasionally, the bell is already vibrating — the ball can pick up extra energy and bounce back **faster** (Anti-Stokes scattering). The change in the ball's speed (frequency) tells us about the bell's (molecule's) vibrational structure.

In Raman spectroscopy, the "tennis ball" is the **photon**, the "wall/bell" is the **molecule**, and the change in speed is the **Raman shift** $\Delta \tilde{\nu}$ (typically measured in **wavenumbers, cm⁻¹**).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{10}$ **cm/s**
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
- **Boltzmann's constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- **Raman shift unit:** $\tilde{\nu}$ in **cm⁻¹** (reciprocal centimeters)
- **Typical laser wavelengths:** **532 nm**, **633 nm**, **785 nm**, **1064 nm** (FT-Raman)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Reminder:** Wavenumber $\tilde{\nu} = 1/\lambda$ (in cm) and the relationship to frequency is $\nu = c \tilde{\nu}$. Thus, $1\,\text{cm}^{-1} \approx 29.979\,\text{GHz} \approx 0.12398\,\text{meV}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

When an electromagnetic wave of electric field $\mathbf{E}$ interacts with a molecule, it induces a time-varying dipole moment $\mathbf{P}$:

$$\mathbf{P} = \alpha \mathbf{E}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **polarizability tensor** of the molecule. The polarizability is **not constant** — it fluctuates as the molecule vibrates. If the molecular vibration $Q$ modulates the polarizability, i.e., if $\left(\dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$, the vibration is **Raman active**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Raman Process

1. **Excitation:** A monochromatic laser photon ($h\nu_0$) strikes the molecule.
2. **Distortion:** The oscillating electric field distorts the electron cloud of the molecule, inducing a dipole.
3. **Virtual State:** The molecule is momentarily promoted to a short-lived **"virtual" energy state** (not a true stationary state — it exists for $\sim 10^{-14}$ s).
4. **Scattering:** The molecule relaxes back, emitting a scattered photon.
5. **Three Outcomes:**
   - **Rayleigh:** Molecule returns to the *original* vibrational level → same frequency.
   - **Stokes:** Molecule returns to a *higher* vibrational level → photon loses energy ($\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$).
   - **Anti-Stokes:** Molecule returns to a *lower* vibrational level → photon gains energy ($\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$).

## 2.3 Selection Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raman Selection Rule (Kernal Rule):** A vibration is Raman active if and only if there is a change in the polarizability of the molecule during the vibration.
> $$\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$$
>
> **IR Selection Rule:** A vibration is IR active if and only if there is a change in the dipole moment.
> $$\left(\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$$
>
> **Mutual Exclusion Principle:** For molecules with a center of inversion (centrosymmetric molecules like CO₂, benzene), no vibration can be **both** Raman and IR active.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarizes the **most examination-relevant** Raman equations. Memorize the symbols, units, and the physical meaning of each.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tilde{\nu}_{Stokes} = \tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_{vib}$ | Stokes line position | cm⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\tilde{\nu}_{Anti\text{-}Stokes} = \tilde{\nu}_0 + \tilde{\nu}_{vib}$ | Anti-Stokes line position | cm⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_{s} = \tilde{\nu}_{vib}$ | Raman shift (independent of $\tilde{\nu}_0$) | cm⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\mathbf{P} = \alpha \mathbf{E}$ | Induced dipole moment (tensor form) | C·m |
| 5 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$ | Raman activity condition | — |
| 6 | $\dfrac{N_{Anti\text{-}Stokes}}{N_{Stokes}} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{h \nu_{vib}}{k_B T}\right)$ | Boltzmann population ratio (≈ 0.01 at 300 K for $\tilde{\nu}_{vib}$ = 1000 cm⁻¹) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{I_{AS}}{I_{S}} = \left(\dfrac{\tilde{\nu}_0 + \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{\tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}\right)^{4} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{h c \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T}\right)$ | Full intensity ratio (includes $\nu^4$ scattering) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\alpha(Q) = \alpha_0 + \left(\dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q + \cdots$ | Taylor expansion of polarizability | — |
| 9 | $I_{Rayleigh} \propto \nu_0^{4}$ | Rayleigh intensity (blue sky) | W/m² |
| 10 | $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_0} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_s}$ | Experimental determination of shift | cm⁻¹ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical QC:** Polymorph identification in drug formulations (e.g., distinguishing crystalline vs. amorphous forms of carbamazepine).
- **CNT/Graphene Quality:** The intensity ratio of the D-band (disorder, ~1350 cm⁻¹) to the G-band (graphitic, ~1580 cm⁻¹) gives the defect density of carbon nanomaterials — a critical metric for nano-electronics.
- **In-situ Process Monitoring:** Reaction kinetics in CVD graphene growth using real-time Raman.
- **Forensics/Archeometry:** Identification of pigments in artworks (e.g., vermilion HgS, lapis lazuli).
- **Biomedical Diagnostics:** SERS-based biosensors for cancer marker detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Classical Derivation of the Raman Effect

The classical model treats the molecule as a system of oscillating charges whose polarizability is modulated by the nuclear vibrations.

### Step 1 — Define the Vibrational Coordinate

Let the normal-mode vibration be described by a generalized coordinate $Q(t)$:

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_{vib} t)$$

**Logic:** The atoms oscillate about their equilibrium positions with frequency $\nu_{vib}$ and amplitude $Q_0$.

### Step 2 — Define the Incident Electric Field

The laser provides a sinusoidally oscillating electric field:

$$E(t) = E_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_0 t)$$

**Logic:** Monochromatic light of frequency $\nu_0$ drives the electron cloud.

### Step 3 — Taylor-Expand the Polarizability

Because the polarizability depends on the nuclear coordinates, we expand it as a Taylor series about the equilibrium geometry $Q = 0$:

$$\alpha(Q) = \alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 \alpha}{\partial Q^2}\right)_0 Q^2 + \cdots$$

**Logic:** For small-amplitude vibrations, the second and higher-order terms are negligible (harmonic approximation). We retain only the linear term.

### Step 4 — Compute the Induced Dipole Moment

The induced dipole is $P(t) = \alpha(Q) \cdot E(t)$. Substituting:

$$P(t) = \left[\alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_{vib} t)\right] E_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_0 t)$$

**Logic:** We now have a product of two cosines — a mathematical signature of a mixing/frequency-modulation process.

### Step 5 — Apply the Trigonometric Product-to-Sum Identity

Using the identity $2 \cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$P(t) = \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_0 t) + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 E_0 \Big[\cos(2 \pi (\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}) t) + \cos(2 \pi (\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}) t)\Big]$$

**Logic:** The dipole now radiates at **three distinct frequencies**:

| Frequency Component | Physical Origin | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\nu_0$ | Constant polarizability | **Rayleigh** scattering |
| $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$ | Modulated polarizability (lower) | **Stokes** Raman |
| $\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$ | Modulated polarizability (higher) | **Anti-Stokes** Raman |

### Step 6 — Derive the Raman Activity Condition

For the modulated terms to exist, the coefficient $\left(\dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0$ **must be non-zero**. Hence:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0 \quad \text{(Raman active)}}$$

**Logic:** This is the heart of the classical selection rule. A vibration that does not alter the electron cloud's deformability cannot be observed in the Raman spectrum.

---

## 3.2 Quantum Mechanical Picture — Population Ratio

The intensity asymmetry between Stokes and Anti-Stokes lines arises from the **Boltzmann population distribution** of vibrational energy levels at temperature $T$.

### Step 1 — Vibrational Energy Level Populations

The population $N_n$ of the $n$-th vibrational level is:

$$N_n \propto g_n \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_n}{k_B T}\right) = g_n \exp\!\left(-\frac{(n + \tfrac{1}{2}) h \nu_{vib}}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Logic:** Higher vibrational levels are exponentially less populated. At room temperature ($k_B T \approx 207\,\text{cm}^{-1}$ at 298 K), the ground state ($n=0$) dominates for typical $\nu_{vib} > 500\,\text{cm}^{-1}$.

### Step 2 — Population Ratio Between $|n=0\rangle$ and $|n=1\rangle$

For non-degenerate levels ($g_0 = g_1 = 1$):

$$\frac{N_1}{N_0} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h \nu_{vib}}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Logic:** Anti-Stokes scattering requires the molecule to **start** in $|n=1\rangle$, while Stokes scattering starts from $|n=0\rangle$. The ratio directly gives the population imbalance.

### Step 3 — Convert to Intensity Ratio (Including $\nu^4$ Factor)

The scattered intensity scales as $I \propto \nu^4 N$ (Rayleigh-type dependence from classical EM theory):

$$\frac{I_{AS}}{I_{S}} = \left(\frac{\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}}{\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}}\right)^{4} \exp\!\left(-\frac{h \nu_{vib}}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Logic:** Anti-Stokes is further suppressed by (i) the $\nu^4$ prefactor (its frequency is higher, but $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$ has a larger denominator-weighted contribution) and (ii) the Boltzmann factor.

### Step 4 — Numerical Sanity Check (Worked Example)

For a typical C–C stretch at $\tilde{\nu}_{vib} = 1000\,\text{cm}^{-1}$ at $T = 298\,\text{K}$:

$$\frac{h c \tilde{\nu}_{vib}}{k_B T} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{10})(1000)}{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(298)} \approx 4.83$$

$$\frac{N_1}{N_0} = e^{-4.83} \approx 0.00797 \approx 0.8\%$$

**Logic:** Only ~0.8% of molecules occupy the first excited vibrational level at 298 K — explaining why **Stokes lines are roughly 100× more intense** than anti-Stokes lines at room temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For High-Temperature Analysis:** The Anti-Stokes/Stokes intensity ratio can be inverted to **measure local temperature** with micrometer resolution — a powerful technique in materials science (e.g., non-contact thermometry of microelectronic devices, study of Joule heating in graphene).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Equation Maps to a KTU Problem

| KTU Problem Type | Primary Equation(s) to Apply | Key Steps |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Calculate Raman shift from $\lambda_0$, $\lambda_s$ | Eq. 10: $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = 1/\lambda_0 - 1/\lambda_s$ | Convert nm → cm, take difference |
| Predict Stokes/Anti-Stokes line position | Eq. 1 & 2 | Add/subtract $\tilde{\nu}_{vib}$ from $\tilde{\nu}_0$ |
| Compute population ratio | Eq. 6 | Evaluate exponent, take $e^{-x}$ |
| Compute intensity ratio | Eq. 7 | Apply $\nu^4$ prefactor + Boltzmann factor |
| Justify Raman activity | Eq. 5 | Show $\partial\alpha/\partial Q \neq 0$ from symmetry |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Modern Dispersive Raman Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LS["LASER SOURCE 532 nm or 785 nm"]
    FL1["BAND PASS FILTER"]
    SAMP["SAMPLE STAGE Micropositioner"]
    OBJV["MICROSCOPE OBJECTIVE 50x or 100x"]
    NF["NOTCH or EDGE FILTER Rayleigh Rejection OD 6"]
    SLIT["ENTRANCE SLIT"]
    GRAT["DIFFRACTION GRATING 1800 lines per mm"]
    CCD["CCD DETECTOR Thermoelectric Cooled minus 70 C"]
    DAQ["DATA ACQUISITION AND COMPUTER"]
    SPEC["RAMAN SPECTRUM Plot of Intensity vs Raman Shift"]
    LS --> FL1
    FL1 --> SAMP
    SAMP --> OBJV
    OBJV --> NF
    NF --> SLIT
    SLIT --> GRAT
    GRAT --> CCD
    CCD --> DAQ
    DAQ --> SPEC
```

**Logic of the Block Flow:**

1. The **laser** provides a clean monochromatic excitation line.
2. The **band-pass filter** cleans the laser of plasma lines and fluorescence sidebands.
3. The **sample** is irradiated through a microscope objective (for micro-Raman) or a free-space lens.
4. The scattered light is collected in a back-scattering geometry (180°), passed through a **notch filter** that blocks the unshifted Rayleigh line (typically by 6 orders of magnitude), dispersed by a **grating**, and recorded on a **CCD**.
5. The computer plots **intensity vs. Raman shift (cm⁻¹)**, producing the spectrum.

## 4.2 Energy Level Diagram — Quantum Picture of Stokes and Anti-Stokes Scattering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VSTATE1["VIRTUAL STATE Short lived 10 to the power of minus 14 seconds"]
    LEVEL0["VIBRATIONAL GROUND STATE n equals 0"]
    LEVEL1["FIRST EXCITED VIBRATIONAL STATE n equals 1"]
    HNU0["INCIDENT PHOTON h times nu 0"]
    STOKES["STOKES PHOTON h times nu 0 minus nu vib"]
    ANTISTOKES["ANTI STOKES PHOTON h times nu 0 plus nu vib"]
    ABS0["ABSORPTION FROM n equals 0"]
    ABS1["ABSORPTION FROM n equals 1"]
    STOKESEM["EMISSION TO n equals 1"]
    ASEM["EMISSION TO n equals 0"]
    LEVEL0 --> ABS0
    ABS0 --> VSTATE1
    VSTATE1 --> STOKESEM
    STOKESEM --> LEVEL1
    STOKESEM -.-> STOKES
    LEVEL1 --> ABS1
    ABS1 --> VSTATE1
    VSTATE1 --> ASEM
    ASEM --> LEVEL0
    ASEM -.-> ANTISTOKES
```

**Logic of the Quantum Picture:**

- The molecule is excited to a **virtual state** — a non-stationary superposition that exists for only $\sim 10^{-14}$ s. This is **not** a real electronic state.
- **Stokes path** (solid arrows): Ground state → Virtual state → First excited vibrational state. Net: molecule gains one vibrational quantum.
- **Anti-Stokes path** (dashed arrows): First excited state → Virtual state → Ground state. Net: molecule loses one vibrational quantum.
- The energy difference appears as a shift in the scattered photon's frequency.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Identifying the Scattering Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is scattered photon frequency equal to incident frequency?"}
    A1["RAYLEIGH SCATTERING Elastic No energy change"]
    Q2{"Is scattered photon frequency LESS than incident?"}
    A2["STOKES RAMAN SCATTERING Photon loses energy"]
    Q3{"Is scattered photon frequency GREATER than incident?"}
    A3["ANTI STOKES RAMAN SCATTERING Photon gains energy"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q1 -- No --> Q3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Raman Effect. Distinguish between Stokes and Anti-Stokes Raman lines. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Raman Effect** is the inelastic scattering of monochromatic light by molecules, in which the scattered photon undergoes a frequency shift corresponding to the vibrational energy levels of the scattering species. It was discovered by **C. V. Raman in 1928** (Nobel Prize, 1930).

| Feature | Stokes Line | Anti-Stokes Line |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Frequency** | $\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$ (lower) | $\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$ (higher) |
| **Energy exchange** | Photon loses energy | Photon gains energy |
| **Initial state** | Ground vibrational state ($n=0$) | Excited vibrational state ($n \geq 1$) |
| **Relative intensity at 298 K** | Strong | Weak (~1% of Stokes) |
| **Origin of asymmetry** | — | Lower Boltzmann population of $n \geq 1$ |

### Question 2: State and explain the selection rule for Raman activity. Why is it complementary to IR spectroscopy? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A molecular vibration is **Raman active** if it produces a change in the **polarizability** $\alpha$ of the molecule during the vibration. Mathematically:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$$

**Complementarity to IR:** A vibration is **IR active** if it produces a change in the **dipole moment** $\mu$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \mu}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$$

For **centrosymmetric molecules** (those possessing a center of inversion, e.g., CO₂, benzene, N₂), the **Mutual Exclusion Principle** applies: a vibration cannot be both Raman and IR active. For non-centrosymmetric molecules, both techniques yield complementary information — together they provide a complete vibrational fingerprint.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Classical & Quantum Theory of the Raman Effect

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — Derive the classical theory of the Raman Effect. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vibrational coordinate and incident field.** The normal-mode displacement and the laser electric field are written as:

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_{vib} t), \qquad E(t) = E_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_0 t)$$

**Step 2 — Expand polarizability in Taylor series about equilibrium:**

$$\alpha(Q) = \alpha_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q + \mathcal{O}(Q^2)$$

**Step 3 — Compute induced dipole $P = \alpha E$:**

$$P(t) = \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_0 t) + \left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 E_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_{vib} t)\cos(2 \pi \nu_0 t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the product-to-sum trigonometric identity** $2 \cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$P(t) = \alpha_0 E_0 \cos(2 \pi \nu_0 t) + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 Q_0 E_0 \Big[\cos 2\pi(\nu_0 - \nu_{vib})t + \cos 2\pi(\nu_0 + \nu_{vib})t\Big]$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the three frequency components.** The first term gives **Rayleigh scattering** at $\nu_0$. The bracketed terms give **Stokes** ($\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}$) and **Anti-Stokes** ($\nu_0 + \nu_{vib}$) lines, provided $\left(\partial\alpha/\partial Q\right)_0 \neq 0$.

**Step 6 — State the selection rule:**

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0}$$

#### Part (b) — With a neat energy level diagram, explain the quantum mechanical picture of Stokes and Anti-Stokes Raman scattering. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

In the quantum picture, the molecule is treated as a quantized vibrator with discrete energy levels $E_n = (n + \tfrac{1}{2}) h \nu_{vib}$. The incident photon $h\nu_0$ promotes the molecule to a short-lived **virtual state** (lifetime $\sim 10^{-14}$ s, **not** a stationary state).

- **Stokes process:** Molecule starts in $|n=0\rangle$, absorbs $h\nu_0$, reaches the virtual state, emits $h(\nu_0 - \nu_{vib})$, and ends in $|n=1\rangle$. **Energy conservation:** $h\nu_0 = h(\nu_0 - \nu_{vib}) + h\nu_{vib}$. The molecule gains one vibrational quantum.
- **Anti-Stokes process:** Molecule starts in $|n=1\rangle$, absorbs $h\nu_0$, reaches the virtual state, emits $h(\nu_0 + \nu_{vib})$, and ends in $|n=0\rangle$. The molecule loses one vibrational quantum.

**Population asymmetry:** Since $N_1/N_0 = \exp(-h\nu_{vib}/k_B T) \ll 1$ at room temperature, anti-Stokes lines are far weaker. (Refer to the energy level diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Instrumentation & Applications of Raman Spectroscopy

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — With a block diagram, describe the instrumentation of a Raman spectrometer. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A modern dispersive Raman spectrometer consists of the following components (refer to Section 4.1 for the block diagram):

| # | Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Laser source** (532 / 633 / 785 / 1064 nm) | Provides intense, monochromatic excitation. |
| 2 | **Band-pass filter** | Removes plasma lines and sideband fluorescence. |
| 3 | **Sample illumination system** | Microscope objective or free-space lens. |
| 4 | **Notch / edge filter (OD 6)** | Blocks the dominant Rayleigh line (critical step!). |
| 5 | **Entrance slit** | Defines spectral resolution. |
| 6 | **Diffraction grating** (typically 1800 lines/mm) | Disperses Raman-shifted light. |
| 7 | **CCD detector** (thermoelectrically cooled) | Records the entire spectrum simultaneously. |
| 8 | **Computer / software** | Generates the $I$ vs. $\Delta\tilde{\nu}$ plot. |

**Key Notes:**
- The **notch filter** is the single most important component — without it, the weak Raman signal would be drowned by the $10^6$–$10^8$ times stronger Rayleigh line.
- For **FT-Raman** (Fourier Transform), a 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser is used with an interferometer instead of a grating — this minimizes fluorescence from the sample.

#### Part (b) — Discuss the applications of Raman spectroscopy in nanomaterial characterization. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Raman spectroscopy is one of the most powerful techniques for nanomaterial characterization. The five most important applications are:

1. **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs) and Graphene Quality Assessment:** The ratio of the disorder-induced **D-band (~1350 cm⁻¹)** to the graphitic **G-band (~1580 cm⁻¹)**, $I_D/I_G$, is directly proportional to the defect density. Lower $I_D/I_G$ implies higher crystalline quality. Raman is also used to identify single-walled vs. multi-walled CNTs via the radial breathing mode (RBM) below 300 cm⁻¹.

2. **Size and Strain Analysis of Si Nanocrystals:** The position and asymmetric broadening of the Si Raman peak at ~520 cm⁻¹ reveal quantum confinement effects in nanoparticles smaller than ~10 nm.

3. **Phase Identification of 2D Materials (MoS₂, WS₂, hBN):** Raman fingerprints the number of layers and stacking order. E.g., the $E_{2g}^1$ and $A_{1g}$ modes of MoS₂ show a characteristic separation of ~19 cm⁻¹ (monolayer) vs. ~25 cm⁻¹ (bulk).

4. **Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) for Trace Detection:** Noble-metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag) produce localized surface plasmon resonances that enhance the Raman cross-section by factors of $10^6$–$10^{10}$, enabling single-molecule detection — applied in chemical sensing, biosensing, and security tags.

5. **In-situ Monitoring of Nanomaterial Synthesis:** Real-time Raman during CVD growth of graphene or nanowires enables precise control of layer number, defect density, and phase composition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Deductions):**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion:** Students often write Raman shift in nm instead of cm⁻¹. **Always** convert: $\tilde{\nu} = 1/\lambda_{\text{cm}}$.
> 2. **Confusing the $\nu^4$ factor:** The intensity ratio has TWO multiplicative factors — the $\nu^4$ prefactor AND the Boltzmann factor. Dropping either is a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Inverting the selection rule:** Some students write "Raman needs change in dipole moment" — that is the **IR** selection rule. The Raman rule is **change in polarizability**. Confusing these will cost full marks.
> 4. **Drawing incomplete energy diagrams:** A complete diagram must show: ground state, excited vibrational state, virtual state (clearly labeled as virtual), both incident and scattered photon arrows, and labels of Stokes vs. Anti-Stokes.
> 5. **Omitting the notch filter:** A block diagram without the Rayleigh-rejection filter is incomplete and will lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Use this for last-minute revision.**

- [x] **Raman Effect** = inelastic scattering of light; discovered 1928 (C. V. Raman, Nobel 1930).
- [x] **Raman shift** $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_0 - \tilde{\nu}_s = \tilde{\nu}_{vib}$ is **independent** of excitation wavelength.
- [x] **Stokes:** molecule gains vibrational quantum; **Anti-Stokes:** molecule loses vibrational quantum.
- [x] **Anti-Stokes is weaker** than Stokes due to lower Boltzmann population of $n \geq 1$ states.
- [x] **Raman selection rule:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$ (change in **polarizability**).
- [x] **IR selection rule:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial Q}\right)_0 \neq 0$ (change in **dipole moment**).
- [x] **Mutual exclusion principle** holds for centrosymmetric molecules only.
- [x] **Three frequencies** emerge from classical theory: $\nu_0$ (Rayleigh), $\nu_0 \pm \nu_{vib}$ (Raman).
- [x] **Population ratio** $N_1/N_0 = \exp(-h\nu_{vib}/k_B T)$; **intensity ratio** also carries a $\nu^4$ prefactor.
- [x] **Virtual state** lifetime $\sim 10^{-14}$ s — **not** a real stationary energy level.
- [x] **D-band (~1350 cm⁻¹)** = disorder; **G-band (~1580 cm⁻¹)** = graphitic; $I_D/I_G$ = quality metric.
- [x] **SERS** enhancement on Au/Ag nanoparticles = $10^6$–$10^{10}$× — single-molecule detection.
- [x] **Notch filter** is the most critical instrument component (rejects Rayleigh by OD 6).
- [x] **Unit of Raman shift:** cm⁻¹ (wavenumber). Always convert nm → cm before calculations.
- [x] **Common laser wavelengths:** 532 nm, 633 nm, 785 nm, 1064 nm (FT-Raman).
- [x] **Raman is complementary to IR:** together they give the full vibrational fingerprint of a material.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (NMR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (NMR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧲 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy** is a non-destructive, quantitative analytical technique that exploits the magnetic properties of certain atomic nuclei to investigate the molecular structure, dynamics, chemical environment, and composition of a sample.

The technique is based on the absorption of **radio-frequency (RF) radiation** by nuclei (commonly **¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, ³¹P**) placed in a strong, static external magnetic field, when the RF frequency matches the **Larmor precession frequency** of the nucleus.

$$ \text{NMR active nuclei possess: } I \neq 0 $$

where **I** is the **nuclear spin quantum number**.

### 1.2 Course Outcome Alignment (KTU 2024 Scheme)

| CO Mapping | Outcome Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CO3** | Apply spectroscopic techniques to characterize nanomaterials |
| **Bloom's Level** | Apply / Analyze |
| **Skill Tag** | Instrumental Analysis |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Top in a Magnetic Field

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a spinning top tilted slightly off vertical. It does not fall over; instead, it wobbles in a circular motion around the vertical axis. This wobble is called **precession**.

Now, replace the spinning top with an **NMR-active nucleus** (e.g., a proton, ¹H). It has an inherent angular momentum because it is spinning. When you place this spinning nucleus inside a strong external magnetic field (**B₀**), it does not align with the field but **precesses** around the field axis — just like the wobbling top.

If you now send in a **radio-frequency pulse** at exactly the same frequency as the precession, the nucleus **absorbs energy** and "flips" to a higher energy state. This is **resonance** — the core of NMR.

### 1.4 Standard Constants & Metrics (Bold High-Yield Values)

- **Gyromagnetic ratio (γ) of ¹H:** $ \mathbf{2.675 \times 10^8 \, \text{rad} \cdot \text{T}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}} $
- **Larmor frequency of ¹H at 7.05 T:** $ \mathbf{300 \, \text{MHz}} $
- **Standard reference compound:** **Tetramethylsilane (TMS)**, $ \delta = 0 \, \text{ppm} $
- **Typical magnetic field strength:** 1 T – 21 T (clinical to ultra-high field)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Principles

### **Step 1 — The Magnetic Moment of a Nucleus**
A spinning, charged nucleus behaves like a tiny bar magnet with a magnetic moment **μ**, proportional to its spin angular momentum **I**:

$$ \vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{I} $$

where **γ** is the **gyromagnetic ratio** — a nucleus-specific constant.

### **Step 2 — Zeeman Splitting in an External Field B₀**
When the nucleus is placed in an external static field $B_0 $ (conventionally along the z-axis), the degenerate spin states split into $2I + 1$ discrete energy levels. For ¹H ($I = 1/2$), this gives **two energy levels** separated by:

$$ \Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 = h \nu_0 $$

### **Step 3 — Resonance Condition**
Resonance occurs when the applied RF frequency **ν** exactly matches the **Larmor precession frequency** $ \nu_0$:

$$ \nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} $$

The population difference between the two states is given by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$ \frac{N_{\text{upper}}}{N_{\text{lower}}} = e^{-\Delta E / k_B T} \approx 1 - \frac{\gamma \hbar B_0}{k_B T} $$

### **Step 4 — Shielding and Chemical Shift**
The electrons surrounding a nucleus generate their own small magnetic field, **shielding** the nucleus from $B_0$. The effective field is:

$$ B_{\text{eff}} = B_0 (1 - \sigma) $$

where **σ** is the **shielding constant**. This shifts the resonance frequency, giving rise to the **chemical shift (δ)**:

$$ \delta \, (\text{ppm}) = \frac{\nu_{\text{sample}} - \nu_{\text{reference}}}{\nu_{\text{spectrometer}}} \times 10^6 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference standard:** Tetramethylsilane (TMS) for ¹H and ¹³C NMR. TMS is chosen because it is chemically inert, magnetically isotropic, and resonates upfield of most organic compounds.

### **Step 5 — Spin-Spin Coupling (J-Coupling)**
Neighbouring NMR-active nuclei split the resonance peak into **multiplets**. The number of peaks follows the **$n+1$ rule**, where **n** is the number of equivalent neighbouring nuclei. The spacing between multiplet lines is the **coupling constant J** (in Hz).

### **Step 6 — Relaxation Processes**
After excitation, the system returns to equilibrium via two principal mechanisms:

- **T₁ (Spin-Lattice / Longitudinal Relaxation):** Energy exchange with the surroundings (lattice).
- **T₂ (Spin-Spin / Transverse Relaxation):** Loss of phase coherence among spins.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Larmor (angular) frequency | $ \omega_0 = \gamma B_0 $ | rad·s⁻¹ | Precession rate |
| Larmor (linear) frequency | $ \nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi $ | Hz | Equal to RF applied |
| Energy gap | $ \Delta E = h \nu_0 = \gamma \hbar B_0 $ | J | Two-level system |
| Chemical shift | $ \delta = (\nu_s - \nu_{\text{ref}})/\nu_{\text{spec}} \times 10^6 $ | ppm | Independent of $B_0$ |
| Shielding constant | $ B_{\text{eff}} = B_0(1 - \sigma) $ | T | σ dimensionless |
| Multiplicity (¹H NMR) | $ \text{Peaks} = n + 1 $ | — | n = neighbouring H |
| Boltzmann population | $ N_\alpha / N_\beta = e^{-\Delta E / k_B T} $ | — | Sensitivity driver |
| Bloch relaxation | $ M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1}) $ | — | Longitudinal |
| Bloch relaxation | $ M_{x,y}(t) = M_{x,y}(0) \cdot e^{-t/T_2} $ | — | Transverse |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Structure elucidation of drug molecules and polymorph screening.
> - **Polymer Nanotechnology:** Determining tacticity, crystallinity, and chain dynamics in polymeric nanomaterials.
> - **Materials Science:** Quantifying surface functionalization of nanoparticles (e.g., PEG-coated quantum dots).
> - **Medical Imaging (MRI):** ¹H NMR forms the basis of Magnetic Resonance Imaging, mapping water density in tissues.
> - **Metabolomics:** Identifying small molecules in biological fluids using 2D NMR techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Resonance Condition (Larmor Equation)

We start from the classical equation of motion for a magnetic moment in an external field — the **torque equation**:

$$ \frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B} $$

Substituting $ \vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{I}$ and taking $ \vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$:

$$ \frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times B_0 \hat{z} $$

Writing the components:

$$ \frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0, \quad \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma \mu_x B_0, \quad \frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0 $$

The third equation shows that $ \mu_z$ is conserved. Differentiating the first and substituting the second:

$$ \frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma^2 B_0^2 \mu_x $$

This is a simple harmonic differential equation with angular frequency:

$$ \omega_0 = \gamma B_0 $$

The Larmor frequency in Hz:

$$ \nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} $$

This is the **resonance condition** — the nucleus precesses at $ \omega_0$, and a transverse RF field oscillating at this frequency will induce transitions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Chemical Shift Expression

The applied spectrometer frequency is $ \nu_{\text{spec}}$, locked to $B_0$ such that:

$$ \nu_{\text{spec}} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} $$

The resonance frequency of a shielded sample is:

$$ \nu_s = \frac{\gamma B_{\text{eff}}}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0 (1 - \sigma)}{2\pi} = \nu_{\text{spec}}(1 - \sigma) $$

The absolute frequency difference relative to a reference (TMS) is:

$$ \Delta \nu = \nu_s - \nu_{\text{ref}} = \nu_{\text{spec}}(\sigma_{\text{ref}} - \sigma_s) $$

Dividing by the spectrometer frequency and multiplying by $10^6$ to obtain a **field-independent** quantity:

$$ \delta = \frac{\nu_s - \nu_{\text{ref}}}{\nu_{\text{spec}}} \times 10^6 \quad \text{(ppm)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ppm?** Since $ \Delta \nu$ is on the order of a few hundred Hz and $ \nu_{\text{spec}}$ is in hundreds of MHz, the ratio is a very small number (≈ 10⁻⁶). Scaling to ppm yields convenient, machine-independent chemical shifts.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ¹H NMR Quick Reference for Functional Groups

| Functional Group | Approx. δ (ppm) | Multiplicity (typical) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Alkyl –CH₃ | 0.9 | Singlet / Triplet |
| Alkyl –CH₂– | 1.3 | Quartet / Multiplet |
| Aromatic –CH | 6.5 – 8.0 | Multiplet |
| Aldehyde –CHO | 9.5 – 10.0 | Singlet |
| Carboxylic –COOH | 10 – 13 | Broad singlet |
| Hydroxyl –OH | 1 – 5 (variable) | Broad singlet |
| Alkene =CH– | 5.0 – 6.5 | Multiplet |

---

## 3.4 Multiplicity Derivation — The (n + 1) Rule

Consider a nucleus **A** with $n$ equivalent neighbouring nuclei **X** ($I = 1/2$ for protons). The total spin of the n X-nuclei can take values from $m_I = -n/2$ to $+n/2$ in integer/half-integer steps. Each distinct $m_I$ value corresponds to one local field at A, producing one line.

Number of distinct $m_I$ values = $n + 1$.

Hence the multiplicity:

$$ \text{Number of peaks} = n + 1 $$

The relative intensities follow **Pascal's triangle** (1 : 1, 1 : 2 : 1, 1 : 3 : 3 : 1, ...).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧭 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an NMR Spectrometer

Below is a Mermaid block-level functional architecture showing the major subsystems of a Fourier Transform NMR (FT-NMR) spectrometer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sample Holder\nin Magnet Bore] --> B[Strong Static Magnet B0]
    B --> C[Probe\nRF Coils\nGradient Coils]
    C --> D[Pulsed RF Transmitter\nFrequency Synthesizer]
    D --> E[Pulse Programmer\nand Timing Unit]
    E --> F[Receiver\nAmplifier and\nPhase Sensitive Detector]
    F --> G[ADC\nAnalog to Digital Converter]
    G --> H[Computer Workstation\nFourier Transform\nPhase Correction]
    H --> I[Final NMR Spectrum\nin ppm vs Intensity]
    C --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the flow:** Sample → Magnet → RF Pulse → Free Induction Decay (FID) → Digital Signal → Fourier Transform → Spectrum.

---

## 4.2 Energy-Level / Resonance Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Nucleus with Spin I = 1/2] --> B[Placed in B0 Field]
    B --> C[Zeeman Splitting\nEnergy Gap Delta E]
    C --> D[RF Pulse at Larmor Frequency]
    D --> E[Absorption of Energy]
    E --> F[Spin Flip to Higher State]
    F --> G[Emission of Signal\nFree Induction Decay]
    G --> H[Detection and Fourier Transform]
    H --> I[NMR Spectrum Output]
```

---

## 4.3 Pulse Sequence Architecture (Simple 1-Pulse Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Spectrometer
    participant RF_Tx as RF Transmitter
    participant Sample
    participant RF_Rx as RF Receiver
    Spectrometer->>RF_Tx: 90 degree Pulse\nDuration t_p\nAmplitude omega_1
    RF_Tx->>Sample: Excite Spins\nMagnetization tips to XY plane
    Sample->>RF_Rx: Emit FID\nDecays with T2
    RF_Rx->>Spectrometer: Digitize FID
    Spectrometer->>Spectrometer: Apply Fourier Transform
    Spectrometer-->>Spectrometer: Store Spectrum
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of NMR Data Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Step 1\nSample Insertion\nShimming to Homogenize B0] --> P2[Step 2\nLock Channel\nStabilize Field Drift]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3\nApply RF Pulse\nTip Magnetization]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4\nAcquire Free Induction Decay]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5\nSignal Averaging\nNS Scans]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6\nFT and Phase Correction]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7\nSpectrum Plot\nPeaks Referenced to TMS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define chemical shift. Why is TMS used as the reference standard in ¹H NMR?**

**Model Answer:**

> Chemical shift (δ) is the relative displacement of the resonance frequency of a nucleus from a standard reference, expressed in parts per million (ppm).
> $$ \delta = \frac{\nu_{\text{sample}} - \nu_{\text{TMS}}}{\nu_{\text{spectrometer}}} \times 10^6 \, \text{ppm} $$
> **TMS is used because:** (1) It is chemically inert. (2) It has twelve equivalent protons giving a single sharp peak. (3) It is highly shielded and resonates upfield of nearly all organic compounds, so its δ is set to 0 ppm. (4) It is volatile, allowing easy recovery of the sample.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between T₁ and T₂ relaxation processes in NMR.**

**Model Answer:**

> **T₁ (Spin-Lattice / Longitudinal Relaxation):** The excited nuclei transfer energy to the surrounding molecular framework (lattice) and the net magnetization along B₀ recovers. It governs **how fast the z-magnetization regrows**.
> **T₂ (Spin-Spin / Transverse Relaxation):** Spins lose phase coherence among themselves, causing the transverse magnetization in the xy-plane to decay. It governs **FID decay** and **linewidth**.
> Generally, $T_1 \geq T_2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectroscopy. Derive the Larmor equation and discuss its significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the origin of chemical shift. List the factors affecting chemical shift with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle:**
NMR is based on the absorption of radio-frequency radiation by nuclei possessing a non-zero spin ($I \neq 0$) when placed in a strong external magnetic field $B_0$. The resonance condition is fulfilled when the applied RF frequency equals the Larmor precession frequency of the nucleus.

**Derivation of Larmor Equation:**
A nucleus with magnetic moment $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{I}$ in a field $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$ experiences a torque:

$$ \vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B} $$

This leads to the equation of motion:

$$ \frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times B_0 \hat{z} $$

Resolving into components:

$$ \frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma B_0 \mu_y, \quad \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma B_0 \mu_x, \quad \frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0 $$

Differentiating the first and substituting the second:

$$ \frac{d^2 \mu_x}{dt^2} + (\gamma B_0)^2 \mu_x = 0 $$

This is the equation of a harmonic oscillator with:

$$ \omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{(Larmor angular frequency)} $$

Converting to Hz:

$$ \nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} $$

**Significance:**
- The Larmor equation is the **fundamental resonance condition** in NMR.
- It enables frequency mapping in MRI.
- It governs the design of spectrometer frequency channels.
- It is essential in computing **γ**-dependent population differences and **Boltzmann sensitivity**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Origin of Chemical Shift:**
The local electronic environment of a nucleus generates a small induced magnetic field opposing the applied field, **shielding** the nucleus. The effective field is $B_{\text{eff}} = B_0(1 - \sigma)$. Variations in σ across different chemical environments shift the resonance frequency, giving rise to the **chemical shift**.

$$ \delta = \frac{\nu_s - \nu_{\text{TMS}}}{\nu_{\text{spectrometer}}} \times 10^6 \, \text{ppm} $$

**Factors Affecting Chemical Shift:**

| Factor | Effect | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inductive effect (–I)** | Deshielding, δ increases | –CH₂–Cl (~3.5 ppm) |
| **Resonance effect (+M)** | Delocalisation shifts δ | Vinyl protons (~5–6.5 ppm) |
| **Hydrogen bonding** | Strong deshielding | Carboxylic –COOH (10–13 ppm) |
| **Hybridisation** | sp > sp² > sp³ deshielding | Alkynes (~2.5 ppm), alkenes (~5.5 ppm) |
| **Aromatic ring current** | Strong deshielding at periphery | Benzene protons (~7.3 ppm) |
| **Electronegativity of substituent** | Greater δ downfield | –CH₂–F (~4.3 ppm) |

---

### 📌 Question B — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supple Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the components and working of a Fourier Transform NMR (FT-NMR) spectrometer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss spin–spin coupling and the (n+1) rule. How is the coupling constant J used to deduce molecular structure? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram and Working of FT-NMR Spectrometer:**

The major subsystems are:

1. **Magnet:** A superconducting magnet (typically 7 – 21 T) provides the static $B_0$ field along the z-axis.
2. **Probe:** Houses the sample and contains RF coils for transmitting the excitation pulse and receiving the FID.
3. **Pulsed RF Transmitter:** Generates short (microsecond) RF pulses at the Larmor frequency.
4. **Pulse Programmer:** Controls the timing, phase, and amplitude of RF pulses.
5. **Receiver and Amplifier:** Detects the weak FID signal (microvolts) and amplifies it.
6. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Digitizes the FID at high sampling rates.
7. **Computer / Workstation:** Performs Fourier Transform, phase correction, baseline correction, and spectrum plotting.

**Working Principle:**
A short, intense RF pulse at the Larmor frequency simultaneously excites all nuclei in the sample. The excited spins precess freely, inducing a decaying oscillating voltage in the receiver coil — the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**. The FID is digitized and subjected to a **Fourier Transform**, converting the time-domain signal into a frequency-domain spectrum.

The **signal-to-noise ratio (S/N)** is enhanced by repeating the pulse sequence and averaging N scans, since S/N ∝ √N.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FT?** FT-NMR enables the simultaneous acquisition of all frequencies (Fellgett advantage), reduces scan time, and provides high throughput — superior to the older continuous-wave (CW) method.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Spin–Spin Coupling (J-Coupling):**
J-coupling is the indirect interaction between two NMR-active nuclei mediated through the **bonding electrons** (not through space). It results in the splitting of a resonance peak into **multiplets**, providing critical information on molecular connectivity.

**The (n+1) Rule:**
A nucleus coupled to *n* equivalent neighbouring nuclei of spin $I = 1/2$ will be split into $n+1$ peaks. For example:

| Neighbours (n) | Multiplicity | Pattern (Pascal) |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | Singlet | 1 |
| 1 | Doublet | 1 : 1 |
| 2 | Triplet | 1 : 2 : 1 |
| 3 | Quartet | 1 : 3 : 3 : 1 |

**Use of Coupling Constant (J) in Structure Elucidation:**
- **Magnitude of J** is a fingerprint of the bond framework (e.g., vicinal $^3J \approx 6$–8 Hz; aromatic $^3J \approx 7$ Hz; geminal $^2J \approx 0$–3 Hz).
- **J is independent of $B_0$**, unlike chemical shift in Hz.
- Comparing experimental J values with reference values helps **assign diastereotopic protons**, identify **cis/trans isomers**, and confirm **ring size** in cyclic systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> - ❌ **Confusing δ in Hz and δ in ppm:** δ is **dimensionless** (ppm). Hz is an absolute frequency difference.
> - ❌ **Forgetting the ×10⁶ scaling** in the chemical shift formula — this is a 1-mark loss.
> - ❌ **Mixing T₁ and T₂:** T₁ affects z-magnetization recovery (longitudinal), T₂ affects transverse decay (FID).
> - ❌ **Writing the Larmor equation without derivation steps:** Just writing $ \nu_0 = \gamma B_0/2\pi$ without deriving from the torque equation costs full marks for that sub-part.
> - ❌ **Omitting units** in numerical substitutions (e.g., MHz vs Hz, T vs gauss).
