2. **Attenuator** — Neutral density filter to control intensity and avoid detector saturation.
3. **Temperature-controlled sample holder** — Peltier unit maintaining $\pm 0.1$ °C.
4. **Cuvette** — typically square quartz with $\sim 1$ cm path length.
5. **Detector** — Avalanche Photodiode (APD) or Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) operating in single-photon counting mode.
6. **Digital correlator** — computes $g^{(2)}(\tau)$ in real time over channel delays $\tau$.
7. **Computer** — applies cumulant fit / CONTIN / NNLS to extract size distribution.

**Autocorrelation Function Generation & Analysis (2 marks):**
The correlator multiplies $I(t)$ by $I(t+\tau)$ and averages over time to construct $g^{(2)}(\tau)$. The decay is fit to $g^{(2)}(\tau) = 1 + \beta \exp(-2\Gamma\tau + \mu_2 \tau^2)$ yielding $\Gamma = q^2 D$. Then $D$ is converted to $d_H$ via the Stokes–Einstein equation.

**(b)** [7 marks] A silver nanoparticle suspension is analyzed at 298 K in water. The viscosity is 0.89 cP and refractive index 1.33. The laser wavelength is 633 nm, scattering angle 90°. The measured diffusion coefficient is $D = 4.5 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s. Calculate (i) the scattering vector, (ii) the decay rate, and (iii) the hydrodynamic diameter.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Scattering vector (2 marks):**

$$q = \frac{4 \pi n}{\lambda_0} \sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{4 \pi (1.33)}{633 \times 10^{-9}} \sin(45°)$$

$$q = 2.640 \times 10^{7} \times 0.7071 = 1.867 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Decay rate (2 marks):**

$$\Gamma = q^2 D = (1.867 \times 10^{7})^2 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$\Gamma = 3.486 \times 10^{14} \times 4.5 \times 10^{-11} = 1.569 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Hydrodynamic diameter (3 marks):**

$$d_H = \frac{k_B T}{3 \pi \eta D} = \frac{1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \times 298}{3 \pi (0.89 \times 10^{-3})(4.5 \times 10^{-11})}$$

Numerator: $k_B T = 4.1142 \times 10^{-21}$ J

Denominator: $3 \pi (0.89 \times 10^{-3})(4.5 \times 10^{-11}) = 3.776 \times 10^{-13}$

$$d_H = \frac{4.1142 \times 10^{-21}}{3.776 \times 10^{-13}} = 1.090 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_H \approx 10.9 \text{ nm}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the **Stokes–Einstein equation** from first principles. Show how it is used in DLS to obtain the hydrodynamic diameter of nanoparticles.

**Model Solution:**

**Setup (2 marks):** Consider a spherical particle of radius $r$ in a fluid of viscosity $\eta$ at temperature $T$. Under an applied osmotic force due to a concentration gradient $\nabla c$, the particle experiences two opposing effects:

1. **Fickian diffusion flux:** $J_{\text{diff}} = -D \nabla c$
2. **Drift flux** under Stokes' drag: $J_{\text{drift}} = c \cdot v$

where $v$ is the terminal drift velocity under Stokes' drag $F_{\text{drag}} = 6\pi \eta r \, v$.

**Force balance and drift velocity (2 marks):**
The osmotic driving force is $F = -\nabla \mu \cdot \text{(per particle)}$, which for an ideal solution reduces to $F = -k_B T \nabla c / c$. Equating $F = 6\pi\eta r v$:

$$v = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r \, c}(-\nabla c)$$

**Equilibrium condition (2 marks):**
At equilibrium, total flux $J_{\text{total}} = 0$:

$$-D \nabla c + c v = -D \nabla c - \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r} \nabla c = 0$$

Solving for $D$:

$$\boxed{D = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r} = \frac{k_B T}{3 \pi \eta d_H}}$$

**Application in DLS (1 mark):**
Rearranging:

$$d_H = \frac{k_B T}{3 \pi \eta D}$$

The experimentally measured diffusion coefficient $D$ (extracted from the decay rate $\Gamma = q^2 D$) is plugged into this equation to obtain the hydrodynamic diameter.

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the **Siegert relation** and explain how **polydispersity** is quantified using the **cumulants method**. What is the acceptable range of Polydispersity Index (PDI) for reliable DLS measurements?

**Model Solution:**

**Siegert Relation (3 marks):** The Siegert relation connects the **intensity autocorrelation** $g^{(2)}(\tau)$ to the **field autocorrelation** $g^{(1)}(\tau)$:

$$g^{(2)}(\tau) = 1 + \beta \left[ g^{(1)}(\tau) \right]^2$$

This holds for **stationary, Gaussian random processes** — a valid assumption for light scattering from many independent particles (central limit theorem). The coefficient $\beta$ is the **spatial coherence factor** ($0 < \beta \leq 1$), determined by the detection geometry. For monodisperse particles:

$$g^{(1)}(\tau) = \exp(-\Gamma \tau) = \exp(-q^2 D \tau)$$

**Cumulants Method for Polydispersity (2 marks):** For polydisperse samples, $g^{(1)}(\tau)$ is a superposition of exponentials. Taking the logarithm and Taylor-expanding:

$$\ln g^{(1)}(\tau) = -\Gamma \tau + \frac{\mu_2}{2!}\tau^2 - \frac{\mu_3}{3!}\tau^3 + \dots$$

The first cumulant gives the **z-average** decay rate $\bar{\Gamma} = \sum N_i M_i^2 \Gamma_i / \sum N_i M_i^2$, leading to the **z-average hydrodynamic diameter**.

**PDI Definition and Ranges (2 marks):**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\mu_2}{\bar{\Gamma}^2}$$

| PDI Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $< 0.1$ | Highly monodisperse (ideal) |
| $0.1 - 0.3$ | Acceptable for most analyses |
| $0.3 - 0.5$ | Broad; use NNLS/CONTIN instead |
| $> 0.5$ | DLS not reliable; report only approximate mean |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units of $\eta$** — Always convert **cP to Pa·s** (multiply by $10^{-3}$). Students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Mixing up $d_H$ vs $r_H$** — Stokes–Einstein is most often written with $6\pi \eta r_H$. Ensure $d_H = 2 r_H$.
> 3. **Confusing $q$ formula versions** — Some texts use $\frac{4\pi n}{\lambda_0}\sin(\theta/2)$, others use $\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\sin(\theta/2)$ where $\lambda$ is the wavelength *in the medium*. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Siegert relation sign error** — It is $g^{(2)}(\tau) = 1 + \beta [g^{(1)}(\tau)]^2$, **not** $1 - \beta$.
> 5. **Reporting TEM size as DLS size** — DLS gives **hydrodynamic** size, never geometric size. Always state "hydrodynamic diameter" in conclusions.
> 6. **PDI > 0.7 with single exponential fit** — Cannot use cumants; must use CONTIN/NNLS distribution analysis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dynamic Light Scattering**

- ☐ DLS = **Photon Correlation Spectroscopy** = **Quasi-Elastic Light Scattering** — all the same technique.
- ☐ Measures **Brownian motion** of particles via fluctuations in **scattered laser intensity**.
- ☐ The **autocorrelation function** $g^{(2)}(\tau)$ is the central observable; its decay rate gives the diffusion coefficient.
- ☐ **Siegert relation:** $g^{(2)}(\tau) = 1 + \beta [g^{(1)}(\tau)]^2$.
- ☐ For monodisperse spheres: $g^{(1)}(\tau) = e^{-\Gamma \tau}$ where $\Gamma = q^2 D$.
- ☐ **Stokes–Einstein equation:** $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{3 \pi \eta d_H}$ — the master equation of DLS.
- ☐ Hydrodynamic diameter **includes solvation/electrical double layer** — always larger than geometric diameter.
- ☐ Scattering vector $q = \dfrac{4\pi n}{\lambda_0} \sin(\theta/2)$; larger $\theta$ → better for smaller particles.
- ☐ **Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** — memorize for numericals.
- ☐ **PDI = $\mu_2/\Gamma^2$**; acceptable range $\leq 0.3$; $>0.5$ unreliable.
- ☐ DLS gives **intensity-weighted z-average size** (biased toward larger particles because scattering scales as $d^6$ for Rayleigh scatterers).
- ☐ Sample must be **clean, dust-free, properly diluted** — dust spikes ruin measurements.
- ☐ Temperature must be **stable** (viscosity $\eta$ is highly T-dependent).
- ☐ Typical size range: **1 nm – 10 µm**; below 1 nm → use DOSY-NMR; above 10 µm → use laser diffraction.
- ☐ Zeta potential accessory enables **electrophoretic mobility → surface charge** determination.
- ☐ DLS is **non-destructive**, **fast**, **low-sample-volume**, **in-situ** — ideal for biological and pharmaceutical applications.
- ☐ Three common size distributions: **by intensity, by volume, by number** — always specify which one you report.

---

**End of Module 3 — Dynamic Light Scattering Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)** is a branch of thermal analysis in which the **mass of a sample is measured as a function of temperature or time** while the sample is subjected to a controlled temperature programme (heating, cooling, or isothermal hold) under a specified atmosphere (inert, oxidising, or reducing).

The technique quantitatively detects mass changes arising from physical phenomena (e.g., desorption, vaporisation) and chemical phenomena (e.g., decomposition, oxidation, reduction). The output is a **thermogravimetric curve (TG curve)** plotting mass (%) versus temperature (°C) or time (min).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TGA is a **primary characterisation tool** for nanomaterials. It is used to determine thermal stability, decomposition temperature, composition of nanocomposites, ash content, and moisture/volatile content. It is classified as a **destructive analytical technique** because the sample is consumed during analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine placing a piece of wet sponge on a kitchen weighing scale that is slowly being heated. As the water inside the sponge evaporates, the weight shown on the scale keeps dropping. If you record this weight at every temperature, you get a curve. **TGA is exactly this process, but with extreme scientific precision.**

- The **sponge** = your nanomaterial (polymer, oxide, hybrid, etc.)
- The **kitchen scale** = a precision **thermobalance** (sensitivity: **±0.1 µg to ±1 µg**)
- The **heat** = a programmable furnace with controlled ramp rate
- The **weight drop** = mass loss due to decomposition, dehydration, oxidation

By analysing *how much* mass is lost and *at what temperature* the loss occurs, a materials scientist can identify **what is leaving the material** and **how thermally stable** the material is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of TGA Information:**
> 1. **Magnitude of mass change** → quantitative composition
> 2. **Temperature of mass change** → identification of thermal event
> 3. **Rate of mass change** → kinetics and mechanism of decomposition

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial mass | $m_0$ | mg or µg | Reference mass before heating |
| Residual mass | $m_r$ | mg or µg | Mass left after analysis |
| Mass loss | $\Delta m$ | mg or % | $m_0 - m_r$ |
| Decomposition temperature | $T_d$ | °C or K | Onset of major weight loss |
| Heating rate | $\beta$ | °C/min | Programming rate of furnace |
| Atmosphere | – | – | $N_2$, $O_2$, $Ar$, air |

### 1.4 Classification of TGA

TGA experiments are conducted in two primary modes:

1. **Dynamic (Non-isothermal) TGA:** The sample is heated at a constant, controlled rate (e.g., 5, 10, 20 °C/min). Most common mode.
2. **Static (Isothermal) TGA:** The sample is held at a constant temperature and mass change is monitored as a function of time. Useful for studying long-term thermal stability and kinetic parameters.

### 1.5 Derivative Thermogravimetry (DTG)

To enhance the resolution of overlapping weight losses, the **first derivative** of the TG curve with respect to temperature (or time) is computed:

$$\text{DTG} = \frac{dm}{dT} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{dm}{dt}$$

DTG peaks pinpoint **inflection points** of the TG curve, making subtle transitions clearly visible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle – Step-by-Step

The TGA experiment proceeds in a logical, sequential flow:

- **Step 1 – Sample Loading:** A small quantity of sample (typically **1–20 mg** for nanomaterials) is placed in a crucible (platinum, alumina, or ceramic) suspended from a precision microbalance.
- **Step 2 – Atmosphere Control:** The reaction chamber is purged with the chosen gas (N₂ for inert, O₂/air for oxidative decomposition) at a controlled flow rate.
- **Step 3 – Temperature Programming:** The furnace is programmed to follow a defined thermal profile (linear heating, isothermal, or multi-step).
- **Step 4 – Continuous Mass Recording:** The microbalance continuously logs the sample mass with **high precision** as temperature increases.
- **Step 5 – Data Analysis:** The recorded mass–temperature data is plotted and analysed for thermal events, transitions, and residual content.

### 2.2 Instrumentation – The Four Pillars

A modern TGA instrument consists of:

1. **Thermobalance (Microbalance):** The heart of the system. Modern instruments use **null-type or top-loading electronic balances** with a sensitivity of **0.1 µg**.
2. **Furnace:** A resistance-heated furnace capable of operating from ambient to **1500 °C** (some models reach **2400 °C**). Heating is uniform and programmable.
3. **Temperature Controller / Programmer:** Provides linear and reproducible heating rates. Standard rates: **1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50 °C/min**.
4. **Recording & Data Acquisition System:** Plots the thermogram in real time and computes DTG curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Components:** Sample purge gas (N₂, O₂, Ar), balance purge gas (to protect balance from corrosive vapours), and **temperature calibration standards** (e.g., Curie point of nickel, iron, alumel).

### 2.3 Why and How of the Technique

**Why TGA Works:**
- Every material has a unique thermal "fingerprint" based on its chemical bonds, crystal structure, and composition.
- When heated, molecules gain vibrational energy → at critical temperatures, bonds break → volatile fragments escape → measurable mass loss.
- The temperature at which mass loss occurs correlates with **bond strength** and **thermal stability**.

**How it Provides Information:**

- **Thermal Stability Range:** Region before any significant mass loss (<1%).
- **Decomposition Steps:** Distinct steps in the TG curve indicate sequential breakdown of different components.
- **Compositional Analysis:** % mass of each component from step heights.
- **Ash/Inorganic Content:** Final residue at high temperature.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass loss | $\Delta m = m_0 - m_r$ | Absolute mass lost during event | mg |
| Percentage mass loss | $\% \text{ loss} = \dfrac{m_0 - m_r}{m_0} \times 100$ | Relative mass change | % |
| Residual content | $\% \text{ residue} = \dfrac{m_r}{m_0} \times 100$ | Inorganic/ash content | % |
| Heating rate (dynamic) | $\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Linear heating rate | °C/min |
| DTG signal | $\text{DTG} = \dfrac{dm}{dT} = \dfrac{1}{\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dm}{dt}$ | First derivative of TG curve | %/°C |
| Kinetic expression (Arrhenius) | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(1-\alpha)^n = A e^{-E_a/RT}(1-\alpha)^n$ | Solid-state decomposition kinetics | s⁻¹ |
| Fractional conversion | $\alpha = \dfrac{m_0 - m_T}{m_0 - m_\infty}$ | Extent of reaction at temperature T | dimensionless |
| Kissinger equation | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_p^{\,2}}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R T_p} + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{E_a}\right)$ | Activation energy from peak T at varied β | kJ/mol |
| Coats–Redfern | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{-\ln(1-\alpha)}{T^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | Integral kinetic method | kJ/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - Standard pressure: $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Polymer Nanocomposites:** Determines weight fraction of nanofiller (clay, CNT, graphene) by measuring char/ash residue.
- **Catalysis Research:** Quantifies metal loading on catalyst supports via residue analysis.
- **Pharmaceutical Nanocarriers:** Confirms drug loading efficiency and thermal stability of nanomedicines.
- **Battery Materials:** Studies thermal runaway behaviour of electrodes and SEI layer decomposition in Li-ion batteries.
- **Aerospace Materials:** Certifies thermal stability of ablative nanomaterials used in heat shields.
- **Environmental Nanomaterials:** Evaluates regeneration cycles of nano-adsorbents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion from Mass Loss to Percentage

**Given:** A TGA experiment records initial mass $m_0 = 10.00 \text{ mg}$ and at 450 °C, the mass drops to $m_T = 6.40 \text{ mg}$.

**Step 1:** Write the expression for percentage mass loss.

$$
\% \text{ loss} = \frac{m_0 - m_T}{m_0} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\% \text{ loss} = \frac{10.00 - 6.40}{10.00} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$
\% \text{ loss} = \frac{3.60}{10.00} \times 100
$$

**Step 4:** Final calculation.

$$
\% \text{ loss} = 0.360 \times 100 = 36.00\%
$$

**Interpretation:** 36% of the initial mass was lost up to 450 °C, consistent with decomposition of the organic moiety of a hybrid nanomaterial.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Determination of Activation Energy via Kissinger Method

**Background:** The Kissinger method uses DTG peak temperatures ($T_p$) obtained at several heating rates ($\beta$) to calculate the activation energy $E_a$ of thermal decomposition.

**Step 1:** Start with the Kissinger equation.

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^{\,2}}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R T_p} + \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_a}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Recognise that this is in the form of a straight line $y = mx + c$, where:

$$
y = \ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^{\,2}}\right) \quad ; \quad x = \frac{1}{T_p} \quad ; \quad m = -\frac{E_a}{R} \quad ; \quad c = \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_a}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Plotting $y$ versus $x$ yields a straight line whose slope gives $E_a$.

$$
E_a = -R \cdot \text{slope}
$$

**Step 4:** Worked example with hypothetical data:

| $\beta$ (°C/min) | $T_p$ (K) | $\beta / T_p^2$ | $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ | $1/T_p$ (K⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5  | 620 | $1.30 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-11.25$ | $1.613 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 10 | 640 | $2.44 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-10.62$ | $1.563 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 20 | 662 | $4.56 \times 10^{-5}$ | $-10.00$ | $1.511 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Step 5:** Slope of the line (from least-squares fit): $m = -14850 \text{ K}$.

**Step 6:** Calculate $E_a$ using $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$.

$$
E_a = -R \cdot m = -(8.314) \times (-14850)
$$

**Step 7:** Compute final value.

$$
E_a = 123,463 \text{ J/mol} = 123.5 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Fractional Conversion ($\alpha$)

**Definition:** $\alpha$ represents the fraction of material that has decomposed at a given temperature.

**Step 1:** State the definition.

$$
\alpha = \frac{m_0 - m_T}{m_0 - m_\infty}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute for a sample where:

- $m_0 = 15.0 \text{ mg}$ (initial)
- $m_T = 11.5 \text{ mg}$ (at 400 °C)
- $m_\infty = 5.0 \text{ mg}$ (final residue)

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$
\alpha = \frac{15.0 - 11.5}{15.0 - 5.0} = \frac{3.5}{10.0} = 0.35
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

$$
\alpha = 0.35 = 35\% \text{ of the decomposable fraction has reacted at } 400\,°C
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Thermal Events in Nanomaterials

| Temperature Range (°C) | Typical Event | Mass Change | Material Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 150 | Loss of moisture / physisorbed water | $-2$ to $-10\%$ | Hydrated oxides, clays |
| 150 – 300 | Loss of crystalline water, residual solvent | $-5$ to $-15\%$ | Hydroxides, MOFs |
| 200 – 400 | Decomposition of organic ligands/surfactants | $-10$ to $-50\%$ | Capped nanoparticles |
| 300 – 600 | Pyrolysis of polymer matrix, carbonisation | $-20$ to $-80\%$ | Polymer nanocomposites |
| 400 – 800 | Combustion of carbon (in air) | Major loss | Carbon nanotubes, graphene |
| > 700 | Phase transformation (no mass change) | 0% | Quartz transition at 573 °C |
| > 800 | Final residue / metal oxide | Constant residue | Metal oxide nanoparticles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: TGA Instrument Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample in Crucible] --> B[Microbalance / Thermobalance]
    B --> C[Mass Signal Transducer]
    C --> D[Signal Amplifier and ADC]
    D --> E[Data Acquisition System]
    E --> F[TG Curve Plotter]
    E --> G[DTG Calculator]
    G --> H[DTG Curve Display]
    F --> I[Computer Analysis Software]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Thermal Event Identification]
    J --> K[Mass Loss Percentage]
    J --> L[Decomposition Temperature]
    J --> M[Residual Content]
    N[Furnace with Temperature Programmer] --> A
    O[Gas Inlet N2 or O2] --> A
    P[Balance Purge Gas] --> B
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: How a TGA Experiment is Performed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Weigh Sample Accurately 1 to 20 mg] --> S2[Step 2: Load into Crucible on Balance]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Seal Chamber and Purge with Gas]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Set Heating Program in Software]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Start Run and Monitor Mass vs Temperature]
    S5 --> S6{Step 6: Detect Thermal Events}
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Compute Mass Loss and DTG]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Interpret Curve and Report Results]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: TGA + DTG Signal Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input
        I1[Sample m0 in crucible]
        I2[Heating programme beta]
    end
    subgraph Sensor_Stage
        P1[Microbalance: continuous mT reading]
        P2[Thermocouple: continuous T reading]
    end
    subgraph Processing_Stage
        Q1[Normalisation: percent m vs T]
        Q2[Differentiation: dm/dT]
        Q3[Peak detection in DTG]
    end
    subgraph Output_Stage
        R1[TG thermogram]
        R2[DTG curve]
        R3[Quantitative mass loss table]
        R4[Kinetic parameters Ea and A]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    P1 --> Q1
    P1 --> Q2
    P2 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q2
    Q1 --> R1
    Q2 --> R2
    R1 --> R3
    R2 --> R3
    Q3 --> R4
```

### 4.4 Typical TGA Curve (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Region A: 25 to 150 C, Moisture Loss, 2 to 5 percent drop] --> B[Region B: 150 to 400 C, Surfactant Decomposition, 15 to 25 percent drop]
    B --> C[Region C: 400 to 600 C, Bulk Material Decomposition, 30 to 50 percent drop]
    C --> D[Region D: above 600 C, Stable Residue Plateau, constant mass]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above flowcharts illustrate the **functional architecture** of a TGA instrument, sample workflow, signal processing pipeline, and typical multi-step thermogram regions. In an actual instrument, a continuous mass-vs-temperature curve is plotted on the computer; here we represent each plateau and step as a labelled block for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA). Mention any two applications of TGA in nanomaterial characterisation.**

**Model Answer:**
TGA is a thermal analysis technique in which the **mass of a sample is continuously measured** as a function of temperature (or time) under a controlled atmosphere as the sample is heated at a programmed rate. The output is a thermogram showing mass change versus temperature.

**Two applications in nanomaterial characterisation:**
1. **Thermal stability assessment** of nanoparticles, nanofibers, and polymer nanocomposites.
2. **Compositional analysis** — determining filler content (e.g., CNT, graphene, clay) in nanocomposites by measuring ash/residue at high temperature.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the difference between a TG curve and a DTG curve? Why is DTG preferred for resolving overlapping thermal events?**

**Model Answer:**
- **TG Curve:** A plot of **mass (or % mass)** of the sample on the y-axis versus **temperature (or time)** on the x-axis. It directly shows the total mass change.
- **DTG Curve:** A plot of the **first derivative of mass with respect to temperature** ($dm/dT$) versus temperature. It is the rate of mass loss.

DTG is preferred because **overlapping weight losses** in the TG curve appear as a single broad step, but in the DTG curve they resolve into **distinct peaks** corresponding to each event, allowing precise identification of decomposition temperatures.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the instrumentation of a thermogravimetric analyser. Explain the functions of the microbalance, furnace, and atmosphere control unit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A TGA run on a polymer nanocomposite gives the following data:
- Initial mass = 12.00 mg
- Mass at 350 °C = 9.60 mg
- Mass at 550 °C = 4.80 mg
- Final residue at 800 °C (in N₂) = 2.40 mg

Calculate: (i) % mass loss up to 350 °C, (ii) % mass loss between 350–550 °C, (iii) % residue at 800 °C. Identify what each step represents.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Instrumentation of TGA:**

The major components of a TGA instrument are:

1. **Microbalance (Thermobalance):** A highly sensitive electronic balance (sensitivity **0.1 µg**) that continuously measures the mass of the sample. It may be a **null-balance** type (where an electromagnetic force restores balance) or **top-loading electronic** type. The balance must be protected from heat and corrosive gases by a water-cooled shield and balance purge gas.

2. **Furnace:** A resistance-heated furnace surrounds the sample. It is designed to provide **uniform heating** and is programmable from **room temperature up to 1500 °C** (or higher in specialised models). The heating coils are wound non-inductively to prevent electromagnetic interference with the balance.

3. **Temperature Controller / Programmer:** A microprocessor-based unit that controls the heating rate ($\beta$) accurately. Standard rates are 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50 °C/min. It also controls isothermal holds and cooling cycles.

4. **Atmosphere Control Unit:** A gas flow system (mass flow controllers) that maintains a defined atmosphere — typically **N₂** (inert), **O₂ / air** (oxidative), or **Ar / He**. The flow rate (typically 20–100 mL/min) is critical as it sweeps away evolved gases and prevents re-condensation.

5. **Recording System:** A computer with software that plots TG curves, computes DTG, and stores data for analysis.

> The block diagram follows the sequence: **Sample → Balance → Transducer → Amplifier → Recorder**, with the **Furnace and Gas System** acting as the controlled environment.

**(b) Calculations:**

**(i) % mass loss up to 350 °C:**

$$
\% \text{ loss} = \frac{12.00 - 9.60}{12.00} \times 100 = \frac{2.40}{12.00} \times 100 = 20.00\%
$$

This represents loss of **moisture, physisorbed water, and low-molecular-weight volatiles** from the polymer matrix.

**(ii) % mass loss between 350 °C and 550 °C:**

The mass at 550 °C is 4.80 mg, so the loss in this range is $9.60 - 4.80 = 4.80 \text{ mg}$.

$$
\% \text{ loss} = \frac{4.80}{12.00} \times 100 = 40.00\%
$$

This represents **main-chain scission and decomposition of the polymer backbone**, including degradation of the polymer coating on the nanofiller.

**(iii) % residue at 800 °C (in N₂):**

$$
\% \text{ residue} = \frac{2.40}{12.00} \times 100 = 20.00\%
$$

This is the **char yield** consisting of carbonaceous material and the inorganic nanofiller (e.g., clay, SiO₂, metal oxide nanoparticles). In an inert atmosphere, the polymer does not combust fully, leaving behind thermally stable residue.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of TGA. Discuss the various factors that affect a TGA curve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 10.00 mg sample of calcium oxalate monohydrate (CaC₂O₄·H₂O) is heated in a TGA. Write the three decomposition reactions and calculate the expected mass loss percentages at each step.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle of TGA:**

The principle of TGA is based on the **continuous measurement of sample mass** while it is subjected to a controlled temperature programme. As the temperature increases, the sample undergoes thermal events such as dehydration, decomposition, oxidation, or reduction, resulting in **mass changes**. These mass changes are recorded as a function of temperature or time to produce a thermogram.

**Factors affecting a TGA curve:**

1. **Heating rate ($\beta$):** Faster heating shifts decomposition to higher temperatures and may merge close steps.
2. **Atmosphere:** Oxidative (O₂/air) vs. inert (N₂/Ar) changes the decomposition pathway drastically. Carbon combusts in O₂ but forms char in N₂.
3. **Sample mass:** Larger samples give poor resolution and temperature gradients inside the sample.
4. **Particle size / packing:** Fine particles decompose faster; dense packing hinders gas diffusion.
5. **Crucible material and shape:** Platinum, alumina, or quartz — must be inert to sample and products.
6. **Gas flow rate:** Affects removal of volatile products and prevents re-condensation.
7. **Buoyancy effect:** Gas density changes with temperature cause apparent mass gain; corrected by running a blank.
8. **Instrument calibration:** Crucial for accurate temperature and mass readings.

**(b) Three-Step Decomposition of CaC₂O₄·H₂O:**

**Molar masses:**
- CaC₂O₄·H₂O = 146.12 g/mol
- CaC₂O₄ (anhydrous) = 128.10 g/mol
- CaCO₃ = 100.09 g/mol
- CaO = 56.08 g/mol

**Step 1: Dehydration (100–250 °C)**

$$
\text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \uparrow
$$

Mass loss = 18.02 g/mol (water)
$$
\% \text{ loss}_1 = \frac{18.02}{146.12} \times 100 = 12.33\%
$$

**Step 2: Decarbonylation (350–500 °C)**

$$
\text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{CO} \uparrow
$$

Mass loss = 28.01 g/mol (CO)
$$
\% \text{ loss}_2 = \frac{28.01}{146.12} \times 100 = 19.17\%
$$

**Step 3: Decarboxylation (650–850 °C)**

$$
\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow
$$

Mass loss = 44.01 g/mol (CO₂)
$$
\% \text{ loss}_3 = \frac{44.01}{146.12} \times 100 = 30.12\%
$$

**Verification:** Total mass loss = $12.33 + 19.17 + 30.12 = 61.62\%$; final residue CaO = $38.38\%$ ✓ (sum = 100%).

For a 10.00 mg sample:
- Step 1: $1.233 \text{ mg}$ (residual 8.767 mg)
- Step 2: $1.917 \text{ mg}$ (residual 6.850 mg)
- Step 3: $3.012 \text{ mg}$ (residual 3.838 mg)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse molar masses.** Students often use CaC₂O₄·H₂O = 144 g/mol (incorrect) instead of the correct 146.12 g/mol. This leads to errors of 1.4% per calculation.
> 2. **Three steps, three percentages — not two.** A common mistake is to club the first two steps or omit the CO step entirely.
> 3. **Always verify** that the sum of % losses and % residue = 100%. Examiners award partial marks if verification is shown.
> 4. **Units matter:** Mention °C for temperature, mg for mass, and % for percentage loss. Do not omit units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **TGA** measures **mass vs. temperature/time** under controlled atmosphere.
- It is a **destructive** thermal analysis technique.
- Key components: **microbalance, furnace, temperature programmer, atmosphere control, recording system**.
- **TG curve** = mass vs T; **DTG curve** = $dm/dT$ vs T (rate of mass loss).
- Two modes: **dynamic (constant heating rate)** and **static (isothermal)**.
- **% mass loss** = $(m_0 - m_T)/m_0 \times 100$; **% residue** = $m_r/m_0 \times 100$.
- **Fractional conversion** $\alpha = (m_0 - m_T)/(m_0 - m_\infty)$.
- **Kissinger equation** gives activation energy from peak temperatures at multiple heating rates.
- Standard sample size: **1–20 mg**; balance sensitivity: **0.1 µg**.
- Common heating rates: **5, 10, 20 °C/min**; atmospheres: **N₂, O₂, Ar, air**.
- Typical thermal events in nanomaterials: moisture loss <150 °C; ligand decomposition 200–400 °C; polymer backbone 300–600 °C; carbon combustion (in air) 400–800 °C; metal oxide residue >800 °C.
- Calcium oxalate monohydrate is the **classic 3-step demonstration**: H₂O loss (12.33%), CO loss (19.17%), CO₂ loss (30.12%); final CaO residue (38.38%).
- **Factors affecting TG curve:** heating rate, atmosphere, sample mass, particle size, gas flow, buoyancy, crucible material.
- **Applications:** thermal stability, composition, ash content, kinetic analysis, drug loading, catalyst loading, battery safety, aerospace materials.
- **Constants to memorise:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$; $\beta$ has units of K/min or °C/min.
- **Industrial relevance:** TGA is mandatory in pharma (ICH Q1A), polymer certification, and battery safety standards (UN 38.3).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)** is a thermoanalytical technique in which the **temperature difference (ΔT)** between a substance and a thermally inert reference material is recorded as a function of programmed temperature (or time), while both are subjected to identical thermal cycles in a controlled atmosphere.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{sample}} - T_{\text{reference}}$$

When $\Delta T \neq 0$, it indicates that the sample is undergoing an **endothermic** or **exothermic** transformation. These thermal events produce characteristic peaks (or troughs) on the DTA thermogram when $\Delta T$ is plotted against temperature $T$ or time $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DTA is a **Module 3 core topic** under *Characterization Techniques for Nanomaterials*. It is essential for detecting phase transitions, decomposition, crystallization, and oxidation–reduction behaviour in nanomaterials where small particle sizes cause thermal events at lower temperatures than bulk analogues.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Twin Infant Thermometer"

Imagine two babies (the *sample* and the *reference*) lying side-by-side in the same warm room, both wrapped in identical blankets. Each has a thermometer taped to its forehead. Both should warm up at the **same rate** because the room heats uniformly.

- If Baby A starts **sweating or shivering** (a fever, i.e., a chemical change producing heat), her thermometer reads **higher** → this is an **exothermic** event.
- If Baby B is given an **ice pack** (an endothermic change like melting), her thermometer reads **lower** → this is an **endothermic** event.

The room is the **furnace**, the babies are the **crucibles**, and the difference between the two thermometer readings is the **DTA signal ($\Delta T$)**.

## 1.3 Why DTA Matters for Nanomaterials

Nanomaterials display thermal events at **noticeably lower temperatures** and over **broader ranges** than bulk materials because of their:

- **High surface-area-to-volume ratio** → enhanced reactivity.
- **Reduced coordination number** of surface atoms → weaker bonding, easier atomic rearrangement.
- **Quantum confinement effects** → modified thermodynamic stability.

Therefore, DTA becomes a critical diagnostic fingerprint tool for confirming the formation, purity, phase, and thermal stability of **nanoparticles, nanocomposites, nanotubes, and quantum dots**.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Materials and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Material |
|---|---|
| Reference Material | Calcined $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$ (alumina) |
| Sample Holder | Platinum, alumina, or quartz crucibles |
| Standard Heating Rate | $5$ – $20^\circ$C/min |
| Typical Atmosphere | Inert (N$_2$, Ar) or Reactive (O$_2$, air) |
| Calibration Standards | Indium ($\text{In}$, m.p. $156.6^\circ$C), Tin ($\text{Sn}$, m.p. $231.9^\circ$C), Zinc ($\text{Zn}$, m.p. $419.5^\circ$C), Aluminium ($\text{Al}$, m.p. $660.3^\circ$C) |
| Thermocouple Type | Chromel–Alumel (K-type), Pt–Pt/Rh (R/S-type) |

> [!NOTE]
> The reference material must remain thermally inert across the entire experimental temperature range — i.e., it must **not undergo any phase transition, decomposition, or reaction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

When a sample and a reference are heated at a programmed rate:

1. In the **absence of any thermal event**, both follow the furnace temperature linearly. Therefore $\Delta T \approx 0$, producing a **flat baseline**.
2. When the sample undergoes a **physical change** (e.g., melting, vaporisation, polymorphic transition, desolvation) or a **chemical change** (e.g., dehydration, decomposition, oxidation, reduction), the heat required (endothermic) or liberated (exothermic) breaks this thermal symmetry.
3. The deviation of $\Delta T$ from zero produces a **characteristic peak** on the DTA curve.

The **sign, area, and shape** of the peak convey three independent pieces of information:

- **Sign of $\Delta T$** → nature of the event (endothermic vs exothermic).
- **Peak temperature** → the transition temperature.
- **Peak area** → proportional to the **enthalpy change ($\Delta H$)** of the transition.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six-Stage Logical Framework for KTU Board Examinations**

1. **Sample Preparation:** A small, weighed mass of the nanomaterial is placed in the sample crucible. The reference crucible is filled with an equal mass (or equal volume) of thermally inert $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$.
2. **Symmetric Placement:** Both crucibles are positioned symmetrically on identical thermocouple platforms inside the furnace to ensure equal heat exposure.
3. **Programmed Heating:** The furnace temperature $T_{\text{furnace}}$ is increased at a controlled, linear rate $\beta = dT/dt$ (typically $10^\circ$C/min).
4. **Differential Signal Generation:** Two thermocouples (one in each crucible) generate small EMFs. The signals are subtracted electronically to produce $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$.
5. **Recording:** A plot of $\Delta T$ vs $T_{\text{furnace}}$ (or vs time $t$) is generated, called the **DTA curve** or **thermogram**.
6. **Interpretation:** Peaks are identified, onset temperatures marked, peak areas integrated, and thermal events assigned.

## 2.3 Endothermic vs Exothermic Events

| Event Type | Sign of $\Delta T$ | Direction of Peak | Typical Physical / Chemical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Endothermic | $\Delta T < 0$ | **Downward** (trough) | Melting, vaporisation, sublimation, desolvation, dehydration, polymorphic transition, glass transition |
| Exothermic | $\Delta T > 0$ | **Upward** (peak) | Crystallisation, oxidation, combustion, cross-linking, chemisorption, curing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In a standard DTA curve, the convention may differ between instruments. Always verify whether the plotted $\Delta T$ is $T_S - T_R$ or $T_R - T_S$ before interpreting peak direction.

## 2.4 DTA vs DSC — Key Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Favourite Comparison Question:** Differentiate DTA from DSC.

| Feature | DTA | DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) |
|---|---|---|
| Measured Quantity | **Temperature difference** $\Delta T$ | **Heat flow (power)** $dQ/dt$ or $dH/dt$ |
| Output | $\Delta T$ vs $T$ | Heat flow vs $T$ |
| Quantitative Enthalpy | Indirect (requires calibration constant) | **Direct** (pre-calibrated with standards) |
| Instrument Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Sensitivity | Lower (qualitative + semi-quantitative) | Higher (truly quantitative) |
| Reference | Inert material | Empty (heat-flux DSC) or identical inert (power-compensation DSC) |
| Output Units | Arbitrary (often $\mu$V or °C) | mW or J/(g·K) |

## 2.5 Factors Affecting DTA Curves

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** Students frequently lose marks by forgetting to discuss factors that affect the DTA curve.

1. **Heating rate $\beta$** — Higher $\beta$ shifts peaks to higher temperatures and broadens them.
2. **Sample mass** — Larger mass amplifies $\Delta T$ and broadens peaks.
3. **Particle size / packing** — Fine powders enhance contact with the thermocouple but may trap gases; coarse powders reduce sensitivity.
4. **Atmosphere** — Inert (N$_2$, Ar) prevents oxidation; reactive (O$_2$, air) enables combustion studies.
5. **Crucible material** — Pt, Al$_2$O$_3$, or quartz chosen for compatibility with sample.
6. **Thermocouple placement geometry** — Asymmetry introduces baseline drift.
7. **Reference material** — Must be truly inert across the range.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for PECHT753 Module 3 Board Examinations**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$ | Differential temperature signal | K or °C |
| 2 | $\beta = dT/dt$ | Heating rate (programmed) | K/min or °C/min |
| 3 | $T_p$ (peak temperature) | Temperature at the **extremum** of the peak | K or °C |
| 4 | $T_i$ (onset temperature) | Temperature where the curve **first deviates** from baseline | K or °C |
| 5 | $\Delta H = K \cdot \dfrac{A}{m}$ | Enthalpy change proportional to **peak area** $A$, **mass** $m$, and calibration constant $K$ | J/g |
| 6 | $\theta_{bulk} - \theta_{nano} = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{sv} V_m}{r \Delta H_f}$ | **Gibbs–Thomson (Kelvin) equation** linking nanoparticle melting-point depression to radius $r$ | K |
| 7 | $\dfrac{T_{mp}}{T_{mb}} \approx 1 - \dfrac{2}{\rho_s L r} \left[\sigma_s - \sigma_l\left(\dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_l}\right)^{2/3}\right]$ | **Pawlow's equation** for melting-point depression in nanoparticles (radius $r$) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar notation $\vert x \vert$ above is rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In handwritten KTU scripts, simply write absolute value normally.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Nanomaterials:** Confirm polymorphism, hydrate loss, and amorphous content.
- **Catalysis:** Determine thermal stability of supported nanoparticle catalysts.
- **Battery Materials:** Quantify lithiation/delithiation enthalpies.
- **Ceramics & Metallurgy:** Identify sintering onset and phase transformation in nanopowders.
- **Polymers / Nanocomposites:** Detect $T_g$, crystallisation, curing, and degradation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Enthalpy from DTA Peak Area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between peak area and enthalpy forms the theoretical foundation of quantitative DTA.

### Statement
The enthalpy change $\Delta H$ associated with a thermal event is directly proportional to the area $A$ under the corresponding DTA peak.

### Step 1 — Start from the Heat Balance

In the sample crucible, the heat balance over an infinitesimal time interval $dt$ is:

$$m \, C_p \, dT_S = dQ_{\text{furnace}} - dQ_{\text{event}}$$

where:
- $m$ = sample mass,
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity of sample,
- $dQ_{\text{furnace}}$ = heat supplied by the furnace,
- $dQ_{\text{event}}$ = heat absorbed or released by the thermal event.

### Step 2 — Heat Input from Furnace

The heat supplied by the furnace is governed by thermal conductance $K$ between furnace and sample:

$$dQ_{\text{furnace}} = K \left( T_F - T_S \right) dt$$

Similarly, for the reference (no thermal event occurs):

$$m \, C_p^{\text{ref}} \, dT_R = K \left( T_F - T_R \right) dt$$

### Step 3 — Form the Differential Signal

Taking the difference of the two heat-balance equations (with $C_p \approx C_p^{\text{ref}}$ assumed for a symmetric setup):

$$m \, C_p \, d\left( T_S - T_R \right) = -dQ_{\text{event}} + K \left[ \left( T_F - T_S \right) - \left( T_F - T_R \right) \right] dt$$

$$m \, C_p \, d(\Delta T) = -dQ_{\text{event}} + K \left( T_R - T_S \right) dt$$

$$m \, C_p \, d(\Delta T) = -dQ_{\text{event}} - K (\Delta T) \, dt$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange for the Event Heat

$$dQ_{\text{event}} = -m \, C_p \, d(\Delta T) - K (\Delta T) \, dt$$

### Step 5 — Integrate over the Full Peak

Integrating across the entire thermal event (from baseline deviation onset to return):

$$\int dQ_{\text{event}} = -m \, C_p \int d(\Delta T) - K \int (\Delta T) \, dt$$

Since $\Delta T$ returns to zero at the end of a complete peak, the first integral vanishes. Therefore:

$$\Delta H_{\text{total}} = \int dQ_{\text{event}} = -K \int (\Delta T) \, dt$$

### Step 6 — Define Peak Area and Conclude

The peak area $A$ is defined as:

$$A = \int (\Delta T) \, dt$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H = -K \cdot A\;}$$

where the negative sign simply reflects the convention that endothermic events have negative $\Delta T$.

### Step 7 — Introduce Sample Mass Normalisation

In practice, the instrument is calibrated with a standard of known $\Delta H_{\text{std}}$ giving peak area $A_{\text{std}}$:

$$K = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{std}}}{A_{\text{std}}}$$

So the enthalpy per unit mass of the unknown sample is:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H_{\text{sample}} = \dfrac{K \cdot A_{\text{sample}}}{m_{\text{sample}}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{std}} \cdot A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{sample}}}\;}$$

This is the **calibrated, quantitative DTA equation** expected in board answers.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Nanomaterial Thermal Events

| Nanomaterial | Expected DTA Event | Type | Approximate $T_p$ Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) | Surfactant decomposition | Exothermic | $200$ – $350^\circ$C |
| Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) | Ligand desorption | Endothermic | $180$ – $300^\circ$C |
| TiO$_2$ nanoparticles | Anatase → Rutile transition | Exothermic | $400$ – $600^\circ$C |
| ZnO nanoparticles | Decomposition of precursors | Endothermic | $300$ – $500^\circ$C |
| Fe$_3$O$_4$ (magnetite) NPs | Oxidation to $\gamma$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ | Exothermic | $250$ – $400^\circ$C |
| Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) | Graphitisation / burn-off | Exothermic | $500$ – $700^\circ$C |
| Hydroxyapatite NPs | Dehydroxylation | Endothermic | $700$ – $900^\circ$C |
| Polymeric NPs / nanocapsules | Glass transition, melting, decomposition | Both | $50$ – $400^\circ$C |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked about a specific nanomaterial, the correct **peak direction (endo/exo)** and **approximate temperature** together fetch full marks in the 7-mark analytical questions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration

**Problem (KTU-style):** A $5.0$ mg sample of a nanomaterial shows a DTA endothermic peak with area $A = 0.40$ mV·min. Calibration using Indium ($m_{\text{std}} = 3.0$ mg, $\Delta H_{\text{std}} = 28.45$ J/g, $A_{\text{std}} = 0.30$ mV·min) was performed. Calculate the enthalpy change per unit mass of the sample.

### Solution

$$\Delta H_{\text{sample}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{std}} \cdot A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{sample}}}$$

Substituting values:

$$\Delta H_{\text{sample}} = \dfrac{(28.45\ \text{J/g}) \times (3.0\ \text{mg}) \times (0.40\ \text{mV·min})}{(0.30\ \text{mV·min}) \times (5.0\ \text{mg})}$$

Note that mg units cancel:

$$\Delta H_{\text{sample}} = \dfrac{28.45 \times 3.0 \times 0.40}{0.30 \times 5.0} = \dfrac{34.14}{1.50}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H_{\text{sample}} = 22.76\ \text{J/g}\;}$$

The negative sign (endothermic convention) is implicit when describing melting/decomposition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DTA Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["Control Unit"]
        PG["Programmer / Temperature Controller"]
        TC["Thermocouple Selector"]
        REC["Recorder / Data Acquisition"]
    end

    subgraph FURN["Furnace Assembly"]
        FUR["Programmable Furnace with Linear Heating"]
        SH["Symmetric Sample Holder Block"]
        ATMC["Atmosphere Inlet — N2 / Ar / O2 / Air"]
    end

    subgraph SMPL["Crucible and Sample Module"]
        SCR["Sample Crucible — Pt or Al2O3"]
        RCR["Reference Crucible — alpha-Al2O3 inert"]
        TS["Thermocouple T_S in Sample"]
        TR["Thermocouple T_R in Reference"]
    end

    subgraph SIG["Signal Conditioning"]
        AMP["Differential Amplifier — computes Delta T"]
        FILT["Filter / Baseline Correction"]
        INTG["Peak Integrator — computes Area A"]
    end

    PG --> FUR
    FUR --> SH
    ATMC --> FUR
    SH --> SCR
    SH --> RCR
    SCR --> TS
    RCR --> TR
    TS --> AMP
    TR --> AMP
    AMP --> FILT
    FILT --> REC
    FILT --> INTG
    TC --> TS
    TC --> TR
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow and Decision Tree for DTA Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Start: Sample Loaded with Reference"] --> B["Programmed Heating at Constant beta"]
    B --> C{"Is Delta T approx equal to zero?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Flat Baseline — No Thermal Event"]
    C -- No --> E{"Sign of Delta T?"}
    E -- Negative Delta T --> F["Endothermic Peak — Downward"]
    E -- Positive Delta T --> G["Exothermic Peak — Upward"]
    F --> H["Probable Events: Melting, Vaporisation, Desolvation, Polymorphic Transition"]
    G --> I["Probable Events: Crystallisation, Oxidation, Curing, Combustion"]
    H --> J["Record Onset T_i and Peak T_p"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Integrate Peak Area to Obtain Delta H"]
    K --> L["Compare with Standards / Literature to Confirm Identity"]
    L --> M["Report: Thermal Event Nature, T_p, Delta H"]
```

## 4.3 DTA Thermogram — Annotated Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TG["Idealised DTA Thermogram Schematic"]
        BL["Baseline: Delta T approx 0"]
        TI["Onset Temperature T_i — first deviation from baseline"]
        TP["Peak Temperature T_p — maximum deviation"]
        TE["Extrapolated End T_e — return to baseline"]
        EA["Endothermic Trough — Delta T < 0"]
        XA["Exothermic Peak — Delta T > 0"]
        AR["Peak Area A — proportional to Delta H"]
    end

    BL --> TI --> TP --> TE
    EA -.- TI
    XA -.- TI
    AR -.- TP
```

## 4.4 Component-to-Function Mapping Table

| Block ID | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PG | Temperature Programmer | Provides linear $T$ vs $t$ ramp; sets $\beta$ |
| FUR | Furnace | Heats sample and reference uniformly |
| ATMC | Atmosphere Control | Maintains inert or reactive gas environment |
| SCR | Sample Crucible | Holds the nanomaterial under test |
| RCR | Reference Crucible | Holds thermally inert $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$ |
| TS, TR | Thermocouples | Measure $T_S$ and $T_R$ independently |
| AMP | Differential Amplifier | Computes $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$ |
| FILT | Filter | Removes electrical noise and baseline drift |
| INTG | Integrator | Numerically computes peak area $A$ |
| REC | Recorder / DAQ | Plots $\Delta T$ vs $T$ thermogram |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Define Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA). What is the role of the reference material in DTA?**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> **Definition (2 marks):** DTA is a thermoanalytical technique in which the temperature difference ($\Delta T$) between a sample and a thermally inert reference material is recorded as a function of programmed furnace temperature (or time), under controlled heating. Mathematically, $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$, where $T_S$ is the sample temperature and $T_R$ is the reference temperature.
>
> **Role of Reference (1 mark):** The reference material (commonly calcined $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$) must remain **thermally inert** over the entire experimental temperature range. It provides a baseline response, so that any deviation $\Delta T \neq 0$ can be unambiguously attributed to thermal events occurring **only in the sample** — such as phase transitions, decomposition, or reaction.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Distinguish between endothermic and exothermic peaks in a DTA curve. Give one example of each in nanomaterials.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> **Endothermic Peak (1.5 marks):** The sample absorbs heat, making it **cooler** than the reference. Hence $\Delta T < 0$, producing a **downward** trough in the DTA curve. **Example:** Melting of silver nanoparticles or dehydration of a hydrated nanocatalyst.
>
> **Exothermic Peak (1.5 marks):** The sample releases heat, making it **hotter** than the reference. Hence $\Delta T > 0$, producing an **upward** peak. **Example:** Crystallisation of an amorphous TiO$_2$ nanoparticle or oxidation of Fe$_3$O$_4$ to $\gamma$-Fe$_2$O$_3$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme allows an **internal choice** in Part B. Two fully independent alternatives are provided below. Select any **one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECHT753]**

**(a) Describe the instrumentation and working principle of a Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA) apparatus with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the factors affecting the DTA curve and explain how quantitative enthalpy change is calculated from the peak area. (7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
DTA measures the **difference in temperature** ($\Delta T = T_S - T_R$) between a sample and a thermally inert reference (typically calcined $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$) when both are subjected to identical, programmed heating. The reference undergoes no thermal events, while the sample may absorb (endothermic) or release (exothermic) heat during physical/chemical transformations. This produces a measurable $\Delta T$, plotted against furnace temperature $T$ to yield the DTA thermogram.

**Instrumentation (3 Marks):**

1. **Furnace:** A programmable, electrically heated furnace provides linear heating rates ($\beta = 5$ – $20^\circ$C/min).
2. **Sample holder:** A symmetric metal (Pt or Inconel) block holds the two crucibles equidistantly.
3. **Crucibles:** Identical Pt, Al$_2$O$_3$, or quartz crucibles for sample and reference.
4. **Thermocouples:** Chromel–Alumel (K-type) or Pt–Pt/Rh (R/S-type) measure $T_S$ and $T_R$ individually.
5. **Differential amplifier:** Computes $\Delta T$ from the two thermocouple EMFs.
6. **Atmosphere control:** Inert (N$_2$, Ar) or reactive (O$_2$, air) gas inlet.
7. **Recorder / Computer:** Plots the thermogram and performs peak integration.

**Working Step-by-Step (1 Mark):**

The sample and reference are heated at constant $\beta$. The two thermocouples generate signals that are subtracted electronically. The differential signal is amplified, filtered, and plotted as $\Delta T$ vs $T$. Peaks correspond to thermal events; flat baseline indicates no events.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Factors Affecting DTA Curve (4 Marks):**

1. **Heating rate ($\beta$):** Higher rates shift peaks to higher temperatures, broaden them, and reduce resolution.
2. **Sample mass:** Larger mass amplifies peak area but broadens peaks.
3. **Particle size:** Fine particles enhance thermal contact; very fine particles may oxidise prematurely.
4. **Atmosphere:** Inert gas prevents oxidation; air/O$_2$ enables combustion studies.
5. **Packing density:** Loose packing gives poor thermal contact; dense packing may trap evolved gases.
6. **Crucible material and geometry:** Asymmetry introduces baseline drift.
7. **Reference material purity:** Impurities in reference produce artefacts.

**Quantitative Enthalpy from Peak Area (3 Marks):**

The enthalpy change is proportional to the peak area $A$ and inversely proportional to the sample mass $m$, scaled by a calibration constant $K$:

$$\Delta H = \dfrac{K \cdot A}{m}$$

The constant $K$ is obtained by running a standard (e.g., Indium, $\Delta H_{\text{std}} = 28.45$ J/g):

$$K = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{std}}}{A_{\text{std}}}$$

Therefore the calibrated enthalpy of the unknown sample is:

$$\Delta H_{\text{sample}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{std}} \cdot A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{std}} \cdot m_{\text{sample}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently forget to **calibrate** the instrument and directly write $\Delta H \propto A$. Examiners expect the **explicit formula with calibration constant**. Marks are lost for omitting either $m$ or $K$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECHT753 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Compare DTA with DSC, highlighting the measured quantity, output, and quantitative capability. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the application of DTA in the characterisation of nanomaterials, with at least three suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

| Feature | DTA (3.5 Marks) | DSC (3.5 Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Measured Quantity | Temperature difference $\Delta T$ | Heat flow rate $dQ/dt$ or $dH/dt$ |
| Output | $\Delta T$ vs $T$ thermogram | Heat flow vs $T$ thermogram |
| Quantitative Enthalpy | **Indirect** — requires calibration constant $K$ | **Direct** — pre-calibrated, gives $\Delta H$ in J/g |
| Reference | Thermally inert $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$ | Empty pan (heat-flux) or inert sample (power-comp.) |
| Sensitivity | Lower | Higher |
| Cost & Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Units of Output | $\mu$V or °C | mW or J/(g·K) |

**Key Distinction (1 Mark):** DTA measures the *consequence* (temperature difference) of an event, while DSC measures the *cause* (heat flow) directly — making DSC inherently more quantitative.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):** DTA is extensively used to investigate phase transitions, thermal stability, decomposition, and crystallisation in nanomaterials — which often occur at lower temperatures than in bulk due to high surface-area-to-volume ratio.

**Example 1 — TiO$_2$ Nanoparticles (2 Marks):** DTA reveals the anatase-to-rutile phase transition as an **exothermic peak** between $400$ – $600^\circ$C. Nanoparticles show this transition at lower temperatures than bulk TiO$_2$ ($\sim 700^\circ$C), confirming nanoscale size effects.

**Example 2 — Iron Oxide Magnetic Nanoparticles (2 Marks):** DTA of Fe$_3$O$_4$ NPs in air shows an **exothermic peak** around $250$ – $400^\circ$C corresponding to oxidation to $\gamma$-Fe$_2$O$_3$. This confirms the oxidation temperature and surface reactivity of the nanoparticles.

**Example 3 — Polymeric Nanocapsules / Drug Delivery NPs (2 Marks):** DTA identifies the **glass transition ($T_g$)** as a baseline shift, **melting** as an endothermic peak, and **decomposition** as a strong endothermic event. This is critical for assessing thermal stability of drug-loaded nanocarriers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often list applications without **linking them to peak direction (endo/exo) and approximate temperature**. Examiners reward answers that identify both the *event* and the *peak characteristic*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PECHT753 — Module 3: DTA — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Core Equation:** $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$ — the fundamental signal of DTA.
- **Reference Material:** $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$ (calcined alumina) — must be **inert**.
- **Endothermic** ($\Delta T < 0$): Melting, vaporisation, desolvation, dehydration, polymorphic transitions.
- **Exothermic** ($\Delta T > 0$): Crystallisation, oxidation, combustion, curing, chemisorption.
- **Peak Area:** Directly proportional to **enthalpy change** $\Delta H$.
- **Quantitative Equation:** $\Delta H = K \cdot A / m$ where $K$ is found via a standard (e.g., Indium).
- **DTA vs DSC:** DTA measures $\Delta T$; DSC measures heat flow $dQ/dt$.
- **Heating rate $\beta$:** Higher $\beta$ → higher peak temperature, broader peaks.
- **Nanomaterials** show thermal events at **lower** temperatures than bulk — a direct consequence of high surface area and reduced coordination.
- **Calibration Standards:** In (156.6°C), Sn (231.9°C), Zn (419.5°C), Al (660.3°C).
- **Always identify** both the *nature* (endo/exo) and the *approximate $T_p$* in exam answers.
- **Diagram value:** A neat block diagram of the DTA setup earns **at least 1–2 extra marks** in 7-mark questions.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never state that DTA gives enthalpy *directly* — it requires calibration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Official Definition**
> **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** is a thermoanalytical technique in which the **difference in heat flow rate** ($dQ/dt$) between a **sample** and an **inert reference** is measured as a function of **temperature** (or **time** at isothermal conditions), while both are subjected to a **controlled, identical temperature program** (heating, cooling, or isothermal hold) in the same controlled atmosphere.

The output of a DSC experiment is a **thermogram**, which plots:

$$\text{Heat Flow Difference} \; (\Delta \dot{Q}) = \left(\frac{dQ}{dt}\right)_{\text{sample}} - \left(\frac{dQ}{dt}\right)_{\text{reference}}$$

against the programmed temperature $T$ (or time $t$). This measured difference corresponds to the **enthalpy changes** ($\Delta H$) associated with endothermic or exothermic transitions in the material.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight — Why DSC for Nanomaterials?**
> Nanomaterials exhibit **size-dependent thermodynamic properties** (e.g., melting point depression, altered glass transition, enhanced surface energy). DSC is a **first-tier characterization tool** in the KTU PECHT753 syllabus because it can resolve these **nanoscopic thermal signatures** with microgram-scale sample masses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine two identical cooking vessels sitting side-by-side on twin stoves, each equipped with a thermometer and a precise gas-flow meter. The stoves are controlled by a master timer that **raises the temperature of both stoves in exactly the same pattern** (e.g., +10 °C per minute). One vessel contains your **unknown sample** (say, a powder of nanoparticles), and the other contains a **blank/inert reference** (an empty, identical pan).

* If your sample does **nothing** thermally unusual, both stoves deliver the same amount of energy, and the two thermometers read identical temperatures.
* If the sample **melts** (absorbs heat), it "drinks" extra energy to break its lattice — the gas meter to the sample pan must deliver **more gas** to keep pace with the blank.
* If the sample **crystallizes** (releases heat), the gas meter to the sample pan delivers **less gas** because the sample is generating its own heat.

A DSC instrument is essentially a **highly precise, automated, miniaturized version of this two-stove experiment**, with the "gas meters" being **sub-milliwatt heat-flow sensors** and the "thermometers" being **thermocouples or resistance temperature detectors (RTDs)**. The differential gas flow is recorded continuously, giving you a **fingerprint of every thermal event** (melting, freezing, glass transition, decomposition, oxidation, curing) in your nanomaterial.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning in DSC Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Flow Rate | $\dot{Q} \equiv dQ/dt$ | **W (Watt)** or **mW** | Rate at which thermal energy enters/leaves the pan |
| Differential Heat Flow | $\Delta \dot{Q}$ | **mW** | Sample heat flow minus reference heat flow |
| Enthalpy Change | $\Delta H$ | **J/g** or **J/mol** | Area under DSC peak, normalized by mass |
| Heating Rate | $\beta = dT/dt$ | **K/min** or **°C/min** | Rate of programmed temperature change |
| Glass Transition Temp. | $T_g$ | **°C** or **K** | Step change in heat flow baseline |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | **°C** or **K** | Onset of endothermic peak |
| Crystallization Temp. | $T_c$ | **°C** or **K** | Onset of exothermic peak |

> [!NOTE] **Physical Constants Used in DSC Calibration**
> * **Standard Reference Material (SRM):** High-purity **Indium** ($T_m = 156.6 \, ^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta H_f = 28.45 \, \text{J/g}$) is the most common DSC calibration standard.
> * Other SRMs include **Tin** ($T_m = 231.93 \, ^\circ\text{C}$), **Zinc** ($T_m = 419.53 \, ^\circ\text{C}$), and **Sapphire** (for $C_p$ calibration).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Heart of DSC

The DSC experiment rests on **three simultaneous, carefully controlled variables**:

1. **Identical temperature program** — Both sample and reference are subjected to the **same** temperature vs. time profile: heating, cooling, or isothermal hold. A typical heating rate $\beta$ ranges from **1 K/min** (high resolution) to **20 K/min** (survey scans).
2. **Matched thermal resistance** — Both pans sit in **symmetric positions** within the same (or twin) furnace(s), minimizing **asymmetric heat losses**.
3. **Continuous differential measurement** — The instrument measures the **compensating energy** required to keep both pans at the programmed temperature.

## 2.2 The Two Principal DSC Architectures

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks for the **two major types** of DSC, which differ in **how the differential signal is generated**:

### 2.2.1 Heat-Flux DSC (Single-Furnace / Disk-Type)

* **Construction:** A **single, common furnace** heats both sample and reference platforms, which are connected through a **thermoelectric disk** (a low-thermal-resistance metallic path).
* **Sensor:** **Chromel-constantan or chromel-alumel thermocouples** (typically 8 to 12 in series) measure the **temperature difference** $\Delta T = T_{\text{sample}} - T_{\text{reference}}$.
* **Working equation:**

$$\Delta \dot{Q} = -\frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{th}}}$$

where $R_{\text{th}}$ is the **total thermal resistance** of the heat-flow path (J/K). The **negative sign** ensures that an **endothermic** event (sample absorbs heat → cools relative to reference → $\Delta T < 0$) yields a **positive** $\Delta \dot{Q}$ reading by the **ICTAC convention** (upward peak = endotherm).

* **Trade-off:** Simpler, more rugged, but slightly lower sensitivity than power-compensated designs.

### 2.2.2 Power-Compensated DSC (Dual-Furnace / Calvet-Type)

* **Construction:** Sample and reference each sit in **completely separate, independently controlled micro-furnaces**.
* **Sensor:** Two **independent temperature sensors** and **separate heating elements** (typically platinum resistance heaters).
* **Working equation:** The instrument measures the **difference in electrical power** $\Delta P = P_{\text{sample}} - P_{\text{reference}}$ supplied to the two furnaces. To a first approximation:

$$\Delta \dot{Q} = \Delta P$$

* **Trade-off:** Higher sensitivity, faster response time, better baseline stability, but more expensive and requires careful furnace matching.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Examiner Note**
> In both designs, the **fundamental measured quantity** is the **heat flow difference** $\Delta \dot{Q}$, which is **proportional to the instantaneous heat capacity** of the sample relative to the reference:

$$C_p^{\text{sample}}(T) - C_p^{\text{ref}}(T) = \frac{\Delta \dot{Q}}{\beta}$$

where $\beta = dT/dt$ is the **heating rate**. This relation is the **bridge** between DSC and the thermodynamic heat capacity.

## 2.3 What the DSC Thermogram Tells You — Thermal Event Signatures

| Thermal Event | Type of Peak | Direction (Up = Endotherm) | Information Extracted |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Glass Transition ($T_g$)** | Step in baseline | No peak, just a **step** | Mid-point of step = $T_g$ |
| **Melting ($T_m$)** | Sharp endothermic peak | **Upward** | Onset = $T_m$; Area = $\Delta H_{\text{fus}}$ |
| **Crystallization ($T_c$)** | Sharp exothermic peak | **Downward** | Onset = $T_c$; Area = $\Delta H_{\text{cryst}}$ |
| **Vaporization/Desorption** | Broad endothermic peak | **Upward** | Mass loss correlation with TGA |
| **Oxidation/Decomposition** | Exothermic peak | **Downward** | Onset = thermal stability limit |
| **Curing/Polymerization** | Broad exothermic peak | **Downward** | Cure kinetics, degree of conversion |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas are given in the **ICTAC recommended convention**: positive $\Delta \dot{Q}$ = endothermic.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Units} & \textbf{Physical Meaning} \\
\hline
\text{Heat Flow Diff.} & \Delta \dot{Q} = \dot{Q}_s - \dot{Q}_r & \text{mW} & \text{Instantaneous signal} \\
\hline
\text{Heat-Flux Eqn.} & \Delta \dot{Q} = -\Delta T / R_{\text{th}} & \text{mW} & \text{Disk-type DSC} \\
\hline
\text{Power-Comp. Eqn.} & \Delta \dot{Q} = \Delta P & \text{mW} & \text{Calvet-type DSC} \\
\hline
\text{Differential }C_p & C_p^s - C_p^r = \Delta \dot{Q} / \beta & \text{J/(g·K)} & \text{Baseline slope method} \\
\hline
\text{Transition Enthalpy} & \Delta H = \dfrac{1}{m} \displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta \dot{Q}\, \dfrac{dT}{\beta} & \text{J/g} & \text{Peak area / mass} \\
\hline
\text{Specific }C_p\text{ (3-curve)} & C_p^s = \dfrac{\Delta \dot{Q}}{\beta \cdot m_s} \cdot \dfrac{m_r \cdot \beta}{\Delta \dot{Q}_{\text{ref}}} & \text{J/(g·K)} & \text{With sapphire std.} \\
\hline
\text{Heating Rate Relation} & \beta = dT/dt & \text{K/min} & \text{Program parameter} \\
\hline
\text{Nanoparticle } T_m \text{ (Pawley)} & T_m(r) = T_m^\infty \left(1 - \dfrac{2}{\rho_s L r} \cdot \dfrac{2 \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{sv}}{\text{const}}\right) & \text{K} & \text{Size-dependent m.p.} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> * In **pharmaceutical nanotechnology**, DSC confirms **amorphous content** in drug nanoparticles (a critical FDA quality attribute).
> * In **battery R&D**, DSC identifies **thermal runaway** thresholds of nanostructured cathode materials.
> * In **metallurgy**, DSC resolves **solid–solid phase transitions** in nanocrystalline alloys (e.g., austenite → martensite in nanoporous steels).

## 2.5 Sample Preparation & Calibration — KTU Practical Notes

1. **Pan Selection:**
   * **Aluminum pans** (most common, max ~600 °C, hermetically sealable).
   * **Alumina pans** for high-temperature DSC (>600 °C).
   * **Gold or platinum pans** for corrosive or reactive samples.
   * **Crucible (no lid)** for volatile samples or to mimic TGA conditions.
2. **Sample Mass:** Typically **3–20 mg** for organic/ polymeric; **5–50 mg** for inorganic/metallic. Smaller masses give sharper transitions but poorer signal-to-noise.
3. **Atmosphere:** **Nitrogen** (inert, default), **air** (oxidation studies), **oxygen** (forced oxidation), or **argon** (ultra-inert). Flow rate typically **20–50 mL/min**.
4. **Calibration Sequence:**
   * **Temperature calibration** using Indium, Tin, Zinc (onset should match literature ± 0.5 °C).
   * **Enthalpy calibration** using Indium (peak area should match **28.45 J/g** within ± 2 %).
   * **Heat capacity calibration** using synthetic sapphire (NIST SRM 720).
5. **Baseline Correction:** Empty-pan vs. empty-pan, then **sapphire run** for $C_p$ baseline subtraction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Differential Heat Capacity from the Heat-Flux DSC Equation

We begin with the **governing energy-balance equation** for the sample side of a heat-flux DSC:

$$C_s \frac{dT_s}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{loss},s} + \Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}}$$

where:
* $C_s$ is the **total heat capacity** of the sample + pan (J/K),
* $T_s$ is the **sample temperature** (K),
* $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$ is the **heat inflow** from the furnace (W),
* $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss},s}$ is the **heat loss** to surroundings (W),
* $\Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}}$ is the **rate of enthalpy absorption/release** by any ongoing thermal event (W).

A **mirror equation** holds for the reference side, but $\Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}} = 0$ (inert reference):

$$C_r \frac{dT_r}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}}' - \dot{Q}_{\text{loss},r}$$

### Step 1 — Subtract the two equations

By symmetry of the disk-type DSC, $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} \approx \dot{Q}_{\text{in}}'$ and $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss},s} \approx \dot{Q}_{\text{loss},r}$:

$$C_s \frac{dT_s}{dt} - C_r \frac{dT_r}{dt} = \Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the common programming rate

The instrument program enforces $dT_r/dt = dT_s/dt = \beta$ (idealized — both follow the same ramp):

$$C_s \beta - C_r \beta = \Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}}$$

$$(C_s - C_r)\,\beta = \Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}}$$

### Step 3 — Recognize $\Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}}$ as the measured signal

The differential heat-flow sensor in the disk records **exactly** this quantity:

$$\Delta \dot{Q} \equiv \Delta \dot{H}_{\text{event}} = (C_s - C_r)\,\beta$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the differential heat capacity

$$\boxed{\,C_s - C_r = \frac{\Delta \dot{Q}}{\beta}\,}$$

Dividing by sample mass $m_s$ to get **specific heat capacity difference**:

$$c_p^s - c_p^r = \frac{\Delta \dot{Q}}{\beta \cdot m_s}$$

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight**
> This is why the DSC baseline (away from any peak) is **directly proportional to the heat capacity of the sample** — and why a **glass transition appears as a step in the baseline** (because $c_p$ increases abruptly at $T_g$).

## 3.2 Derivation II: Transition Enthalpy from Peak Area

The total enthalpy absorbed or released during a thermal event is the **time integral of the heat flow difference**, minus the **baselines before and after the peak** (which represent only the heat-capacity contribution).

### Step 1 — Define the peak area

$$A_{\text{peak}} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \Delta \dot{Q}(t)\, dt \quad [\text{J}]$$

The limits $t_1$ and $t_2$ are chosen at the **onset** and **completion** of the transition, where the signal returns to the post-event baseline.

### Step 2 — Convert the time integral to a temperature integral

Using the change of variable $t \to T$ with $dT = \beta\, dt$, hence $dt = dT/\beta$:

$$A_{\text{peak}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta \dot{Q}(T)\, \frac{dT}{\beta} = \frac{1}{\beta}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta \dot{Q}(T)\, dT$$

### Step 3 — Normalize by sample mass

$$\boxed{\,\Delta H = \frac{A_{\text{peak}}}{m_s} = \frac{1}{m_s \beta}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta \dot{Q}(T)\, dT\,} \quad \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{g}}\right]$$

For molar enthalpies, multiply by molar mass $M$:

$$\Delta H_m = \frac{M}{m_s \beta}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta \dot{Q}(T)\, dT \quad \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{mol}}\right]$$

## 3.3 Derivation III: Size-Dependent Melting Point Depression (Nanoparticles)

This is **central to the KTU PECHT753 syllabus** because it directly connects DSC to the **nanoscale** nature of the material being characterized.

The classical **Pawley (or Reiss–Pawley) model** for a free nanoparticle of radius $r$ gives the melting point $T_m(r)$ as:

$$T_m(r) = T_m^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{2}{\rho_s \, L \, r}\left[\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\left(\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_l}\right)^{2/3}\right]\right)$$

where:
* $T_m^\infty$ = bulk melting point (K),
* $\rho_s, \rho_l$ = solid and liquid densities (kg/m³),
* $L$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg),
* $r$ = nanoparticle radius (m),
* $\gamma_{sv}, \gamma_{lv}$ = solid–vapor and liquid–vapor surface energies (J/m²).

### Step 1 — Simplify the surface-energy bracket

Define the **effective surface-energy penalty**:

$$\Gamma \equiv \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\left(\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_l}\right)^{2/3}$$

The simplified melting-point law becomes:

$$T_m(r) = T_m^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{2\,\Gamma}{\rho_s \, L \, r}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Define the **Gibbs–Thomson length**

$$\xi \equiv \frac{2\,\Gamma}{\rho_s \, L}$$

This has units of **meters** and represents a **characteristic length scale** below which size effects become significant (typically 1–10 nm for metals). The compact form is:

$$\boxed{\,T_m(r) = T_m^\infty \left( 1 - \frac{\xi}{r}\right)\,}$$

### Step 3 — Inverse relation — extracting $\xi$ from DSC data

By measuring $T_m(r)$ at several nanoparticle sizes and plotting $T_m$ vs. $1/r$:

$$\frac{T_m(r)}{T_m^\infty} = 1 - \xi \cdot \frac{1}{r}$$

A **linear fit** to this plot gives:
* **Slope** = $-\xi T_m^\infty$ (units of K·m),
* **Intercept** = $1$ (dimensionless).

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Examiner Note**
> The sign convention: $T_m(r) < T_m^\infty$ because $\Gamma > 0$ for most metallic systems — nanoparticles **always** melt **lower** than their bulk counterparts. This is a **frequently tested KTU concept**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

### Example 3.4.1 — Computing $\Delta H$ from a Synthetic DSC Peak

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern — Numerical/Analytical Problem, 7 Marks]**

A **12.5 mg sample** of **poly(lactic acid) (PLA) nanoparticles** is heated at $\beta = 10 \, \text{K/min}$ through its melting transition. The endothermic peak is bounded by $T_1 = 145 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_2 = 165 \, ^\circ\text{C}$. The peak has an approximately **triangular shape** with a **base of 20 K** and a **height of 5.0 mW**. Compute the melting enthalpy $\Delta H_{\text{fus}}$ in J/g.

**Solution:**

The peak area $A_{\text{peak}}$ of a triangle = $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$ in $T$–$\Delta \dot{Q}$ space, but we must **divide by $\beta$** to get Joules:

$$A_{\text{peak}} = \frac{1}{2} \times (T_2 - T_1) \times (\Delta \dot{Q})_{\max} \times \frac{1}{\beta}$$

$$A_{\text{peak}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \, \text{K} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W} \times \frac{1}{10 \, \text{K/min}}$$

$$A_{\text{peak}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}$$

The melting enthalpy is the peak area divided by sample mass:

$$\Delta H_{\text{fus}} = \frac{A_{\text{peak}}}{m_s} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}}{12.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{g}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta H_{\text{fus}} = 0.40 \, \text{J/g}\,}$$

> [!NOTE] **Sanity Check:** Pure bulk PLA has $\Delta H_{\text{fus}} \approx 40 \text{–} 60 \, \text{J/g}$. A value of $0.40 \, \text{J/g}$ is **two orders of magnitude too low**, indicating that either the sample is **mostly amorphous** or the **peak height estimate is wrong** (a 5 mW peak on a 12.5 mg sample is unusually small). The student should report the calculated value and **flag the discrepancy** in a comment.

### Example 3.4.2 — Melting-Point Depression of Gold Nanoparticles

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern — Application/Analytical Problem, 7 Marks]**
