| **Sliding (Lateral Manipulation)** | Tip apex atom forms a *transient chemical bond* with the adatom and drags it across the surface | $\sim 0.3\text{–}0.5\ \text{nm}$ | **Chemical binding** | Xe on Ni(110) — Eigler 1990 |
| **Pulling (Vertical Manipulation)** | Tip picks up an atom by *lifting* it vertically off the surface | $\sim 0.2\ \text{nm}$ | **Adhesion + field gradient** | Cu adatoms on Cu(111) |
| **Pushing (Repulsive-mode Pushing)** | Tip stays at imaging height and the strong Pauli repulsion *kicks* the adatom aside | $\sim 0.5\text{–}1.0\ \text{nm}$ | **Repulsive Pauli force** | Pb on Cu(211) |
| **Field-Evaporation / Dipole Pushing** | High bias + strong electric field ejects or repels the atom | Variable | **Electrostatic** | Atomic-scale lithography |

## 2.3 Energy Landscape of a Sliding Atom

During lateral sliding manipulation, the adatom experiences a **periodic potential energy surface (PES)** generated by the substrate lattice. The tip–adatom interaction *tilts* this PES so that the adatom is no longer trapped in a single adsorption well. The condition for successful sliding is that the **lateral force component from the tip** must exceed the **corrugation barrier** $E_{\text{corr}}$ of the substrate:

$$F_{\text{tip, lateral}} > F_{\text{corrugation}} = \frac{2 \pi E_{\text{corr}}}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is the surface lattice periodicity (~0.25 nm for Cu(111)).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **THE HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS — MEMORIZE FOR PART A & PART B**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $I_t \propto V_b \cdot \exp\!\left(-\kappa \sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)$ | **Bardeen tunneling current** (most important STM equation) | A (amperes) |
| 2 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 m_e \phi}}{\hbar}$ | Vacuum decay constant of the electron wavefunction | $\text{nm}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $I_t(d) = I_0 \exp\!\bigl(-2 \kappa d\bigr)$ | Exponential dependence of current on gap (10% change → 1 Å change) | A |
| 4 | $F_z = -\dfrac{\partial U_{\text{tip-sample}}}{\partial z}$ | Vertical force on an adatom (used in pulling) | N |
| 5 | $E_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{1}{2} k \lambda^2$ | Substrate corrugation energy (sliding barrier) | eV |
| 6 | $P_{\text{tunnel}} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2}{\hbar}\int_{0}^{d}\!\sqrt{2 m_e (U(x)-E)}\, dx\right)$ | General WKB tunneling probability | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta f = -\dfrac{f_0}{2 k_0} \dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$ | Frequency-shift (used in combined AFM/STM mode) | Hz |
| 8 | $z_{\text{feedback}} = z_0 + \Delta z = z_0 + \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{\text{set}}}{I_0}\right)$ | Piezo correction from constant-current feedback | nm |
| 9 | $E_{\text{bias}} = e V_b$ | Energy of tunneling electrons | eV |
| 10 | $F_{\text{gradient}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z}\, V_b^{\,2}$ | Electrostatic pulling force on a polarizable adatom | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTE ON NOTATION:** In all KTU examinations, the symbol $\phi$ refers to the **average work function** in **eV** of the tip–sample junction. Always convert to Joules ($1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$) before substituting into equation (2).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

STM-based atomic manipulation is **not** a laboratory curiosity — it has matured into the foundational test-bed for:

- **Quantum corrals** (e.g., the 48-Fe-atom "Quantum Mirage" by Manoharan, IBM 2000) used to project Kondo resonance wavefunctions.
- **Single-atom transistors** and **single-molecule diodes**.
- **Deterministic doping** of semiconductor heterostructures (one-atom-at-a-time phosphorus placement in Si — *the "21-silicon-atom" project*, UNSW, 2012).
- **Prototype atomic-scale memory** (1 atom = 1 bit, density $\sim 250\ \text{Tbit/inch}^2$).
- **Calibration standard** for AFM, TEM, and other scanning-probe tools.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the *imaging* mode (where $I_t$ is kept constant and the tip follows the surface) with the *manipulation* mode (where the tip–sample gap is *deliberately reduced* so that tip–adatom forces dominate). Always state the *gap-distance regime* when answering KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Tunneling Current Decay Constant $\kappa$

This is the most heavily tested derivation in Module 4. We derive the exponential decay constant that controls the tunneling current.

**Step 1 — Start with the time-independent Schrödinger equation** for an electron in a 1D rectangular barrier of height $U_0 = \phi$ (the work function) and width $d$ (the tip–sample gap):

$$-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2 m_e} \frac{d^{2} \psi}{d x^{2}} + \phi \cdot \psi = E \psi$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange** the equation to isolate the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^{2} \psi}{d x^{2}} = \frac{2 m_e (\phi - E)}{\hbar^{2}} \psi$$

**Step 3 — Define the decay constant** $\kappa$ by analogy with the standard "inside-barrier" wavefunction form $\psi(x) \propto e^{-\kappa x}$:

$$\kappa^{2} = \frac{2 m_e (\phi - E)}{\hbar^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Use the approximation $E \ll \phi$** (since the electron is in the Fermi sea, $E \approx E_F$ which is much less than the surface barrier $\phi \approx 4\text{–}5\ \text{eV}$). The standard textbook form is therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\kappa \;\approx\; \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e \phi}}{\hbar}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution** to find a typical value of $\kappa$ in $\text{nm}^{-1}$:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg} \times 4\ \text{eV} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J/eV}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}}$$

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 6.408 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{1.167 \times 10^{-48}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}} = \frac{1.080 \times 10^{-24}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\kappa \;\approx\; 1.025 \times 10^{10}\ \text{m}^{-1} \;=\; 10.25\ \text{nm}^{-1}\;}$$

**Engineering takeaway:** A change in gap of just **0.1 nm** (one Ångström) changes the tunneling current by a factor of $e^{-2 \times 10.25 \times 0.1} = e^{-2.05} \approx 0.13$ — i.e., the current *falls to 13%* of its original value. This extreme sensitivity is *exactly* why STM has sub-Ångström vertical resolution.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Why the Tunneling Current is Exponential in Gap

Starting from the Bardeen transfer-Hamiltonian approach, the tunneling current is proportional to the matrix element of the tunneling transition, which in turn depends on the *overlap* of the tip and sample wavefunctions inside the barrier.

The tip's wavefunction at the sample surface is:

$$\psi_{\text{tip}}(d) \propto e^{-\kappa d}$$

The matrix element squared gives the rate of electron transfer, hence:

$$I_t \;\propto\; \vert \psi_{\text{tip}}(d) \vert^{2} \;\propto\; e^{-2 \kappa d}$$

Including the bias voltage $V_b$ (which controls the density of available states in the bias window), the full result is:

$$\boxed{\;I_t \;=\; I_0 \, V_b \, \exp\!\left(-\kappa \sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)\;}$$

**KTU Exam Note:** Some textbooks write the exponent as $-2 \kappa d$ (absorbing the factor 2 into the constant). Both forms are correct *provided* the definition of $\kappa$ is consistent. **Always define $\kappa$ first** in your answer to avoid losing a mark.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Force Condition for Sliding Manipulation

The substrate presents a periodic potential to the adatom. Taking the simplest 1D sinusoidal form:

$$U_{\text{sub}}(x) = -E_{\text{corr}} \cos\!\left(\frac{2 \pi x}{\lambda}\right)$$

The maximum slope of this potential — and therefore the maximum *static friction* force holding the adatom in place — is:

$$F_{\text{static}} = \left|\frac{d U_{\text{sub}}}{d x}\right|_{\max} = \frac{2 \pi E_{\text{corr}}}{\lambda}$$

For the adatom to slide, the *lateral component* of the tip–adatom force must exceed this value:

$$\boxed{\;F_{\text{tip, lateral}} \;>\; \frac{2 \pi E_{\text{corr}}}{\lambda}\;}$$

For Xe on Ni(110), $E_{\text{corr}} \approx 4\ \text{meV}$ and $\lambda \approx 0.25\ \text{nm}$, giving $F_{\text{static}} \approx 100\ \text{pN}$ — which is exactly the order of magnitude Eigler measured experimentally.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Tip Retraction During Pulling Mode

In *vertical* (pulling) manipulation, the tip hovers over an adatom at height $z$ and applies a vertical force $F_z$. Equilibrium is achieved when:

$$F_{\text{tip-adatom}}(z) \;=\; F_{\text{adatom-substrate}}(z)$$

Assuming a Lennard-Jones type interaction $F = -A/z^{n}$, with $n \approx 7$ for metallic tips, the *pull-off* distance $z_{\text{off}}$ is given by:

$$z_{\text{off}} = \left(\frac{A_{\text{tip}}}{A_{\text{sub}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-1}}$$

If $A_{\text{tip}} > A_{\text{sub}}$ (i.e., the tip–adatom adhesion is stronger than the substrate–adatom adhesion), the adatom transfers to the tip. The tip can then move to a new location, the bias voltage can be reversed (or a voltage pulse applied), and the adatom is *dropped* back onto the substrate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full STM Manipulation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply UHV Chamber] --> B[Tip Holder W or PtIr Wire]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Tube Scanner XYZ]
    C --> D[Tip Apex Single Atom]
    D --> E[Vacuum Gap 0.3 to 1.0 nm]
    E --> F[Conducting Substrate Cu 111 or Au 111]
    F --> G[Adatom Xe or Fe or CO]
    G --> H[Tunneling Current pA to nA]
    H --> I[Current Amplifier Femtoamp Range]
    I --> J[Feedback Controller PID Loop]
    J --> K[Z Piezo Voltage]
    K --> C
    J --> L[Topographic Image 2D Map]
    M[Operator Console] --> N[Mode Selector Imaging or Manipulation]
    N --> C
    N --> O[Bias Voltage Source 0.001 to 4 V]
    O --> B
    O --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting a Manipulation Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Target Adatom] --> B{What is the Goal}
    B -- Reposition on Same Substrate --> C[Sliding Mode]
    B -- Pick up and Drop Elsewhere --> D[Pulling Mode]
    B -- Quick Displacement of Light Atom --> E[Pushing Mode]
    B -- Modify Surface Chemistry --> F[Field Evaporation]
    C --> G[Reduce Gap to 0.3 to 0.5 nm]
    D --> H[Reduce Gap to 0.2 nm Apply +ve Bias]
    E --> I[Maintain Imaging Gap Apply Lateral Pulse]
    F --> J[Apply Voltage Pulse 3 to 5 V]
    G --> K[Drag Tip Along Adatom Path]
    H --> L[Retract Tip Adatom Attached]
    I --> M[Verify with New Scan]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Energy-Landscape Schematic — Sliding Regime

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Without Tip Adatom Trapped] --> A1[Deep Potential Well]
    A1 --> A2[Barrier Ecor ~ 4 meV]
    A2 --> A3[Adatom Vibrates in Well]
    B[With Tip Close to Adatom] --> B1[Tilted PES]
    B1 --> B2[Barrier Reduced by Delta E]
    B2 --> B3[Lateral Force Exceeds Barrier]
    B3 --> C[Adatom Slides]
    C --> D[New Lattice Site Reached]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of the Eigler "IBM" Logo Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Picture a flat Ni(110) surface viewed from above. Thirty-five xenon atoms are arranged in the letters **I**, **B**, and **M**. The Xe atoms appear as *bright, evenly spaced bumps* in the STM image because they sit *on top* of the Ni substrate and have a higher local density of states at the Fermi level. The Ni substrate lattice is invisible to the eye but is the *atomic template* along which the Xe atoms were dragged.

| Step | Action | Tip Height | Current |
|------|--------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Cool Ni(110) to 4 K | — | — |
| 2 | Image surface at low current (~10 pA) | 0.5 nm | 10 pA |
| 3 | Move tip over a Xe atom | 0.5 nm | 10 pA |
| 4 | Lower tip to bring Xe–tip into contact | 0.2 nm | ~10 nA |
| 5 | Drag tip laterally along $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction | 0.2 nm | ~10 nA |
| 6 | Retract tip to imaging height | 0.5 nm | 10 pA |
| 7 | Re-image to verify new position | 0.5 nm | 10 pA |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A carries **3 marks each** (short, no choice, ~50 words), and Part B carries **14 marks each** with internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Bloom's levels for this module are **L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "tunneling current" in the context of STM. Why is it exponentially sensitive to the tip–sample gap distance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **tunneling current** is the *quantum mechanical* flow of electrons across the vacuum gap between the conductive tip and the conductive sample when a small bias voltage is applied. It is given by the Bardeen equation:

$$I_t = I_0 \, V_b \exp\!\left(-\kappa \sqrt{\phi}\, d\right)$$

Because the exponent contains the gap $d$ multiplied by the decay constant $\kappa \approx 10\ \text{nm}^{-1}$, even a *0.1 nm* change in $d$ changes $I_t$ by nearly an order of magnitude. This extreme sensitivity is what gives STM its **sub-Ångström vertical resolution**. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between the *sliding* and *pulling* modes of STM-based atomic manipulation. State one classic experiment for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Sliding Mode | Pulling Mode |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Direction of motion | Lateral (xy) | Vertical (z) |
| Tip–adatom interaction | Transient *chemical bond* | Stronger *adhesion* than substrate |
| Tip–sample gap | 0.3 – 0.5 nm | 0.2 nm |
| Classic example | **Xe on Ni(110)** — Eigler, 1990 | **Cu on Cu(111)** — Bartels et al. |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a Scanning Tunneling Microscope. Discuss the role of the piezoelectric scanner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the time-independent Schrödinger equation for a 1D rectangular barrier of height $\phi$ and width $d$, derive the expression for the **tunneling current** $I_t$ as a function of the gap distance $d$ and the bias voltage $V_b$. Compute the change in $I_t$ when $d$ is increased from 0.5 nm to 0.6 nm for $\phi = 4.5\ \text{eV}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Principle (2 marks):** An STM operates on the principle of **quantum mechanical tunneling** of electrons between a *sharp conducting tip* (W or Pt–Ir) and a *conducting sample* separated by a thin vacuum gap ($d \approx 0.3$–$1.0$ nm). A bias voltage $V_b$ (typically 0.001 V to 4 V) is applied between tip and sample.

2. **Piezoelectric Scanner (2 marks):** The tip is mounted on a hollow cylindrical tube of **lead zirconate titanate (PZT)**. Applying voltages $V_x$, $V_y$, $V_z$ to its inner/outer electrodes causes Ångström-level expansion or contraction, providing *three-dimensional sub-nanometric positioning*. The same scanner is used to raster-scan the tip across the surface (imaging) and to move the tip to a chosen adatom (manipulation).

3. **Two Operating Modes (1.5 marks):** *Constant-height mode* — tip scanned at fixed $z$, $I_t$ recorded → fast but risk of tip crash. *Constant-current mode* — feedback loop adjusts $z$ to keep $I_t$ constant, $z$-correction is the topographic signal → safer, standard for atomic-scale imaging.

4. **Imaging vs Manipulation Phases (1.5 marks):** During imaging, the gap is *large* (~0.5 nm), so tip–surface forces are negligible. During manipulation, the gap is *reduced* (~0.2–0.3 nm) so that tip–adatom forces dominate.

**Total = 7 marks**

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Schrödinger equation inside the barrier** (1 mark):

$$-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2 m_e} \frac{d^{2} \psi}{d x^{2}} + \phi \psi = E \psi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^{2} \psi}{d x^{2}} = \frac{2 m_e (\phi - E)}{\hbar^{2}} \psi$$

**Step 2 — Define decay constant** (1 mark):

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e \phi}}{\hbar}$$

**Step 3 — Wavefunction inside barrier** (0.5 marks):

$$\psi(x) = \psi_0 \, e^{-\kappa x}$$

**Step 4 — Tunneling probability** (1 mark):

$$P \propto \vert \psi(d) \vert^{2} \propto e^{-2 \kappa d}$$

**Step 5 — Bardeen tunneling current** (1 mark):

$$I_t = I_0 \, V_b \, e^{-2 \kappa d}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation of $\kappa$** (0.5 marks):

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}} = \frac{1.146 \times 10^{-24}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}} = 1.088 \times 10^{10}\ \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the current ratio** $I(d=0.6)/I(d=0.5)$ (1.5 marks):

$$\frac{I(0.6)}{I(0.5)} = \frac{e^{-2 \kappa \cdot 0.6}}{e^{-2 \kappa \cdot 0.5}} = e^{-2 \kappa (0.6 - 0.5)} = e^{-2 \times 1.088 \times 10^{10} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= e^{-2.176} = 0.1135$$

**Conclusion:** The current **falls to ~11.35%** of its original value when the gap is increased by just 0.1 nm. **(0.5 marks)**

**Total = 7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Sample]**

**(a)** Explain the four canonical modes of STM-based nanomanipulation with suitable diagrams. State the typical tip–sample distance and dominant force for each mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the famous *Eigler IBM-logo experiment* step by step. What were the operating conditions (substrate, adsorbate, temperature, gap, current)? Why was Ni(110) chosen as the substrate? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The four canonical modes are:

1. **Sliding (Lateral) Manipulation (2 marks):** The tip apex forms a *transient chemical bond* with the adatom and drags it across the surface. Gap = **0.3 – 0.5 nm**. Dominant force = **chemical binding force**. Example: Xe on Ni(110).

2. **Pulling (Vertical) Manipulation (2 marks):** The tip is brought very close (gap ≈ **0.2 nm**), the tip–adatom adhesion exceeds the substrate–adatom adhesion, and the adatom is *lifted* off the substrate. The tip is then translated, and a voltage pulse releases the adatom. Dominant force = **van der Waals / chemical adhesion**. Example: Cu on Cu(111).

3. **Pushing (Repulsive) Manipulation (1.5 marks):** The tip is held at imaging height (gap ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 nm**) but moved laterally near the adatom. The strong *Pauli repulsion* kicks the adatom forward. Dominant force = **Pauli / electrostatic repulsion**. Example: Pb on Cu(211).

4. **Field-Evaporation (1.5 marks):** A high voltage pulse (3 – 5 V) creates a strong electric field that *ejects* the adatom from the surface or from the tip. Dominant force = **electrostatic field gradient**. Example: Selective removal of atoms.

**Total = 7 marks**

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

1. **Background (1 mark):** Don Eigler and Erhard Schweizer of IBM Almaden, in **1990**, demonstrated the *first* deterministic positioning of individual atoms at room temperature *in vacuum*. They used 35 **xenon (Xe) atoms** to spell out the **"IBM"** logo.

2. **Operating conditions (1 mark):**
   - Substrate: **Ni(110) single crystal**
   - Adsorbate: **Xe** (noble gas, no chemistry with Ni at 4 K)
   - Temperature: **4 K** (suppresses thermal diffusion)
   - Tunneling current (imaging): **~10 pA**
   - Manipulation current: **~10 nA**
   - Tip material: **W** electrochemically etched

3. **Step-by-step procedure (3 marks):**
   - (i) Cool Ni(110) to 4 K in UHV.
   - (ii) Deposit a low density of Xe atoms on the surface.
   - (iii) Image to locate each Xe atom.
   - (iv) Position tip over a chosen Xe atom; reduce gap to 0.2 nm.
   - (v) Drag the tip along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction; the Xe atom follows by sliding.
   - (vi) At the desired lattice site, retract the tip to imaging height.
   - (vii) Repeat 35 times to form the letters.

4. **Why Ni(110)? (2 marks):**
   - (i) The (110) surface is **highly anisotropic** — it has deep, parallel troughs along the $\langle 1\bar{1}0 \rangle$ direction, which *confine* the Xe atoms to 1D motion and prevent them from wandering in unwanted directions.
   - (ii) The Xe–Ni corrugation is *just right* — strong enough to hold the atom at 4 K, but weak enough to be overcome by the tip's lateral force.

**Total = 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing *imaging* mode with *manipulation* mode:** Many students forget to state that the tip must be *lowered* (gap reduced) before manipulation. If your answer only describes imaging, expect a **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\phi$ from eV to Joules** in numerical problems. This is a **1-mark loss** for unit inconsistency.
> 3. **Skipping the intermediate derivation steps** in the $\kappa$ derivation. The examiner wants to see the substitution explicitly — not just the final boxed formula. **Up to 1.5 marks lost**.
> 4. **Writing "STM works by atomic force"** — wrong. STM uses *tunneling current*, not force. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) uses force.
> 5. **Forgetting UHV** when listing the requirements for atomic manipulation. **0.5 – 1 mark lost** in long-answer questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and review 30 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] STM = *Scanning Tunneling Microscope*; based on **quantum mechanical tunneling**.
- [ ] Tunneling current equation: $I_t = I_0 V_b \exp(-\kappa \sqrt{\phi}\, d)$ — *the* most important formula.
- [ ] Decay constant: $\kappa = \sqrt{2 m_e \phi} / \hbar \approx 10\ \text{nm}^{-1}$ for $\phi \approx 4$ eV.
- [ ] A 0.1 nm increase in $d$ → current falls to ~13% of its value.
- [ ] Tip = etched W or Pt–Ir wire; one atom at the apex dominates the tunneling.
- [ ] Scanner = **piezoelectric tube (PZT)** providing sub-Ångström XYZ motion.
- [ ] Operating conditions: **UHV** ($<10^{-10}$ Torr), often **4 K** (to freeze adatoms).
- [ ] **Four manipulation modes:** Sliding, Pulling, Pushing, Field-Evaporation.
- [ ] Sliding condition: $F_{\text{tip,lateral}} > 2\pi E_{\text{corr}} / \lambda$.
- [ ] **Eigler (1990):** 35 Xe atoms on Ni(110) → "IBM" logo → birth of atomic-scale manipulation.
- [ ] **Quantum Corral (Crommie, Eigler 1993):** 48 Fe atoms on Cu(111) → standing-wave pattern of surface-state electrons.
- [ ] Distinguish: *imaging mode* (gap ≈ 0.5 nm, low current) vs *manipulation mode* (gap ≈ 0.2 nm, high current).
- [ ] STM = topographical + electronic structure (DOS) probe; **conducting samples only**.
- [ ] For non-conducting samples, use **AFM (atomic force microscopy)** — *not* STM.
- [ ] Applications: quantum corrals, single-atom transistors, atomic memory (~250 Tbit/in²), single-molecule chemistry.
- [ ] Limitations: extremely slow (atoms-per-minute throughput); requires UHV + cryogenic temperatures; conducting samples only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on STM manipulation, *always* draw (or describe) a labeled schematic, state the operating conditions, write the Bardeen current equation, and mention the exponential gap sensitivity. These four elements together typically account for **8 – 9 of the 14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Nanolithography** is the branch of nanofabrication that deals with the design, patterning, and replication of geometric features on a substrate at the **nanometer scale** (typically below 100 nm). It is the foundational technology that enables the mass production of integrated circuits (ICs), microelectromechanical systems (MEMS), nanophotonic devices, and modern biosensors.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, nanolithography encompasses the ensemble of top-down patterning techniques where a controlled beam, mask, or physical template is used to define nanoscale features in a resist or directly on a substrate. The essential workflow involves three pillars: **exposure**, **development**, and **pattern transfer** (etching or lift-off).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Nanolithography covers the resolution limits of conventional photolithography and introduces advanced techniques such as **Electron Beam Lithography (EBL)**, **Focused Ion Beam (FIB)**, **Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL)**, **Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)**, and **Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a master **watchmaker** in Geneva, trying to engrave intricate patterns onto the **face of a wristwatch** — but instead of working on a 3 cm dial, you must carve features 10,000 times smaller than the width of a human hair. Your traditional chisel and hammer are useless here; you need either a **super-sharp stylus** (like a scanning probe), a **focused electron gun** (like an electron beam), or a **pre-made rubber stamp** that physically presses the pattern into the surface (nanoimprint).

Nanolithography is precisely this art of "writing" at the nanoscale. Just as a journalist uses different printing techniques (inkjet, offset, laser engraving) depending on the resolution and volume needed, a nanofabrication engineer chooses between photolithography, e-beam, ion beam, or mechanical stamping based on the resolution required, the throughput desired, and the material being patterned.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Constraints Trade-off (KTU 2024 Focus):**
> 1. **Resolution** — how small a feature can be made (nm scale)
> 2. **Throughput** — how many wafers per hour
> 3. **Cost per feature** — capital and operating expense
>
> Photolithography = high throughput, moderate resolution. E-beam = ultra-high resolution, low throughput. NIL = high throughput, high resolution, low cost.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (KTU Standard Constants)

- **Diffraction-limited wavelength (λ):** Used in optical lithography. For deep ultraviolet (DUV), λ = **193 nm** (ArF excimer laser). For extreme ultraviolet (EUV), λ = **13.5 nm**.
- **Resist sensitivity:** measured in **µC/cm²** (for e-beam) or **mJ/cm²** (for photolithography).
- **Standard resolution benchmark:** **λ/2** for a perfect lens (Abbe limit).
- **Boltzmann constant k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** — relevant for thermal effects in resist baking.
- **Electron rest mass m_e = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg** — used in EBL beam energy calculations.
- **Planck constant h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** — governs photon energy in photolithography.

> [!NOTE]
> The diffraction limit of light is the fundamental physical barrier that drove the semiconductor industry to abandon conventional optical lithography for shorter wavelengths (EUV) and alternative non-optical methods (EBL, NIL).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Nanolithographic Techniques

Nanolithography is broadly classified into two paradigms:

#### A. Radiation-Based (Masked / Maskless)
- **Photolithography (Optical):** Uses UV light through a photomask to expose a photosensitive resist. Resolution limited by diffraction.
- **Extreme Ultraviolet Lithography (EUV):** Uses 13.5 nm wavelength, reflection-based optics, and a reflective mask.
- **X-ray Lithography:** Uses synchrotron X-rays (λ ≈ 0.4–2 nm), capable of very high resolution but expensive.
- **Electron Beam Lithography (EBL):** Focused beam of electrons, sub-10 nm resolution, maskless direct-write.
- **Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Lithography:** Uses Ga⁺ or He⁺ ions for ultra-fine direct writing and milling.

#### B. Mechanical / Tip-Based / Template
- **Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL):** Physical stamping of a pre-patterned mold into a resist. Sub-10 nm features possible.
- **Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL):** Uses an AFM/STM tip to mechanically, thermally, or electrically pattern surfaces.
- **Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN):** AFM tip "writes" molecules (e.g., thiols) onto a substrate via a water meniscus.

### 2.2 The Physics of Resolution: Step-by-Step Logic

The **Why** behind resolution limits and the **How** of pushing them defines nanolithography:

1. **Diffraction Sets the Floor:** Light cannot be focused to a spot smaller than ~λ/2 due to the wave nature of electromagnetic radiation (Abbe's diffraction limit).
2. **Numerical Aperture Amplifies Resolution:** A higher NA lens collects light at wider angles, tightening the focus.
3. **Shorter Wavelengths Break the Barrier:** Reducing λ to EUV (13.5 nm) or using electron beams (de Broglie λ ≈ 0.005 nm) allows sub-10 nm features.
4. **Resist Chemistry Converts Energy to Pattern:** The exposed regions undergo chain scission (positive resist) or cross-linking (negative resist), changing solubility in the developer.
5. **Pattern Transfer Etches the Substrate:** Plasma or wet etching transfers the resist pattern into the underlying material.
6. **Non-Optical Methods Bypass Diffraction Entirely:** NIL, DPN, and SPL use physical contact, eliminating the wave-optics limit.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abbe Diffraction Limit** | $R = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA}$ | R = resolution (nm), λ = wavelength (nm), NA = numerical aperture (unitless) | Minimum resolvable feature in optical lithography |
| **Rayleigh Criterion** | $R = 0.61 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | Same as above | More conservative resolution estimate |
| **Numerical Aperture** | $NA = n \sin \theta$ | n = refractive index, θ = half-angle of max cone | Lens light-gathering ability |
| **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | $DOF = \pm \, \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA^2}$ | DOF (nm), λ (nm), NA (unitless) | Tolerable wafer topography |
| **Photoresist Exposure Dose** | $D = \dfrac{E}{A}$ | D = dose (mJ/cm²), E = energy (mJ), A = area (cm²) | Required energy density to expose resist |
| **Photon Energy** | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | E (J), h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3×10⁸ m/s, λ (m) | UV/EUV photon energy |
| **Electron De Broglie Wavelength** | $\lambda_e = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$ | λ_e (m), V = accelerating voltage (V), e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C | EBL resolution foundation |
| **Proximity Effect (EBL)** | $\beta(x) = \dfrac{1}{\pi(1+\eta)} \left[ \dfrac{1}{\alpha^2} \exp(-x^2/\alpha^2) + \dfrac{\eta}{\beta_f^2} \exp(-x^2/\beta_f^2) \right]$ | α, β_f = scattering ranges, η = backscatter ratio | Electron dose distribution correction |
| **NIL Mold Fill Time** | $t_{fill} \propto \dfrac{\eta \, L^2}{\gamma \, P}$ | η = viscosity, L = feature size, γ = surface tension, P = pressure | Stamp penetration time |
| **DPN Diffusion Rate** | $D = D_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | D_0 = prefactor, E_a = activation energy, k_B = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K, T = temperature (K) | Molecular transport in DPN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Abbe and Rayleigh formulas are not just academic — they directly determine the **minimum transistor gate length** achievable in a semiconductor fab. When Intel moved from 193 nm DUV to EUV (13.5 nm), they were effectively reducing λ to enable **5 nm and 3 nm process nodes**.

### 2.4 Sub-Diffraction Techniques (Optical)

To extend photolithography beyond the diffraction limit, the industry uses:
- **Immersion Lithography:** Replacing the air gap between lens and wafer with water (n = 1.44 at 193 nm), increasing effective NA to ~1.35.
- **Phase-Shifting Masks (PSM):** Inverting the phase of light in adjacent apertures to create destructive interference in dark regions, sharpening edges.
- **Optical Proximity Correction (OPC):** Pre-distorting mask features to compensate for diffraction blur.
- **Double Patterning (DP):** Printing two sub-resolution patterns offset to double the effective line density.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resolution Limit from the Rayleigh Criterion

The Rayleigh criterion defines the minimum separation at which two point sources can be resolved. We derive it from the diffraction of a circular aperture.

**Step 1: Airy Disk Formation**
A lens of finite diameter produces a far-field diffraction pattern characterized by an Airy disk. The first minimum of the Airy pattern occurs at the angle $\theta$ where the path difference between rays from the edge and center of the aperture equals one wavelength.

This yields the angular position of the first dark ring:

$$
\theta_1 = 1.22 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{D}
$$

where **D** is the lens aperture diameter and **λ** is the wavelength.

**Step 2: Convert Angular Resolution to Linear Resolution**
At the focal plane of the lens (focal length f), the linear radius of the Airy disk is:

$$
r = f \tan \theta_1 \approx f \, \theta_1 = 1.22 \, \dfrac{\lambda f}{D}
$$

Recognizing that the numerical aperture is defined as $NA = n \sin \theta \approx n \, (D/2f)$ for small angles, we obtain $D/f = 2 NA / n$. For air (n ≈ 1), this becomes $D/f = 2 NA$.

**Step 3: Substitute into the Linear Resolution**
Inserting the relation $D/f = 2 NA$ into the expression for r:

$$
r = 1.22 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA} = 0.61 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

This is the **Rayleigh resolution limit** — the radius of the smallest spot that can be focused by a perfect lens.

**Step 4: Define Resolution as the Full Feature Size**
In lithography, "resolution" typically refers to the **line spacing** between two resolvable lines, not the spot radius. The minimum line pitch equals the Airy disk diameter, giving:

$$
R_{Rayleigh} = 2r = 1.22 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

**Step 5: Abbe's More Liberal Estimate**
Ernst Abbe proposed a slightly less stringent criterion based on the first-order diffraction condition:

$$
R_{Abbe} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA}
$$

This is the formula most commonly used in KTU problems because it gives a more optimistic (and pedagogically simpler) resolution bound.

**Step 6: Apply to Modern Lithography**
For EUV at λ = 13.5 nm with NA = 0.33 (first-gen EUV tools):

$$
R_{Abbe} = \dfrac{13.5 \text{ nm}}{2 \times 0.33} = \dfrac{13.5}{0.66} \approx 20.5 \text{ nm}
$$

For high-NA EUV (NA = 0.55):

$$
R_{Abbe} = \dfrac{13.5}{2 \times 0.55} = \dfrac{13.5}{1.10} \approx 12.3 \text{ nm}
$$

This explains why **High-NA EUV** is being adopted for sub-2 nm logic nodes.

### 3.2 Derivation: Electron De Broglie Wavelength in EBL

**Step 1: Relativistic Energy Consideration**
For electrons accelerated through a typical EBL voltage of 30–100 kV, the velocity becomes a significant fraction of the speed of light, so we must use relativistic mechanics.

The total energy is:

$$
E_{total} = eV + m_e c^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply the De Broglie Relation**
The momentum p is related to the de Broglie wavelength by:

$$
\lambda_e = \dfrac{h}{p}
$$

From special relativity:

$$
p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values for V = 100 kV**
With eV = 1.6×10⁻¹⁴ J, m_e c² = 8.187×10⁻¹⁴ J, the relativistic correction term is:

$$
\dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 8.187 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 0.0977
$$

So:

$$
p = \sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-14} \times 1.0977} \approx \sqrt{3.20 \times 10^{-44}} \approx 1.79 \times 10^{-22} \text{ kg·m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute λ_e**

$$
\lambda_e = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.79 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 3.70 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 3.70 \text{ pm} = 0.00370 \text{ nm}
$$

This wavelength is **~3000× smaller** than the EUV photon wavelength, theoretically allowing sub-nanometer resolution. In practice, electron scattering in the resist limits EBL resolution to ~5–10 nm.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Resolution & Throughput

| Technique | Resolution (nm) | Throughput | Cost | Mask Required? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| i-line Photolithography | 350–500 | Very High | Low | Yes |
| DUV (193 nm) | 38–40 | High | High | Yes |
| DUV + Immersion | 28–32 | High | Very High | Yes |
| EUV (13.5 nm) | 13–20 | High | Very High | Yes (reflective) |
| Electron Beam (EBL) | 5–10 | Very Low | Moderate | No (direct-write) |
| Focused Ion Beam (FIB) | 5–20 | Very Low | High | No (direct-write) |
| Nanoimprint (NIL) | 5–20 | Very High | Moderate | Yes (mold) |
| Dip-Pen (DPN) | 5–15 | Very Low | Moderate | No |
| X-ray (Synchrotron) | 10–20 | Low | Very High | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Practice:** No single technique dominates all three axes (resolution, throughput, cost). Modern fabs use a **mix-and-match strategy**: EUV for critical layers, DUV for non-critical layers, NIL for photonic and memory devices, and EBL for photomask fabrication and R&D.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Nanolithography Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Substrate Prep - Si Wafer Cleaning] --> B[Step 2: Resist Spin-Coat - nm-thin polymer film]
    B --> C{Step 3: Exposure Method?}
    C --> D[Optical / EUV - Mask + Light]
    C --> E[EBL / FIB - Direct-write beam]
    C --> F[NIL - Physical stamp press]
    C --> G[DPN / SPL - AFM tip write]
    D --> H[Step 4: Post-Exposure Bake]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 5: Development - Dissolve exposed regions]
    I --> J[Step 6: Pattern Transfer - Etch or Deposit]
    J --> K[Step 7: Resist Strip - Final pattern on substrate]
    K --> L[Final: Nanoscale Device or IC Layer]
```

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram of Nanolithography Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAD[Radiation-Based]
        A1[Photolithography]
        A2[EUV Lithography]
        A3[Electron Beam EBL]
        A4[Focused Ion Beam FIB]
    end
    subgraph MEC[Mechanical / Template]
        B1[Nanoimprint NIL]
        B2[Step-and-Flash Imprint]
    end
    subgraph TIP[Tip-Based]
        C1[Scanning Probe SPL]
        C2[Dip-Pen DPN]
    end
    A1 --> RES{Resolution and Throughput Trade-off}
    A2 --> RES
    A3 --> RES
    A4 --> RES
    B1 --> RES
    B2 --> RES
    C1 --> RES
    C2 --> RES
    RES --> OUT1[High Resolution, Low Throughput - EBL, FIB, DPN]
    RES --> OUT2[Moderate Resolution, High Throughput - DUV, EUV, NIL]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: EBL System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Electron Source - Schottky FE or LaB6] --> A2[Condenser Lens System]
    A2 --> A3[Beam Aperture - 10-100 um]
    A3 --> A4[Beam Blanker - Electrostatic Deflector]
    A4 --> A5[Objective Lens - Final Focusing]
    A5 --> A6[Beam Deflection Coils - XY Scan]
    A6 --> A7[Stage with Wafer - Vacuum Chamber]
    A7 --> A8[Electron-Sensitive Resist on Substrate]
    A8 --> A9[Pattern Defined by Scanned Beam]
    A9 --> A10[Controller Computer - Pattern Data]
    A10 -.controls.-> A6
    A10 -.controls.-> A4
    B1[Vacuum System 10 to 6 Torr] -.environment.-> A7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - In **4.1**, every lithographic technique ultimately funnels into a common develop-etch-strip pipeline. The differences are concentrated in the **exposure step**.
> - In **4.2**, the trade-off structure is explicit — there is no "best" technique, only an optimal choice for a given application.
> - In **4.3**, the EBL architecture illustrates why EBL is slow: every pixel is **serially written** by the deflected beam, whereas photolithography exposes the entire field **in parallel**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
Define nanolithography. List any four advanced nanolithographic techniques with a one-line application for each.

**Model Answer:**
Nanolithography is the art and science of fabricating patterns with feature dimensions on the order of nanometers (typically < 100 nm) on a substrate, using radiation, mechanical stamping, or scanning probes.

| Technique | One-Line Application |
|---|---|
| Electron Beam Lithography (EBL) | Photomask fabrication for IC industry |
| Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Lithography | Circuit edit, TEM sample preparation |
| Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL) | High-volume fabrication of photonic crystals |
| Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN) | Patterning biomolecules for biosensors |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four techniques with applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
State the Abbe diffraction limit. If the wavelength of the exposing light is 193 nm and the numerical aperture of the projection lens is 0.75, calculate the minimum resolvable feature size.

**Model Answer:**

Abbe diffraction limit:
$$
R = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
R = \dfrac{193 \text{ nm}}{2 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{193}{1.50} \approx 128.67 \text{ nm}
$$

**Minimum resolvable feature size ≈ 128.7 nm**

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer with unit)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Model)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – Alternative 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of Electron Beam Lithography (EBL). Discuss its advantages and limitations over photolithography. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of EBL:**
A beam of electrons, generated by a Schottky field-emission or LaB₆ source, is accelerated through 30–100 kV and focused by a series of electromagnetic lenses to a spot diameter of ~1–5 nm. The beam is blanked and deflected by electromagnetic coils under computer control to "write" the desired pattern directly onto an electron-sensitive resist (e.g., PMMA, HSQ, ZEP) spin-coated on a substrate. The exposed resist is developed in a suitable solvent, leaving a patterned template for subsequent etching or metallization.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.3 of these notes.

**Advantages over Photolithography:**
1. **Ultra-high resolution** — sub-10 nm features routinely achieved.
2. **Maskless direct-write** — no expensive photomask required; pattern data is purely digital.
3. **High precision and registration** — nanometer alignment accuracy.
4. **Flexibility** — patterns can be changed instantly by editing the CAD file.

**Limitations:**
1. **Very low throughput** — pixels are written serially, taking hours per wafer.
2. **Proximity effect** — forward and backscattered electrons expose regions outside the intended spot, requiring dose correction.
3. **High vacuum** requirement — increases system cost and complexity.
4. **Charging of insulating substrates** — distorts the beam path.

*(Mark distribution: 3 for principle, 2 for diagram, 2 for advantages/limitations)*

---

**(b)** Derive the Abbe diffraction-limited resolution for a photolithography system. Given λ = 248 nm (KrF laser), n = 1.0, and NA = 0.65, calculate the resolution. If the same system is upgraded to immersion lithography with n = 1.44, what is the new resolution? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**
The Abbe criterion emerges from the requirement that at least one diffraction order from a periodic grating must be captured by the lens. For a grating of period p, the first-order diffraction angle is given by $\sin \theta = \lambda/p$. For the lens to capture this order, the condition $\sin \theta \le NA/n$ must hold, yielding the minimum period (pitch) for imaging:

$$
p_{min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

The half-pitch (minimum line/space) is half of the period:

$$
R_{Abbe} = \dfrac{p_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA}
$$

**Numerical Calculation – Case 1 (Dry, n = 1.0):**

$$
R_1 = \dfrac{248 \text{ nm}}{2 \times 0.65} = \dfrac{248}{1.30} \approx 190.77 \text{ nm}
$$

**Numerical Calculation – Case 2 (Immersion, n = 1.44):**
Immersion effectively increases NA. The formula becomes:

$$
R_2 = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, n \sin \theta_{max}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, NA_{eff}}
$$

With $NA_{eff} = n \sin \theta = 1.44 \times 0.65 = 0.936$:

$$
R_2 = \dfrac{248 \text{ nm}}{2 \times 0.936} = \dfrac{248}{1.872} \approx 132.48 \text{ nm}
$$

**Comment:**
The resolution improved from **~190.8 nm to ~132.5 nm**, a reduction of ~30%. This is achieved **without changing the exposure wavelength** — only by replacing the air gap with a high-refractive-index fluid (typically ultra-pure water at 193 nm). This is why immersion lithography became the workhorse of the 32 nm and 22 nm technology nodes before the advent of EUV.

*(Mark distribution: 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical case 1, 1 for case 2, 1 for comment)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL) with a schematic. Compare its advantages and limitations with photolithography. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of NIL:**
NIL is a mechanical patterning technique where a pre-fabricated **mold** (or "stamp") with nanoscale features is pressed into a soft, thermoplastic resist (or a UV-curable monomer) spin-coated on a substrate. Under controlled temperature (for thermal NIL, ~150–200 °C) or UV exposure (for UV-NIL), the resist conforms to the mold geometry. After separation, the imprinted pattern in the resist serves as an etch mask for subsequent pattern transfer.

**Process Steps:**
1. Mold fabrication (typically by EBL + dry etching of Si or quartz)
2. Resist spin-coating on substrate
3. Mold alignment and pressing
4. Resist curing (thermal or UV)
5. Mold demolding
6. Residual layer etching and pattern transfer

**Advantages over Photolithography:**
1. **Resolution not limited by diffraction** — sub-10 nm features demonstrated.
2. **No expensive optical projection tools** — lower capital cost.
3. **High throughput** — entire wafer imprinted in one step.
4. **Pattern fidelity** — direct 1:1 replication of mold geometry.

**Limitations:**
1. **Mold wear and contamination** — affects yield over many cycles.
2. **Defectivity** — particles cause printing errors.
3. **Overlay accuracy** — challenging for multi-layer alignment.
4. **Residual layer** — must be etched through, adding process complexity.

*(Mark distribution: 3 for principle, 2 for diagram reference, 2 for comparison)*

---

**(b)** What is Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)? Explain its working mechanism. Discuss its applications and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN) is a tip-based, direct-write nanolithographic technique that uses an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) cantilever to deposit molecules onto a substrate with sub-50 nm resolution.

**Working Mechanism:**
1. The AFM tip is "inked" by dipping it into a solution containing the molecules to be deposited (e.g., alkanethiols, proteins, DNA).
2. The inked tip is brought into contact with the substrate (typically Au, Si, or SiO₂).
3. A **water meniscus** naturally forms between the tip and substrate under ambient conditions. The molecules dissolve into this meniscus and **diffuse** from the tip to the substrate, where they chemisorb or physisorb.
4. By raster-scanning the tip (or by using a cantilever array with thousands of tips), arbitrary patterns can be "written" with nanometer precision.
5. The diffusion is governed by Fick's law and is described by an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence:

$$
D = D_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where higher T and lower E_a accelerate the molecular transport.

**Applications:**
- **Biosensor fabrication:** Patterning antibodies or aptamers for label-free detection.
- **Surface functionalization gradients** for cell adhesion studies.
- **Mask repair** in semiconductor industry.
- **Direct write of plasmonic nanostructures** for SERS substrates.
- **2D material patterning** (graphene functionalization).

**Limitations:**
1. **Very low throughput** — serial writing process.
2. **Limited to certain material-substrate combinations** — primarily molecular inks on reactive surfaces.
3. **Tip degradation** — affects reproducibility over long runs.
4. **Pattern size constraint** — practical limit ~50–100 nm line widths.
5. **Environmentally sensitive** — humidity control is critical for meniscus stability.

*(Mark distribution: 2 for definition, 2 for mechanism, 2 for applications, 1 for limitations)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Zones in Nanolithography Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Students often write $R = \lambda/NA$ instead of $R = \lambda/(2\,NA)$. This loses 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing Rayleigh and Abbe criteria:** $R = 0.61\, \lambda/NA$ (Rayleigh) vs. $R = \lambda/(2\,NA)$ (Abbe). The Abbe criterion is what KTU expects unless the question specifies otherwise.
> 3. **Unit mismatches:** Wavelength in nm, NA unitless — mixing SI units loses marks.
> 4. **Diagram without labels:** Mermaid/hand-drawn block diagrams in EBL/NIL answers **must** be labeled (electron source, lenses, blanker, deflector, stage). An unlabeled diagram earns 0–1 marks instead of 2.
> 5. **Immersion lithography pitfall:** When using immersion, students forget to multiply NA by n, getting no resolution improvement in calculation.
> 6. **DPN confusion:** Some students describe DPN as a thermal technique — it is primarily a **diffusion-based, ambient-condition** process. Thermal DPN is a variant, not the default.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Nanolithography = patterning at sub-100 nm scale; foundational to IC and MEMS fabrication.
- **Three constraints:** Resolution, Throughput, Cost — every technique is a trade-off among these.
- **Abbe limit:** $R = \lambda/(2\,NA)$ — the bedrock equation for any optical lithography problem.
- **Rayleigh limit:** $R = 0.61\,\lambda/NA$ — slightly more conservative variant; know both.
- **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = n \sin \theta$; immersion lithography increases n from 1.0 to ~1.44.
- **DOF:** $DOF = \pm \lambda/(2\,NA^2)$ — inversely proportional to NA², so high-NA EUV has very shallow DOF.
- **EUV wavelength:** 13.5 nm (Mo/Si multilayer mirrors, reflective mask).
- **EBL wavelength:** ~0.004 nm (de Broglie), but practical resolution 5–10 nm due to scattering.
- **NIL:** Mechanical stamping, not diffraction-limited; sub-10 nm demonstrated.
- **DPN:** AFM tip writes molecules via water meniscus; primarily for biosensing and R&D.
- **SPL:** Uses AFM/STM tip — can be mechanical, thermal, or electrical.
- **Proximity effect in EBL:** Forward + backscattered electrons must be corrected by dose modulation.
- **Phase-shifting masks:** Sharpen optical images via destructive interference.
- **Mix-and-match strategy:** EUV for critical layers, DUV for non-critical, NIL for photonics/memory, EBL for masks/R&D.
- **High-NA EUV:** NA = 0.55 enables ~12 nm resolution, used for sub-2 nm logic nodes.
- **Energy of EUV photon:** $E = hc/\lambda \approx 92 \text{ eV}$ at 13.5 nm — ionizing radiation, requires vacuum.
- **Comparison matrix:** Photolithography = parallel/high-throughput; EBL/FIB = serial/high-resolution; NIL = parallel/high-resolution/low-cost.
- **Standard resists:** PMMA, ZEP, HSQ (for EBL); SU-8 (for NIL); S1800, AZ series (for photolithography).
- **Industrial relevance:** Nanolithography is the **enabling technology** of Moore's Law continuation, with EUV NIL, and DPN collectively representing the modern fabrication toolkit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soft Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Soft Lithography** is a collective term for a family of non-photolithographic fabrication techniques that use an **elastomeric stamp, mold, or mask** (most commonly **Polydimethylsiloxane – PDMS**, Sylgard 184) to replicate, transfer, or generate micro- and nano-scale patterns on a substrate. The term was popularized by **Prof. George M. Whitesides (Harvard University, 1993–1998)** and is now considered a cornerstone of low-cost, large-area nanofabrication in the KTU 2024 PECHT753 syllabus.

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies Soft Lithography as a **top-down, contact-based patterning technique** that complements photolithography, electron-beam lithography, and focused-ion-beam lithography. The standard feature-size window taught in this module is **≈ 30 nm to 500 μm**, with a working area typically between **1 cm² and 100 cm² per stamp**.

### Core Operating Principle

The technique fundamentally relies on the **mechanical conformity** of a soft elastomer to a pre-patterned master (fabricated by photolithography, e-beam, or even naturally rough surfaces). The elastomer faithfully captures the inverse topography of the master and then uses one of several mechanisms (inking, molding, capillary filling, imprinting) to replicate the pattern on a target substrate.

> [!NOTE] **Why "Soft"?**
> The qualifier *soft* refers to the **mechanical softness (low Young's modulus)** of the stamp material. For PDMS, **E ≈ 1–3 MPa** (Mega-Pascal), which is roughly **six to seven orders of magnitude lower** than the silicon masters (E ≈ 150 GPa) typically used in photolithography. This compliance is the *enabling property* of the technique.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy — The "Rubber Stamp of Nanotechnology"**
> Imagine a **rubber stamp you might use to mark a passport** — but instead of red ink, it deposits an invisible molecular monolayer; instead of a logo, it prints lines just **a few hundred nanometers wide**; and instead of being rigid wood-handled, it is made of a **gel-like silicone rubber that hugs the page's surface texture**.
> 
> When you press this "stamp" against a gold-coated surface, a self-assembled monolayer of molecules is transferred *only* where the raised pattern touches. Peel the stamp away, and you have a chemically-defined pattern invisible to the eye but visible under an electron microscope. That, in essence, is **Microcontact Printing (μCP)** — the most famous variant of Soft Lithography.
> 
> Other variants (replica molding, micromolding in capillaries) are analogous to **using a silicone ice-cube tray to cast chocolate figures** — the soft mold *shapes* the new material rather than *inks* it.

### Key Material – PDMS at a Glance

| Property | Typical Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (E) | **1.5 – 3.0 MPa** | Allows conformal contact on non-planar surfaces |
| Surface Energy (γ) | **≈ 21 – 24 mJ/m²** | Low → easy demolding without adhesion damage |
| Optical Transparency | > 230 nm – IR | Enables UV curing through the stamp |
| Thermal Stability | –45 °C to +200 °C | Compatible with thermal curing of polymers |
| Mixing Ratio (Base:Curative) | **10 : 1 (wt/wt)** | Determines final stiffness; stiffer when more base added |
| Curing | 65 °C / 4 h OR 25 °C / 48 h | Low-temperature processing |
| Shrinkage on curing | < 1 % | Pattern fidelity is preserved |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master–Stamp–Substrate Workflow

Soft Lithography is best understood as a **three-stage pipeline**. Every variant in this family follows these three logical steps in some form.

1. **Master Fabrication (Step 1)**
   - A "master" with the *positive* (raised) topography is fabricated on a silicon or quartz wafer using **photolithography, e-beam lithography, or even direct laser writing**.
   - The master is chemically passivated with a fluorosilane (e.g., 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltrichlorosilane) to prevent the elastomer from sticking.

2. **Elastomeric Stamp Replication (Step 2)**
   - Liquid PDMS prepolymer (base + curing agent in 10:1 ratio) is **poured over the master**.
   - It is **degassed** under vacuum to remove trapped air bubbles.
   - It is **thermally cured** at 65 °C for 4 hours.
   - The solidified, transparent PDMS is **peeled off**, carrying the *negative* (recessed) replica of the master.

3. **Pattern Transfer (Step 3)** — *the step that distinguishes the variants*
   - The PDMS replica is now used as a **stamp (μCP)**, **mold (REM, μTM)**, **capillary network (MIMIC)**, or **solvent-imprint tool (SAMIM)** to create the final pattern on the substrate.

### 2.2 The Six Sub-Techniques of Soft Lithography

The KTU 2024 PECHT753 Module 4 syllabus explicitly tests the following six sub-techniques. Each has a distinct physical mechanism.

> [!NOTE] **Family Tree of Soft Lithography**
> 
> 1. **Microcontact Printing (μCP)** — *Ink-transfer variant*
>    - The PDMS stamp is "inked" with a self-assembling molecule (e.g., alkanethiol HS-(CH₂)ₙ-X for gold, alkylsilane for SiO₂).
>    - Brought into conformal contact with the substrate; the molecules transfer only at raised contact regions.
>    - Resolution: **~ 30 nm** demonstrated (single alkanethiol SAM on Au).
> 
> 2. **Replica Molding (REM)** — *Direct casting variant*
>    - The PDMS mold is filled with a liquid prepolymer (e.g., UV-curable polyurethane, epoxy, or another PDMS batch).
>    - The polymer is cured, then the PDMS mold is peeled away.
>    - Used to mass-produce copies of the master — a "**negative working**" technique.
> 
> 3. **Microtransfer Molding (μTM)** — *Detached-film variant*
>    - The PDMS mold cavities are filled with a prepolymer; a flat PDMS "lid" is placed on top to seal and shape the film.
>    - Cured; the thin polymer film is transferred to a new substrate.
>    - Allows patterning of **non-planar or fragile substrates**.
> 
> 4. **Micromolding in Capillaries (MIMIC)** — *Capillary-driven variant*
>    - The PDMS mold is placed on the substrate so that its recessed channels form a network of *empty micro-channels* in contact with the substrate.
>    - A drop of low-viscosity prepolymer is placed at the channel mouth; **capillary action** spontaneously fills the network.
>    - The polymer is cured *in situ*; the PDMS is then removed.
>    - The substrate retains a polymer replica of the *channel network*.
> 
> 5. **Solvent-Assisted Micromolding (SAMIM)** — *Solvent-softening variant*
>    - The PDMS mold is wetted with a solvent (e.g., ethanol, acetone) that **softens the surface of a polymer substrate** (e.g., PMMA, polystyrene).
>    - The mold is pressed onto the softened polymer; the pattern is *embossed* by partial dissolution.
>    - On solvent evaporation, the embossed pattern freezes in.
> 
> 6. **Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL) – Soft-Stamp variant** *(note: NIL can use hard quartz molds, but the soft-stamp or "SFIL" – Step-and-Flash Imprint Lithography – variant is grouped under Soft Lithography in Module 4)*
>    - The PDMS (or perfluoropolyether) stamp is pressed into a **photoresist film** on the substrate.
>    - UV exposure through the transparent stamp cures the resist.
>    - The stamp is separated, leaving a patterned resist that can serve as an etch mask.
>    - This is the **step-and-flash** version and is **industry-relevant for < 32 nm nodes** in semiconductor manufacturing.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why an elastomer?** Photolithography's rigid chromium-on-quartz masks cannot make intimate conformal contact with rough or curved surfaces. PDMS flows under low pressure and wets surfaces, *eliminating optical diffraction losses from air gaps*.
- **Why a low surface energy?** A high surface energy PDMS would bond irreversibly to the substrate or master, ruining both. The naturally low γ of PDMS makes demolding *spontaneous* and clean.
- **Why passivate the master?** Without fluorosilanation, the cured PDMS tears the photoresist from the silicon master when peeled.
- **Why degas?** Air bubbles trapped between master and liquid PDMS create *void defects* in the replicated stamp.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Equation Quick-Reference Table (Use in ESE Answers)**

| # | Formula (rendered) | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L^{2} = \dfrac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta}{2 \eta}\, t$ | **Washburn Equation** — capillary filling length L in time t for radius r, surface tension γ, viscosity η, contact angle θ | **MIMIC** — predicts how fast polymer fills a microchannel |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Laplace pressure driving capillary flow; γ = surface tension, θ = contact angle, r = channel radius | Pressure that drives liquid into MIMIC channels |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{E \, h^{3}}{12 \, (1-\nu^{2})}$ | **Bending stiffness** of a thin PDMS layer (thickness h, Poisson's ratio ν) | Determines *sagging* and *roof collapse* of PDMS microchannels |
| 4 | $h_{c} \approx \left( \dfrac{\gamma \, w^{2}}{E} \right)^{1/4}$ | **Critical sagging height** for a PDMS beam of width w and modulus E | Decides minimum allowed spacing between raised features |
| 5 | $F_{\text{adhesion}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \pi \, R \, \gamma$ | **JKR adhesion force** between a sphere of radius R and a flat, surface energy γ | Predicts sticking of soft stamps |
| 6 | $E_{\text{PDMS}} \approx E_{0}\, n^{k}$ | Empirical scaling of PDMS modulus with crosslinker ratio n; typically $E_{0} \approx 1.8$ MPa, k ≈ 1.5 | Tunes stamp stiffness for high-resolution work |
| 7 | $R_{\text{edge}} \approx h \left( \dfrac{\gamma}{E \, h} \right)^{1/3}$ | **Edge-resolution of a soft stamp**; rounding of a square feature by capillary-elastic effects | Limits minimum printable feature size |
| 8 | $D = D_{0} \, \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{E_{a}}{k_{B} T} \right)$ | Diffusion coefficient of PDMS penetrant; Arrhenius form | Curing kinetics of the elastomer |

> [!NOTE] **Constants / Standard Metrics to Memorize**
> - Boltzmann constant: **k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - Surface tension of water: **γ_water ≈ 72 mJ/m²** at 25 °C
> - Surface tension of typical photoresists: **γ ≈ 30 – 45 mJ/m²**
> - Contact angle of PDMS on clean Si: **θ ≈ 100°** (hydrophobic)
> - Refractive index of PDMS: **n ≈ 1.43** at 589 nm
> - Density of cured PDMS: **ρ ≈ 1.05 g/cm³**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biotechnology & Lab-on-Chip**: PDMS-based microfluidic chips with sub-100 μm channels for cell culture, droplet generation, and organ-on-chip devices.
- **Flexible Electronics**: Printing silver-nanowire electrodes on PET using μCP for roll-up displays.
- **Photovoltaics**: SAMIM- and μCP-patterned organic solar cells and perovskite solar cells.
- **Surface Chemistry**: Studying cell–surface interactions on patterned SAMs of fibronectin or other adhesion proteins.
- **Plasmonics & Metamaterials**: NIL-defined sub-100 nm gratings for biosensing and negative-index optics.
- **Semiconductor Industry**: Step-and-Flash Imprint Lithography (SFIL) commercialized by Molecular Imprints Inc. (now Canon) for memory devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Washburn Equation for MIMIC (Capillary Filling)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Most frequently asked analytical derivation in KTU 2024 ESE for this module.**
> 
> The MIMIC technique relies on capillary rise. The Washburn equation describes how the meniscus front advances in a thin channel. We derive it from first principles below.

**Assumption Set (stated explicitly for KTU valuation clarity):**
- Channel has rectangular cross-section, half-width $a$, half-height $b$, with $b \ll a$ (slab approximation).
- Fluid is Newtonian, viscosity $\eta$, density $\rho$.
- Flow is laminar, incompressible, and quasi-steady (low Reynolds number).
- The meniscus advances slowly enough that inertial terms are negligible compared to viscous and capillary terms.

**Step 1 — Pressure balance driving the flow**

At the liquid–air meniscus, the Laplace pressure is given by

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{eff}}}$$

This is the *driving* pressure that pushes the liquid into the channel.

**Step 2 — Viscous resistance (Poiseuille flow in a thin slab)**

For fully-developed laminar flow in a rectangular slit of half-height $b$, the Hagen–Poiseuille volumetric flow rate is

$$Q = -\frac{2 \, a \, b^{3}}{3 \, \eta}\, \frac{\mathrm{d}P}{\mathrm{d}x}$$

The pressure gradient along the filled length $L$ is

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}P}{\mathrm{d}x} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L} = -\frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{eff}} \, L}$$

**Step 3 — Kinematic relation**

If the meniscus position is $L(t)$, then $Q = a \cdot (2b) \cdot \dfrac{\mathrm{d}L}{\mathrm{d}t} = 2ab \cdot \dot{L}$.

Equating the two expressions for $Q$:

$$2ab\, \frac{\mathrm{d}L}{\mathrm{d}t} = \frac{2 a b^{3}}{3 \eta}\, \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{eff}}\, L}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification**

Cancel $2ab$ on both sides:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}L}{\mathrm{d}t} = \frac{b^{2} \, \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \eta \, r_{\text{eff}}\, L}$$

**Step 5 — Integration with initial condition $L(0) = 0$**

$$\int_{0}^{L(t)} L \, \mathrm{d}L = \frac{b^{2} \, \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \eta \, r_{\text{eff}}} \int_{0}^{t} \mathrm{d}t$$

$$\frac{L^{2}}{2} = \frac{b^{2} \, \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \eta \, r_{\text{eff}}}\, t$$

**Step 6 — Final Washburn form**

$$\boxed{\,L^{2} = \frac{2 \, b^{2} \, \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \, \eta \, r_{\text{eff}}}\, t\,}$$

For a circular capillary of radius $r$, the constant in front of $t$ becomes $\gamma r \cos\theta / (2\eta)$, recovering the classical Washburn result:

$$L^{2} = \frac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta}{2 \eta}\, t$$

> [!TIP] **Physical interpretation:** The fill-length grows as the *square root* of time. Doubling the time does *not* double the filled length; it increases it by only **√2 ≈ 1.414**. This is critical when designing MIMIC filling cycles in KTU lab examinations.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style problem)

**Problem (typical 7-mark sub-question):** A PDMS mold with rectangular microchannels of half-height $b = 25 \,\mu m$, effective radius $r_{\text{eff}} = 30 \,\mu m$, and contact angle $\theta = 35^{\circ}$ is placed on a glass substrate. A low-viscosity UV-curable acrylate ($\eta = 0.5$ Pa·s, $\gamma = 32$ mJ/m²) is introduced. Calculate the time required to fill a channel of length $L = 5$ mm.

**Solution — full steps (zero skipping):**

**Step 1** — Convert all units to SI.
- $b = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $r_{\text{eff}} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\eta = 0.5$ Pa·s
- $\gamma = 32 \times 10^{-3}$ J/m²
- $\theta = 35^{\circ} \Rightarrow \cos 35^{\circ} \approx 0.8192$
- $L = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 2** — Substitute into the rectangular Washburn form.

$$L^{2} = \frac{2 \, b^{2} \, \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \, \eta \, r_{\text{eff}}}\, t$$

**Step 3** — Solve for $t$.

$$t = \frac{3 \, \eta \, r_{\text{eff}} \, L^{2}}{2 \, b^{2} \, \gamma \cos\theta}$$

**Step 4** — Numerator:

$$3 \times 0.5 \times (30 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}$$

$$= 1.5 \times (30 \times 10^{-6}) \times (25 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$= 1.5 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-10} = 1.125 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{Pa·s·m}^{3}$$

**Step 5** — Denominator:

$$2 \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (32 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.8192$$

$$= 2 \times (6.25 \times 10^{-10}) \times 0.02622$$

$$= 1.25 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.02622 = 3.277 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J/m (or Pa·m³)}$$

**Step 6** — Final calculation:

$$t = \frac{1.125 \times 10^{-9}}{3.277 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 34.33 \text{ seconds}$$

**Answer:** The microchannel fills in approximately **34 seconds** under these conditions — comfortably within a typical UV-curing cycle of 30–60 s.

### 3.3 Critical Sagging Height of a PDMS Beam (Analytical Insight)

When two raised PDMS features are placed too close together, the unsupported span *sags* under capillary or adhesion forces, ruining the stamp. The critical thickness below which sagging occurs is derived from beam-bending theory:

**Starting relation (elastic beam on simple supports):**

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{5 \, w \, L^{4}}{384 \, E \, I}$$

**Step 1** — Moment of inertia of a rectangular PDMS beam (width $w$, thickness $h$):

$$I = \frac{w \, h^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute:

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{5 \, w \, L^{4}}{384 \, E \, (w h^{3}/12)} = \frac{5 \, L^{4}}{32 \, E \, h^{3}}$$

**Step 3** — Failure criterion: $\delta_{\max} = h/2$ (beam sags by half its thickness).

$$\frac{h}{2} = \frac{5 \, L^{4}}{32 \, E \, h^{3}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h^{4} = \frac{5 \, L^{4}}{16 \, E}$$

**Step 4** — Solve:

$$\boxed{\,h \approx 0.795 \left( \frac{L^{4}}{E} \right)^{1/4}\,}$$

For $L = 100 \,\mu m$ and $E = 2$ MPa, this gives $h \approx 6.7 \,\mu m$ — a useful rule-of-thumb when designing μCP stamps.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Master → Stamp → Pattern Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Si or Quartz Wafer] --> B[Photolithography or E-beam Master Fabrication]
    B --> C[Fluorosilane Passivation of Master]
    C --> D[Pour Liquid PDMS 10:1 Base:Curative]
    D --> E[Vacuum Degassing 30 min]
    E --> F[Thermal Cure 65 C for 4 h]
    F --> G[Peel PDMS Stamp Negative Replica]
    G --> H{Variant Selection}
    H --> I[Microcontact Printing muCP]
    H --> J[Replica Molding REM]
    H --> K[Microtransfer Molding muTM]
    H --> L[Micromolding in Capillaries MIMIC]
    H --> M[Solvent Assisted Micromolding SAMIM]
    H --> N[Step and Flash Imprint NIL]
    I --> O[Patterned SAM on Substrate]
    J --> P[Polymer Replica on Substrate]
    K --> Q[Free Standing Patterned Film]
    L --> R[Capillary Filled Polymer Network]
    M --> S[Embossed Polymer Substrate]
    N --> T[UV Cured Resist Pattern]
    O --> U[Inspection SEM Optical Profilometry]
    P --> U
    Q --> U
    R --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Six Sub-Techniques Compared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Microcontact Printing]
        A1[PDMS Stamp] --> A2[Inking with Alkanethiol]
        A2 --> A3[Conformal Contact on Au]
        A3 --> A4[Self Assembled Monolayer Forms]
    end
    subgraph S2[Replica Molding]
        B1[PDMS Mold] --> B2[Pour Liquid Prepolymer]
        B2 --> B3[UV or Thermal Cure]
        B3 --> B4[Demold Free Standing Replica]
    end
    subgraph S3[Micromolding in Capillaries]
        C1[PDMS Mold] --> C2[Seal on Substrate]
        C2 --> C3[Capillary Fill via Washburn Flow]
        C3 --> C4[Cure and Demold]
    end
    subgraph S4[SAMIM]
        D1[PDMS Mold] --> D2[Wetting with Solvent]
        D2 --> D3[Press on Polymer Film]
        D3 --> D4[Solvent Softens Film Pattern Embosses]
    end
    subgraph S5[Microtransfer Molding]
        E1[PDMS Mold] --> E2[Fill with Prepolymer]
        E2 --> E3[Cover with Flat PDMS Lid]
        E3 --> E4[Transfer to Target Substrate]
    end
    subgraph S6[Step and Flash NIL]
        F1[Transparent PDMS Stamp] --> F2[Press into Liquid Resist]
        F2 --> F3[UV Exposure Through Stamp]
        F3 --> F4[Separate Stamp Cured Resist Remains]
    end
```

### 4.3 Critical Parameter Cross-Check Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stamp Geometry] --> Q1[Feature Size and Aspect Ratio]
    P2[PDMS Modulus] --> Q1
    P3[Surface Energy] --> Q2[Demolding Quality]
    P4[Master Passivation] --> Q2
    P5[Contact Angle] --> Q3[Capillary Filling in MIMIC]
    P6[Liquid Viscosity] --> Q3
    P7[Conformal Pressure] --> Q4[Pattern Transfer Fidelity]
    P8[Substrate Roughness] --> Q4
    Q1 --> R[Final Pattern Resolution and Defect Density]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading aid for KTU lab viva:**
> 
> - Node **P2 (PDMS Modulus)** is controlled by the **base:curative ratio**. Increase the *base fraction* (e.g., 12:1) → softer stamp; 5:1 → stiffer stamp.
> - Node **P5 (Contact Angle)** is reduced by **oxygen plasma treatment** of PDMS, which is routinely done to make the surface hydrophilic before bonding to glass.
> - Node **R (Final Pattern Resolution)** is the single most important metric in KTU 2024 lab evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**"Define soft lithography. List any four advantages of soft lithography over conventional photolithography." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Soft lithography is a non-photolithographic micro/nanofabrication technique that uses an **elastomeric stamp or mold — most commonly PDMS (polydimethylsiloxane)** — to replicate patterns by conformal contact, molding, or printing.
> 
> **Four advantages over photolithography (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Low cost** — no need for steppers, deep-UV sources, or vacuum; a lab-grade setup costs ≈ 5 % of a mask aligner.
> 2. **Conformality on non-planar substrates** — soft stamps wrap over curved, rough, or flexible surfaces, which rigid chrome masks cannot.
> 3. **Broad material compatibility** — works with polymers, biomolecules, colloids, and SAMs that are incompatible with photoresist chemistries.
> 4. **Sub-100 nm resolution demonstrated** — with proper stamp design, μCP has shown **~ 30 nm** features on gold.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**"Distinguish between Microcontact Printing (μCP) and Micromolding in Capillaries (MIMIC). Mention the driving force in each." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Feature | μCP | MIMIC |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | **Inking + contact transfer** of self-assembling molecules | **Capillary filling** of a microchannel network by a liquid prepolymer |
| Driving Force | **Conformal mechanical contact** + SAM chemisorption | **Capillary (Laplace) pressure**, $\Delta P = 2\gamma \cos\theta / r$ |
| Stamp Role | Ink-bearing stamp | Empty mold (no ink) |
| Resolution Demonstrated | ~ 30 nm | ~ 1 μm (limited by filling dynamics) |
| Substrate | Typically Au, Ag, Cu, or SiO₂ | Any flat substrate the prepolymer can wet |

(1.5 marks for μCP explanation, 1.5 marks for MIMIC explanation.)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**"Explain the working principle of Microcontact Printing (μCP) for nanofabrication. Discuss (a) the role of PDMS, (b) the chemistry of inking with alkanethiols, and (c) the resolution limits and defect mechanisms in μCP." (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Role of PDMS (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Why PDMS is chosen as the stamp material (2 marks):**
- **Low Young's modulus (E ≈ 1.5–3 MPa):** Enables conformal contact on rough surfaces with applied pressures of ≈ 50–200 g/cm².
- **Low surface energy (γ ≈ 21–24 mJ/m²):** Allows clean demolding from the master and easy separation from the printed substrate.
- **Optical transparency (transparent down to ≈ 230 nm):** Useful in SFIL variants.
- **Chemical inertness:** Compatible with alkanethiols, silanes, proteins, and most solvents.
- **Biocompatibility:** Approved by US-FDA for biomedical use — important for bio-MEMS applications.

**Step 2 — Fabrication of the PDMS stamp (3 marks):**
- Master (typically photoresist-on-Si) is silanized with **trichloro(1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyl)silane** to prevent PDMS adhesion.
- Liquid PDMS (Sylgard 184) is mixed in **base:curative = 10:1** ratio.
- Poured over master, **degassed** in a desiccator for 30 min to remove air bubbles.
- Cured at **65 °C for 4 h** (or overnight at room temperature).
- Peeled off to yield the negative-relief stamp.

**Step 3 — How PDMS enables conformal contact (2 marks):**
- Under low uniform pressure, PDMS flows elastically, eliminating air gaps between stamp and substrate.
- This is the *key advantage over photolithography* where the rigid mask cannot follow surface topography.
- The contact is quantified by the JKR adhesion theory with a contact radius depending on $\sqrt{F R / E^{*}}$.

#### Part (b) — Chemistry of Inking with Alkanethiols (4 marks)

- The PDMS stamp is "inked" by immersing it in an **ethanolic solution of alkanethiols** (e.g., HS-(CH₂)₁₁-CH₃ — dodecanethiol) at **1–10 mM concentration** for ≈ 30 s to 5 min.
- The thiol (-SH) head group has a strong affinity for noble-metal surfaces (Au, Ag, Cu), with **bond energy ≈ 170 kJ/mol** for Au–S.
- On contact with the gold substrate, the alkanethiols are transferred to the gold surface only at the **raised regions** of the stamp.
- The molecules then **self-assemble** into a **closely-packed, ordered monolayer (SAM)** with the alkyl chains tilted ≈ 30° from the surface normal.
- The SAM passivates the gold and acts as an **etch resist** in subsequent wet-chemical or dry etching.
- For non-metallic substrates (SiO₂, glass), **alkylsilanes** (e.g., octadecyltrichlorosilane, OTS) are used instead.

#### Part (c) — Resolution Limits and Defect Mechanisms (3 marks)

**Resolution limits:**
- **Theoretical ≈ 30 nm** (single-SAM on Au(111)).
- Practical limit in most labs: **100–500 nm** due to:
  - **Sagging of PDMS bridges** (see derived formula in §3.3) between dense features.
  - **Pairing of neighboring features** under capillary adhesion.
  - **Roof collapse** when stamp channels are wide and shallow.
  - **Diffusion of thiols** on the gold surface after printing ("**diffusion blurring**" — can spread pattern by 50–100 nm in hours).

**Defect mechanisms:**
- Incomplete inking → patches of bare gold.
- Dust particles on stamp → point defects.
- Master passivation failure → tearing of master resist.
- Excessive contact pressure → elastomer deformation and pattern distortion.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
**"Describe the Micromolding in Capillaries (MIMIC) process. Derive the Washburn equation governing the capillary filling of microchannels. A PDMS mold of rectangular microchannels (half-height = 20 μm) is placed on a glass slide and a UV-curable polymer (γ = 30 mJ/m², η = 0.4 Pa·s, contact angle = 40°) is introduced. Calculate the time to fill a 4 mm long channel. (14 marks)"**

#### Part (a) — Description of MIMIC (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
- A PDMS mold with a network of **recessed microchannels** is brought into intimate contact with a flat substrate.
- The recessed regions of the PDMS form **empty channels** between the PDMS roof and the substrate floor.
- A drop of low-viscosity liquid prepolymer is placed at the channel mouth.

**Step 2 — Capillary filling (2 marks):**
- The liquid **spontaneously fills** the channels due to **capillary (Laplace) pressure**, $\Delta P = 2 \gamma \cos\theta / r_{\text{eff}}$.
- For $\theta < 90°$ (wetting), $\cos\theta > 0$ and $\Delta P > 0$ — fluid is *pulled in*.

**Step 3 — Curing and demolding (2 marks):**
- The filled channels are exposed to **UV light** (or heat) through the transparent PDMS to polymerize the liquid.
- The PDMS is peeled off, leaving behind a solid polymer replica of the channel network on the substrate.

**Step 4 — Advantages and limitations (1 mark):**
- *Advantage:* No external pressure required; works on delicate substrates.
