- *Limitation:* Filling time scales as $L^{2}$, restricting practical channel length to ≈ 1 cm.
- *Limitation:* Requires $\theta < 90°$ — surface modification of PDMS may be needed.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Washburn Equation (5 marks)

**Step 1 — Laplace pressure driving flow (1 mark):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{\text{eff}}}$$

**Step 2 — Hagen–Poiseuille flow in a thin rectangular channel (1.5 marks):**

$$Q = -\frac{2 a b^{3}}{3 \eta} \frac{\mathrm{d}P}{\mathrm{d}x}$$

**Step 3 — Continuity and meniscus kinematics (1 mark):**

$$Q = 2ab \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}L}{\mathrm{d}t}$$

**Step 4 — Combine and integrate (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}L}{\mathrm{d}t} = \frac{b^{2} \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \eta \, r_{\text{eff}} \, L}$$

$$\Rightarrow \int_{0}^{L} L\, \mathrm{d}L = \frac{b^{2} \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \eta \, r_{\text{eff}}} \int_{0}^{t} \mathrm{d}t$$

$$\boxed{\,L^{2} = \frac{2 b^{2} \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \eta \, r_{\text{eff}}}\, t\,}$$

#### Part (c) — Numerical Problem (2 marks)

**Given:** $b = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\gamma = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ J/m², $\eta = 0.4$ Pa·s, $\theta = 40°$, $L = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $r_{\text{eff}} \approx 2b = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ m (slit approximation).

**Step 1 — Compute $\cos 40° \approx 0.766$.**

**Step 2 — Numerator of t:**

$$3 \eta r_{\text{eff}} L^{2} = 3 \times 0.4 \times (40 \times 10^{-6}) \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^{2}$$

$$= 1.2 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} = 7.68 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator of t:**

$$2 b^{2} \gamma \cos\theta = 2 \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times 0.030 \times 0.766$$

$$= 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.02298 = 1.838 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 4 — Final t:**

$$t = \frac{7.68 \times 10^{-10}}{1.838 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 41.8 \text{ s}$$

**Answer:** The microchannel fills in approximately **42 seconds** under the given conditions.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Soft Lithography Answers**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "soft" with "photolithography" terminology** — students often write "soft lithography uses light" and lose 1 full mark. The light is only used *optionally* in SFIL for curing, not for exposure-based pattern definition.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to mention fluorosilane passivation of the master** — examiners specifically look for this line; omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark in ESE.
> 
> 3. **Stating wrong Young's modulus for PDMS** — the correct value is **1.5–3 MPa**, not GPa. Writing "PDMS has E ≈ 150 GPa (like silicon)" is a fatal error.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the assumption list in Washburn derivations** — KTU 2024 valuation keys *explicitly check* for the assumptions: Newtonian fluid, laminar flow, negligible inertia, constant contact angle. Always write them.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up μCP and MIMIC driving forces** — μCP = conformal contact + chemisorption; MIMIC = Laplace/capillary pressure. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are swapped.
> 
> 6. **Numerical errors in unit conversion** — typical slip: leaving $b$ in μm without converting to meters in the Washburn formula. This can shift the answer by 6–12 orders of magnitude. Always write the SI conversion step.
> 
> 7. **Not stating the base:curative ratio (10:1)** when describing PDMS stamp fabrication — costs 0.5 mark.
> 
> 8. **Forgetting to discuss sagging/roof collapse** when describing resolution limits — a frequent 2-mark question in Module 4 of PECHT753.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (final 60-second scan before exam)**
> 
> - **Soft lithography = elastomer-based, contact-mode nanofabrication** using **PDMS** (Sylgard 184, 10:1 base:curative).
> - **Six sub-techniques:** μCP, REM, μTM, MIMIC, SAMIM, NIL/SFIL.
> - **PDMS properties to memorize:** E ≈ 1.5–3 MPa; γ ≈ 21–24 mJ/m²; transparent to UV; cured at 65 °C / 4 h.
> - **Master passivation** is done with a **fluorosilane** to prevent adhesion.
> - **μCP inking molecule** for gold = **alkanethiol (HS-(CH₂)ₙ-X)**; for SiO₂ = **alkylsilane**.
> - **Driving force in MIMIC** = **Laplace pressure**, $\Delta P = 2 \gamma \cos\theta / r$.
> - **Washburn equation:** $L^{2} = \dfrac{\gamma r \cos\theta}{2 \eta}\, t$ (circular) or $\dfrac{2 b^{2} \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \eta r_{\text{eff}}}\, t$ (slit).
> - **Resolution limit:** ≈ **30 nm demonstrated**; **practical 100–500 nm** limited by PDMS sagging, pairing, and thiol diffusion.
> - **Sagging height** scales as $(L^{4}/E)^{1/4}$ — for $L = 100 \,\mu m$ and $E = 2$ MPa, $h \approx 6.7 \,\mu m$.
> - **JKR adhesion** controls how a soft stamp sticks: $F = \frac{3}{2}\pi R \gamma$.
> - **Discovered / popularized by George M. Whitesides (Harvard, 1993).**
> - **Industrial impact:** Canon Molecular Imprints uses Step-and-Flash Imprint Lithography (SFIL) for memory devices at sub-32 nm nodes.
> - **Advantages over photolithography:** low cost, conformality on rough/curved surfaces, broad material compatibility, biocompatible.
> - **Limitations:** throughput lower than projection lithography for sub-22 nm nodes; pattern distortion due to elastomer deformation; requires clean-room conditions to avoid particle defects.
> - **Capillary filling time scales as $L^{2}$** — practical channel lengths are limited to ≈ 1 cm in MIMIC.
> - **Oxygen plasma treatment** of PDMS makes it hydrophilic (contact angle drops from ≈ 100° to ≈ 10°), enabling irreversible bonding to glass for microfluidics.
> - **Solvent used in SAMIM** must be a good solvent for the substrate polymer but a poor solvent/non-solvent for PDMS — ethanol, acetone, and toluene are typical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning Probe Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scanning Probe Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)** is a class of direct-write nanolithography techniques that employs an atomically sharp physical probe — rastered in close proximity (sub-nanometer to a few nanometers) to a substrate surface — to create nanoscale patterns, features, or topographical/chemical modifications. Unlike photon- or electron-based lithography, SPL uses **near-field interactions** (tunneling current, atomic/molecular forces, capillary transport, or thermal gradients) between the probe apex and the substrate to deposit, remove, or transform material with sub-10 nm to a few-nanometer resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** *"Scanning Probe Lithography refers to a set of nanofabrication techniques in which a sharp tip is scanned across a substrate surface while a controlled physical, electrical, chemical, or thermal interaction is used to pattern the surface at the nanometer scale."*

### 1.2 Major Sub-Families of SPL

| Sub-Technique | Physical Mechanism | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| STM Lithography (Scanning Tunneling Microscopy) | Quantum mechanical tunneling current | **1–20 nm** |
| AFM/SFM Lithography (Atomic/Scanning Force Microscopy) | Mechanical force, anodic oxidation, thermal expansion | **5–50 nm** |
| Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN) | Capillary ink transport from tip to substrate | **10–50 nm** |
| NSOM/SNOM Lithography (Near-field Scanning Optical) | Sub-wavelength optical near-field | **20–100 nm** |
| Thermal/Plasmonic SPL | Localized heat or plasmonic hot spots | **10–30 nm** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Nano-Pencil & Nano-Eraser"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Scanning Probe Lithography as a 'nano-pencil':**
> - Imagine writing on a sheet of paper, but instead of a pencil tip that is **1 mm wide**, you are using a tip that is **a few atoms wide** (radius of curvature ≈ 1–10 nm).
> - The "paper" is a clean silicon, gold, or mica wafer.
> - As you drag the tip across the surface (under computer control), the tip either **scratches the surface mechanically**, **paints it chemically with an "ink" molecule (DPN)**, **burns/melts it locally using heat**, or **draws patterns of oxide by electric field (anodic oxidation)**.
> - The tip is connected to a **flexible cantilever (in AFM)** or held at a **fixed tunneling gap (in STM)** — this allows the system to "feel" the surface and track its contours while it writes, much like how a blind person reads Braille.

**The 5 Essential Ingredients of Every SPL Tool:**
1. An **atomically sharp tip** (apex radius 1–50 nm)
2. A **piezoelectric scanner** (X, Y, Z motion with sub-Å precision)
3. A **feedback loop** (electronic circuit maintaining constant tip-sample interaction)
4. A **pattern generator** (computer translating CAD drawing into tip motion)
5. A **substrate** (Si, Au, SiO₂, mica, etc.)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics — Why does the tip "see" and "write" the nanoscale?

Every SPL technique exploits a **near-field interaction** that decays rapidly (typically exponentially) with tip-sample distance `d`. This rapid decay is the fundamental reason SPL achieves nanometer resolution: only the **single atom at the tip apex** is close enough to interact strongly with the substrate.

### 2.2 Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) Lithography

- A **metallic tip** (typically W or Pt-Ir) is brought within **0.3–1 nm** of a conductive substrate.
- A small bias voltage (typically **0.1–10 V**) is applied between tip and substrate.
- Electrons **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through the vacuum gap, producing a measurable tunneling current.
- The tip is rastered in X-Y while the feedback loop maintains constant current (constant-height or constant-current mode).

**Two operating modes for lithography:**
- **Voltage-pulse mode**: Tip is positioned at a fixed point, then a voltage pulse (3–10 V, µs–ms duration) is applied → local field evaporates atoms or induces surface modification.
- **Field-emission / Mechanical mode**: Tip is dragged under high field (high bias + low current) → material transfer, desorption, or local heating occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Requirement:** The substrate **must be electrically conductive** (metals, doped Si, HOPG). This is a major limitation of STM lithography.

### 2.3 Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM/SFM) Lithography

AFM uses a **microfabricated cantilever** (typically Si or Si₃N₄) with a sharp tip at its end. As the tip interacts with the surface, the cantilever bends. A laser reflects off the cantilever into a photodiode, measuring the **deflection**, which corresponds to force.

**AFM Lithography Modes:**

**a) Mechanical (Scratching/Indentation) Mode:**
- Tip is pressed into a soft surface (e.g., polymer resist, PMMA) with a high normal load (typically **1–100 µN**).
- Tip is then dragged, physically plowing a groove of controlled depth.
- Groove depth ≈ **1–100 nm**, width ≈ tip radius (typically **10–30 nm**).
- Used for direct patterning of polymer resists without any chemical development step.

**b) Anodic Oxidation (Local Oxidation) Mode:**
- The most common AFM lithography mode for hard substrates.
- A **biased conductive tip** (typically Pt-Ir coated, V = −5 to −15 V) is scanned over a surface covered with a thin adsorbed water layer.
- The strong electric field + water meniscus causes **local oxidation** of the surface (e.g., Si → SiO₂, Ti → TiO₂).
- The oxide pattern remains as a **chemical/topographical mask** for subsequent etching.
- This was the technique famously used by IBM researchers (Eigler, 1990) and Quate/Minz (1996) to write the world's smallest letters "IBM" with Xe atoms on Ni using STM.

**c) Thermal (Thermo-mechanical) Mode:**
- The tip is locally heated (using a resistive heater in the cantilever, T = **100–500 °C**).
- A thermoplastic resist (e.g., PMMA) is softened locally, and the tip indents a pit.
- This is the principle behind **Millipede** data storage (IBM Zurich, ≈ 2000).

**d) Force Modulated / Dynamic Ploughing:**
- Resonant oscillation of the cantilever is used to enhance energy transfer.
- Reduces required contact force, enabling softer materials.

### 2.4 Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)

- Invented by Chad Mirkin's group (Northwestern University, **1999**).
- An AFM tip is "inked" with a solution of molecules (e.g., alkanethiols, proteins, DNA, metal precursors).
- The inked tip is brought into contact with a substrate (e.g., Au, SiO₂).
- **Capillary transport** of the ink molecules occurs through a **water meniscus** that forms naturally between tip and substrate.
- The diffusion of the ink across the substrate surface creates dots or lines as the tip is rastered.
- Resolution: ≈ **15 nm line width, < 5 nm pitch** for thiols on Au.
- DPN is **chemo-specific** — the chemistry is dictated by the choice of "ink."

### 2.5 Near-Field Scanning Optical Microscopy (NSOM/SNOM) Lithography

- A **sub-wavelength aperture** (typically 50–100 nm diameter) at the end of a tapered, metal-coated fiber-optic probe delivers light to a sub-diffraction area.
- The aperture must be held within the **near-field distance** (d ≪ λ) of the substrate.
- Used to expose **photoresists** at sub-wavelength resolution, or for **local photopolymerization / photobleaching**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Tunneling Current (STM) | $I_t = I_0 \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$ | $I_t$: tunneling current (nA), $d$: tip-sample gap (Å), $\kappa$: decay constant ≈ **0.5–1.0 Å⁻¹** |
| Decay Constant | $\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m\Phi}}{\hbar}$ | $m$: electron mass, $\Phi$: work function (eV), $\hbar$: reduced Planck constant |
| Cantilever Force (Hooke's Law) | $F = k \cdot \Delta z$ | $F$: force (N), $k$: spring constant (N/m), $\Delta z$: deflection (m) |
| Cantilever Spring Constant (Rectangular) | $k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | $E$: Young's modulus, $w,t,L$: width, thickness, length of cantilever |
| Thermal Expansion (Pit Depth) | $\Delta z = \alpha L \Delta T$ | $\alpha$: thermal expansion coefficient, $L$: heater length, $\Delta T$: temperature change |
| Anodic Oxide Thickness | $d_{ox} \approx V \cdot \alpha_{ox}$ | $V$: applied bias, $\alpha_{ox}$ ≈ **2 nm/V** for Si oxidation |
| Anodic Oxide Width (Rule of Thumb) | $w_{ox} \approx 2 \cdot R_{tip} \cdot \sqrt{V/V_0}$ | $R_{tip}$: tip radius, $V_0$ ≈ **1 V** reference |
| DPN Diffusion Length | $L_D = \sqrt{D \tau}$ | $D$: ink diffusivity on substrate (cm²/s), $\tau$: tip-substrate contact time |
| Optical Near-Field Decay | $I(d) \propto e^{-d/d_{nf}}$ | $d_{nf}$: near-field decay length ≈ aperture radius |
| Resolution Limit (Heisenberg-like) | $\Delta x \approx \sqrt{R_{tip} \cdot d}$ | $R_{tip}$: tip radius of curvature, $d$: tip-sample distance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The exponential dependence of tunneling current on gap distance ($I_t \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$) is the **single most-asked concept** in any Scanning Probe Lithography question. A change of just **1 Å** changes the current by ≈ **10×** for $\kappa \approx 1$ Å⁻¹.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quantum device prototyping:** DPN has been used to deposit single-molecule transistors and protein arrays.
- **Data storage:** IBM Millipede used thermo-mechanical SPL to achieve **> 1 Tb/in²** areal density.
- **Biomolecular patterning:** DPN of proteins/DNA for biosensing and tissue engineering.
- **MEMS/NEMS fabrication:** AFM scratching used to fabricate nanocantilevers and grating structures.
- **Repair of photomasks:** AFM-based local etching/deposition for defect repair in extreme ultraviolet (EUV) masks.
- **Single-atom manipulation:** Eigler's famous 1989 demonstration of writing "IBM" with 35 Xe atoms on Ni.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tunneling Current in STM

The starting point is the **Bardeen transfer Hamiltonian** for electron tunneling between two electrodes separated by a thin insulating barrier (vacuum).

**Step 1 — Wavefunction in the gap:**
The electron wavefunction in the classically forbidden region (the vacuum gap of width $d$) decays exponentially:

$$
\psi(z) = \psi(0) \, e^{-\kappa z}
$$

where $\kappa$ is the decay constant of the wavefunction in the barrier.

**Step 2 — Express the decay constant:**
For an electron of mass $m$ and energy $E$ tunneling through a barrier of height $\Phi$ (work function), the Schrödinger equation in the barrier region ($E < \Phi$) gives:

$$
-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m} \frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + \Phi \psi = E \psi
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} = \frac{2m(\Phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi = \kappa^2 \psi
$$

Therefore:

$$
\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(\Phi - E)}}{\hbar} = \frac{\sqrt{2m\Phi_e}}{\hbar}
$$

where $\Phi_e = \Phi - E$ is the effective barrier height (≈ work function for low bias).

**Step 3 — Tunneling probability:**
The transmission probability through the barrier of width $d$ is:

$$
T \propto e^{-2\kappa d}
$$

The factor of **2** in the exponent arises because the wavefunction both enters and exits the barrier, decaying in both directions.

**Step 4 — Tunneling current:**
Since current is proportional to transmission, the tunneling current is:

$$
I_t = I_0 \, e^{-2\kappa d}
$$

where $I_0$ depends on the local density of states of tip and sample and the applied bias $V$.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the magnitude of $\kappa$:**
For $\Phi \approx 4$ eV (typical work function of W or Au) and $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg:

$$
\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11\times 10^{-31} \times 4 \times 1.6\times 10^{-19}}}{1.055\times 10^{-34}} \approx 1.0 \text{ Å}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Implication for resolution:**
A change of $\Delta d = 1$ Å in the gap causes a current change of:

$$
\frac{I_t(d+1)}{I_t(d)} = e^{-2\kappa \cdot 1\text{Å}} = e^{-2.0} \approx 0.135
$$

i.e., the current drops by **~87% per Angstrom** of gap change. This is why STM has **atomic-scale vertical sensitivity** and why only the **single atom at the tip apex** dominates the current.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: AFM Cantilever Spring Constant

A rectangular cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$ behaves as an elastic beam.

**Step 1 — Euler-Bernoulli beam equation:**
For a cantilever with end load $F$:

$$
\Delta z = \frac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}
$$

This is the standard deflection formula for a beam of uniform cross-section under a point load at its free end.

**Step 2 — Solve for spring constant:**
By Hooke's Law, $F = k \cdot \Delta z$. Therefore:

$$
k = \frac{F}{\Delta z} = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Typical numerical estimate:**
For a Si cantilever: $E \approx 1.3 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $L = 200$ µm, $w = 30$ µm, $t = 2$ µm:

$$
k = \frac{1.3\times 10^{11} \times 30\times 10^{-6} \times (2\times 10^{-6})^3}{4 \times (200\times 10^{-6})^3}
$$

$$
k = \frac{1.3\times 10^{11} \times 3.0\times 10^{-5} \times 8.0\times 10^{-18}}{4 \times 8.0\times 10^{-15}}
$$

$$
k = \frac{3.12 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 0.97 \text{ N/m}
$$

This is consistent with a **soft contact-mode cantilever** (typical $k \approx 0.01$–$10$ N/m).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of SPL Sub-Techniques

| Parameter | STM Lithography | AFM Mechanical | AFM Anodic Oxidation | DPN | NSOM Lithography |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 1–20 nm | 10–50 nm | 5–30 nm | 10–50 nm | 50–150 nm |
| Substrate requirement | **Conductive** | Any | Conductive/oxidizable | Au, SiO₂ | Photoresist |
| Environment | UHV or ambient | Ambient | Ambient (humid) | Ambient | Ambient |
| Throughput | Very low | Low | Low | **Moderate** (1D arrays) | Low |
| Tip wear | Severe (high field) | Moderate | Moderate | Rapid contamination | Moderate |
| Chemical specificity | No | No | Oxide only | **Yes (ink chemistry)** | Photochemistry only |
| Depth of pattern | Surface only | Bulk grooves | Few nm oxide | Monolayer | Few–100 nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick for the exam:** *"STM = sub-atomic precise but needs conductors; AFM = flexible but tip wears out; DPN = chemistry on demand but slow; NSOM = photon's little brother, needs resists."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Scanning Probe Lithography System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pattern Generator: CAD Layout] --> B[Controller and DAC]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric XYZ Scanner]
    C --> D[Sharp Probe Tip: Radius 1 to 50 nm]
    D --> E[Substrate: Si, Au, Mica]
    E --> F[Signal Source: Tunneling Current / Force / Heat / Light]
    F --> G[Feedback Loop: PID Controller]
    G --> C
    D --> H[Pattern Formation: Scratching, Oxidation, Deposition]
    H --> I[Fabricated Nanostructure]
    J[Coarse Approach Motor] --> D
    K[Optical / Capacitive Position Sensor] --> G
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Working Principle of Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AFM Cantilever with Sharp Tip] --> B[Inking Step: Dip into Molecule Solution]
    B --> C[Tip Loaded with Ink Molecules]
    C --> D[Water Meniscus Forms Naturally at Tip Apex]
    D --> E[Capillary Transport of Molecules via Meniscus]
    E --> F[Diffusion of Ink on Au or SiO2 Substrate]
    F --> G[Raster Scan of Tip in X Y Plane]
    G --> H[Pattern of Self Assembled Monolayer Forms]
    H --> I[Final Functional Nanopattern: Dots, Lines, Grids]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of STM and AFM Lithography Signal Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Tip Sample Gap: 0.3 to 1 nm] --> B1[Bias Voltage Applied]
    B1 --> C1[Electron Tunneling through Vacuum]
    C1 --> D1[Tunneling Current in nA]
    D1 --> E1[Constant Current Feedback]
    E1 --> F1[STM Lithography]

    A2[Tip in Contact with Surface] --> B2[Normal Load 1 to 100 microNewton]
    B2 --> C2[Cantilever Deflection]
    C2 --> D2[Laser Reflected to Photodiode]
    D2 --> E2[Force Feedback]
    E2 --> F2[AFM Lithography]

    G[Common Platform] --> A1
    G --> A2
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram — Anodic Oxidation AFM Lithography

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conductive AFM Tip: PtIr or Au Coated] --> B[Bias Voltage: minus 5 to minus 15 V]
    B --> C[Strong Local Electric Field under Tip]
    C --> D[Adsorbed Water Layer on Substrate]
    D --> E[Electrochemical Reaction: OH minus and O ions]
    E --> F[Local Oxidation: Si to SiO2 or Ti to TiO2]
    F --> G[Patterned Oxide Mask on Substrate]
    G --> H[Optional Etching Step: HF or KOH]
    H --> I[Final 3D Nanostructure on Substrate]
```

### 4.5 Process Flow — Thermo-Mechanical SPL (IBM Millipede Principle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Resist Layer: PMMA on Si] --> B[Heated AFM Tip: 100 to 500 C]
    B --> C[Tip Softens Polymer Locally]
    C --> D[Tip Indents a Nanopit]
    D --> E[Array of Tips: 32 by 32]
    E --> F[Parallel Writing of Data Pits]
    F --> G[Areal Density more than 1 Tb per square inch]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Differentiate between Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) lithography and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) lithography. Mention two key advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | STM Lithography | AFM Lithography |
|---|---|---|
| Operating signal | Quantum tunneling current | Cantilever deflection / force |
| Tip-sample gap | 0.3–1 nm (non-contact) | 0 (contact) to few nm (tapping) |
| Substrate requirement | **Must be conductive** | Can pattern any surface |
| Resolution | 1–20 nm | 5–50 nm |
| Lateral force | None (gentle) | High (tip wear possible) |

**Advantages of STM:** atomic-scale resolution, sub-Å vertical sensitivity, non-contact (no tip wear during imaging).
**Advantages of AFM:** works on insulating substrates, supports multiple modes (mechanical, electrical, thermal), versatile patterning (grooves, oxide, ink).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]`
**What is Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)? Explain the role of the water meniscus in the patterning process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** DPN is an SPL technique developed by C. A. Mirkin (1999) in which an AFM tip is used as a "nanoscale pen" to deposit molecules ("ink") onto a substrate through a water meniscus.
- **Meniscus role (1 Mark):** A thin water meniscus forms naturally between the AFM tip and the substrate due to capillary condensation. The meniscus acts as the **conduit for mass transport** of ink molecules from tip to substrate.
- **Mechanism (1 Mark):** Molecules diffuse from the tip apex across the meniscus and adsorb onto the substrate, forming a self-assembled monolayer (SAM) pattern. The diffusion is governed by Fick's law, and the linewidth depends on contact time and tip-substrate scan speed.

---

### PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** in SPL questions by **not specifying the substrate type or environment** (UHV vs. ambient) for each technique.
> - **Numerical substitutions** must be shown clearly with units; in tunneling current derivations, omitting the factor of 2 in the exponent $e^{-2\kappa d}$ costs **1 full mark**.
> - For DPN questions, many students forget to mention the **role of the water meniscus** — this is a compulsory point worth **2 marks**.
> - In AFM anodic oxidation, mentioning **KOH/HF etching as the post-patterning step** distinguishes a 7-mark answer from a 5-mark answer.

---

**PART B – QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, instrumentation, and working modes of Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) lithography.** Discuss the materials requirements and the two primary lithographic modes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **Principle (2 Marks):** STM lithography exploits the **quantum mechanical tunneling effect** between a conductive tip and a conductive substrate separated by a thin vacuum gap (0.3–1 nm). A bias voltage of 0.1–10 V is applied. The tunneling current is:
$$I_t = I_0 \, e^{-2\kappa d}$$
The exponential dependence on $d$ gives atomic-scale sensitivity.

2. **Instrumentation (2 Marks):**
   - Atomically sharp tip (W or Pt-Ir, etched electrochemically, radius < 50 nm).
   - Piezoelectric XYZ scanner (resolution ~ 0.01 Å).
   - Coarse approach mechanism (inertial drive or stick-slip).
   - Feedback loop (PID controller) maintaining constant $I_t$ or constant height.
   - Vibration isolation (typically active + passive, e.g., eddy-current dampers).

3. **Working Modes for Lithography (2 Marks):**
   - **Voltage-pulse mode:** The tip is held stationary; a voltage pulse (3–10 V, 10 µs–10 ms) is applied. The high field causes **field evaporation, desorption, or local melting** of surface atoms, creating a single-atom pit.
   - **Constant-current scanning mode:** The tip is rastered under high bias (5–10 V) at low current (1 pA). Atoms are transferred from tip to surface, producing a line pattern. Eigler used this to spell "IBM" with 35 Xe atoms on a Ni(110) surface in 1990.

4. **Material Requirements (1 Mark):** Both tip and substrate must be **electrically conductive** (metals, doped Si, HOPG). Non-conductors are inaccessible to STM lithography without a conductive coating.

---

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **instrumentation and working of an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)**. How is it used for **mechanical lithography (scratching)** and **anodic oxidation lithography**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **AFM Principle (2 Marks):** A sharp tip mounted on a flexible cantilever is scanned over a surface. Interatomic forces (repulsive at contact, attractive at separation < 1 nm) deflect the cantilever. Deflection is measured by reflecting a laser beam off the cantilever backside onto a position-sensitive photodiode (PSPD). The signal is fed to a feedback loop that adjusts the Z-piezo to maintain a **setpoint force**.

2. **Block Diagram (1 Mark):** [See Section 4.1 for the equivalent block-level architecture — Tip + Cantilever → Laser → PSPD → Feedback → Z-Piezo → Sample.]

3. **AFM Instrumentation (1 Mark):**
   - Microfabricated Si/Si₃N₄ cantilever (typical $k$ = 0.01–50 N/m, $f_0$ = 10–500 kHz).
   - Piezoelectric scanner.
   - Optical deflection sensor (laser + PSPD).
   - Computer with image processing.

4. **Mechanical Lithography (Scratching) (1.5 Marks):**
   - The tip is operated in **contact mode** with a high normal load (1–100 µN), well above the imaging threshold.
   - The tip is moved along a programmed path, **plowing a groove** into a soft resist (e.g., PMMA, LB film).
   - Groove width ≈ tip radius (10–30 nm); depth depends on load and number of scans.
   - **No development step is needed** — the pattern is directly physical.

5. **Anodic Oxidation Lithography (1.5 Marks):**
   - A **conductive (Pt-Ir or Au-coated) tip** is biased negative (-5 to -15 V) with respect to a substrate (Si, Ti, Ta).
   - A **water meniscus** adsorbed on the surface acts as the electrolyte.
   - The strong local electric field + water electrolysis produces $\text{O}^-$ and $\text{OH}^-$ ions that react with the substrate to form a **local oxide** (SiO₂, TiO₂, etc.).
   - Oxide thickness $d_{ox} \approx 2 \text{ nm/V} \times V$.
   - The oxide pattern serves as a **mask for subsequent wet etching** (e.g., KOH etches Si ~1000× faster than SiO₂).

---

**PART B – QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle and mechanism of Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)**. Discuss the resolution, materials, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **Principle (2 Marks):** DPN uses an AFM tip as a "nanoscale pen" to deliver "ink" molecules (e.g., alkanethiols, proteins, DNA, alkoxysilanes) to a substrate ("paper") via a **water meniscus** that forms by capillary condensation.
   - The tip is first **inked** by dipping into a solution of the molecule of interest.
   - The tip is then brought into contact with the substrate (e.g., Au for thiols).
   - **Molecular transport** occurs from tip → meniscus → substrate.

2. **Mechanism (2 Marks):**
   - Molecules diffuse across the water meniscus (thickness ≈ 1–10 nm) and adsorb on the substrate to form a **self-assembled monolayer (SAM)**.
   - The diffusion length is $L_D = \sqrt{D \tau}$, where $D$ is the surface diffusivity and $\tau$ is the contact time.
   - The pattern is built by rastering the tip (or holding it stationary for varying times to vary dot size).
   - Ink transport rate depends on relative humidity, tip-substrate contact force, ink chemistry, and temperature.

3. **Resolution and Materials (1 Mark):**
   - **Resolution:** ~15 nm linewidth, < 5 nm pitch demonstrated; smallest dots < 5 nm.
   - **Inks:** alkanethiols, biomolecules (proteins, DNA), silanes, polymers, metal salts.
   - **Substrates:** Au (for thiols), SiO₂ (for silanes), mica, etc.

4. **Advantages (1 Mark):**
   - **Chemical specificity** (any molecule can be "ink").
   - Operates in ambient conditions.
   - Direct-write, no resist processing.
   - Compatible with biological molecules (gentle conditions).

5. **Limitations (1 Mark):**
   - Slow serial writing (though 1D cantilever arrays exist: 55,000 tips in parallel — "polymer pen lithography").
   - Patterning of 2D areas is time-consuming; mostly limited to small features and arrays.
   - Ink may diffuse uncontrollably; sensitive to humidity.

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast **STM, AFM, and DPN** lithography with respect to resolution, substrate requirement, mechanism, and typical applications. Why is Scanning Probe Lithography considered essential for **nanoscience research** despite its low throughput? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | STM | AFM | DPN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Tunneling current | Mechanical / electrical / thermal force | Capillary molecular transport |
| Resolution | 1–20 nm | 5–50 nm | 15–50 nm |
| Substrate | Conductive only | Any (insulator ok) | Surface-chemistry specific (Au, SiO₂) |
| Environment | UHV–ambient | Ambient | Ambient (humid controlled) |
| Application | Atom manipulation, atomic switches | NEMS, mask repair, oxide patterning | Biosensors, molecular electronics |
| Throughput | Extremely low | Low | Moderate (1D arrays) |

2. **Why SPL is essential despite low throughput (4 Marks):**
   - **Atomic resolution:** No other technique can manipulate single atoms/molecules routinely.
   - **Direct-write, maskless:** Ideal for **prototyping and research**, not mass production.
   - **Multi-modal:** A single AFM tool can image AND pattern the same area — closed-loop nanomanufacturing.
   - **Quantum device fabrication:** SPL is the only practical way to fabricate single-electron transistors, single-molecule devices, and quantum dots with control over **individual atomic positions**.
   - **Educational and research value:** SPL platforms (especially AFMs) are the workhorse of every nanoscience laboratory.
   - **Complementary to photolithography:** For features below ~10 nm, photolithography is limited by wavelength; SPL bridges the gap for research and prototyping.
   - **Repair and customization:** SPL is the only practical way to repair nanometer-scale defects in masks and devices.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [x] **SPL = direct-write, maskless, near-field patterning** using a sharp tip.
- [x] **Tunneling current equation:** $I_t = I_0 e^{-2\kappa d}$, with $\kappa \approx 1$ Å⁻¹ for typical work functions. 1 Å gap change → ~10× current change.
- [x] **STM needs conductive substrate**; AFM does not.
- [x] **AFM mechanical lithography** = plowing grooves with high force; no development needed.
- [x] **AFM anodic oxidation** = biased tip + water meniscus + negative bias → local oxide → etch mask. Oxide thickness ≈ 2 nm/V.
- [x] **Cantilever spring constant** (rectangular): $k = E w t^3 / (4 L^3)$.
- [x] **DPN (Mirkin, 1999):** water meniscus is the conduit for molecular transport; resolution ~15 nm; works for SAMs, proteins, DNA.
- [x] **NSOM** = sub-wavelength optical aperture for near-field exposure of photoresist.
- [x] **Eigler (1990):** Wrote "IBM" with 35 Xe atoms on Ni using STM.
- [x] **Millipede (IBM):** Thermo-mechanical SPL → 1 Tb/in² storage demonstration.
- [x] **Resolution limit:** roughly $\Delta x \approx \sqrt{R_{tip} \cdot d}$ — sharper tip = better resolution.
- [x] **Throughput is the biggest weakness** of all SPL techniques; use parallel arrays (polymer pen lithography) to scale up.
- [x] **SPL is for research, prototyping, and repair** — not for industrial mass production.
- [x] **Remember the four key interactions:** tunneling (STM), force (AFM), capillary (DPN), photon-near-field (NSOM).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Photolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Photolithography** (also termed **Optical Lithography** or **UV Lithography**) is a microfabrication and nanopatterning technique that transfers a geometric pattern from a **photomask** onto a photosensitive polymer film (called a **photoresist**) coated on a substrate (typically a silicon wafer), through selective exposure to electromagnetic radiation (usually ultraviolet light), followed by chemical development to reveal the desired topographical or chemical pattern.

The technique is the **cornerstone of the semiconductor industry** and is extensively used in nanofabrication laboratories for creating features ranging from **micrometers (μm)** down to the **deep sub-100 nm regime**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Technical Terms (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)**
> 
> | Term | Definition |
> |------|------------|
> | **Photoresist** | A light-sensitive organic polymer that undergoes chemical modification upon UV exposure |
> | **Photomask (Reticle)** | A transparent plate (usually fused quartz) with opaque (chrome) patterns that define the geometry to be transferred |
> | **Exposure Dose** | The amount of radiant energy incident on the photoresist, measured in **mJ/cm²** |
> | **Resolution (R)** | The smallest feature that can be reliably patterned, typically expressed in **nanometers (nm)** |
> | **Numerical Aperture (NA)** | A dimensionless number characterizing the light-gathering ability of the optical projection system |
> | **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | The tolerance in the vertical (z-axis) position of the wafer for acceptable imaging |

### Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — "The Stencil Sun-Print Method"**
> 
> Imagine you want to draw a perfect, intricate pattern on a dark sheet of paper. If you tried freehand, you'd make mistakes. Instead, imagine:
> 
> 1. You cover the paper with a **special invisible ink** that only "activates" (turns invisible into visible) when sunlight hits it (**photoresist coating**).
> 2. You place a **stencil (photomask)** with cut-out patterns on top of the paper (**mask alignment**).
> 3. You shine a flashlight (UV light source) through the stencil. The light passes only through the cut-out areas and "activates" the ink underneath (**exposure**).
> 4. You sprinkle a special powder (**developer**) that sticks only to the *unactivated* regions. You shake off the excess, and your pattern is perfectly replicated (**development**).
> 
> Photolithography is essentially this same idea — but executed at the **nanoscale** with extraordinary precision, where the "sunlight" is **deep-UV or extreme-UV (EUV)** light, the "paper" is a silicon wafer, and the "invisible ink" is a sophisticated polymer like **poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)** or **SU-8**.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Wavelengths Used in Modern Photolithography**
> 
> - **g-line**: $\lambda = \mathbf{436 \text{ nm}}$ (Mercury lamp, older technology)
> - **i-line**: $\lambda = \mathbf{365 \text{ nm}}$ (Mercury lamp, mainstream 1990s)
> - **KrF Excimer Laser**: $\lambda = \mathbf{248 \text{ nm}}$ (Deep UV, ~250 nm node)
> - **ArF Excimer Laser**: $\lambda = \mathbf{193 \text{ nm}}$ (Deep UV, current production standard down to 7 nm via immersion)
> - **ArF Immersion**: $\lambda = \mathbf{193 \text{ nm}}$ with water ($\text{n} = 1.44$) — effective $\lambda \approx \mathbf{134 \text{ nm}}$
> - **EUV (Extreme UV)**: $\lambda = \mathbf{13.5 \text{ nm}}$ (Current leading-edge, sub-7 nm nodes)
> 
> **Standard Acceleration Voltage**: **5–100 keV** (for electron-beam lithography as a complement)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Photolithographic Process — Operational Logic

The photolithography process is a sequence of carefully controlled unit operations. Each step has a precise thermal, chemical, or mechanical purpose. Below is a structured breakdown:

#### Step 1: Substrate Preparation (Cleaning & Dehydration)
- **Logic**: Organic residues, native oxides, and particulates destroy pattern fidelity. The substrate (typically a **4", 6", 8", or 12" silicon wafer**) is cleaned using **RCA-1** (NH₄OH + H₂O₂) and **RCA-2** (HCl + H₂O₂) chemical baths.
- **Why**: A pristine, **hydrophobic** surface ensures uniform photoresist adhesion.
- **Common practice**: **Hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS)** priming is applied to promote resist-to-substrate adhesion by forming self-assembled monolayers.

#### Step 2: Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating)
- **Logic**: A precise volume (typically **1–5 mL**) of liquid photoresist is dispensed onto the wafer, which is then spun at high RPM (typically **1000–6000 rpm**) for **30–60 seconds**.
- **Why**: Centrifugal force spreads the resist into a uniform thin film. Film thickness $t$ depends on spin speed $\omega$ and resist viscosity $\eta$ via the empirical relationship:
$$t \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Two Types of Photoresist**
> 
> | Type | Behavior | Example | Use Case |
> |------|----------|---------|----------|
> | **Positive Photoresist** | Exposed regions become **soluble** in developer | **PMMA, AZ-series, S1800** | Most IC fabrication; sharper sidewalls |
> | **Negative Photoresist** | Exposed regions become **insoluble** (crosslink) | **SU-8, ma-N 2400, NR7** | MEMS, thick structures, high-aspect-ratio patterns |

#### Step 3: Soft Bake (Pre-Exposure Bake)
- **Logic**: The wafer is heated to **90–120°C** for **60–120 seconds** on a hotplate.
- **Why**: Evaporates residual solvent, densifies the resist film, improves adhesion, and stabilizes the film for exposure.

#### Step 4: Alignment & Exposure
- **Logic**: The photomask is precisely aligned to the wafer using **alignment marks**, and the wafer is exposed to UV radiation through the mask. Two primary exposure modes:
  - **Contact / Proximity Printing**: Mask directly contacts or sits close to wafer. Simple but mask-damaging.
  - **Projection Printing**: A high-NA lens system demagnifies (typically **4× or 5×**) the mask image onto the wafer. This is the **industry standard** for advanced nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The exposure follows the Beer-Lambert Law**:
> $$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$$
> where $I_0$ is the incident intensity, $\alpha$ is the resist absorption coefficient, and $z$ is the depth into the resist. For positive resists, the **critical dose** $D_c$ (typically **50–200 mJ/cm²** for g/i-line) defines the minimum energy needed for full solubility.

#### Step 5: Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)
- **Logic**: Wafer heated to **100–130°C** for **60–90 seconds**.
- **Why**: For chemically amplified resists (CARs), this step drives the **photoacid-catalyzed deprotection reaction**, which is critical for high-resolution patterning at 193 nm and below.

#### Step 6: Development
- **Logic**: The wafer is immersed in or sprayed with a developer solution:
  - **TMAH (Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide), 0.26 N, 2.38%** for positive resists.
  - **Xylene or SU-8 developer** for negative resists.
- **Why**: Selectively dissolves either the exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions, transferring the latent image into a physical relief pattern.

#### Step 7: Hard Bake
- **Logic**: Heat treatment at **120–180°C** for **30–120 seconds**.
- **Why**: Hardens the remaining resist, improving its etch resistance.

#### Step 8: Pattern Transfer (Etching or Ion Implantation)
- **Logic**: The patterned resist serves as a mask for:
  - **Wet Etching**: Chemical solutions (e.g., HF for SiO₂, KOH for Si).
  - **Dry Etching (RIE/ICP)**: Plasma-based anisotropic etching.
  - **Ion Implantation**: Doping with B, P, As ions at **5–200 keV**.

#### Step 9: Photoresist Stripping
- **Logic**: Remove the residual photoresist using **acetone, NMP, O₂ plasma ashing**, or commercial strippers like **Piranha solution** (H₂SO₄ + H₂O₂).

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examinations**
> 
> | # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
> |---|---------|-------------|------------------|
> | 1 | $R = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | **Rayleigh Resolution** — minimum resolvable feature | $\lambda$ in nm; NA dimensionless |
> | 2 | $DOF = k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}$ | **Depth of Focus** | $\lambda$ in nm; depends on $NA^{2}$ |
> | 3 | $I(z) = I_{0} \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$ | **Beer-Lambert Absorption** in resist | $I_{0}$ = incident intensity |
> | 4 | $t = \dfrac{k \cdot C \cdot \eta^{a}}{\omega^{b}}$ | **Spin-coating thickness** (empirical) | $\omega$ = angular velocity (RPM) |
> | 5 | $NA = n \cdot \sin \theta$ | **Numerical Aperture** of projection lens | $n$ = refractive index of medium |
> | 6 | $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{n_{\text{medium}}}$ | **Immersion lithography** effective wavelength | $n_{\text{water}} \approx 1.44$ |
> | 7 | $D = \text{Exposure Time} \times \text{Intensity}$ | **Exposure Dose** | mJ/cm² |
> | 8 | $MTF(\nu) = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \left[\arccos\left(\dfrac{\nu}{\nu_{c}}\right) - \dfrac{\nu}{\nu_{c}}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\nu}{\nu_{c}}\right)^{2}}\right]$ | **Modulation Transfer Function** | $\nu$ = spatial frequency |
> | 9 | $k_{1} = \dfrac{R \cdot NA}{\lambda}$ | **Process Difficulty Factor** | Typically 0.4 (production), 0.25 (theoretical) |
> | 10 | $M = \dfrac{\text{Mask Feature}}{\text{Wafer Feature}}$ | **Reduction Magnification** | Typically 4× or 5× |

> **Process k-factors (Rayleigh constants):**
> - $k_1 \approx 0.25$ (theoretical minimum, classical limit)
> - $k_1 \approx 0.4$ (high-volume production, modern fabs)
> - $k_2 \approx \pm 0.5$ (depth of focus tolerance)

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] **Where Photolithography is Indispensable**
> 
> 1. **VLSI / ULSI Integrated Circuits**: Every transistor in modern CPUs/GPUs (Intel, TSMC, Samsung) is patterned using 193 nm immersion or EUV lithography. A modern **Apple M-series chip** contains **>50 billion transistors** fabricated via photolithography.
> 2. **MEMS / NEMS Devices**: Accelerometers, gyroscopes, microphones, and pressure sensors.
> 3. **Photonic Crystals & Metasurfaces**: Sub-wavelength patterning for optics.
> 4. **Flat Panel Displays**: TFT-LCD and OLED patterning on glass substrates.
> 5. **Biochips / Lab-on-a-Chip**: Microfluidic channels for medical diagnostics.
> 6. **Quantum Devices**: Superconducting qubits, quantum dot arrays.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Rayleigh Resolution Limit

The minimum resolvable feature size in a projection lithography system is derived from the **Fraunhofer diffraction** theory applied to a circular aperture (the lens). When two point sources are imaged, the **Airy disk** formed by one source defines the resolution limit.

**Starting Point — Airy Disk First Minimum:**
For a circular aperture of diameter $D$ illuminated by light of wavelength $\lambda$, the angular position of the first diffraction minimum is:
$$\sin \theta = \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$$

**Step 1**: Express the aperture diameter $D$ in terms of Numerical Aperture. By definition, the NA of an optical system with focal length $f$ is:
$$NA = n \cdot \sin \alpha \approx \frac{D}{2f}$$
where $\alpha$ is the half-angle of the maximum cone of light that can enter the lens. Solving for $D$:
$$D = 2 f \cdot NA$$

**Step 2**: Substitute $D$ into the Airy equation:
$$\sin \theta = \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{2 f \cdot NA}$$

**Step 3**: In the focal plane, the lateral distance $r$ from the optical axis to the first Airy minimum is:
$$r = f \cdot \sin \theta = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

**Step 4**: By the **Rayleigh criterion**, two point sources are considered "just resolved" when the maximum of one Airy pattern coincides with the first minimum of the other. This separation equals the Rayleigh resolution $R$:
$$\boxed{R = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}}$$

where $k_1 \approx 0.61$ in the classical Rayleigh sense, and $k_1 \approx 0.4$ in modern production processes due to **Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RETs)** such as **Optical Proximity Correction (OPC)**, **Phase-Shifting Masks (PSM)**, and **Off-Axis Illumination (OAI)**.

---

### Derivation 2: Depth of Focus (DOF)

**Starting Point**: Consider the wavefront curvature tolerance at the image plane. A point at the focal plane produces a perfect Airy pattern. A point shifted by $\pm \Delta z$ along the optical axis produces a wavefront error.

**Step 1**: The optical path difference (OPD) at the edge of the lens aperture (radius $r$) due to a defocus $\Delta z$ is:
$$\Delta W = \Delta z \cdot (1 - \cos \alpha) \approx \frac{\Delta z \cdot \alpha^{2}}{2} \quad \text{(for small } \alpha\text{)}$$

**Step 2**: Since $NA = n \cdot \sin \alpha \approx n \cdot \alpha$ for small angles, we have $\alpha \approx NA / n$. Substituting:
$$\Delta W \approx \frac{\Delta z \cdot NA^{2}}{2 n^{2}}$$

**Step 3**: By the Rayleigh quarter-wave criterion, the maximum tolerable OPD is $\lambda/4$. Setting $\Delta W = \lambda / 4$:
$$\frac{\Delta z \cdot NA^{2}}{2 n^{2}} = \frac{\lambda}{4}$$

**Step 4**: Solving for the total tolerance $\Delta z$ (twice the half-tolerance, since defocus can occur in either direction):
$$\Delta z = \frac{\lambda \cdot n^{2}}{2 \cdot NA^{2}}$$

For air ($n = 1$), this gives the **Depth of Focus**:
$$\boxed{DOF = \pm \, k_{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}}$$
where $k_2 = \pm 0.5$ for the classical Rayleigh criterion. In production, $k_2 \approx \pm 0.15$ to $\pm 0.30$ is more realistic.

---

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE] **Problem**: A stepper uses an **ArF laser** ($\lambda = 193 \text{ nm}$) with an immersion lens having $NA = 1.35$ and immersion water with $n = 1.44$. Calculate: (i) the resolution, (ii) the depth of focus, (iii) the effective wavelength in the immersion medium.

**Solution**:

**(i) Effective Wavelength:**
$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n_{\text{water}}} = \frac{193 \text{ nm}}{1.44} \approx 134 \text{ nm}$$

**(ii) Resolution (assuming $k_1 = 0.4$):**
$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda_{\text{eff}}}{NA} = 0.4 \cdot \frac{134 \text{ nm}}{1.35} \approx 39.7 \text{ nm}$$

**(iii) Depth of Focus (assuming $k_2 = 0.5$, air-equivalent):**
$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_0}{NA^{2}} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193 \text{ nm}}{(1.35)^{2}} \approx 0.5 \cdot \frac{193}{1.8225} \approx 52.96 \text{ nm}$$

**Conclusion**: This is approximately the lithographic regime used to manufacture **5 nm and 3 nm logic nodes** in commercial foundries.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Lithographic Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT] **Comprehensive Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)**
> 
> | Parameter | UV Photolithography | EUV Lithography | Electron-Beam (e-beam) | Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL) |
> |-----------|--------------------|-----------------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
> | **Wavelength / Probe** | 193–365 nm | 13.5 nm | 0.005–0.05 nm (electron) | None (mechanical) |
> | **Resolution** | ~38 nm (immersion) | ~8–13 nm | <10 nm | ~10 nm |
> | **Throughput** | High (100+ wafers/hr) | Moderate (~100 wafers/hr) | Very low (serial) | High |
> | **Mask Cost** | Moderate | Very High (~$300K) | None (direct-write) | Moderate (mold) |
> | **Capital Cost** | Very High (~$80M per tool) | Extreme (~$200M+ per tool) | High (~$5–10M) | Moderate |
> | **NA Range** | 0.5–1.35 | 0.33–0.55 (NA-EUV future: 0.75) | N/A (electron optics) | N/A |
> | **Best For** | Mature nodes (28 nm and above) | Leading-edge (<7 nm) | R&D, photomask making, small volumes | Patterning of media, photonics |

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Examinations**
> 
> - The **light source** (typically a laser) provides monochromatic, coherent radiation.
> - The **condenser** uniformizes the illumination across the mask field.

> - The **objective lens** demagnifies and projects the pattern onto the wafer.
> - The **alignment system** uses **laser interferometry** to maintain overlay accuracy of **<2 nm** between successive lithography layers.
> - The **wafer stage** moves in **X, Y, Z, and θ** (rotation) with sub-nanometer precision.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **Define photolithography. Differentiate between positive and negative photoresists with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Photolithography is a nanopatterning technique that uses ultraviolet light to transfer a geometric pattern from a photomask onto a light-sensitive polymer (photoresist) coated on a substrate, enabling the fabrication of micro- and nano-scale features used in semiconductor and MEMS devices.

| Feature | Positive Photoresist | Negative Photoresist |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Exposure Effect** | Exposed regions become **soluble** | Exposed regions become **insoluble** (crosslinked) |
| **Developer Action** | Dissolves **exposed** regions | Dissolves **unexposed** regions |
| **Final Image** | Same as mask pattern | Inverse of mask pattern |
| **Resolution** | Higher (preferred for IC) | Lower (swelling issues) |
| **Example** | **PMMA, S1813, AZ5214** | **SU-8, NR7-1500P** |
| **Application** | VLSI/ULSI circuits | MEMS, thick microstructures |

**[3 Marks — KTU Standard Mark Distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks differentiation table]**

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]

> **State and explain the Rayleigh criterion for resolution in photolithography. How is it modified for production processes?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Rayleigh criterion** states that the minimum resolvable feature size $R$ in an optical lithography system is given by:

$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

where $\lambda$ is the exposure wavelength, $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the projection lens, and $k_1$ is a process-dependent factor.

- **Classical (Airy) Limit**: $k_1 = 0.61$ (derived from diffraction theory for a circular aperture).
- **Production Reality**: $k_1 \approx 0.4$ due to Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RETs).

**Methods to reduce $k_1$ in production:**
1. **Optical Proximity Correction (OPC)** — adds sub-resolution serifs/assist features to mask.
2. **Phase-Shifting Masks (PSM)** — introduces $\pi$-phase shifts to improve contrast.
3. **Off-Axis Illumination (OAI)** — uses dipole/quadrupole illumination to enhance resolution.

**[3 Marks — KTU Standard Mark Distribution: 1 mark equation + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark modification]**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You are given an **internal choice** — you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Describe the complete step-by-step process of photolithography with a neat process flow diagram. Explain the role of soft bake and post-exposure bake. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for resolution and depth of focus in a projection lithography system. A 193 nm immersion lithography system uses water ($n = 1.44$) and a lens of NA = 1.35. Calculate the minimum resolvable feature size and depth of focus (use $k_1 = 0.4$, $k_2 = 0.5$). **[7 Marks]**

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

The complete photolithographic process consists of the following sequential unit operations:

1. **Wafer Cleaning**: The silicon substrate is cleaned using RCA-1 and RCA-2 chemical baths to remove organic contaminants, native oxide, and metallic impurities. HMDS priming follows.

2. **Photoresist Coating**: Liquid photoresist is dispensed onto the wafer and spin-coated at 1000–6000 rpm to form a uniform film of 100–500 nm thickness.

3. **Soft Bake (Pre-Bake)**: The wafer is heated to **95–120°C** for 60–120 seconds.
   - **Role**: Removes residual solvent, reduces film stress, improves adhesion to substrate, and stabilizes the resist for exposure.

4. **Alignment and Exposure**: The photomask is aligned to existing patterns on the wafer using alignment marks. UV light is exposed through the mask, transferring the latent image into the resist. For chemically amplified resists, exposure generates photoacid.

5. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)**: The wafer is heated to **110–130°C** for 60–90 seconds.
   - **Role**: Drives the photoacid-catalyzed deprotection reaction in chemically amplified resists. Controls diffusion length, which directly affects line-edge roughness (LER) and critical dimension (CD).

6. **Development**: The wafer is immersed in TMAH developer (0.26 N). Exposed regions (positive resist) dissolve, revealing the pattern.

7. **Hard Bake**: Heating to 120–180°C to densify the resist and improve etch resistance.

8. **Pattern Transfer**: The patterned resist serves as an etch mask or implant mask.

9. **Stripping**: Residual photoresist is removed using acetone, NMP, or O₂ plasma ashing.

**Process Flow Diagram** (as drawn in Section 4 above; this earns 2 marks if drawn correctly in the exam).

**[7 Marks Distribution: 1 mark step listing + 2 marks soft bake/PEB explanation + 2 marks diagram + 2 marks completeness of process]**

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Derivation of Resolution** (as in Section 3, Derivation 1):

For a circular lens aperture, the Airy disk's first minimum defines resolution. Following the Fraunhofer diffraction analysis:
$$R = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

**Derivation of Depth of Focus** (as in Section 3, Derivation 2):

A defocus $\Delta z$ introduces wavefront error $\Delta W = \frac{\Delta z \cdot NA^2}{2n^2}$. By the Rayleigh quarter-wave tolerance $\Delta W = \lambda/4$:
$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Effective wavelength in water:
$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n_{\text{water}}} = \frac{193 \text{ nm}}{1.44} \approx 134.03 \text{ nm}$$

Resolution:
$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda_{\text{eff}}}{NA} = 0.4 \cdot \frac{134.03}{1.35} \approx 39.71 \text{ nm}$$

Depth of Focus:
$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_0}{NA^2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193}{(1.35)^2} \approx 52.96 \text{ nm}$$

**Final Answers**:
- $\boxed{R \approx 39.7 \text{ nm}}$
- $\boxed{DOF \approx 53.0 \text{ nm}}$

**[7 Marks Distribution: 2 marks derivation (R) + 2 marks derivation (DOF) + 2 marks numerical evaluation + 1 mark final answers with units]**

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> - **Pitfall 1**: Many students forget to convert $\lambda$ to $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$ in immersion systems. **Always divide by $n_{\text{medium}}$** when using immersion lithography.
> - **Pitfall 2**: Using $k_1 = 0.61$ (Airy) instead of $k_1 = 0.4$ (production). The question must specify; if unspecified, the production value is preferred in modern context.
> - **Pitfall 3**: Failing to draw the process flow diagram in part (a) loses **2 marks** immediately. Always include a labeled diagram.
> - **Pitfall 4**: Confusing **soft bake** (pre-exposure) with **post-exposure bake**. Soft bake removes solvent; PEB drives photoacid chemistry.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]

> **(a)** Explain the principle of immersion lithography. How does it extend the resolution limit compared to dry lithography? Discuss the engineering challenges. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compare photolithography with electron-beam lithography and nanoimprint lithography. Discuss at least four distinguishing parameters. **[7 Marks]**

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Principle of Immersion Lithography:**

In immersion lithography, the air gap between the final optical element of the projection lens and the wafer photoresist is replaced with a high-refractive-index liquid, typically **ultra-pure deionized water** ($n = 1.44$ at 193 nm) or **high-index fluids** (e.g., $n \approx 1.65$).

**How it Extends Resolution:**

From the resolution formula:
$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{n \cdot \sin \theta}$$

The wavelength in the medium becomes $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda_0 / n$. For water at 193 nm:
$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \frac{193 \text{ nm}}{1.44} \approx 134 \text{ nm}$$

This effectively reduces the wavelength by ~30%, improving resolution proportionally. Additionally, since $NA = n \cdot \sin \theta$, higher $n$ allows higher NA values (exceeding 1.0, theoretically up to $n$).

**Engineering Challenges:**
1. **Bubble Formation**: Tiny bubbles in the immersion liquid cause scattering defects; requires degassed water and precise fluid dynamics.
2. **Fluid Contamination**: Resist leaching into water causes lens contamination; requires immersion-specific top coats.
3. **Temperature Control**: Water absorption at 193 nm is temperature-sensitive; needs ±0.01°C stability.
4. **Index Mismatch**: At lens-water-wafer interfaces, index mismatches cause reflections and polarization effects.
5. **Mechanical Stability**: Scanning at high speeds (>500 mm/s) while maintaining a uniform 1–2 mm water film is challenging.

**[7 Marks Distribution: 2 marks principle + 2 marks resolution extension + 3 marks challenges]**

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Comparison Table** (as in Section 3, Analytical Matrix):

| Parameter | Photolithography | Electron-Beam Lithography | Nanoimprint Lithography |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Probe/Mechanism** | UV light (193–365 nm) | Focused electron beam | Mechanical deformation of resist |
| **Resolution** | ~38 nm (immersion) | <10 nm (direct-write) | ~10 nm (mold-limited) |
| **Throughput** | Very high (parallel projection) | Very low (serial writing) | High (parallel stamping) |
| **Mask Required?** | Yes (photolithographic mask) | No (direct-write, but slow) | Yes (template/mold, but cheaper) |
| **Application** | Mass IC production | R&D, photomask making, low-volume | Photonics, data storage, biochips |

**Key Distinguishing Points (minimum 4 required):**

1. **Resolution vs. Throughput Trade-off**: Photolithography is mass-production-friendly; e-beam has the highest resolution but is slow.

2. **Mask vs. Maskless**: Photolithography needs expensive photomasks; e-beam is maskless; NIL uses inexpensive hard molds.

3. **Cost Structure**: Photolithography has huge capital cost (~$80–200M per tool); NIL is far cheaper (~$1–5M).

4. **Physical Mechanism**: Photolithography is optical (uses photons); e-beam uses electron-matter interactions; NIL is purely mechanical (no optics).

5. **Overlay Accuracy**: Photolithography excels (<2 nm overlay); e-beam is moderate; NIL is moderate but improving.

**[7 Marks Distribution: 4 marks for comparison table + 3 marks for distinguishing points]**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Photolithography (Module 4)**
> 
> - **Definition**: Photolithography = light-driven pattern transfer from mask to photoresist.
> - **9-step process**: Cleaning → Spin Coat → Soft Bake → Align/Expose → PEB → Develop → Hard Bake → Etch/Implant → Strip.
> - **Two resist types**: Positive (exposed = soluble) and Negative (exposed = insoluble).
> - **Key equations**:
>   - Resolution: $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$
>   - Depth of Focus: $DOF = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$
>   - NA = $n \sin \theta$
>   - Immersion: $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \lambda_0 / n$
> - **Modern standard**: ArF immersion at 193 nm, $NA \approx 1.35$, $k_1 \approx 0.4$ → ~38 nm features.
> - **Future direction**: EUV at 13.5 nm, High-NA EUV (NA = 0.55 → 0.75).
> - **RETs** (Resolution Enhancement Techniques): OPC, PSM, OAI.
> - **Industry leaders**: TSMC (3 nm), Samsung (3 nm GAA), Intel (20A/18A nodes).
> - **Common pitfall**: Forgetting to divide $\lambda$ by $n$ in immersion problems.
> - **Memorize the wavelengths**: g-line (436 nm), i-line (365 nm), KrF (248 nm), ArF (193 nm), EUV (13.5 nm).
> - **Photoresist examples**: PMMA (positive, e-beam), SU-8 (negative, thick), S1813 (positive, g/i-line), AZ5214 (image-reversal).
> - **Dimensional units**: $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 10 \text{ \AA}$. The current leading edge is **<3 nm** nodes.
> - **Spin-coating relationship**: Thickness decreases with the square root of RPM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: E-Beam Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** E-Beam Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **E-Beam Lithography (EBL)** is a specialized **direct-write, mask-less lithographic technique** that uses a finely focused beam of accelerated electrons (typically accelerated to **1 – 200 kV**) to expose a resist-coated substrate, creating user-defined nanoscale patterns with sub-10 nm resolution.

In the language of the KTU 2024 PECHT753 syllabus (Module 4 – *Advanced Patterning Techniques at the Nanoscale*), EBL is classified as a **charged-particle lithography** process belonging to the family of *direct-writing serial nanolithography* methods. It is the foundational technique for fabricating nanostructures, photomasks for optical lithography, X-ray masks, and templates for nanoimprint lithography.

**Core Operating Principle:** The technique exploits the extremely short de Broglie wavelength of accelerated electrons (≈ **0.005 nm at 50 kV**), which is roughly **100,000 times shorter than the 193 nm wavelength of deep-UV lithography**, thereby overcoming the optical diffraction limit entirely.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Precision Pen-Writer"**

Imagine you have a **very fine, invisible "needle-pen"** that can draw on a tiny canvas:
- Instead of a normal ink pen, the "ink" is a stream of **high-energy electrons**.
- Instead of moving your hand, **electromagnetic lenses act as invisible lenses** that focus and steer the electron "needle" with nanometer precision.
- The canvas is a **special polymer (resist)** that changes its chemistry wherever the electrons touch it.
- A **computer-controlled pattern generator** plays the role of your brain, instructing the pen where to draw.
- The whole drawing happens inside a **vacuum chamber**, because electrons would be scattered by air molecules (just as dust would scatter sunlight in a movie projector).

Just as an artist with a fine brush can paint miniature masterpieces, EBL uses focused electrons to "draw" patterns that are invisible to the naked eye — used in everything from smartphone chips to quantum devices.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Numbers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Constants and Metrics:**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Accelerating Voltage | **1 – 200 kV** (commonly 50, 100 kV) | Controls penetration depth and beam spot size |
| Electron Wavelength (λ) | **~ 0.005 nm at 50 kV** | Far below optical wavelengths — no diffraction limit |
| Resolution | **Sub-10 nm** (down to **2 nm** demonstrated) | Smallest feature practically achievable |
| Beam Current | **10 pA – 100 nA** | Trade-off between resolution and throughput |
| Vacuum Required | **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ Torr** | Prevents electron scattering by gas molecules |
| Stage Positioning Accuracy | **± 1 – 2 nm** (laser interferometer) | Critical for stitching and overlay |
| Field Size | **~ 100 μm × 100 μm to 1 mm × 1 mm** | Area written before stage movement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 4 emphasizes that EBL, despite being the highest-resolution lithography, suffers from **low throughput** due to its **serial writing nature** — a key comparison point with optical/EUV lithography.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Chain of an EBL System

The system operates through a tightly choreographed sequence of physical phenomena:

- **Step 1 — Electron Generation:** Electrons are emitted from a cathode via thermionic emission (W, LaB₆) or field emission (cold FEG / Schottky FEG) inside an ultra-high vacuum.
- **Step 2 — Acceleration:** Electrons are accelerated by a high negative potential (**1 – 200 kV**) towards a grounded anode. The accelerating voltage **V** determines the final kinetic energy: **E = eV**.
- **Step 3 — Beam Conditioning:** Condenser lenses (magnetic or electrostatic) collimate the divergent electron beam; apertures trim the beam diameter and define beam current.
- **Step 4 — Beam Blanking:** An electrostatic beam blanker rapidly deflects the beam into a Faraday cup when the system must "lift the pen" while moving between pattern regions.
- **Step 5 — Beam Deflection & Focusing:** Electromagnetic deflection coils scan the beam across the writing field in a raster or vector pattern under computer control.
- **Step 6 — Exposure:** The focused electron beam strikes the resist, where scattering (forward + backscatter) deposits energy and breaks/forms polymer chains.
- **Step 7 — Development:** A chemical developer dissolves either the exposed (positive resist) or unexposed (negative resist) regions, leaving the 3-D pattern.
- **Step 8 — Pattern Transfer:** The developed pattern is transferred into the substrate by **reactive ion etching (RIE)**, **ion-beam etching**, or **lift-off metallization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:**
> - **Why vacuum?** Air molecules would scatter electrons and ruin resolution.
> - **Why high voltage?** Higher voltage → shorter wavelength → smaller spot → higher resolution. Also, higher voltage penetrates deeper (useful for thick resists and dense substrates).
> - **Why apertures?** They control beam current and shape, balancing resolution against writing speed.

## 2.2 Types of Electron Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three primary electron source categories are tested in KTU exams:**

| Source Type | Material | Brightness (A/cm²·sr) | Energy Spread | Cathode Life | Cost |
|-------------|----------|------------------------|---------------|--------------|------|
| **Thermionic (W hairpin)** | Tungsten filament | ~ 10⁵ | 1 – 3 eV | ~ 100 hrs | Low |
| **Thermionic (LaB₆)** | Lanthanum hexaboride | ~ 10⁶ | 1 – 2 eV | ~ 1000 hrs | Medium |
| **Field Emission — Schottky** | ZrO₂/W | ~ 10⁸ | 0.7 – 1 eV | > 5000 hrs | High |
| **Field Emission — Cold FEG** | W(310) single crystal | ~ 10⁹ | 0.2 – 0.4 eV | > 10000 hrs | Very High |

**Higher brightness + lower energy spread = smaller achievable spot size = higher resolution.** This is why modern research-grade EBL tools use Schottky or cold FEG sources.

## 2.3 Resists Used in EBL

- **Positive Resists (most common):**
  - **PMMA (Poly-methyl methacrylate)** — high resolution (~10 nm), low sensitivity, most widely used.
  - **ZEP-520** — high sensitivity and etch resistance.
- **Negative Resists:**
  - **HSQ (Hydrogen Silsesquioxane)** — ultra-high resolution (< 5 nm), excellent for dense patterns.
  - **ma-N 2400 series** — good etch durability.
- **Chemically Amplified Resists (CARs):** e.g., UV-3 (Shipley), FEP-171 — much higher sensitivity but suffer from **post-exposure delay** and LER issues.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU-relevant formulas for E-Beam Lithography:**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variable Description | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p}$$ | de Broglie wavelength (non-relativistic, foundational) | m |
| 2 | $$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$ | Non-relativistic approximation, V in volts (V ≪ 2 m_e c²/e ≈ 10⁶ V) | m |
| 3 | $$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{e V}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$ | Relativistic-corrected wavelength | m |
| 4 | $$\lambda \approx \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6}\, V\right)}}\;\text{nm}$$ | Practical electron wavelength (V in volts) | nm |
| 5 | $$f(r) = \dfrac{1}{\pi(1+\eta)} \left[\dfrac{1}{\alpha^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{r^2}{\alpha^2}\right) + \dfrac{\eta}{\beta^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{r^2}{\beta^2}\right)\right]$$ | Double-Gaussian proximity function; α = forward-scatter radius, β = backscatter radius, η = backscatter coefficient | nm⁻² |
| 6 | $$d_{min} \propto \lambda^{3/4}$$ | Empirical resolution dependence (Broers formula) | m |
| 7 | $$t = \dfrac{D \times Q}{I}$$ | Exposure time, D = dose (C/cm²), Q = pattern area, I = beam current | s |
| 8 | $$D = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{A}$$ | Exposure dose definition | C/cm² |
| 9 | $$d_{spot} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{2} d_0\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{2} C_s \alpha^3\right)^2 + \left(C_c \dfrac{\Delta E}{E}\alpha\right)^2 + d_g^2}$$ | Gaussian beam spot size (source size, spherical aberration, chromatic aberration, gaussian image) | m |

**Where:**
- **h** = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **mₑ** = electron rest mass = **9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
- **e** = elementary charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **c** = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Cₛ** = spherical aberration coefficient, **C_c** = chromatic aberration coefficient
- **ΔE / E** = fractional energy spread of the source

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Table Formatting:** Absolute-value and similar notations are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (not the bare pipe character) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

EBL is the **workhorse for R&D and low-volume production** of nanodevices:
1. **Photomask Fabrication** — virtually all advanced photomasks (EUV/ArF) are written using EBL.
2. **Quantum Device Fabrication** — single-electron transistors, quantum dots, Josephson junctions.
3. **Photonics & Plasmonics** — sub-wavelength metamaterials, optical antennas.
4. **Nanoelectronics Beyond CMOS** — nanowires, 2-D material (graphene, MoS₂) patterning.
5. **Template Master Creation** — masters for nanoimprint lithography.
6. **MEMS / NEMS** — high-aspect-ratio nanostructures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Industrial Limitation:** EBL throughput is roughly **10⁵ – 10⁶ times slower** than optical steppers, restricting it to **research, mask-making, and niche production** rather than high-volume manufacturing (HVM).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Electron de Broglie Wavelength

This derivation is **directly tested in KTU Module 4 numerical and descriptive questions**.

### Step 1 — Start with de Broglie's hypothesis

Every particle with momentum *p* has an associated wavelength given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

where **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** is Planck's constant. This step asserts that an accelerated electron, being a quantum particle, behaves like a wave of wavelength *λ*.

### Step 2 — Relate kinetic energy to accelerating voltage

When an electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference **V**, the work done by the electric field equals its final kinetic energy (non-relativistic regime, V ≪ 10⁶ V):

$$E_k = eV = \frac{p^2}{2 m_e}$$

*Conversion logic:* The electron (charge **e**) traverses potential V, gaining energy **eV** from the field. Equating kinetic energy to the standard non-relativistic form **p²/(2 mₑ)** gives momentum.

### Step 3 — Solve for momentum

Rearranging:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$$

*Conversion logic:* Taking the square root of both sides isolates p. We discard the negative root because momentum magnitude is non-negative.

### Step 4 — Substitute back into de Broglie relation

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

*Conversion logic:* Direct substitution of the expression for p into λ = h/p yields the foundational non-relativistic wavelength formula.

### Step 5 — Introduce relativistic correction

For accelerating voltages exceeding ~ 30 kV, the electron speed becomes a significant fraction of the speed of light. Relativistic momentum is:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \frac{e V}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}$$

*Conversion logic:* The factor in parentheses arises from the Taylor expansion of the relativistic kinetic energy: **E_k = (γ − 1) mₑ c²**, with γ expanded for γ ≈ 1 + (eV)/(2 mₑ c²) at low velocities. The total energy E = γ mₑ c², so the relativistic momentum squared is:

$$\begin{aligned}
p^2 c^2 &= E^2 - m_e^2 c^4 \\
E &= m_e c^2 + eV \\
p^2 &= \frac{(m_e c^2 + eV)^2 - m_e^2 c^4}{c^2} \\
p^2 &= \frac{2 m_e e V + e^2 V^2 / c^2 \cdot c^2}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{c^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — let's simplify more carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
p^2 c^2 &= (m_e c^2 + eV)^2 - m_e^2 c^4 \\
&= m_e^2 c^4 + 2 m_e c^2 eV + e^2 V^2 - m_e^2 c^4 \\
&= 2 m_e c^2 eV + e^2 V^2 \\
&= 2 m_e eV \left(c^2 + \frac{e V}{2 m_e}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing both sides by c²:

$$p^2 = 2 m_e eV \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)$$

Hence the **relativistically-corrected wavelength**:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{e V}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

### Step 6 — Practical numerical formula (V in volts → λ in nm)

Inserting the physical constants (h, mₑ, e, c) into the relativistic formula and simplifying yields the **working formula** used by engineers:

$$\lambda \approx \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6}\, V\right)}}\;\text{nm}$$

*Conversion logic:* The constants **h / √(2 mₑ e) ≈ 1.227 nm·√V** when V is in volts, and **e / (2 mₑ c²) ≈ 0.978 × 10⁻⁶ V⁻¹**. This compact formula is what EBL software uses internally.

### Step 7 — Numerical example (typical exam value)

For **V = 50 kV = 50,000 V**:

$$\lambda \approx \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{50{,}000 \times (1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \times 50{,}000)}}\;\text{nm}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator factor} &= 50{,}000 \times (1 + 0.0489) \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 1.0489 \\
&\approx 52{,}445 \\
\sqrt{52{,}445} &\approx 229.0 \\
\lambda &\approx \frac{1.227}{229.0} \\
&\approx 0.00536 \;\text{nm} \;\approx\; 5.36 \;\text{pm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** At 50 kV, the electron wavelength is ≈ **5 pm = 0.005 nm** — about **36,000 times shorter than the 193 nm ArF optical wavelength**. This is why EBL is **not diffraction-limited** and can in principle resolve sub-nm features; in practice, resolution is limited by **electron scattering in the resist** and **aberrations of the electromagnetic lenses**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Double-Gaussian Proximity Function

When an electron penetrates the resist and substrate, it undergoes two main types of scattering that deposit unwanted dose in regions adjacent to the intended exposure — the **proximity effect**.

### Step 1 — Define two scattering contributions

- **Forward scattering (radius α, ~ 5 – 30 nm):** Low-angle scattering within the resist; deposits dose within a small radius of the incident beam.
- **Backscattering (radius β, ~ 1 – 10 μm):** Electrons scattered back from the substrate with large angles; deposit dose over a much larger radius.

### Step 2 — Write the energy deposition profile as a sum of two Gaussians

Each scattering process gives a Gaussian intensity profile. The total normalized energy deposition per unit area at radial distance r from the beam axis is:

$$f(r) = \frac{1}{\pi (1+\eta)} \left[ \frac{1}{\alpha^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2}{\alpha^2}\right) + \frac{\eta}{\beta^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2}{\beta^2}\right) \right]$$

*Conversion logic:*
- The first Gaussian (forward-scatter) is normalized by **1/α²** so its 2-D integral equals 1.
- The second Gaussian (backscatter) has amplitude scaled by **η** (backscatter coefficient, typically **0.5 – 0.9**) relative to the forward term.
- The overall factor **1 / [π(1 + η)]** normalizes the *sum* to integrate to 1 over the entire 2-D plane.

### Step 3 — Total dose received at any point

The total dose at a point **r** from an exposure is the convolution of the proximity function with the pattern:

$$D_{\text{total}}(\mathbf{r}) = \iint f(\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert) \, \phi(\mathbf{r}') \, d^2\mathbf{r}'$$
