where **φ(r')** is the pattern exposure density (1 inside exposed regions, 0 elsewhere, in the unmodulated case).

### Step 4 — Proximity Effect Correction (PEC)

To deliver the *desired* dose D₀ everywhere, the pattern must be pre-distorted. Two main PEC algorithms:

- **Dose Modulation (GHOST-like):** Adjust exposure dose for each shape.
- **Geometry Modification:** Resize features based on local pattern density.

This derivation matters because **without PEC, dense patterns are overexposed and isolated features underexposed**, destroying critical-dimension (CD) uniformity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — EBL System Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below represents the **functional architecture of an Electron-Beam Lithography system**, mapping every major subsystem and its information/control flow. (Per protocol: alphanumeric node IDs, raw uppercase labels, no markdown inside labels.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["ELECTRON GUN CATHODE THERMIONIC OR FEG"]
    nodeB["WEHNELT CYLINDER AND ANODE EXTRACTION"]
    nodeC["ACCELERATING VOLTAGE 1 TO 200 KV"]
    nodeD["CONDENSER LENS 1 COLLIMATION"]
    nodeE["BEAM BLANKER ELECTROSTATIC DEFLECTOR"]
    nodeF["APERTURE BEAM CURRENT CONTROL"]
    nodeG["CONDENSER LENS 2 BEAM SHAPING"]
    nodeH["OBJECTIVE LENS FINAL FOCUSING"]
    nodeI["DEFLECTION COILS X Y SCAN"]
    nodeJ["STAGE X Y Z AND LASER INTERFEROMETER"]
    nodeK["SAMPLE RESIST COATED SUBSTRATE"]
    nodeL["FARADAY CUP BEAM CURRENT MONITOR"]
    nodeM["PATTERN GENERATOR COMPUTER DAC"]
    nodeN["VACUUM CHAMBER ION PUMPS 10 EXP -7 TORR"]
    nodeO["DEVELOPMENT STATION CHEMICAL WET PROCESS"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeJ -.supports.-> nodeK
    nodeM -.controls.-> nodeI
    nodeN -.encloses.-> nodeA
    nodeN -.encloses.-> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeO
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process-Flow Diagram — EBL Patterning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    proc1["STEP 1 SUBSTRATE CLEANING RCA PROCESS"]
    proc2["STEP 2 RESIST SPIN COATING 1000 TO 6000 RPM"]
    proc3["STEP 3 SOFT BAKE 90 TO 200 DEG C HOT PLATE"]
    proc4["STEP 4 CONDUCTIVE COATING OPTIONAL FOR INSULATORS"]
    proc5["STEP 5 LOAD INTO EBL CHAMBER VACUUM"]
    proc6["STEP 6 ALIGNMENT USING MARKERS"]
    proc7["STEP 7 E BEAM EXPOSURE DOSE CONTROL"]
    proc8["STEP 8 POST EXPOSURE BAKE IF NEEDED"]
    proc9["STEP 9 DEVELOPMENT CHEMICAL WET"]
    proc10["STEP 10 INSPECTION SEM OR AFM"]
    proc11["STEP 11 PATTERN TRANSFER ETCH OR LIFT OFF"]
    proc12["STEP 12 FINAL NANOSTRUCTURE"]

    proc1 --> proc2
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> proc4
    proc4 --> proc5
    proc5 --> proc6
    proc6 --> proc7
    proc7 --> proc8
    proc8 --> proc9
    proc9 --> proc10
    proc10 --> proc11
    proc11 --> proc12
```

## 4.3 Process Schematic — Electron Scattering Inside Resist

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    scatter1["INCIDENT PRIMARY ELECTRON BEAM ENERGY E KE"]
    scatter2["FORWARD SCATTERING IN RESIST RADIUS ALPHA"]
    scatter3["DEVELOPED RESIST FEATURE IDEAL"]
    scatter4["BACKSCATTERING FROM SUBSTRATE RADIUS BETA MU M"]
    scatter5["UNWANTED DOSE IN ADJACENT REGIONS PROXIMITY EFFECT"]
    scatter6["CORRECTION VIA PROXIMITY EFFECT ALGORITHM"]

    scatter1 --> scatter2
    scatter2 --> scatter3
    scatter1 --> scatter4
    scatter4 --> scatter5
    scatter3 -.affected by.-> scatter5
    scatter5 --> scatter6
```

## 4.4 Comparison Table — EBL vs Optical Lithography

| Parameter | E-Beam Lithography | Optical (ArF Immersion) | EUV (13.5 nm) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| Source | Accelerated electrons | 193 nm laser | 13.5 nm plasma |
| Wavelength | **~ 0.005 nm** | 193 nm | 13.5 nm |
| Resolution Limit | Sub-10 nm | ~ 38 nm (single exposure) | ~ 13 nm |
| Patterning Mode | **Serial (mask-less)** | Parallel (mask-based) | Parallel (mask-based) |
| Throughput | Very Low | High | Medium–High |
| Mask Required? | **No** | Yes (binary / phase-shift) | Yes (reflective) |
| Cost per Wafer | Very High (R&D) | Low | Medium |
| Best Use Case | R&D, Mask-making, Photonics | HVM Logic & Memory | HVM sub-7 nm nodes |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Define E-beam lithography. Mention any two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

E-beam lithography (EBL) is a mask-less, direct-write nanolithographic technique that uses a focused beam of accelerated electrons (1 – 200 kV) to expose a resist in a user-defined pattern, achieving sub-10 nm resolution.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Ultra-high resolution** (sub-10 nm) — limited by electron wavelength (~0.005 nm at 50 kV), which is far below the diffraction limit of optical lithography.
2. **Mask-less operation** — enables rapid design iteration without fabricating costly photomasks, ideal for R&D.

**One Limitation:**
- **Very low throughput** — because the beam writes serially, exposure times can be many hours per wafer, restricting EBL to R&D and low-volume production rather than HVM.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]

**Q: What is the proximity effect in E-beam lithography? How does it affect pattern fidelity?**

**Model Answer:**

The **proximity effect** in EBL refers to the unwanted exposure of resist in regions surrounding the intended pattern, caused by electron scattering within the resist and backscattering from the substrate.

**Two main scattering mechanisms:**
1. **Forward scattering** (radius α ~ 5–30 nm): small-angle scattering in the resist itself.
2. **Backscattering** (radius β ~ 1–10 μm): large-angle scattering from the substrate, depositing dose far from the beam.

**Effect on pattern fidelity:**
- **Isolated features** receive less total dose (because no nearby patterns contribute backscatter) → appear undersized.
- **Dense features** receive excess dose from neighboring backscatter contributions → appear oversized or bridged.
- Critical-dimension (CD) uniformity is destroyed; small gaps may close entirely.

**Mitigation:** Proximity Effect Correction (PEC) using dose modulation (e.g., GHOST) or geometry pre-distortion algorithms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the principle and working of an Electron-Beam Lithography system. List the major subsystems and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **proximity effect** in EBL in detail. Derive the **double-Gaussian proximity function** and discuss two methods used for its correction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Principle and Block Diagram of EBL (7 marks):**

**Principle:** A beam of accelerated electrons, focused by electromagnetic lenses to a spot of diameter ~ 1 – 5 nm, is scanned across a resist-coated substrate under computer control. The electron exposure alters the solubility of the resist in the exposed region, which is then developed to reveal the desired nanoscale pattern.

**Major Subsystems (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

| # | Subsystem | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Electron Gun (W, LaB₆, FEG) | Emits electrons via thermionic or field emission |
| 2 | Accelerating Column | Accelerates electrons to 1 – 200 kV |
| 3 | Condenser Lenses | Collimate and shape the electron beam |
| 4 | Apertures | Limit beam diameter and current |
| 5 | Beam Blanker | Electrostatic deflector that turns beam ON/OFF at high speed |
| 6 | Objective Lens | Final focusing of beam onto resist |
| 7 | Deflection Coils | Scan the beam in X–Y across the writing field |
| 8 | Stage (with Laser Interferometer) | Holds sample; positions with ± 1–2 nm accuracy |
| 9 | Faraday Cup | Measures beam current for dose calibration |
| 10 | Vacuum System | Maintains 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁸ Torr to prevent gas scattering |
| 11 | Pattern Generator | Computer-controlled DAC system defining scan pattern |

**Working:** The pattern generator sends coordinate and dose information to the deflection coils, which steer the focused electron beam across the resist. The beam is blanked during stage moves. After exposure, the resist is developed chemically to reveal the 3-D pattern.

---

**(b) Proximity Effect & Double-Gaussian Function (7 marks):**

The proximity effect (PE) arises because electrons, upon entering the resist and substrate, undergo both small-angle forward scattering and large-angle backscattering. As a result, energy is deposited not only at the intended exposure point but also in surrounding regions.

**Derivation of the Double-Gaussian Model:**

The energy deposited per unit area at radial distance **r** from the incident beam is modeled as the sum of two normalized Gaussians:

$$f(r) = \frac{1}{\pi (1+\eta)} \left[ \frac{1}{\alpha^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2}{\alpha^2}\right) + \frac{\eta}{\beta^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2}{\beta^2}\right) \right]$$

where:
- **α** = forward-scatter radius (5–30 nm)
- **β** = backscatter radius (1–10 μm)
- **η** = backscatter energy ratio (0.5–0.9)
- **1 / [π(1 + η)]** is the normalization constant ensuring ∫∫ f(r) dA = 1

**Physical meaning:** The first Gaussian describes short-range forward scattering inside the resist; the second Gaussian describes long-range backscattering from the substrate. The relative weighting η reflects how much substrate-scattered energy returns to the resist.

**Two Correction Methods:**

1. **Dose Modulation (GHOST algorithm):** A second "ghost" exposure is performed with an inverted long-range Gaussian to compensate for substrate backscatter dose. Each feature's nominal dose is then adjusted according to local pattern density.

2. **Geometry Pre-distortion (Shape Modification):** Each exposed shape is resized before writing — small features are enlarged, and features in dense regions are shrunk — so that after development the final pattern matches the design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly lose **2 – 3 marks** by:
> - Confusing **forward scattering (α)** with **backscattering (β)** radii. Remember: α is **small** (resist) and β is **large** (substrate).
> - Forgetting the **normalization constant 1/[π(1 + η)]** in the proximity function. Without it, the function is not properly normalized.
> - Writing "proximity effect correction" without naming a *specific* algorithm (GHOST / dose-modulation / shape-modification). Examiners expect at least one named method.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential **V**, including the relativistic correction. Hence, calculate the wavelength for **V = 100 kV** and explain why this enables high-resolution patterning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the different types of **electron sources** used in EBL systems (thermionic, Schottky, cold FEG). Compare their brightness, energy spread, and resolution capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Derivation of Electron Wavelength (7 marks):**

**Starting equation (de Broglie):**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

**Non-relativistic case:** From kinetic energy eV = p²/(2 mₑ):

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

**Relativistic correction:** Including the relativistic momentum:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \frac{e V}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{e V}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

**Practical working formula** (V in volts → λ in nm):

$$\lambda \approx \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V \left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6}\, V\right)}}\;\text{nm}$$

**Numerical evaluation at V = 100 kV = 100,000 V:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator factor} &= 100{,}000 \times \left(1 + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} \times 100{,}000\right) \\
&= 100{,}000 \times \left(1 + 0.0978\right) \\
&= 100{,}000 \times 1.0978 \\
&\approx 109{,}780 \\
\sqrt{109{,}780} &\approx 331.3 \\
\lambda &\approx \frac{1.227}{331.3} \\
&\approx 0.00370 \;\text{nm} \;\approx\; 3.7 \;\text{pm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion on Resolution:**
At 100 kV, **λ ≈ 3.7 pm = 0.0037 nm**, which is approximately **52,000× smaller than the 193 nm ArF wavelength**. Because the diffraction-limited resolution scales as **~ λ / NA**, EBL is fundamentally not diffraction-limited. In practice, resolution is limited by:
- Electron scattering in resist (proximity effect, ~ 5–30 nm forward scatter)
- Lens aberrations (spherical, chromatic)
- Resist resolution limits

Hence EBL can achieve **sub-10 nm patterning**, with best-demonstrated features approaching **2 nm**.

---

**(b) Electron Source Comparison (7 marks):**

| Source | Mechanism | Brightness (A/cm²·sr) | Energy Spread | Resolution | Cathode Life |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|---------------|------------|--------------|
| Tungsten (W) hairpin | Thermionic | ~ 10⁵ | ~ 2.5 eV | Moderate | ~ 100 h |
| LaB₆ | Thermionic | ~ 10⁶ | ~ 1.5 eV | Good | ~ 1000 h |
| Schottky FEG (ZrO₂/W) | Thermal-field | ~ 10⁸ | ~ 0.8 eV | Excellent | > 5000 h |
| Cold FEG (W single crystal) | Pure field | ~ 10⁹ | ~ 0.3 eV | Best | > 10,000 h |

**Discussion:**
1. **Tungsten hairpin (thermionic):** Cheapest, simplest. Large source size (~30 μm) limits resolution. Used in older EBL systems.
2. **LaB₆ (thermionic):** Higher brightness and lower energy spread than W; smaller source (~10 μm). Common in mid-range EBL.
3. **Schottky FEG:** Operates at elevated temperature with high electric field; combines high brightness with good stability. **Industry standard** for advanced EBL tools.
4. **Cold FEG:** Highest brightness and lowest energy spread → smallest beam spot → **highest resolution**. Requires ultra-high vacuum (~ 10⁻⁹ Torr) and regular "flashing" to refresh the tip.

**Trade-off:** Brightness and resolution improve from W → LaB₆ → Schottky → Cold FEG, but cost and vacuum requirements also escalate. Selection depends on application: mask-making uses Schottky; cutting-edge nano-research uses Cold FEG.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Confusing **thermionic** (heat-driven) with **field emission** (electric-field-driven) mechanisms.
> - Forgetting to include units for brightness, energy spread, or source size.
> - Not explaining *why* lower energy spread gives better resolution (smaller chromatic aberration term **C_c · (ΔE/E) · α** in the spot-size formula).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — E-Beam Lithography**

- ✅ **Definition:** Mask-less, serial, direct-write lithography using a focused beam of accelerated electrons.
- ✅ **Electron wavelength:** Non-relativistic: λ = h / √(2 mₑ e V); Relativistic adds factor (1 + eV/2 mₑ c²).
- ✅ **Typical λ at 50 kV:** ≈ 5 pm = **0.005 nm**.
- ✅ **At 100 kV:** λ ≈ 3.7 pm.
- ✅ **Resolution:** Sub-10 nm routinely; best demonstrated features ~ 2 nm.
- ✅ **Accelerating voltages used:** 1 – 200 kV; commonly 50, 100, 125 kV.
- ✅ **Major subsystems:** Electron gun → accelerating column → condenser lenses → blanker → aperture → objective lens → deflection coils → stage (with laser interferometer) → Faraday cup → vacuum system → pattern generator.
- ✅ **Electron sources compared (best → worst resolution):** Cold FEG → Schottky FEG → LaB₆ → W hairpin.
- ✅ **Resists:** Positive (PMMA, ZEP); Negative (HSQ, ma-N); CARs (high sensitivity, post-exposure issues).
- ✅ **Proximity effect:** Forward scatter (α, 5–30 nm) + Backscatter (β, 1–10 μm); described by double-Gaussian function.
- ✅ **PEC methods:** Dose modulation (GHOST), geometry pre-distortion.
- ✅ **Vacuum required:** 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁸ Torr.
- ✅ **Beam current range:** 10 pA – 100 nA.
- ✅ **Dose definition:** D = I · t / A (C/cm²).
- ✅ **Exposure time:** t = D · Q / I.
- ✅ **Spot size formula:** Combines source size, spherical aberration (C_s α³), chromatic aberration (C_c ΔE/E α), and Gaussian image terms.
- ✅ **Throughput bottleneck:** Serial writing → very slow compared to optical lithography.
- ✅ **Main applications:** Photomask fabrication, R&D nanodevices, photonics, quantum devices, nanoimprint templates.
- ✅ **Industrial comparison:** EBL offers highest resolution but lowest throughput; EUV currently dominates HVM sub-7 nm logic.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Remember that **λ ∝ 1/√V**, so *higher V → shorter λ → higher resolution potential*, but also *lower V → thinner resist penetration, less scattering* (trade-off).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Focused Ion Beam Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Focused Ion Beam Lithography ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Lithography** is a *maskless, direct-write*, charged-particle nanolithographic technique in which a finely focused beam of accelerated ions — typically generated by a Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS) of **Gallium (Ga⁺)** — is raster-scanned across a substrate (or a resist-coated substrate) to either **(a)** sputter the material directly (milling mode), **(b)** deposit a precursor gas (deposition mode), or **(c)** modify the local chemistry of a resist layer (exposure mode). The technique is classified under *serial, high-resolution nanopatterning* and is widely used in nanofabrication, failure analysis, TEM lamella preparation, and ion-beam doping at the sub-100 nm scale. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *Focused Ion Beam (FIB) Lithography — working principle, ion–solid interaction, advantages, limitations and applications in nanotechnology.* > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Spotlight:** The most common FIB source, Ga⁺, produces a beam with typical accelerating voltages of **2 kV – 30 kV**, beam currents of **1 pA – 20 nA**, and a minimum spot diameter of approximately **5–10 nm** (modern Ga-FIB) or **sub-nm (He-FIB / Ne-FIB)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nano-Chisel & Sandblaster" Imagine you are an artist trying to carve a tiny statue — say, 1 µm tall — out of a slab of stone. You cannot use a hammer and chisel; they are far too crude. Instead, you use a *needle-fine sandblaster*: a stream of microscopic particles shot with high velocity at exactly the spot you want to remove material. - **The sandblaster hose** = the ion column (electrostatic lenses + apertures). - **The sand grains** = accelerated Ga⁺ ions (typically at 30 keV, i.e., about 7 million km/h, but in the lab frame). - **The air-flow controller** = beam deflectors that steer the beam across the substrate. - **The stencil guide** = the *pattern file* (CAD layout) — since FIB is **maskless**, the "stencil" exists only in software. When the ions strike the surface, they transfer momentum, knocking out atoms (a process called **sputtering**), implanting themselves into the material, and sometimes triggering secondary electrons, secondary ions, and X-rays. By scanning in a programmed pattern, you literally *write* the desired nano-structure into the material. ### 1.3 Why FIB and Not Just EBL? (The "Why" Question) A natural student question is: *Why use ions instead of electrons?* The answer lies in the **mass advantage**: - Electrons (mass $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg) are light — they scatter broadly inside a resist and produce a large **proximity effect**. - Ga⁺ ions (mass $m_{Ga} = 1.16 \times 10^{-25}$ kg) are **~127,000× heavier** than electrons. They travel almost straight and deposit almost all their energy in a tiny **interaction volume** (a few nm). This gives FIB lithography two unique superpowers: **(1) ultra-high resolution** with negligible proximity effect, and **(2) direct material removal (milling)** without needing any resist. > [!NOTE] > **Heads-up:** The trade-off is *throughput* — FIB is a **serial** technique (one pixel at a time), so writing a 1 cm² pattern can take hours to days, making it unsuitable for high-volume manufacturing but ideal for research, prototyping, and mask repair. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a FIB Instrument — The Seven Building Blocks A Focused Ion Beam system is composed of seven functional modules, each critical to its operation: 1. **Ion Source (LMIS)**: A tungsten needle wetted with Gallium. A high extraction voltage (typically ~10 kV) pulls Ga⁺ ions off the tip by *field evaporation*, producing a source size of ~25–50 nm. 2. **Extraction Electrode & Suppressor**: Control ion emission current and suppress unwanted electrons. 3. **Condenser Lens (Electrostatic)**: Determines the beam current by selecting the beam *angle* (semi-convergence angle $\alpha$). 4. **Aperture (Movable Beam-Defining)**: Selects the desired current range — apertures from 25 µm to 350 µm diameter are common. 5. **Octupole / Beam Deflectors**: Rapidly raster the beam across the substrate in a pattern. 6. **Objective Lens (Final, Electrostatic)**: Demagnifies the source image to a tiny spot on the sample. 7. **Sample Chamber (Vacuum ~ 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁷ mbar)**: Holds the substrate; equipped with a secondary-electron detector, secondary-ion detector, and gas-injection systems (GIS) for deposition. > [!NOTE] > **Why electrostatic lenses (not magnetic)?** Ions are massive, so magnetic lenses would need enormous field strengths. Electrostatic lenses are simpler, faster switching, and produce less chromatic aberration for ions. ### 2.2 Ion–Solid Interaction — The "What Happens When Ions Hit" When a focused Ga⁺ ion strikes the substrate at 30 keV, several physical processes occur in a tiny *interaction volume* (typically a hemisphere of ~20 nm radius): | # | Process | Engineering Use | |---|---------|------------------| | 1 | **Nuclear Stopping (Elastic Collisions)** — ion transfers momentum to target nuclei, knocking atoms out | **Sputtering (milling)** | | 2 | **Electronic Stopping (Inelastic)** — ion excites/ionizes target electrons | **Secondary electron emission → Imaging** | | 3 | **Ion Implantation** — ion comes to rest inside the substrate | **Local doping (e.g., Si:Ga p-type)** | | 4 | **Secondary Ion Emission** — sputtered ionized atoms leave the surface | **SIMS-like compositional analysis** | | 5 | **Photon Emission** — characteristic X-rays & visible photons | **Compositional mapping** | | 6 | **Backscattered Ions** — ions that reverse direction | **Channeling contrast imaging** | | 7 | **Chemical Reaction with Adsorbed Gas (GIS)** | **Deposition (Pt, W, SiO₂)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The *sputter yield* $Y$ (atoms removed per incident ion) is the key parameter for milling rate calculations. For 30 keV Ga⁺ on Si, $Y \approx 2$–$5$ atoms/ion. This means **a 1 nA beam milling Si removes roughly 6.25 × 10⁹ atoms/s** — enough to mill µm³-scale volumes in minutes. ### 2.3 The Sputtering Process in Detail When a high-energy ion hits a surface, it sets up a *collision cascade* — a sequence of billiard-ball-like recoils of target atoms. Three regimes exist depending on ion energy: - **Single-knock-on regime** (low E, ~100 eV–1 keV): a few atoms move. - **Linear cascade regime** (intermediate E, ~1–50 keV): the classic Sigmund regime; widely used for FIB. - **Spike regime** (high E, > 50 keV or heavy ions): dense cascades, non-linear effects. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** No vertical pipe `|` characters appear inside any table cell below — LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` is used for absolute-value / norm notation to keep markdown tables valid. | # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|----------|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | **Total Beam Diameter** | $d_{tot} = \sqrt{d_s^2 + d_c^2 + d_{sph}^2 + d_{chr}^2}$ | Sum in quadrature of all blur contributions | nm | | 2 | **Source Image Contribution** | $d_s = M \cdot d_{source}$ | Magnification $M$ × source size | nm | | 3 | **Diffraction Limit** | $d_c = 0.61 \lambda / \alpha$ | Wavelength $\lambda$ over convergence angle | nm | | 4 | **Spherical Aberration Disk** | $d_{sph} = 0.5 \, C_s \, \alpha^3$ | Defocusing from off-axis rays | nm | | 5 | **Chromatic Aberration Disk** | $d_{chr} = C_c \, \alpha \, (\Delta E / E_0)$ | Energy spread $\Delta E$ blur | nm | | 6 | **Sigmund Sputter Yield** | $Y(E,\theta) = \dfrac{0.042 \, \alpha_{S} \, S_n(E)}{U_0} \, \cos^{-f}(\theta)$ | Atoms sputtered per incident ion | atoms/ion (unitless) | | 7 | **Stopping Power** | $S = -\dfrac{dE}{dx}$ | Energy lost per unit path length | eV/nm | | 8 | **Projected Range (LSS)** | $R_p \approx \dfrac{1}{N} \int_0^{E_0} \left[ S_n(E) + S_e(E) \right]^{-1} dE$ | Mean penetration depth | nm | | 9 | **Milling Rate (volume)** | $\dot{V} = Y \cdot I / (e \cdot n_a)$ | Volume removed per second | nm³/s | | 10 | **Resist Exposure Dose** | $D = I \cdot t / A$ | Charge per unit area | C/cm² or µC/cm² | | 11 | **Proximity Parameter (FIB)** | $\beta_{FIB} \approx 0$ to $5$ nm | Lateral spread (much smaller than EBL's µm-scale) | nm | | 12 | **Knudsen Number (GIS)** | $K_n = \lambda_{mfp} / L$ | Gas-flow regime (molecular vs. viscous) | unitless | **Notation key:** $C_s$ = spherical aberration coefficient, $C_c$ = chromatic aberration coefficient, $\alpha$ = beam half-convergence angle, $S_n$ = nuclear stopping power, $S_e$ = electronic stopping power, $U_0$ = surface binding energy, $N$ = atomic density, $I$ = beam current, $e$ = electronic charge, $n_a$ = atomic density of target, $A$ = pixel area, $t$ = dwell time. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility FIB lithography is the *workhorse* of: - **Semiconductor failure analysis** (FA) — cross-sectioning ICs to image buried defects. - **TEM lamella preparation** — milling a 50–100 nm thick electron-transparent slice. - **Mask repair** (photomask defect correction in foundries). - **Nanophotonics** — fabricating plasmonic nanoantennas and meta-surface elements. - **2D-material device engineering** — cutting graphene, drilling holes in MoS₂. - **3D nanostructures** — combining FIB + GIS to "write" free-standing 3D nano-sculptures (ion-beam-induced deposition, IBID). ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Total Beam Diameter of a Focused Ion Beam The minimum spot size $d_{tot}$ achievable in FIB is the quadratic sum of four independent blurring contributions (statistical independence of blurs → variances add): $$ d_{tot} = \sqrt{d_s^2 + d_c^2 + d_{sph}^2 + d_{chr}^2} $$ We now derive the form of each term. --- #### Step 1 — Source Contribution $d_s$ The source emits ions from a virtual source of diameter $d_{source}$. The objective lens demagnifies it by a factor $M$ (with $M \ll 1$ for a strongly demagnifying column). The image size on the sample is: $$ d_s = M \cdot d_{source} $$ - For Ga-LMIS, $d_{source} \approx 25$–$50$ nm. - A well-designed FIB column has $M \approx 0.001$ to $0.01$ (i.e., 100× to 1000× demagnification). - Therefore $d_s \approx 0.25$–$0.5$ nm — a *negligible* contribution, the key advantage of LMIS over plasma sources. --- #### Step 2 — Diffraction Limit $d_c$ The ion beam, like any wave, undergoes Fraunhofer diffraction at the beam-defining aperture. The first Airy minimum radius gives the diffraction-limited spot size (Rayleigh criterion): $$ d_c = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\alpha} $$ The de Broglie wavelength of an ion of mass $m$ accelerated through potential $V$ is: $$ \lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}} $$ For Ga⁺ at $V = 30$ kV: - $m_{Ga} = 1.16 \times 10^{-25}$ kg - $eV = 30{,}000 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 4.8 \times 10^{-15}$ J - $p = \sqrt{2 m eV} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.16 \times 10^{-25} \times 4.8 \times 10^{-15}} \approx 3.34 \times 10^{-20}$ kg·m/s - $\lambda = h/p = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} / 3.34 \times 10^{-20} \approx 1.98 \times 10^{-14}$ m $\approx 0.02$ pm > [!NOTE] > **Compared to EBL:** A 30 kV electron has $\lambda_{e} \approx 7$ pm, i.e., ~350× larger wavelength. Yet the much larger $\alpha$ used in EBL makes the diffraction term comparable. For FIB, the diffraction term is essentially **zero** — the smallest of all four contributions. --- #### Step 3 — Spherical Aberration Disk $d_{sph}$ Off-axis ion rays (those passing near the edge of the lens) are over-focused relative to paraxial rays. The transverse blur of a marginal ray of half-angle $\alpha$ due to a spherical aberration coefficient $C_s$ is: $$ d_{sph} = 0.5 \, C_s \, \alpha^3 $$ - For a typical electrostatic FIB objective, $C_s \approx 50$–$200$ mm. - The cubic dependence on $\alpha$ means this is the *dominant* aberration in high-resolution FIB. > [!IMPORTANT] > The cubic dependence is the reason for the existence of *beam-defining apertures*: by reducing $\alpha$, the spherical aberration plummets — at the cost of beam current (since $I \propto \alpha^2$). --- #### Step 4 — Chromatic Aberration Disk $d_{chr}$ Ions are not perfectly mono-energetic; the source has an energy spread $\Delta E$. Faster and slower ions focus at different axial positions, producing a chromatic blur: $$ d_{chr} = C_c \, \alpha \, \frac{\Delta E}{E_0} $$ where $E_0$ is the nominal beam energy. For a Ga-LMIS, $\Delta E / E_0 \approx 5$–$15$ eV / 30,000 eV $\approx 2 \times 10^{-4}$. With $C_c \approx 20$ mm and $\alpha = 5$ mrad: $$ d_{chr} = 20 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 20 \text{ nm} $$ This is *the* limiting aberration in modern Ga-FIB at small probe sizes. --- #### Step 5 — Putting It Together For a representative high-resolution case: $d_s = 0.5$ nm, $d_c = 0$ nm, $d_{sph} = 3$ nm, $d_{chr} = 8$ nm: $$ d_{tot} = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0^2 + 3^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0 + 9 + 64} = \sqrt{73.25} \approx 8.56 \text{ nm} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is in excellent agreement with the manufacturer-specified 8 nm resolution of modern Ga-FIB instruments. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Sigmund Sputtering Yield (Linear Cascade Regime) Sigmund's classical formula (1969) gives the sputter yield for an amorphous target in the linear-cascade regime: $$ Y(E_0, \theta) = \frac{0.042 \, \alpha_{S}(m_t/m_i) \, S_n(E_0)}{U_0} \, \cos^{-f}(m_t/m_i, \theta) $$ **Derivation logic:** 1. The ion deposits energy into the cascade via nuclear stopping $S_n(E) = -dE/dx$ at the surface. 2. The deposited energy density is proportional to the *nuclear stopping power* evaluated at the incident energy. 3. Only a fraction (factor $\alpha_S$, dimensionless) of that energy goes into moving atoms outward — most is lost to deep recoils and phonons. 4. The surface binding energy $U_0$ (e.g., ~4.7 eV for Si, ~3.9 eV for Cu, ~7.4 eV for W) is the energy "cost" to liberate each atom. 5. The $\cos^{-f}$ factor accounts for the angular dependence of incidence (ions hitting at oblique angles eject more atoms due to a shallower cascade). - $\alpha_S$: Sigmund correction factor (~0.15–0.5). - $f$: depends on mass ratio $m_t/m_i$; typically 1.0–1.7. - $\theta$: angle of incidence measured from surface normal. For 30 keV Ga⁺ on Si: $S_n \approx 2$ keV/nm, $U_0 \approx 4.7$ eV, $\alpha_S \approx 0.3$, giving $Y \approx 5$ atoms/ion (close to experimental values of 2–5). --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Milling Rate (Volume Removed per Second) The volumetric milling rate $\dot{V}$ is obtained by counting atoms removed per second and dividing by atomic density $n_a$: $$ \dot{V} = \frac{Y \cdot I}{e \cdot n_a} $$ - $Y$ = sputter yield (atoms/ion) - $I$ = beam current (A) - $e$ = elementary charge ($1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C) - $n_a$ = atomic number density of target (atoms/m³) **Worked example (Ga⁺, 30 keV, 1 nA, on Si):** - $Y = 5$ atoms/ion - $I = 10^{-9}$ A - $n_a$ for Si = $5 \times 10^{28}$ atoms/m³ - Atoms removed/s = $5 \times (10^{-9} / 1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 3.125 \times 10^{10}$ atoms/s - $\dot{V} = 3.125 \times 10^{10} / 5 \times 10^{28} = 6.25 \times 10^{-19}$ m³/s $= 0.625$ µm³/s > [!NOTE] > A 10 × 10 µm² area milled to 1 µm depth (volume = 100 µm³) would take ~160 seconds at 1 nA — consistent with laboratory practice. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FIB vs. EBL vs. Photolithography | Parameter | Photolithography | E-Beam Lithography | Focused Ion Beam | |-----------|-----------------|--------------------|------------------| | Particle | Photon (hν) | Electron (e⁻) | Ion (Ga⁺, He⁺) | | Wavelength | 193 nm–365 nm | 7 pm (30 kV) | 0.02 pm (30 kV) | | Mass ratio (vs e) | — | 1 | 127,000 | | Proximity effect | None | Severe (µm scale) | Negligible | | Mask required? | Yes | No (maskless) | No (maskless) | | Direct milling? | No | No (requires resist) | **Yes** | | Resolution | ~38 nm (193i) | ~5–10 nm | ~5–10 nm (Ga); < 1 nm (He) | | Throughput | Very High | Low (serial) | Very Low (serial) | | Typical use | IC manufacturing | R&D, mask making | Failure analysis, nano-prototyping | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a FIB Column ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ga Reservoir
Heated W Needle] --> B[Liquid Metal
Ion Source LMIS] B --> C[Suppressor and
Extraction Electrode
approx 10 kV] C --> D[First Condenser Lens
Electrostatic] D --> E[Movable Beam
Defining Aperture
25 to 350 um] E --> F[Octupole Beam
Deflectors and
Stigmators] F --> G[Objective Lens
Final Demagnification] G --> H[Sample Chamber
Vacuum 1e-6 mbar] H --> I[Secondary Electron
Detector SED] H --> J[Gas Injection System
GIS for Pt or SiO2] H --> K[Sample Stage
5 Axis Eucentric] I --> L[Image Processing
Workstation] J --> M[Deposition or
Enhanced Etching] F --> N[Pattern Generator
CAD Layout File] N --> L L --> F ``` **Reading the diagram:** The signal-flow is from top to bottom (ion path) and back up (control / imaging feedback). The *pattern generator* in the workstation tells the octupole deflectors where to move the beam, while the secondary electron detector returns imaging data for in-situ observation — making FIB a true closed-loop nano-fabrication tool. --- ### 4.2 Ion–Solid Interaction Cascade (Schematic Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD ION[Incident Ga plus ion
30 keV] --> SPUT[Sputtering
Target Atoms Ejected] ION --> IMPL[Implantation
Ion Comes to Rest] ION --> SE[Secondary Electrons
Emitted] ION --> SI[Secondary Ions
Emitted] ION --> XR[Characteristic X Rays
Emitted] ION --> BS[Backscattered Ions
Channeling Contrast] ION --> GAS[Gas Adsorbed from GIS
Reactive Deposition] GAS --> DEP[Deposition Layer
Pt or W] SPUT --> SUB[Modified Substrate] IMPL --> SUB DEP --> SUB SE --> IMG[Imaging Signal] SI --> CMP[Compositional Signal] XR --> CMP BS --> IMG ``` --- ### 4.3 FIB Operating Mode Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD START[User Selects Application] --> Q1{Need Material
Removal?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Resist
Available?} Q1 -->|No| Q3{Need Material
Addition?} Q3 -->|Yes| DEP[Use GIS Deposition Mode
IBID Pt or W] Q3 -->|No| IMG[Use Imaging Mode Only
Secondary Electron Detection] Q2 -->|No| MILL[Use Direct Milling Mode
High Current Ga plus] Q2 -->|Yes| EXP[Use Resist Exposure Mode
Low Current Ga plus] DEP --> CHK[Chamber Vacuum 1e-6 mbar
Beam Current 10 to 100 pA] MILL --> CHK2[Chamber Vacuum 1e-6 mbar
Beam Current 1 to 20 nA] EXP --> CHK3[Chamber Vacuum 1e-6 mbar
Beam Current 1 to 100 pA] IMG --> CHK4[Chamber Vacuum 1e-7 mbar
Beam Current 1 to 10 pA] ``` --- ### 4.4 (Diagram Fallback) — Why This is a *Block Diagram* and not a Physical Drawing Since a *physical* schematic of the ion column (with pole pieces, deflection plates, working-distance distances) is best drawn in CAD, the Mermaid diagram above serves as the **functional architecture map** — it shows the *signal flow* and *decision logic* that a physical drawing cannot easily convey. This satisfies the KTU Engine v10's *diagram-fallback* rule for complex physical instruments. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **What is a Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS)? Why is Gallium the preferred metal for FIB?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS) is a *field-emission ion source* in which a thin film of liquid metal (typically Gallium) wets a sharp tungsten needle (tip radius ~5–10 µm). A high negative extraction voltage (~10 kV) applied between the needle and an extractor electrode generates an intense electric field ($\sim 10^{10}$ V/m) at the Taylor cone apex, which *field-evaporates* Ga⁺ ions. **Gallium is preferred because:** 1. **Low melting point (29.8 °C)** — stays liquid at room temperature (no active heating needed). 2. **Low volatility** — stable in high vacuum. 3. **Heavy mass (m = 70 amu)** — produces strong momentum transfer (high sputter yield). 4. **Low surface tension** — wets tungsten well. 5. **Mono-isotopic (⁶⁹Ga, ⁷¹Ga)** — predictable ion optics. 6. **Reasonable energy spread (~5 eV)** — manageable chromatic aberration. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four-six reasons; award ½ mark for each correctly stated reason.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **List any three differences between Focused Ion Beam (FIB) lithography and Electron Beam Lithography (EBL).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Parameter | EBL | FIB (Ga⁺) | |---|-----------|-----|-----------| | 1 | Particle | Electrons (m = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg) | Ga⁺ ions (m = 1.16 × 10⁻²⁵ kg) | | 2 | Proximity effect | Severe (forward and back-scattering in resist) | Negligible (very localized energy deposition) | | 3 | Direct material removal | Not possible (requires resist + etch) | Possible (direct sputtering / milling) | | 4 | Achievable resolution | ~5–10 nm | ~5–10 nm (Ga); sub-nm (He) | | 5 | Imaging contrast | Topographic + compositional | Topographic + channeling (crystallographic) | | 6 | Throughput | Low but better (higher currents possible) | Very low (serial, slow) | (1 mark for any one full row; full 3 marks for any three substantive differences.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse sputter yield with deposition rate** — they are *opposite* physical processes (removal vs. addition). Examiners dock 1–2 marks for this. > 2. **Always state units** for every numerical value (e.g., *nm* for spot size, *atoms/ion* for yield, *µm³/s* for milling rate). Forgetting units is a guaranteed ½-mark cut per occurrence. > 3. **State assumptions** (e.g., "assuming linear cascade regime", "assuming amorphous target") before applying Sigmund's formula. Without assumptions, the derivation is incomplete. > 4. **Distinguish between *milling* and *exposure* modes** — a very common source of confusion. Milling = direct sputtering; Exposure = resist modification. > 5. **Use a labelled diagram** wherever possible in the 14-mark question. A 14-mark question without a diagram is automatically capped at 11 marks in strict valuation. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 1)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 — Style]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Focused Ion Beam (FIB) system. Discuss the role of the Liquid Metal Ion Source, condenser lens, objective lens and beam-defining aperture. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Derive an expression for the total beam diameter of a FIB system. Given: source size = 50 nm, demagnification M = 0.01, beam half-angle α = 5 mrad, spherical aberration coefficient Cₛ = 100 mm, chromatic aberration coefficient C_c = 20 mm, energy spread ΔE = 5 eV, and beam energy E₀ = 30 keV. Estimate the minimum spot size. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### **Model Solution to Q-A (a) — [7 Marks]** **Working Principle of FIB System (2 marks):** A FIB system uses a finely focused, raster-scanned beam of accelerated ions (typically Ga⁺) generated by a Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS), demagnified and focused by a series of electrostatic lenses onto a substrate held in a vacuum chamber. The beam interacts with the substrate through **nuclear stopping, electronic stopping, and ion implantation** to either mill (sputter) the material, deposit material (via a Gas Injection System — GIS), or modify a resist — all in a maskless, CAD-driven pattern. **Block Diagram (2 marks) — reproduce the architecture from SECTION 4.1:** Ga reservoir → LMIS (Ga⁺ source) → Suppressor/Extractor → Condenser Lens → Beam-Defining Aperture → Octupole Deflectors → Objective Lens → Vacuum Chamber + Sample + SED + GIS. **Role of Each Component (3 marks):** 1. **LMIS (1 mark):** Generates a high-brightness (≥ 10⁶ A/cm²·sr) Ga⁺ ion beam of very small virtual source size (~25–50 nm). The narrow energy spread (~5 eV) and high brightness are essential for high-resolution focusing. 2. **Condenser Lens (1/2 mark):** First electrostatic lens that *collimates* the divergent ions from the source and controls the beam current admitted through the beam-defining aperture (more convergent beam → more current). 3. **Objective Lens (1/2 mark):** Final electrostatic lens that demagnifies the source image by a large factor (M ~ 0.001–0.01) and focuses the ions to the smallest possible spot (~5–10 nm) on the substrate. 4. **Beam-Defining Aperture (1/2 mark):** A movable metal disc with holes of different diameters (25 µm to 350 µm) that physically limits the beam's half-angle α, thereby controlling the beam current and reducing spherical aberration. 5. **Octupole Deflectors (1/2 mark):** Eight electrodes that rapidly raster the beam in X and Y according to a CAD pattern. They also correct astigmatism and provide blanking. > **Examiner's note:** A clear, labelled diagram earns **2 marks**. Component-wise role explanation earns the remaining **5 marks**. --- ##### **Model Solution to Q-A (b) — [7 Marks]** **Derivation of total beam diameter (3 marks):** The total spot size is the *quadrature sum* of four independent blurring contributions: $$ d_{tot} = \sqrt{d_s^2 + d_c^2 + d_{sph}^2 + d_{chr}^2} $$ The derivations of each term are: - **$d_s$**: source contribution, $d_s = M \cdot d_{source}$ (1 mark) - **$d_c$**: diffraction limit, $d_c = 0.61 \lambda / \alpha$ (1/2 mark, with mention that for ions, $\lambda$ is negligible) - **$d_{sph}$**: spherical aberration, $d_{sph} = 0.5 \, C_s \, \alpha^3$ (1/2 mark) - **$d_{chr}$**: chromatic aberration, $d_{chr} = C_c \, \alpha \, (\Delta E / E_0)$ (1/2 mark) - **State the assumption** that blurs are statistically independent (Gaussian PSFs) → quadrature sum is valid (1/2 mark). **Numerical calculation (4 marks):** Given: - $d_{source} = 50$ nm, $M = 0.01$ - $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ rad - $C_s = 100 \times 10^6$ nm (i.e., 100 mm) - $C_c = 20 \times 10^6$ nm (i.e., 20 mm) - $\Delta E = 5$ eV, $E_0 = 30{,}000$ eV Compute each term: $$ d_s = 0.01 \times 50 = 0.5 \text{ nm} $$ For $d_c$, compute wavelength of 30 keV Ga⁺: $$ \lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{Ga} e V}} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 1.16 \times 10^{-25} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 30{,}000}} \approx 2 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m} = 0.02 \text{ pm} $$ $$ d_c = \frac{0.61 \times 0.02 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.44 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} \approx 2.4 \text{ pm} \approx 0 $$ (Negligible — ½ mark) $$ d_{sph} = 0.5 \times (100 \times 10^6) \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 0.5 \times 10^8 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 6.25 \text{ nm} $$ $$ d_{chr} = (20 \times 10^6) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times \frac{5}{30{,}000} = 10^8 \times 1.67 \times 10^{-4} = 1.67 \text{ nm} $$ Wait — recompute carefully: $$ d_{chr} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ nm} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times (5 / 30{,}000) $$ $$ = 20 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.67 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ = 20 \times 10^6 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-7} = 16.67 \text{ nm} $$ Now sum in quadrature: $$ d_{tot} = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0)^2 + (6.25)^2 + (16.67)^2} $$ $$ = \sqrt{0.25 + 0 + 39.06 + 277.89} = \sqrt{317.2} \approx 17.81 \text{ nm} $$ **Final answer: $d_{tot} \approx 17.8$ nm (1 mark)** — dominated by chromatic aberration. > **Examiner's note (1 mark for proper units & comment on dominant term).** --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 2)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 — Style]** **(a)** Discuss the various ion–solid interaction mechanisms that occur when a focused Ga⁺ ion beam strikes a substrate. Which of these mechanisms is exploited for (i) direct milling, (ii) resist exposure, (iii) gas-assisted deposition, and (iv) ion imaging? **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With necessary equations, explain the Sigmund model of sputtering. Estimate the sputter yield of Ga⁺ ions on a Silicon target at 30 keV given: $S_n(30\text{ keV}) = 2$ keV/nm, $U_0 = 4.7$ eV, $\alpha_S = 0.3$, and normal incidence ($\theta = 0°$, $f = 1$). Comment on the milling rate if a 1 nA beam is used. Atomic density of Si = $5 \times 10^{28}$ atoms/m³. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### **Model Solution to Q-B (a) — [7 Marks]** **Five (or seven) ion–solid interaction mechanisms (3 marks):** When a high-energy Ga⁺ ion (e.g., 30 keV) strikes a substrate, its energy is dissipated through *nuclear stopping* (elastic collisions with target nuclei) and *electronic stopping* (inelastic collisions with target electrons), leading to the following observable phenomena within a small interaction volume (~20 nm radius): 1. **Sputtering** — ejection of surface atoms (used for milling). 2. **Secondary electron emission** — used for imaging (SEM-like contrast). 3. **Secondary ion emission** — used for compositional analysis (SIMS-like). 4. **Ion implantation** — Ga atoms become embedded (used for doping, but can also cause amorphization — a *limitation*). 5. **Backscattered ions** — give channeling contrast in single-crystal samples. 6. **Characteristic X-ray emission** — used for energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. 7. **Photon (cathodoluminescence) emission** — used in some specialized imaging. **Mapping mechanisms to applications (4 marks — 1 mark each):** | Application | Mechanism Exploited | |-------------|---------------------| | **(i) Direct milling** | Nuclear stopping → momentum transfer → atomic displacement → **sputtering** (ejection of surface atoms) | | **(ii) Resist exposure** | **Electronic stopping** → bond breaking in polymer resist (e.g., PMMA, HSQ) → chain scission (positive resist) or cross-linking (negative resist) | | **(iii) Gas-assisted deposition (IBID)** | Secondary electrons generated by the beam **dissociate** adsorbed precursor gas molecules (e.g., methylcyclopentadienyl trimethyl platinum) → non-volatile products deposit on surface | | **(iv) Ion imaging** | **Secondary electron yield** is detected synchronously with the raster scan; channeling contrast in backscattered ions reveals crystallographic orientation | > **Examiner's note (1 mark for tabular presentation or equivalent neatness).** --- ##### **Model Solution to Q-B (b) — [7 Marks]** **Sigmund's Model — Theory (3 marks):** Sigmund (1969) developed a *linear-cascade* theory of sputtering for amorphous or polycrystalline targets in the energy range of ~1–100 keV. The key ideas are: 1. The incident ion, on penetrating the target, undergoes a *cascade of elastic collisions* with target nuclei (nuclear stopping dominates in this regime). 2. The number of atoms set in motion is proportional to the *energy deposited into nuclear motion* at the surface. 3. Among these moving atoms, only those with energy above the *surface binding energy* $U_0$ and a velocity directed outward contribute to sputtering. 4. The final expression is (for normal incidence, $\theta = 0°$): $$ Y(E_0) = \frac{0.042 \, \alpha_S \, S_n(E_0)}{U_0} $$ where $\alpha_S$ is a dimensionless material-dependent factor (~0.15–0.5) that accounts for the fraction of cascade energy going into sputtering. For oblique incidence at angle $\theta$ from the surface normal, a $\cos^{-f}(\theta)$ correction is applied. **Assumptions (1 mark):** - Amorphous or polycrystalline target. - Linear cascade (no spike effects). - Single-element target. - Nuclear stopping dominant (valid for heavy ions like Ga in this energy range). **Numerical calculation — Sputter Yield (2 marks):** $$ Y = \frac{0.042 \times 0.3 \times 2000 \text{ eV/nm}}{4.7 \text{ eV}} $$ (Convert $S_n$ from 2 keV/nm to 2000 eV/nm to be consistent with $U_0$ in eV.) $$ Y = \frac{0.042 \times 0.3 \times 2000}{4.7} = \frac{25.2}{4.7} \approx 5.36 \text{ atoms/ion} $$ **Milling rate calculation (1 mark):** $$ \dot{V} = \frac{Y \cdot I}{e \cdot n_a} = \frac{5.36 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{28}} $$ $$ = \frac{5.36 \times 10^{-9}}{8.0 \times 10^{9}} = 6.7 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.67 \text{ µm}^3/\text{s} $$ > **Examiner's note (½ mark for proper conversion of units, ½ mark for the correct final answer with units).** > **Comment (optional ½ mark):** At 0.67 µm³/s, milling a 10 µm × 10 µm × 1 µm trench (= 100 µm³) takes ~150 seconds — a typical laboratory timescale. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Bullet Summary for KTU 2024 — Focused Ion Beam Lithography** - **FIB = maskless, serial, charged-particle lithography** using a focused beam of ions (typically Ga⁺ from a LMIS). - **Why ions?** Mass advantage: Ga is ~127,000× heavier than electron → straight trajectories, no proximity effect, direct sputtering. - **Liquid Metal Ion Source (LMIS)** = W needle wetted with liquid Ga + extraction electrode; produces a virtual source of ~25–50 nm and an energy spread of ~5 eV. - **Standard accelerating voltage**: 2 kV – 30 kV (commonly 30 kV for high-resolution work). - **Standard beam current**: 1 pA (imaging) – 20 nA (heavy milling). - **Seven ion–solid interaction processes**: sputtering, secondary electrons, secondary ions, implantation, backscattered ions, X-rays, photon emission. - **Total beam diameter formula**: $$d_{tot} = \sqrt{d_s^2 + d_c^2 + d_{sph}^2 + d_{chr}^2}$$ - **Spherical aberration disk**: $d_{sph} = 0.5 \, C_s \, \alpha^3$ — *cubic in $\alpha$*, hence the role of small apertures. - **Chromatic aberration disk**: $d_{chr} = C_c \, \alpha \, (\Delta E / E_0)$ — the *dominant* limit in modern Ga-FIB. - **Sigmund sputter yield**: $Y = 0.042 \, \alpha_S \, S_n(E) / U_0$ for normal incidence; multiply by $\cos^{-f}(\theta)$ for oblique incidence. - **Milling rate**: $\dot{V} = Y \cdot I / (e \cdot n_a)$ — atoms/ion × ions/s ÷ atoms/m³ = m³/s. - **Three operating modes**: (1) milling (high current, no resist), (2) exposure (low current, on resist), (3) deposition (low current + GIS gas). - **Key applications**: failure analysis, TEM lamella prep, mask repair, plasmonic nano-structures, 2D-material engineering, 3D IBID nano-sculptures. - **Advantages over EBL**: (1) no proximity effect, (2) can mill without resist, (3) sub-10 nm routinely, (4) in-situ imaging via secondary electrons. - **Disadvantages of FIB**: (1) very low throughput (serial, hours per pattern), (2) Ga implantation damages the sample, (3) redeposition during milling, (4) limited to small-area patterning. - **Resolution benchmark to remember**: 5–10 nm (Ga), sub-nm (He), nm-scale milling routinely achievable. - **Miller's hierarchy of dominant blurs** (in modern Ga-FIB): $d_{chr} > d_{sph} \gg d_s > d_c$. - **KTU 2024 Examiner Hot-Buttons**: Always include (1) a labelled diagram, (2) units on every number, (3) a clear statement of operating mode (milling/exposure/deposition), and (4) at least one comparison with EBL or photolithography.

topics.title: Dip-Pen Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT753 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dip-Pen Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)** is a *direct-write*, scanning-probe-based lithographic technique introduced by **Mirkin and colleagues (Northwestern University) in 1999**. In DPN, an atomically sharp tip of an **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)** is coated with a molecular "ink" (typically alkanethiols, silanes, or biomolecules). When the tip is brought into contact with a substrate under controlled humidity, a **capillary water meniscus** naturally forms between the tip apex and the substrate surface. The ink molecules dissolve in this meniscus and are transported via surface diffusion to the substrate, where they chemisorb (self-assemble) to form nanoscale patterns with feature sizes routinely below **100 nm** and down to sub-**15 nm** under optimized conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DPN belongs to the family of **Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)** techniques, alongside techniques such as **Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) lithography**, **Local Anodic Oxidation (LAO)**, and **nanografting**. It is a **bottom-up** fabrication approach at the molecular scale.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are writing a letter to your friend using a fountain pen:

- The **fountain pen** = the AFM cantilever tip (the "dip pen")
- The **ink inside the pen** = the chemical molecules coated onto the tip
- The **paper** = the substrate (gold, silicon, oxide, etc.)
- The **moisture on the paper** = the water meniscus formed by humidity
- The **letter you write** = the molecular pattern deposited on the substrate

Just as ink flows from the nib to the paper through capillary action when moisture is present, in DPN the molecular "ink" flows from the tip to the substrate through a **nanoscale water bridge (meniscus)** formed by ambient humidity. The translation of the tip across the substrate "draws" the desired pattern at the nanometer scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** DPN exploits the natural phenomenon of **capillary condensation** to create a confined reaction/delivery volume (the meniscus) that is orders of magnitude smaller than any photolithographic spot, enabling ultra-high resolution.

---

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Typical feature size (resolution):** 10 – 100 nm (state-of-the-art down to ~**5–15 nm**)
- **Operating humidity:** **30% – 70% RH** (Relative Humidity)
- **Typical ink molecules:** **Alkanethiols (HS-(CH₂)ₙ-CH₃)** on Au substrates, alkylsilanes on SiO₂
- **Writing speed:** **0.01 – 10 µm/s**
- **Tip-substrate gap during writing:** ~**0.5 – 5 nm**
- **Driving force:** Laplace pressure from capillary condensation + concentration gradient (Fickian diffusion)
- **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — relevant for thermal-activated diffusion modeling

> [!TIP]
> For exam purposes, remember the *three pillars* of DPN: **(i) Tip (Pen), (ii) Meniscus (Capillary Bridge), (iii) Chemisorption (Pattern Fixing)**.

---

## 1.4 Why DPN Matters in Nanotechnology

| Aspect | DPN Advantage |
|---|---|
| Resolution | Sub-50 nm routinely; sub-15 nm with care |
| Pattern Flexibility | Arbitrary, mask-less direct writing |
| Chemistry | Compatible with biomolecules, proteins, DNA |
| Cost | Reuses same tip hundreds of times |
| Throughput | Pen-array parallelization (up to ~55,000 tips in Mirkin's polymer pen arrays) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

DPN operates through a sequence of physical and chemical phenomena:

1. **Tip Coating (Inking):**
   The AFM tip is dipped into a solution containing the desired "ink" molecules. After evaporation of the solvent, a thin film of ink is adsorbed onto the tip surface. The amount deposited is controlled by immersion time and concentration.

2. **Meniscus Formation (Capillary Condensation):**
   When the tip is brought within a few nanometers of the substrate under sufficient relative humidity, water vapor condenses in the narrow gap due to the **Kelvin effect** (capillary condensation). This creates a liquid bridge — the meniscus.

3. **Molecular Transport (Diffusion):**
   The ink molecules dissolve into the water meniscus and migrate from the tip (high chemical potential) to the substrate (low chemical potential) via **surface diffusion** and **bulk diffusion through the meniscus**.

4. **Chemisorption (Pattern Anchoring):**
   The molecules react with the substrate surface (e.g., S–H bond cleavage and S–Au bond formation for thiols on gold) to form a self-assembled monolayer (SAM). This locks the pattern in place.

5. **Pattern Translation:**
   The tip is raster-scanned (in contact or tapping mode) across the substrate under computer control, "writing" the desired 2D pattern.

---

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters

- **Step 1** determines the ink reservoir capacity.
- **Step 2** is critical because meniscus size ∝ humidity; no meniscus → no transport.
- **Step 3** sets the deposition rate; governed by Fick's laws of diffusion.
- **Step 4** ensures pattern stability and chemical specificity.
- **Step 5** enables arbitrary pattern design without masks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kelvin equation: $\ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r \, R T}$ | Governs capillary condensation; relates meniscus radius $r$ to relative humidity $P/P_0$. $\gamma$ = surface tension, $V_m$ = molar volume, $\theta$ = contact angle. | $r$ in m |
| 2 | Fick's First Law: $J = -D \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Flux of ink molecules from tip to substrate; $D$ = diffusion coefficient. | $J$ in mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 3 | Diffusion Coefficient (Arrhenius): $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of surface diffusion. $E_a$ = activation energy. | $D$ in m²/s |
| 4 | Feature size ∝ $\dfrac{D}{\text{tip speed}}$ | Higher scan speed → smaller dot (less time for diffusion). | nm vs µm/s |
| 5 | Meniscus volume scaling: $V_m \approx \dfrac{2\pi R_{tip}^2 \, \Delta}{1 + \dfrac{\Delta}{R_{tip}}}$ | Approximate volume of the water bridge. $R_{tip}$ = tip radius, $\Delta$ = tip-substrate separation. | m³ |
| 6 | Coverage (Langmuir): $\theta(t) = 1 - \exp(-k\,t)$ | Fractional monolayer coverage grows exponentially with time $t$ at constant $k$. | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Tip-substrate force: $F \approx k_c \, \Delta z$ | Cantilever deflection; $k_c$ = spring constant (~0.01 – 50 N/m). | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Table Safety Note:** All mathematical expressions above use $\vert$ alternatives (implicit) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Vertical bars are deliberately not embedded in any cell.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How DPN is Used |
|---|---|
| **Biosensors** | Direct immobilization of proteins/DNA for label-free detection |
| **Molecular Electronics** | Fabrication of single-molecule circuits and electrodes |
| **Catalysis Research** | Patterning catalyst islands at defined nanoscale locations |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | High-throughput screening of surface-bound drug candidates |
| **Cell Adhesion Studies** | Patterning RGD peptides to control cell growth |
| **Anti-counterfeiting** | Sub-wavelength fluorescent tags for security printing |

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks about **comparisons** (e.g., DPN vs photolithography). Memorize the table values above for 7- or 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kelvin Radius of the Capillary Meniscus

The size of the water meniscus is governed by the **Kelvin equation**, which balances the chemical potential of water inside the curved meniscus with that of the surrounding vapor.

### Step 1: Chemical potential equality at equilibrium

At equilibrium between the meniscus and the vapor phase, the chemical potentials must be equal:

$$\mu_{liquid}(T, P_{inside}) = \mu_{vapor}(T, P)$$

where $P_{inside}$ is the pressure inside the curved meniscus and $P$ is the ambient vapor pressure.

### Step 2: Pressure difference across a curved interface (Laplace pressure)

By the **Young–Laplace equation**, the pressure difference across a spherical meniscus of radius $r$ is:

$$\Delta P = P_{inside} - P = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{r}$$

For a meniscus on a non-ideal surface with contact angle $\theta$, the effective radius is $r_{\text{eff}} = r / \cos\theta$, giving:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

### Step 3: Integrate the chemical potential change

The change in chemical potential of the liquid due to pressure change is:

$$\Delta \mu = \int_{P}^{P_{inside}} V_m \, dP = V_m \cdot \Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r}$$

### Step 4: Equate to vapor-phase chemical potential

For an ideal gas, $\mu_{vapor} = \mu^0 + RT \ln(P/P_0)$, so the change in chemical potential on vapor side is:

$$\Delta \mu = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$$

### Step 5: Combine to obtain the Kelvin equation

Setting the two expressions equal:

$$RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r}$$

Solving for the relative humidity (RH) $P/P_0$:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r \, R T}\;}$$

This is the **Kelvin equation**. It shows that a smaller meniscus radius $r$ requires a higher $P/P_0$ (higher humidity) to be thermodynamically stable. This is why **higher RH** → **larger meniscus** → **more ink transport** → **faster writing**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Feature Size from Diffusive Transport

Assume a constant surface concentration $C_0$ at the tip apex and zero concentration far away on the substrate. The diffusive flux is governed by Fick's First Law in the meniscus:

$$J(x, t) = -D \, \dfrac{\partial C(x, t)}{\partial x}$$

### Step 1: Mass conservation in 1D

The accumulation rate equals the divergence of flux:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 2: Boundary conditions

$C(0, t) = C_0$ at the tip contact line, and $C(\infty, t) = 0$ far on the substrate.

### Step 3: Solution for steady-state writing

At steady state ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$), the spatial profile is exponential:

$$C(x) = C_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x}{L}\right)$$

where $L = \sqrt{D \tau}$ is the characteristic diffusion length and $\tau$ is the residence time of the tip at a given location.

### Step 4: Relate tip residence time to scan speed

If the tip moves at scan velocity $v$ across a dot of diameter $d$, the residence time is $\tau = d / v$. The feature width scales as:

$$\boxed{\;d \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{v}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Higher scan speed $v$ → smaller $d$ (finer features). This is a classic KTU exam question type: *qualitatively explain how feature size depends on writing speed.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Langmuir-Type Surface Coverage

The number of adsorbed molecules per unit area $N(t)$ grows as more molecules chemisorb onto the substrate. In Langmuir kinetics:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = k \, (N_{max} - N)$$

### Step 1: Separate variables

$$\dfrac{dN}{N_{max} - N} = k \, dt$$

### Step 2: Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t$

$$\int_0^N \dfrac{dN'}{N_{max} - N'} = \int_0^t k \, dt'$$

### Step 3: Evaluate integrals

$$-\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_{max} - N}{N_{max}}\right) = k t$$

### Step 4: Solve for fractional coverage $\theta = N/N_{max}$

$$\boxed{\;\theta(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t)\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** After a characteristic time $t \approx 3/k$, the monolayer is ~95% complete. This governs the *saturation* time of DPN dots.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameters vs Output

| Process Parameter | Effect on Feature Size | Effect on Deposition Rate | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Relative Humidity** | ↑ (larger meniscus) | ↑ | "Humid = Fat dots" |
| **↑ Temperature** | Mixed (faster D, smaller t) | ↑ | "Hot = Fast, but unstable" |
| **↑ Scan Speed** | ↓ | ↓ | "Speedy = Skinny lines" |
| **↑ Tip Radius** | ↑ (larger contact) | ↑ | "Blunt tip = Broad line" |
| **↑ Ink-chain length (n)** | Mild effect | ↓ (slower diffusion) | "Long chains = Slow flow" |
| **↑ Cantilever $k_c$** | Unchanged | Indirect | "Stiff tip = Stable contact" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow of DPN — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    G -- No --> I[Increase Humidity or Reduce Gap]
    H --> J[Ink Molecules Dissolve into Meniscus]
    J --> K[Surface Diffusion Down Concentration Gradient]
    K --> L[Chemisorption of S-Au Bond Formation on Gold]
    L --> M[Raster Scan Tip via Piezo Scanner]
    M --> N[Pattern of Self Assembled Monolayer Forms]
    N --> O[Retract Tip and Image via AFM or LFM]
    O --> P[End: Nanoscale Pattern Realized]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Define Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN). Mention any two advantages of DPN over conventional photolithography.**

**Model Answer:**
Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN) is a direct-write, mask-less scanning probe lithography technique that uses an AFM tip as a "pen" to deposit molecules onto a substrate through a capillary water meniscus.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Higher Resolution** — DPN achieves sub-50 nm features routinely, far exceeding photolithography's diffraction-limited resolution.
2. **Mask-less Operation** — DPN writes patterns directly under software control, eliminating the need for costly photomasks.
3. (Bonus) **Chemical Flexibility** — Compatible with biomolecules, SAMs, and organic inks; photolithography is largely restricted to photoresists.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for two valid advantages.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What is the role of the water meniscus in Dip-Pen Nanolithography?**

**Model Answer:**
The water meniscus is the capillary bridge that forms between the AFM tip and the substrate due to capillary condensation under controlled humidity. Its role is to:
1. **Transport ink molecules** via surface and bulk diffusion from the tip to the substrate.
2. **Confine the reaction zone** to a nanometer-scale volume, enabling ultra-high-resolution patterning.
3. **Maintain the chemical potential gradient** that drives continuous molecular deposition.

The meniscus size is governed by the **Kelvin equation**: $\ln(P/P_0) = \dfrac{2\gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r R T}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

#### (a) Describe the working principle of Dip-Pen Nanolithography with a neat schematic diagram. Explain the role of the water meniscus. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN) is a scanning probe lithography technique developed by Mirkin et al. in 1999. It uses an AFM tip to directly deposit molecular "ink" onto a substrate with nanometer precision.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (4 marks):**
1. **Inking the Tip:** The AFM tip is coated with the desired ink molecules (e.g., alkanethiols HS-(CH₂)ₙ-CH₃) by dipping in a dilute solution. The solvent evaporates, leaving a thin molecular film on the tip.
2. **Environmental Control:** The experiment is conducted inside a humidity-controlled chamber (typically 30–70% RH).
3. **Meniscus Formation:** As the tip approaches the substrate (gap ~0.5–5 nm), water vapor condenses in the gap due to the **Kelvin effect**, forming a nanoscale water meniscus.
4. **Molecular Transport:** Ink molecules dissolve into the meniscus and diffuse from the tip (high concentration) to the substrate (low concentration) under a chemical potential gradient.
5. **Chemisorption:** On the substrate, the molecules form covalent or strong chemical bonds (e.g., S–Au) producing a stable self-assembled monolayer (SAM).
6. **Patterning:** The piezo-electric scanner moves the tip across the substrate, "writing" the desired 2D pattern under computer control.

**Step 3 — Schematic Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram — must show tip, meniscus, substrate, and arrow indicating ink flow.)*

**Step 4 — Role of Water Meniscus (1 mark):**
The water meniscus is the **conduit for ink transport** and **defines the deposition area**. Its size depends on humidity (via Kelvin equation) — higher RH → larger meniscus → faster but coarser writing. Without the meniscus, no molecular transport occurs.

---

#### (b) Discuss the factors affecting the resolution and deposition rate in DPN. Derive the relationship between feature size and scan speed. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Factors Affecting Resolution and Deposition Rate (4 marks):**

| Factor | Effect on Resolution | Effect on Deposition Rate |
|---|---|---|
| **Relative Humidity** | Higher RH → larger meniscus → coarser features | Higher RH → faster transport → higher rate |
| **Scan Speed** | Higher speed → finer features | Higher speed → less deposition time → lower coverage |
| **Temperature** | Increases molecular diffusion; can broaden features | Increases D exponentially via Arrhenius equation |
| **Tip Radius** | Larger tip → larger contact area → coarser features | Larger tip → larger ink reservoir → higher rate |
| **Ink chain length (n)** | Longer chains → slower diffusion → finer features | Longer chains → lower diffusion coefficient → lower rate |
| **Substrate chemistry** | Strong chemisorption → stable but slower growth | High binding affinity → high initial rate |

**Step 2 — Derivation of Feature Size vs Scan Speed (3 marks):**

From Fick's first law, the steady-state surface diffusion profile is:

$$C(x) = C_0 \exp(-x / L), \quad L = \sqrt{D \tau}$$

The residence time of the tip over a dot of diameter $d$ at scan speed $v$ is $\tau = d / v$. The feature width scales with the diffusion length:

$$d \approx \sqrt{D \tau} = \sqrt{D \cdot \dfrac{d}{v}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$d^2 \approx \dfrac{D \, d}{v} \implies d \approx \dfrac{D}{v}$$

More rigorously, treating $d$ as the diffusion length:

$$\boxed{\;d \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{v}}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing scan speed $v$ reduces feature size $d$ (finer patterns), but also reduces the number of deposited molecules (lighter coverage). This is the classical **trade-off** in DPN optimization.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) Explain the Kelvin equation and discuss its significance in Dip-Pen Nanolithography. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Kelvin Equation (2 marks):**

For a spherical meniscus of radius $r$ in equilibrium with its vapor at temperature $T$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = \dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r \, R T}$$

where:
- $P/P_0$ = relative humidity (RH)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of water (~0.072 N/m at 25°C)
- $V_m$ = molar volume of water (~18 × 10⁻⁶ m³/mol)
- $\theta$ = contact angle of water on tip/substrate
- $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)

**Step 2 — Physical Origin (2 marks):**

The equation arises from equating the chemical potentials of water in the liquid (curved) phase and the vapor phase:

$$RT \ln(P/P_0) = V_m \cdot \Delta P = V_m \cdot \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

The Laplace pressure $\Delta P$ inside a smaller meniscus is higher, requiring a higher ambient humidity to sustain the liquid phase.

**Step 3 — Significance in DPN (3 marks):**
1. **Meniscus Size Control:** The equation tells us how the meniscus radius $r$ depends on RH. To get $r = 10$ nm at 25°C, the required RH is ~70–80%.
2. **Pattern Resolution:** A smaller $r$ (lower RH) leads to smaller feature sizes → finer patterns.
3. **Process Optimization:** By tuning RH, one can directly control the meniscus size and thus the diffusion flux and pattern dimensions.
4. **Environmental Requirement:** DPN cannot operate in ultra-dry environments (RH < 20%) because the meniscus will not form, halting all molecular transport.

---

#### (b) Compare Dip-Pen Nanolithography with conventional photolithography and Electron Beam Lithography (EBL). Mention at least four key parameters. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | DPN | Photolithography | EBL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | 10–100 nm | ~30–50 nm (advanced) | <10 nm |
| **Throughput** | Low (serial) | Very High | Very Low (serial) |
| **Mask Required** | No (mask-less) | Yes | No (mask-less) |
| **Operating Environment** | Controlled humidity | Cleanroom, any humidity | High vacuum |
| **Equipment Cost** | Moderate (~$100k–500k) | Very High (multi-million $) | Very High (~$1–5M) |
| **Ink/Material Flexibility** | Excellent (biomolecules) | Limited (photoresists) | Limited (resists) |
| **Substrate Compatibility** | Au, Si, SiO₂, glass | Mainly flat wafers | Mainly flat wafers |
| **Pattern Type** | Arbitrary 2D | Mask-defined | Arbitrary 2D |

**Key Advantages of DPN (2 marks for these):**
- **Molecular-level chemical specificity** — can deposit biomolecules directly.
- **Low capital cost** — repurposes existing AFM hardware.
- **Tip arrays** — Mirkin's polymer pen arrays enable ~10⁵ parallel tips.

**Limitations of DPN (1 mark):**
- Slow serial writing limits large-area production.
- Highly sensitive to humidity and tip wear.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DPN Questions:**
> 1. **Missing the Meniscus** — Many students forget to mention the *water meniscus* explicitly. Examiners specifically look for "capillary bridge / Kelvin condensation" as the transport medium. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing AFM and STM** — DPN uses **AFM** (atomic force), not STM (tunneling current). Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong Resolution Value** — Photolithography reaches ~30 nm in advanced labs, but DPN's *typical* best is ~10–15 nm. Don't claim DPN routinely hits 1 nm — that is EBL territory.
> 4. **Skipping the Kelvin Equation** — In any 7+ mark question on DPN, the Kelvin equation is **mandatory**. Without it, expect to lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **No Diagram** — A neat, labeled schematic showing tip + meniscus + substrate carries **at least 1 mark**. Always include one.
> 6. **Wrong Inks** — Do not write "DPN uses photoresist." The ink is *molecular* (e.g., alkanethiols), not a polymer resist.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DPN** = direct-write scanning probe lithography using AFM tip + molecular ink.
- **Inventor:** Mirkin's group, Northwestern University, **1999**.
- **Mechanism:** Capillary condensation → water meniscus → molecular diffusion → chemisorption.
- **Kelvin equation** governs meniscus size: $\ln(P/P_0) = 2\gamma V_m \cos\theta / (r R T)$.
- **Feature size** scales as $d \approx \sqrt{D/v}$ — higher scan speed → finer features.
- **Coverage kinetics:** $\theta(t) = 1 - \exp(-kt)$.
- **Typical operating RH:** 30–70%.
- **Typical inks:** Alkanethiols (HS-(CH₂)ₙ-CH₃) on Au; alkylsilanes on SiO₂.
- **Resolution range:** 10–100 nm; best ~5–15 nm.
- **Advantages:** Mask-less, high resolution, chemically flexible, low-cost.
- **Limitations:** Slow serial writing, humidity-sensitive, tip wear.
- **Applications:** Biosensors, molecular electronics, cell adhesion, anti-counterfeiting, catalysis.
- **Comparison memory anchor:**
  - DPN vs Photolithography: DPN = mask-less + biomolecules; Photolithography = fast + wafers.
  - DPN vs EBL: DPN = chemical + moderate cost; EBL = highest resolution + vacuum + slow.
- **Always draw the schematic** showing the AFM tip, water meniscus, and substrate — examiners reward it.
- **Three pillars to mention in any answer:** Tip (Pen) + Meniscus (Bridge) + Chemisorption (SAM).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
